topics.title: Introduction to food and nutrients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to food and nutrients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — Introduction to Food and Nutrients

## 1. Core Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Food?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Food is any substance, whether processed, semi-processed, or raw, which is intended for human consumption. It provides nutrients, which are substances essential for the maintenance, growth, energy, reproduction, and overall health of the living organism.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Food is not just about filling the stomach — it is a *biochemical fuel* and a *structural building material* for the human body. Every bite delivers macronutrients, micronutrients, water, and dietary non-nutrients (like fibre and phytochemicals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by Origin:** Foods are broadly classified as:
> - **Foods of Animal Origin** — milk, meat, fish, eggs
> - **Foods of Plant Origin** — cereals, pulses, vegetables, fruits, nuts
> - **Foods of Microbial Origin** — mushrooms, fermented products, single-cell protein

---

### 1.2 What are Nutrients?

**Formal Definition:**
Nutrients are chemical substances present in food that are utilized by the body to sustain life, support growth, repair tissues, regulate metabolic processes, and provide energy. They are absorbed, assimilated, and utilized at the cellular level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Major Classes of Nutrients (Recognized by ICMR & KTU Syllabus):**
> 1. **Carbohydrates**
> 2. **Proteins**
> 3. **Fats (Lipids)**
> 4. **Vitamins**
> 5. **Minerals**
> 6. **Water**

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building and Fuel" Analogy

Imagine the human body as a **construction site that operates 24/7**:

- **Carbohydrates and Fats** are like the **diesel/petrol** that runs the heavy machinery (energy supply).
- **Proteins** are like the **bricks, steel rods, and cement** used to build walls, repair cracks, and erect new structures (body building and repair).
- **Vitamins and Minerals** are like the **electricians and plumbers** — they don't build, but they manage critical control systems (metabolic regulation).
- **Water** is the **universal solvent and coolant** that keeps everything flowing, dissolves materials, removes waste, and controls temperature.

Without any one of these, the construction site halts — the body fails to function optimally.

---

### 1.4 Macronutrients vs Micronutrients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-Yielding Macronutrients (Calorie Providers):** Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats.
> **Non-Energy Macronutrients:** Water, Fibre.
> **Micronutrients (Required in trace amounts):** Vitamins, Minerals.

The energy unit used universally is the **kilocalorie (kcal)**. One **kilocalorie = 1,000 small calories** = the energy required to raise 1 kg of water by 1 °C. Internationally, the **kilojoule (kJ)** is also used, where **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic of Nutrients

The classification follows a logical hierarchy based on **quantity required** and **function performed**:

### 2.1.1 By Quantity Required
- **Macronutrients** — required in **grams** per day (g/day). Total daily intake > 100 mg.
- **Micronutrients** — required in **milligrams (mg)** or **micrograms (µg)** per day. Daily intake < 100 mg.

### 2.1.2 By Energy Provision
- **Energy-Yielding Nutrients** — Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats (provide kcal).
- **Non-Energy Yielding Nutrients** — Vitamins, Minerals, Water.

### 2.1.3 By Essentiality
- **Essential Nutrients** — cannot be synthesized in the body; must come from diet (e.g., essential amino acids, essential fatty acids, vitamins).
- **Non-Essential Nutrients** — synthesized in the body (e.g., glucose from glycerol, certain amino acids).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Functions of Each Nutrient

### 2.2.1 Carbohydrates
- **Primary source of energy** — provide **4 kcal/g**.
- Sparing protein (preventing its use as fuel).
- Provide dietary fibre, which aids digestion.
- **Simple carbohydrates** — glucose, fructose, sucrose (quick energy).
- **Complex carbohydrates** — starch, glycogen (sustained energy).

### 2.2.2 Proteins
- **Body building and repair** — provide **4 kcal/g**.
- Source of essential amino acids.
- Form enzymes, hormones, antibodies, haemoglobin.
- Maintain fluid and acid-base balance.

### 2.2.3 Fats (Lipids)
- **Concentrated energy source** — provide **9 kcal/g** (more than double carbohydrates).
- Carry fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
- Insulate organs, provide thermal regulation.
- Source of essential fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic).

### 2.2.4 Vitamins
- **Organic compounds** required in small amounts.
- Regulate metabolism, immunity, vision, blood clotting.
- Classified as **fat-soluble (A, D, E, K)** and **water-soluble (B-complex, C)**.

### 2.2.5 Minerals
- **Inorganic elements** that cannot be synthesized.
- Major minerals — Ca, P, K, Na, Mg, S (required in > 100 mg/day).
- Trace minerals — Fe, Zn, Cu, I, Se, Mn (required in < 100 mg/day).
- Role — bone formation, oxygen transport, nerve function, enzyme cofactors.

### 2.2.6 Water
- **Most critical nutrient** — humans can survive weeks without food but only days without water.
- **~ 60% of body weight** in adults.
- Solvent, temperature regulator, transport medium, lubricant.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Summary Tables

> [!NOTE]
> **The following tables are KTU High-Yield Revision Tables — Memorize These for Module 1.**

### Table 1: Energy Provided by Macronutrients

| Nutrient | Energy Yield (per gram) | Energy in kJ | Body Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbohydrates | **4 kcal/g** | **16.7 kJ/g** | Primary fuel for brain and muscles |
| Proteins | **4 kcal/g** | **16.7 kJ/g** | Tissue building and repair |
| Fats | **9 kcal/g** | **37.6 kJ/g** | Concentrated energy, insulation |

### Table 2: Recommended Distribution of Total Energy (ICMR Standards)

| Nutrient | % of Total Daily Energy |
| :--- | :--- |
| Carbohydrates | **55 - 65 %** |
| Proteins | **10 - 15 %** |
| Fats | **20 - 30 %** |

### Table 3: Solubility-Based Vitamin Classification

| Property | Fat-Soluble Vitamins | Water-Soluble Vitamins |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Members | A, D, E, K | B-complex, C |
| Storage | Stored in body fat/liver | Not stored (excreted) |
| Toxicity risk | Higher (can accumulate) | Lower |
| Daily intake | Required regularly | Required daily |

### Table 4: Adult Daily RDA — Reference Values (Approximate)

| Nutrient | Approximate Adult RDA |
| :--- | :--- |
| Energy | 2000 - 2400 kcal/day |
| Protein | **0.83 g/kg body weight/day** |
| Carbohydrate | 130 g/day (minimum) |
| Fat | 20 - 35 % of total energy |
| Vitamin C | 40 mg/day |
| Iron | 17 mg/day (men) / 21 mg/day (women) |
| Calcium | 1000 mg/day |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The energy calculations in food technology have direct applications in:

- **Food labelling & nutritional packaging** — accurate kcal display on product packets.
- **Diet planning software** — used in hospitals, fitness apps, and clinical nutrition.
- **Food fortification programs** — adding micronutrients to staple foods (e.g., iodine in salt, iron in rice).
- **Public health policy** — designing school lunch programmes, ration distributions, and emergency food relief.
- **Sports nutrition & military rations** — precise calorie budgeting for performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Total Caloric Value of a Mixed Meal

This is a **standard 5-mark KTU derivation question**. Understand the logic step-by-step.

**Problem Statement:**
A person consumes in one day:
- 300 g of carbohydrates
- 80 g of proteins
- 70 g of fats

Calculate the total energy intake in **kcal** and **kJ**.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Energy Yield of Each Nutrient

From the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.3, Table 1):
- Carbohydrates → 4 kcal/g
- Proteins → 4 kcal/g
- Fats → 9 kcal/g

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Multiplicative Formula

$$
E_{total} = (m_{carb} \times 4) + (m_{prot} \times 4) + (m_{fat} \times 9)
$$

Where:
- $E_{total}$ = total energy in kcal
- $m_{carb}$ = mass of carbohydrates in grams
- $m_{prot}$ = mass of proteins in grams
- $m_{fat}$ = mass of fats in grams

---

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
E_{total} = (300 \times 4) + (80 \times 4) + (70 \times 9)
$$

$$
E_{total} = 1200 + 320 + 630
$$

$$
E_{total} = 2150 \text{ kcal}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Convert to kilojoules using the Conversion Factor

The conversion identity is:

$$
1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_{kJ} = E_{kcal} \times 4.184
$$

$$
E_{kJ} = 2150 \times 4.184
$$

$$
E_{kJ} = 8995.6 \text{ kJ} \approx 8996 \text{ kJ}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Verification and Conclusion

Cross-check by computing percentage contribution:
- Carbohydrates: $1200 / 2150 = 55.8\%$
- Proteins: $320 / 2150 = 14.9\%$
- Fats: $630 / 2150 = 29.3\%$

These percentages fall within the **ICMR recommended distribution** (55-65% carbs, 10-15% protein, 20-30% fats). Therefore, the meal is **nutritionally balanced** in macronutrient ratio.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Recommended Daily Protein Intake for an Adult

**Problem Statement:**
A healthy adult male weighs **70 kg**. Calculate the **Recommended Daily Allowance (RDA)** of protein using the ICMR standard.

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard Formula:**

$$
\text{Protein RDA (g/day)} = \text{Body Weight (kg)} \times 0.83
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{Protein RDA} = 70 \times 0.83 = 58.1 \text{ g/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**
- A sedentary adult requires 0.83 g/kg/day.
- Pregnant women, lactating mothers, athletes, and children require 1.0 - 1.5 g/kg/day.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Nutrient Deficiency and Excess Disorders

This is a **high-frequency 7-mark KTU question**.

| Nutrient | Major Function | Deficiency Disease / Symptom | Excess Toxicity Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vitamin A | Vision, immunity | Night blindness, xerophthalmia | Hypervitaminosis A (liver damage) |
| Vitamin D | Calcium absorption | Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults) | Hypercalcemia |
| Vitamin C | Collagen synthesis, immunity | Scurvy (bleeding gums) | Kidney stones |
| Iron | Haemoglobin formation | Anaemia | Haemochromatosis |
| Calcium | Bone formation | Osteoporosis | Kidney stones |
| Iodine | Thyroid hormone | Goitre, cretinism | Thyroid dysfunction |
| Protein | Tissue building | Kwashiorkor, marasmus | Renal stress |
| Carbohydrate | Energy | Hypoglycaemia, fatigue | Obesity, diabetes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Food and Nutrients

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FOOD] --> B[Foods of Plant Origin]
    A --> C[Foods of Animal Origin]
    A --> D[Foods of Microbial Origin]

    B --> B1[Cereals and Millets]
    B --> B2[Pulses and Legumes]
    B --> B3[Fruits and Vegetables]
    B --> B4[Nuts and Oilseeds]

    C --> C1[Milk and Dairy]
    C --> C2[Meat and Poultry]
    C --> C3[Fish and Seafood]
    C --> C4[Eggs]

    D --> D1[Fermented Foods]
    D --> D2[Mushrooms]
    D --> D3[Single Cell Protein]

    A --> E[NUTRIENTS]

    E --> F[MACRONUTRIENTS]
    E --> G[MICRONUTRIENTS]

    F --> F1[Carbohydrates 4 kcal per g]
    F --> F2[Proteins 4 kcal per g]
    F --> F3[Fats 9 kcal per g]
    F --> F4[Water]
    F --> F5[ Dietary Fibre]

    G --> G1[Vitamins]
    G --> G2[Minerals]

    G1 --> G1A[Fat Soluble A D E K]
    G1 --> G1B[Water Soluble B Complex and C]

    G2 --> G2A[Major Minerals Ca P Na K]
    G2 --> G2B[Trace Minerals Fe Zn I Se]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Nutrient Roles

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Food Intake] --> P1[Carbohydrates and Fats]
    IN --> P2[Proteins]
    IN --> P3[Vitamins and Minerals]
    IN --> P4[Water]

    P1 --> OUT1[Energy Production ATP]
    P1 --> OUT2[Insulation and Storage]

    P2 --> OUT3[Tissue Building]
    P2 --> OUT4[Enzyme and Hormone Synthesis]
    P2 --> OUT5[Immune Function]

    P3 --> OUT6[Metabolic Regulation]
    P3 --> OUT7[Bone and Teeth Formation]
    P3 --> OUT8[Oxygen Transport]
    P3 --> OUT9[Vision and Nerve Function]

    P4 --> OUT10[Temperature Regulation]
    P4 --> OUT11[Nutrient Transport]
    P4 --> OUT12[Waste Removal]
    P4 --> OUT13[Chemical Reactions Medium]

    OUT1 --> HEALTH[Human Health and Survival]
    OUT2 --> HEALTH
    OUT3 --> HEALTH
    OUT4 --> HEALTH
    OUT5 --> HEALTH
    OUT6 --> HEALTH
    OUT7 --> HEALTH
    OUT8 --> HEALTH
    OUT9 --> HEALTH
    OUT10 --> HEALTH
    OUT11 --> HEALTH
    OUT12 --> HEALTH
    OUT13 --> HEALTH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read:** Food enters the body and is broken down into six nutrient classes. Each class has a specific functional role. The convergence point **HUMAN HEALTH** represents the integrated output — proper intake of all nutrients is required for full physiological well-being. A deficiency in even one branch will compromise the overall system.

---

## 4.3 Diagram Explanation (Plain English)

- **Left block (Input)** — Food we eat.
- **Middle blocks (Processing)** — The four functional nutrient groups, each performing specialized tasks.
- **Right block (Output)** — Sustained human health.

This mirrors a **production line in a food processing industry** — raw material input, intermediate processing, finished product output. The same engineering logic applies to the human digestive-metabolic system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define food. List the six major classes of nutrients.**

**Model Answer:**

Food is any substance consumed by living organisms to provide nutritional support for the body. It supplies nutrients required for growth, energy, maintenance, and repair of body tissues.

The **six major classes of nutrients** are:
1. Carbohydrates
2. Proteins
3. Fats (Lipids)
4. Vitamins
5. Minerals
6. Water

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to include **water** as a nutrient. Water is the most critical nutrient and is always listed as one of the six major classes. Losing this point is common.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between macronutrients and micronutrients. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Macronutrients | Micronutrients |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quantity required | Grams per day | Milligrams or micrograms per day |
| Energy provision | Usually provide energy | Do not provide energy |
| Examples | Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats | Vitamins, Minerals |
| Storage | Can be stored in body | Generally not stored |

**Examples of Macronutrients:** Carbohydrates, Proteins
**Examples of Micronutrients:** Vitamin C, Iron

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from the choice. Each question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the classification of nutrients with suitable examples. Differentiate between essential and non-essential nutrients.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A person consumes 350 g carbohydrates, 90 g protein, and 60 g fat in a day. Calculate the total caloric intake in kcal and kJ. Comment on whether the macronutrient distribution is balanced as per ICMR standards.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Classification of Nutrients:**

Nutrients are classified into the following categories:

**1. Based on Quantity Required:**
- **Macronutrients** — required in large quantities (g/day). Examples: Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats, Water.
- **Micronutrients** — required in trace quantities (mg or µg/day). Examples: Vitamins, Minerals.

**2. Based on Energy Provision:**
- **Energy-yielding nutrients** — Carbohydrates (4 kcal/g), Proteins (4 kcal/g), Fats (9 kcal/g).
- **Non-energy yielding nutrients** — Vitamins, Minerals, Water.

**3. Based on Essentiality:**

**Essential Nutrients** cannot be synthesized by the body and must be obtained from the diet. Examples:
- Essential amino acids (e.g., lysine, tryptophan — 9 in number)
- Essential fatty acids (linoleic acid, alpha-linolenic acid)
- Vitamins (all vitamins)
- Minerals (all minerals)

**Non-Essential Nutrients** can be synthesized by the body. Examples:
- Glucose (synthesized from glycerol and amino acids via gluconeogenesis)
- Non-essential amino acids (e.g., alanine, glutamic acid)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write only "essential = needed, non-essential = not needed." You must explain that *essential* means the body **cannot synthesize it** in adequate amounts. The word "synthesis" must appear in your answer for full marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Carbohydrates = 350 g
- Proteins = 90 g
- Fats = 60 g

**Step 1 — Energy from each macronutrient:**

$$
E_{carb} = 350 \times 4 = 1400 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{prot} = 90 \times 4 = 360 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{fat} = 60 \times 9 = 540 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy:**

$$
E_{total} = 1400 + 360 + 540 = 2300 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kJ:**

$$
E_{kJ} = 2300 \times 4.184 = 9623.2 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Distribution:**

$$
\% \text{ Carbs} = \frac{1400}{2300} \times 100 = 60.87\%
$$

$$
\% \text{ Protein} = \frac{360}{2300} \times 100 = 15.65\%
$$

$$
\% \text{ Fat} = \frac{540}{2300} \times 100 = 23.48\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with ICMR Standards (Carbs 55-65%, Protein 10-15%, Fat 20-30%):**

| Nutrient | Computed | ICMR Range | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbohydrates | 60.87 % | 55 - 65 % | ✓ Balanced |
| Proteins | 15.65 % | 10 - 15 % | ✓ Within limit |
| Fats | 23.48 % | 20 - 30 % | ✓ Balanced |

**Conclusion:** The macronutrient distribution is **balanced and acceptable** as per ICMR guidelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Always show **all four calculation steps** — energy from each nutrient, total energy, conversion to kJ, and percentage distribution. Skipping the percentage table forfeits 2-3 marks. Also, **always state the ICMR range explicitly** before drawing the conclusion.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the functions of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats in the human body. Mention their energy yield.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** What is a balanced diet? Explain the food groups as per the Basic Four Food Group Plan with examples.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Carbohydrates:**
- Primary source of energy for the body and the brain.
- Provide **4 kcal/g**.
- Spare proteins from being used as energy.
- Provide dietary fibre for digestive health.
- Sources: rice, wheat, sugar, fruits, honey.

**Proteins:**
- Essential for growth, repair, and maintenance of body tissues.
- Provide **4 kcal/g** (used as secondary energy source).
- Form enzymes, hormones, antibodies, and haemoglobin.
- Maintain osmotic balance and acid-base equilibrium.
- Sources: milk, eggs, meat, fish, pulses, soy.

**Fats (Lipids):**
- Concentrated source of energy — provide **9 kcal/g**.
- Aid in absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
- Provide insulation and protect vital organs.
- Supply essential fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic).
- Sources: oils, ghee, butter, nuts, fatty fish.

**Summary Table:**

| Nutrient | Energy Yield | Main Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbohydrates | 4 kcal/g | Primary energy |
| Proteins | 4 kcal/g | Tissue building |
| Fats | 9 kcal/g | Concentrated energy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The energy values (**4, 4, 9 kcal/g**) are **must-write** numbers. Writing only "carbs give energy" without the numerical value will cost you 1-2 marks. Examiner's pen is unforgiving on numerical omissions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definition of Balanced Diet:**
A balanced diet is one that provides all the essential nutrients in the correct proportion, quantity, and quality to meet the body's daily requirements for energy, growth, repair, and maintenance of health.

**Basic Four Food Group Plan:**

The Basic Four Food Group Plan divides foods into four groups, and a balanced diet includes appropriate servings from each group daily.

**1. Milk and Milk Products Group:**
- Provides: Protein, calcium, riboflavin, vitamin B12.
- Examples: Milk, curd, paneer, cheese, buttermilk.
- Recommended: 2-3 servings/day.

**2. Meat, Fish, Eggs, and Pulses Group:**
- Provides: High-quality protein, iron, vitamin B12, zinc.
- Examples: Chicken, fish, eggs, dal, beans, soybean.
- Recommended: 2-3 servings/day.

**3. Fruits and Vegetables Group:**
- Provides: Vitamins (especially A and C), minerals, fibre, antioxidants.
- Examples: Spinach, carrot, orange, banana, tomato.
- Recommended: 4-5 servings/day.

**4. Cereals, Millets, and Tubers Group:**
- Provides: Carbohydrates, B-vitamins, energy, fibre.
- Examples: Rice, wheat, ragi, jowar, potato.
- Recommended: 6-11 servings/day.

**Conclusion:** A balanced diet ensures that the body receives all nutrients in adequate amounts. Eating a variety of foods from all four groups daily is the simplest way to maintain good health.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **Basic Four Food Group Plan** with the **Five Food Group Plan** (which adds fats/sugars as a fifth group). Stick to whichever plan the question asks. Also, **servings per day** must be quoted for full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Introduction to Food and Nutrients":**

- ✅ **Food** = any substance providing nutritional support to the body.
- ✅ **Six classes of nutrients** = Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats, Vitamins, Minerals, Water.
- ✅ **Energy values (memorize):** Carbs = 4 kcal/g, Proteins = 4 kcal/g, Fats = 9 kcal/g.
- ✅ **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ** (always quote this conversion factor).
- ✅ **Macronutrients** = grams/day. **Micronutrients** = mg or µg/day.
- ✅ **Fat-soluble vitamins** = A, D, E, K. **Water-soluble** = B-complex, C.
- ✅ **ICMR energy distribution** = Carbs 55-65%, Protein 10-15%, Fat 20-30%.
- ✅ **Protein RDA** = 0.83 g/kg body weight/day.
- ✅ **Essential nutrients** = cannot be synthesized; must come from diet.
- ✅ **Balanced diet** = all nutrients in right proportion and quantity.
- ✅ **Basic Four Food Groups** = (1) Milk group, (2) Meat-pulses group, (3) Fruits-vegetables group, (4) Cereals-millets-tubers group.
- ✅ **Water** = most critical nutrient; ~60% of body weight; survival without water is only 3-5 days.
- ✅ **Deficiency diseases** to remember — Scurvy (Vit C), Rickets (Vit D), Night blindness (Vit A), Goitre (Iodine), Anaemia (Iron), Kwashiorkor & Marasmus (Protein).
- ✅ **Energy calculation formula** for any meal: $E_{total} = (m_{carb} \times 4) + (m_{prot} \times 4) + (m_{fat} \times 9)$.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — "Introduction to Food and Nutrients"**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of food
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of food

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍽️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Food

In the context of **Food Technology** (GDFTT104 – KTU 2024 Scheme), the term *food* is best understood through three complementary lenses — the **Scientific/Nutritional**, the **Legal/Regulatory**, and the **Functional/Physiological** perspective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (Codex Alimentarius, FAO/WHO):**
> *"Food means any substance, whether processed, semi-processed or raw, which is intended for human consumption, and includes drink, chewing gum and any substance which has been used in the manufacture, preparation or treatment of food, but does not include cosmetics or tobacco or substances used only as drugs."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Definition (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 – FSSAI, India):**
> *Section 3(1)(j) of the FSSA, 2006* defines food as:
> *"any substance, whether processed, partially processed or unprocessed, which is intended for human consumption and includes primary food, genetically modified or engineered food or food containing such material, infant food, packaged drinking water, alcoholic drink, chewing gum, and any substance including water used in the food during its manufacture, preparation or treatment, but does not include any animal feed, live animals unless they are prepared or processed for human consumption, plants prior to harvesting, drugs and medicinal products, cosmetics, narcotic or psychotropic substances."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Food as Fuel for a Living Engine"

Imagine the human body as a **car engine**. Just as an engine cannot run without petrol/diesel, the body cannot function without food. But the analogy goes deeper:

- **Petrol = Carbohydrates & Fats** (Energy-yielding macronutrients — the *fuel* that produces **kilocalories, where 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ**).
- **Engine Oil = Proteins** (Build and repair tissues — the *lubricant* and *structural material*).
- **Coolant & Battery Water = Vitamins & Minerals** (Regulate metabolism in trace amounts — the *spark plugs* and *regulators*).
- **Distilled Water = Water** (Universal solvent — the *coolant* itself).

> [!TIP]
> **Just as a high-performance car needs the *correct grade* of fuel, the human body needs a *balanced diet* — a mix of all six classes of nutrients in the right proportions.**

## 1.3 Six Essential Classes of Food Nutrients

Food is a complex matrix composed of **six broad nutrient classes** that must be ingested regularly to sustain life:

| S.No. | Nutrient Class | Chemical Nature | Energy Yield (per gram) | Primary Biological Role |
|:-----:|:---------------|:----------------|:----------------------:|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Carbohydrates** | Polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones | **4 kcal (≈16.7 kJ)** | Primary energy source |
| 2 | **Proteins** | Polymers of amino acids | **4 kcal (≈16.7 kJ)** | Tissue building & repair |
| 3 | **Fats (Lipids)** | Glycerol + fatty acids | **9 kcal (≈37.6 kJ)** | Concentrated energy reserve |
| 4 | **Vitamins** | Organic micronutrients | **0 kcal** | Metabolic regulators |
| 5 | **Minerals** | Inorganic elements | **0 kcal** | Structural & regulatory |
| 6 | **Water** | H₂O | **0 kcal** | Solvent & transport medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Dietary fibre (roughage) is often considered the *seventh* component — though it yields no calories, it is essential for digestive health.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — What Truly Qualifies as "Food"?

A substance is officially classified as *food* only when it satisfies **all four criteria** simultaneously:

1. **Intended for Human Consumption** — The substance must be consumed by mouth (orally) by humans.
2. **Provides Nourishment** — It must supply at least one of the six nutrient classes listed above.
3. **Non-Toxic at Normal Intake Levels** — Must be safe under customary dietary amounts.
4. **Not a Drug, Cosmetic, or Tobacco** — These are explicitly excluded by the FSSAI definition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Buzzword:** The phrase *"intended for human consumption"* is the **operative legal phrase** in the FSSA, 2006 definition — examiners frequently test this single qualifier.

## 2.2 Sources of Food — Two Broad Origins

- **Foods of Plant Origin** (≈ 80% of human diet by volume globally): Cereals, pulses, fruits, vegetables, nuts, oils, sugars, spices.
- **Foods of Animal Origin** (≈ 20% globally): Milk, meat, fish, eggs, poultry, honey.

## 2.3 Functions of Food — The **4-Rs Framework** (High-Yield for KTU)

Every food substance performs one or more of the following four physiological roles:

1. **R₁ – Energy Provision (Calorific Function):** Carbohydrates, fats, and proteins supply the energy required for basal metabolism, physical activity, and thermoregulation.
2. **R₂ – Body Building (Plastic/Structural Function):** Proteins, minerals (Ca, P), and water construct and maintain tissues, bones, and teeth.
3. **R₃ – Regulation & Protection (Regulatory Function):** Vitamins, minerals, and water act as coenzymes, cofactors, and maintain homeostasis and immunity.
4. **R₄ – Palatability & Satisfaction (Psychosocial Function):** Spices, condiments, and flavouring agents provide sensory pleasure — vital for appetite and digestion (cephalic phase of digestion).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Definition of Food

| Parameter | Standard / Value | KTU Exam Relevance |
|:----------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| Standard energy unit in food science | **kilocalorie (kcal)** | $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$ |
| Average adult energy requirement | **2000–2500 kcal/day** (sedentary male), **1800–2000 kcal/day** (sedentary female) | Common numerical question |
| Number of essential nutrient classes | **6 (or 7 with fibre)** | Frequently asked 3-mark question |
| Regulating body (India) | **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) | 3-mark definition question |
| Governing Act (India) | **FSSA, 2006** | Definition question |
| International body | **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** | Theoretical 7-mark question |
| Energy from carbohydrates | **4 kcal/g** | Numericals in nutrition problems |
| Energy from proteins | **4 kcal/g** | Numericals in nutrition problems |
| Energy from fats | **9 kcal/g** | Numericals in nutrition problems |
| Macronutrients | Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats | Differentiate from micronutrients |
| Micronutrients | Vitamins, Minerals | Differentiate from macronutrients |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** In Food Process Engineering, the *definition of food* directly determines the design of **material handling systems, sterilization units, and packaging lines**. A food technologist must first classify the raw material to select the correct processing parameters.

## 2.5 Anti-Nutritional Factors — Why Some "Edibles" Are Not True Food

Some plant-derived substances are *edible* but interfere with nutrient absorption:

- **Phytates** (in cereals) — bind minerals like Zn and Fe.
- **Oxalates** (in spinach, rhubarb) — bind calcium.
- **Tannins** (in tea, sorghum) — inhibit protein digestion.
- **Trypsin inhibitors** (in raw soybeans) — block protein-digesting enzymes.
- **Lectins** (in raw legumes) — toxic to intestinal cells.

> [!NOTE]
> Food processing techniques like **soaking, germination, fermentation, and cooking** are specifically designed to destroy these anti-nutritional factors — a recurring KTU Module 1–2 question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix & Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Since the topic "Definition of Food" is **conceptual and definitional** in nature (rather than mathematical), this section provides an **exhaustive analytical matrix** that systematically breaks down the definition into its constituent logical components — the exact approach KTU board examiners expect for full-mark answers.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the FSSA Definition

The FSSA 2006 definition contains **five explicit inclusions** and **four explicit exclusions**. Understanding these in sequence is a high-yield 7-mark answer.

### **Step 1 — Identify the Subject Matter**
> *Any substance, whether processed, partially processed or unprocessed...*

$$
\text{Scope of "Food"} = \{\text{Processed}\} \cup \{\text{Partially Processed}\} \cup \{\text{Unprocessed}\}
$$

This means the definition covers **raw agricultural produce (e.g., fresh milk), intermediate products (e.g., wheat flour), and finished products (e.g., biscuits)**.

### **Step 2 — Identify the Intended Use**
> *...which is intended for human consumption...*

The **intention** of the manufacturer/seller is the legal test. A substance chemically identical to food but sold as a *cosmetic* or *industrial chemical* is **not legally food**.

### **Step 3 — Identify the Inclusions (Positive List)**

The Act explicitly **includes** the following within the scope of food:

$$
\text{Inclusions} = \begin{cases} \text{Primary food} \\ \text{Genetically Modified (GM) food} \\ \text{Infant food / weaning food} \\ \text{Packaged drinking water} \\ \text{Alcoholic beverages} \\ \text{Chewing gum} \\ \text{Water used during food processing} \end{cases}
$$

### **Step 4 — Identify the Exclusions (Negative List)**

The Act explicitly **excludes** the following from the scope of food:

$$
\text{Exclusions} = \begin{cases} \text{Animal feed (cattle fodder)} \\ \text{Live animals (unless slaughtered & processed)} \\ \text{Standing crops (pre-harvest plants)} \\ \text{Drugs and medicinal products} \\ \text{Cosmetics and personal care items} \\ \text{Narcotic / psychotropic substances} \end{cases}
$$

### **Step 5 — Synthesis**

A substance is *legally food* in India **if and only if**:

$$
\text{IsFood}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } x \in \text{Inclusions} \text{ AND } x \notin \text{Exclusions} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Common Substances

| Substance | Is it legally "Food" under FSSA 2006? | Justification |
|:----------|:------------------------------------:|:--------------|
| Raw cow's milk | ✅ YES | Primary food, unprocessed, for human consumption |
| Wheat flour (atta) | ✅ YES | Partially processed primary food |
| Packaged mineral water | ✅ YES | Explicitly included as "packaged drinking water" |
| Packaged *tonic water* with herbal extracts | ⚠️ CONTEXT-DEPENDENT | If marketed as beverage → food; if as *medicinal tonic* → drug |
| Tobacco (gutka, khaini) | ❌ NO | Explicitly excluded — "tobacco or substances used only as drugs" |
| Cattle feed (oil cake) | ❌ NO | Explicitly excluded — animal feed |
| Sildenafil (Viagra) tablets | ❌ NO | Drug / medicinal product |
| Living aquarium fish | ❌ NO | Live animals not prepared for human consumption |
| Pond water (untreated) | ❌ NO | Not packaged / not intended as drinking water |
| Aloe vera gel sold as beverage | ✅ YES (if labelled for consumption) | If sold as cosmetic → NO |
| Edible gelatin (from bones) | ✅ YES | Processed food ingredient |
| Vitamin C tablets (Ascorbic acid) | ⚠️ CONTEXT-DEPENDENT | Sold as *food supplement* → YES; sold as *drug* → NO |

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Energy Calculation from a Mixed Meal (For Practice)

> **Problem:** A breakfast plate contains **50 g carbohydrates, 15 g proteins, and 10 g fats**. Calculate the total energy provided, in both **kcal** and **kJ**.

**Step 1 — Energy from Carbohydrates:**

$$
E_{\text{CHO}} = 50 \text{ g} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 200 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy from Proteins:**

$$
E_{\text{Prot}} = 15 \text{ g} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 60 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy from Fats:**

$$
E_{\text{Fat}} = 10 \text{ g} \times 9 \text{ kcal/g} = 90 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy in kcal:**

$$
E_{\text{Total}} = E_{\text{CHO}} + E_{\text{Prot}} + E_{\text{Fat}} = 200 + 60 + 90 = 350 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kJ:**

$$
E_{\text{Total}} = 350 \times 4.186 = 1465.1 \text{ kJ}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The breakfast provides **350 kcal (≈ 1465.1 kJ)** of energy.

## 3.4 Historical & Regulatory Context — Quick Reference Table

| Year / Period | Milestone | Significance for KTU |
|:--------------|:----------|:---------------------|
| **1820** | First use of term "Food Science" | Academic origin |
| **1962** | Codex Alimentarius Commission established | Global food standards |
| **2006** | Food Safety and Standards Act (FSSA) enacted in India | Replaced 8 older acts |
| **2008** | FSSAI operationalized | Single regulatory body for food in India |
| **2006** | FSSAI definition of food adopted | Standard KTU answer |
| **2017** | FSSAI introduced *Food Safety Compliance System* (FoSCoS) | Modern licensing |
| **2020+** | FSSAI Vegan Logo, *Jaivik Bharat* (Organic logo) | Emerging food categories |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define food as per the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSA) of India. List any four categories explicitly included under this definition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to Section 3(1)(j) of the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, *food* is defined as *"any substance, whether processed, partially processed or unprocessed, which is intended for human consumption and includes primary food, genetically modified or engineered food or food containing such material, infant food, packaged drinking water, alcoholic drink, chewing gum, and any substance including water used in the food during its manufacture, preparation or treatment, but does not include any animal feed, live animals unless they are prepared or processed for human consumption, plants prior to harvesting, drugs and medicinal products, cosmetics, narcotic or psychotropic substances."*

**Four included categories (any four):**
1. Primary food
2. Genetically Modified (GM) food
3. Infant food / weaning food
4. Packaged drinking water

*(Alternatively: alcoholic drinks, chewing gum, water used in food processing.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the six essential classes of food nutrients. State the energy value (in kcal/g) of each energy-yielding nutrient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six essential classes of food nutrients are:

1. **Carbohydrates** — energy value = **4 kcal/g**
2. **Proteins** — energy value = **4 kcal/g**
3. **Fats (Lipids)** — energy value = **9 kcal/g**
4. **Vitamins** — energy value = **0 kcal/g** (micronutrient)
5. **Minerals** — energy value = **0 kcal/g** (micronutrient)
6. **Water** — energy value = **0 kcal/g** (universal solvent)

Carbohydrates, proteins, and fats are collectively called **macronutrients**, while vitamins and minerals are termed **micronutrients**.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> **Note:** KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 1 carries questions of **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **TWO completely independent alternatives** — examiners set **either Q.A or Q.B**.

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define food according to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)**. Explain in detail the four physiological functions of food with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **foods of plant origin and foods of animal origin**, giving **five examples of each**. Discuss the role of **food as a regulatory and protective agent** in the human body. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** As per the **Codex Alimentarius**, food means *"any substance, whether processed, semi-processed or raw, which is intended for human consumption, and includes drink, chewing gum and any substance which has been used in the manufacture, preparation or treatment of food, but does not include cosmetics or tobacco or substances used only as drugs."*

**Four Physiological Functions of Food (5 marks):**

| S.No. | Function | Description | Examples |
|:-----:|:---------|:------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Energy Provision** | Supplies the metabolic fuel (calories) needed for basal metabolism and physical work | Rice (carbohydrates), ghee (fats), dal (proteins) |
| 2 | **Body Building (Plastic Function)** | Provides amino acids, calcium, phosphorus for tissue growth, repair, and maintenance | Milk (casein), egg (albumin), ragi (calcium) |
| 3 | **Regulatory & Protective Function** | Vitamins, minerals, and water regulate metabolic pathways, immunity, and fluid balance | Citrus fruits (Vit C), leafy greens (iron), iodised salt (iodine) |
| 4 | **Palatability & Satisfaction** | Sensory appeal stimulates appetite and cephalic-phase digestion | Spices, condiments, beverages, sugar |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Plant Origin | Animal Origin |
|:----------|:-------------|:--------------|
| Cell wall | Present (cellulose) | Absent | 
| Cholesterol | Absent | Present |
| Fibre content | High | Negligible |
| Vitamin B12 | Absent | Rich source |
| Shelf-life | Longer | Shorter (perishable) |

**Five examples each (1.5 marks):**
- **Plant origin:** Rice, wheat, mango, spinach, groundnut
- **Animal origin:** Milk, egg, chicken, fish, honey

**Role of food as regulatory and protective agent (2 marks):**
Foods rich in **vitamins (A, C, E)** and **minerals (Zn, Se, Fe)** act as *antioxidants* and *immunomodulators*. They scavenge free radicals, prevent oxidative stress, and reduce the risk of chronic diseases. For example, **Vitamin A** maintains epithelial integrity; **Vitamin C** boosts immunity; **Zinc** supports enzyme function and wound healing.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six classes of essential nutrients** found in food, classifying them into **macronutrients and micronutrients**. State the **energy value (in kcal/g and kJ/g)** of each macronutrient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define food as per the **FSSA, 2006**. List and briefly explain **four explicit exclusions** from the legal definition of food in India. Why are anti-nutritional factors important in food technology? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) (7 marks)**

**Classification (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Food Nutrients} = \begin{cases} \text{Macronutrients} & \{ \text{CHO, Proteins, Fats, Water} \} \\ \text{Micronutrients} & \{ \text{Vitamins, Minerals} \} \end{cases}
$$

**Six Classes — Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Carbohydrates:** Saccharides (mono-, di-, poly-) — primary energy source. Glycemic control, sparing protein.
2. **Proteins:** Polymers of 20 amino acids — building blocks of muscle, enzymes, hormones.
3. **Fats:** Triglycerides of fatty acids — concentrated energy, insulation, absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
4. **Vitamins:** Organic compounds required in microgram/milligram quantities — coenzymes in metabolism. Two types: **fat-soluble (A, D, E, K)** and **water-soluble (B-complex, C)**.
5. **Minerals:** Inorganic elements (Ca, Fe, I, Zn, K, Na) — structural (bones, teeth) and regulatory (nerve impulse, osmotic balance).
6. **Water:** ~60% of adult body weight — solvent, transport medium, thermoregulation.

**Energy Values Table (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{CHO}} &= 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 16.74 \text{ kJ/g} \\
E_{\text{Prot}} &= 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 16.74 \text{ kJ/g} \\
E_{\text{Fat}} &= 9 \text{ kcal/g} = 37.66 \text{ kJ/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) (7 marks)**

**FSSA 2006 Definition (2 marks):** *(Write verbatim or paraphrased — as given in Section 1 of these notes.)*

**Four Exclusions from the FSSA Definition (3 marks):**

1. **Animal feed** — Substances intended for animals, not humans.
2. **Live animals** — Unless slaughtered, dressed, and processed for human consumption.
3. **Drugs, medicinal products, cosmetics** — Regulated under separate Acts (Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940).
4. **Narcotic and psychotropic substances** — Regulated under the NDPS Act, 1985.
5. *(Bonus: Plants prior to harvesting, tobacco)*

**Anti-Nutritional Factors — Importance in Food Technology (2 marks):**
Anti-nutritional factors (ANFs) such as **phytates, oxalates, tannins, trypsin inhibitors, and lectins** reduce the bioavailability of nutrients and may cause toxicity. Food technology employs **soaking, germination, fermentation, dehulling, and thermal processing** to inactivate ANFs, thereby enhancing the **nutritional quality, digestibility, and safety** of plant-based foods.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "food" with "nutrient".** Food is the *substance*; nutrients are the *components* of food. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Energy values must be in kcal/g OR kJ/g — not both simultaneously in the same line without the conversion factor 4.184.** Stating "4 kcal/g = 4 kJ/g" is a classic 1-mark deduction error.
> 3. **FSSAI vs FSSA** — FSSA is the *Act*; FSSAI is the *implementing body*. Writing "FSSAI Act" will cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Do NOT forget the 4-Rs** (Energy, Body-building, Regulation, Satisfaction) in long answers — examiners allocate 1.5 marks specifically for this functional classification.
> 5. **Anti-nutritional factors are Module 1–2 territory** — frequently linked with "Definition of Food" to test whether the student understands that *not everything edible is nutritious food*.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

✅ **Definition of food** has **three perspectives**: Scientific (Codex), Legal (FSSA 2006), and Physiological (4-Rs).

✅ **FSSA 2006, Section 3(1)(j)** is the **legal bible** for food definition in India — know it verbatim for full marks.

✅ **FSSAI** is the *body*; **FSSA** is the *Act* — never interchange.

✅ **Six nutrient classes** = Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats, Vitamins, Minerals, Water (7th: dietary fibre).

✅ **Energy values** to memorize: CHO = **4 kcal/g**, Protein = **4 kcal/g**, Fat = **9 kcal/g**, and **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ**.

✅ **Macronutrients** supply calories; **Micronutrients** regulate — clear distinction is KTU-favourite.

✅ **Four physiological functions of food** = Energy, Body-building, Regulation, Satisfaction (palatability).

✅ **Sources of food** = Plant origin (cereals, pulses, fruits, vegetables, nuts) and Animal origin (milk, meat, fish, eggs, honey).

✅ **Legal exclusions** = Animal feed, live animals, drugs, cosmetics, narcotics, tobacco, pre-harvest plants.

✅ **Anti-nutritional factors** (phytates, oxalates, tannins, trypsin inhibitors, lectins) must be deactivated by **soaking, germination, fermentation, or cooking**.

✅ **Codex Alimentarius** = international FAO/WHO body; **FSSAI** = Indian national regulator.

✅ **GM food, infant food, packaged water, alcoholic drinks, chewing gum** are *explicitly included* in the FSSA definition.

✅ A substance is *legally food* **only if** it is (a) intended for human consumption, (b) provides nourishment, (c) is non-toxic at normal doses, and (d) is not a drug/cosmetic/tobacco.

> **🎓 Final Tip:** For 14-mark KTU questions on this topic, always structure the answer as — **(i) Definition → (ii) Components / Classification → (iii) Functions → (iv) Examples → (v) Diagram or Table**. This 5-part structure aligns perfectly with the KTU 2024 marking scheme and consistently fetches **12+ out of 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Food and Its Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food** can be formally defined as any solid, semi-solid, or liquid substance that, when consumed, provides the body with **nutrients** — the chemical substances essential for sustaining life, supporting growth, repairing tissues, and regulating vital physiological processes.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** and standard **KTU 2024 Food Technology** terminology, food is classified not merely as a source of pleasure or satiety, but as a *biological fuel and structural raw material* that performs specific physiological roles in the human body.

The **functions of food** refer to the **specific physiological and biochemical roles** that dietary components play in maintaining human health, growth, and overall well-being. They are broadly categorized into **five principal functions**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Core Functions of Food (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> 1. **Provision of Energy** — fuels bodily activities
> 2. **Growth and Development** — builds new tissues
> 3. **Repair and Maintenance** — heals and regenerates worn-out cells
> 4. **Regulation of Metabolic Processes** — controls biochemical pathways
> 5. **Protection against Disease** — boosts immunity and provides defense

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Car Engine Model:**
> Think of the human body as a sophisticated **car engine**:
> - **Energy (Fuel)** = Petrol/Diesel that makes the car move → Macronutrients (carbohydrates & fats) provide this.
> - **Growth and Development** = Spare parts used to expand the car's frame → Proteins and minerals build muscle and bone.
> - **Repair and Maintenance** = Mechanic fixing a worn-out piston → Protein and water repair damaged tissues.
> - **Regulation** = Engine coolant, oil sensors, and ECU that control temperature and timing → Vitamins and minerals regulate metabolism.
> - **Protection** = Airbags, seatbelts, and rust-proof coating → Antioxidants and immune-nutrients defend the body.

In simple terms: **Food is to the human body what fuel, parts, lubricants, sensors, and safety systems are to an engine.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Food Science

The following standard metrics govern how food function is quantified:

- **Kilocalorie (kcal)** — the unit of energy supplied by food. **1 kcal = 4.184 kilojoules (kJ)**.
- **Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA)** — the average daily intake level sufficient to meet the nutrient requirements of nearly all (97–98%) healthy individuals in a particular life stage and gender group.
- **Body Mass Index (BMI)** — a numerical index used to classify underweight, normal, overweight, and obesity states.
- **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** — the amount of energy expended per unit time at complete rest.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Five Functions of Food

### 🔹 2.1.1 Function 1 — Provision of Energy
Food supplies the **chemical energy** required for:
- **Basal metabolism** (breathing, circulation, cell production)
- **Physical activity** (walking, exercise, labour)
- **Thermic effect of food** (energy used to digest food itself)

**Energy-yielding nutrients:**
- **Carbohydrates** → primary energy source (**4 kcal/g**)
- **Fats (Lipids)** → concentrated energy source (**9 kcal/g**)
- **Proteins** → secondary energy source (**4 kcal/g**)

The energy requirement of an average adult is approximately **2000–2500 kcal/day** (sedentary) and **2500–3000 kcal/day** (moderately active).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are fats more energy-dense?** Fats have a higher proportion of **C–H bonds** (hydrocarbon chains) which release more energy upon oxidation than carbohydrates, which contain oxygen atoms already partially oxidized.

### 🔹 2.1.2 Function 2 — Growth and Development
This function is particularly critical during:
- **Infancy, childhood, adolescence, and pregnancy**

**Growth-supporting nutrients:**
- **Proteins** → building blocks of muscles, skin, enzymes, hormones (**amino acids**)
- **Calcium & Phosphorus** → bone and teeth formation
- **Iron, Zinc, Iodine** → essential for skeletal and cognitive development
- **Vitamin A & D** → cellular differentiation and skeletal growth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protein Quality** is measured by the **Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS)**. Animal proteins (egg, milk, meat) have a PDCAAS of **1.0**, while most plant proteins score lower (0.5–0.7) due to limiting amino acids.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Function 3 — Repair and Maintenance of Body Tissues
The human body constantly undergoes **wear and tear**:
- Cells die and are replaced (e.g., red blood cells live ~120 days)
- Tissues regenerate after injury
- Enzymes, hormones, and antibodies need continuous replenishment

**Repair nutrients:**
- **Proteins** (amino acids) → replace damaged cells
- **Water** → maintains cell turgidity and transport medium
- **Vitamin C** → collagen synthesis (connective tissue repair)
- **Iron** → haemoglobin regeneration

### 🔹 2.1.4 Function 4 — Regulation of Metabolic Processes
Food acts as **chemical messengers and regulators** that:
- Maintain acid-base balance (pH of blood ~7.35–7.45)
- Regulate body temperature
- Control osmotic pressure
- Enable nerve transmission and muscle contraction
- Catalyse biochemical reactions (via enzymes and coenzymes)

**Regulatory nutrients:**
- **Vitamins** (especially B-complex) — coenzymes in metabolic pathways
- **Minerals** (Na, K, Ca, Mg) — electrolyte balance and nerve signalling
- **Water** — solvent and temperature regulator
- **Dietary fibre** — regulates bowel movement and gut microbiota

### 🔹 2.1.5 Function 5 — Protection against Disease
This function provides **immune defence** and **antioxidant protection**:
- **Vitamin C, E, β-carotene, Selenium** — antioxidants neutralizing free radicals
- **Vitamin A, D, Zn** — strengthen immune barrier
- **Probiotics & Prebiotics** — gut health and immunity
- **Phytochemicals** (flavonoids, polyphenols) — anti-inflammatory and anti-carcinogenic properties

> [!TIP]
> **Modern Concept:** Food is increasingly viewed as **"functional food"** — beyond basic nutrition, it provides health benefits and reduces disease risk (e.g., probiotic yogurt, fortified cereals, omega-3 eggs).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Standard Values / Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy from Carbohydrates | $E_{CHO} = m \times 4$ | $m$ in grams, $E$ in kcal |
| Energy from Fats | $E_{Fat} = m \times 9$ | $m$ in grams, $E$ in kcal |
| Energy from Proteins | $E_{Prot} = m \times 4$ | $m$ in grams, $E$ in kcal |
| **Atwater Total Energy** | $E_{total} = 4m_{CHO} + 9m_{Fat} + 4m_{Prot}$ | kcal per serving |
| **Body Mass Index (BMI)** | $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ | $W$ in kg, $H$ in m, unit = kg/m² |
| **BMR — Harris-Benedict (Males)** | $BMR = 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$ | kcal/day |
| **BMR — Harris-Benedict (Females)** | $BMR = 655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$ | kcal/day |
| **Energy Conversion** | $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$ | SI conversion |
| **Specific Dynamic Action (SDA)** | $SDA_{CHO} \approx 5\%$, $SDA_{Fat} \approx 2\%$, $SDA_{Prot} \approx 30\%$ | % of energy intake |
| **PDCAAS Reference** | $PDCAAS = \dfrac{\text{Limiting AA mg in 1g test protein}}{\text{Same AA mg in 1g reference protein}} \times \text{True Digestibility}$ | Score 0.0 – 1.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atwater Factors (4-9-4 Rule)** are the **gold standard** in food science for calculating metabolizable energy of food. Always remember: **Carbohydrate = 4, Fat = 9, Protein = 4 kcal/g**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Technology Engineering

The five functions of food underpin critical **food industry applications**:

- **Energy function** → Drives the formulation of **energy-dense sports nutrition** (energy bars, isotonic drinks).
- **Growth function** → Guides the design of **infant formula** and **complementary foods** fortified with iron, calcium, and DHA.
- **Repair function** → Essential in **medical nutrition therapy** (post-surgical recovery diets, protein supplements).
- **Regulation function** → Foundation of **functional foods** (probiotic curd, vitamin-fortified milk, electrolyte drinks).
- **Protection function** → Basis of the **nutraceutical and antioxidant supplement industry** (e.g., turmeric capsules, green tea extracts).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Atwater Energy Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A serving of food contains **50 g carbohydrates, 20 g fat, and 15 g protein**. Calculate the total metabolizable energy of the serving using Atwater factors.

### Step 1: Recall the Atwater Energy Equation

The total energy of a food is the sum of energy contributions from each macronutrient:

$$
E_{total} = E_{CHO} + E_{Fat} + E_{Prot}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Atwater Factors (4-9-4 Rule)

The energy contribution of each macronutrient is:

$$
E_{CHO} = m_{CHO} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g}
$$

$$
E_{Fat} = m_{Fat} \times 9 \text{ kcal/g}
$$

$$
E_{Prot} = m_{Prot} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Mass Values

Given: $m_{CHO} = 50$ g, $m_{Fat} = 20$ g, $m_{Prot} = 15$ g.

$$
E_{CHO} = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{Fat} = 20 \times 9 = 180 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{Prot} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ kcal}
$$

### Step 4: Sum All Energy Contributions

$$
E_{total} = 200 + 180 + 60 = 440 \text{ kcal}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Kilojoules (Optional — KTU Frequently Asks)

Using $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$:

$$
E_{kJ} = 440 \times 4.184 = 1840.96 \text{ kJ} \approx 1841 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Percentage Contribution of Each Macronutrient

The percentage of total energy from each nutrient is critical for dietary labelling:

$$
\%E_{CHO} = \frac{200}{440} \times 100 = 45.45\%
$$

$$
\%E_{Fat} = \frac{180}{440} \times 100 = 40.91\%
$$

$$
\%E_{Prot} = \frac{60}{440} \times 100 = 13.64\%
$$

### Step 7: Final Verification

Sum of percentages: $45.45 + 40.91 + 13.64 = 100.00\%$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The food serving provides **440 kcal (≈ 1841 kJ)** of metabolizable energy, with carbohydrates contributing the most (**45.45%**), followed by fat (**40.91%**) and protein (**13.64%**).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — BMI Classification

**Problem Statement:** A person weighs **75 kg** and is **1.70 m** tall. Calculate BMI and classify the health status.

### Step 1: Recall the BMI Formula

$$
BMI = \frac{W}{H^2}
$$

where $W$ = body weight in kg, $H$ = height in metres.

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
BMI = \frac{75}{(1.70)^2}
$$

### Step 3: Square the Height

$$
(1.70)^2 = 2.89 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Division

$$
BMI = \frac{75}{2.89} = 25.95 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

### Step 5: Classify According to WHO Standards

| **BMI Range (kg/m²)** | **Classification** |
| :--- | :--- |
| $< 18.5$ | Underweight |
| $18.5 - 24.9$ | Normal weight |
| $25.0 - 29.9$ | Overweight |
| $\geq 30.0$ | Obese |

Since $BMI = 25.95$, the person falls in the **Overweight** category.

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The individual has a BMI of **25.95 kg/m²**, classified as **overweight** — indicating an energy intake exceeding energy expenditure (imbalance of the *energy function* of food).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Nutrient-to-Function Mapping

| **Nutrient Class** | **Energy** | **Growth** | **Repair** | **Regulation** | **Protection** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Carbohydrates | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Proteins | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Fats (Lipids) | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Vitamins (A, C, D, E, K, B) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Minerals (Ca, Fe, Zn, I) | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ |
| Water | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ |
| Dietary Fibre | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ |

*(✓✓✓ = Primary role, ✓ = Secondary role, ✗ = Minimal/No direct role)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

