# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define food. List the five principal functions of food.**

**Model Answer:**
Food is any substance, whether solid, liquid, or semi-solid, that when consumed provides the body with nutrients required for energy, growth, repair, regulation, and protection against disease.

The five principal functions are:
1. Provision of energy
2. Growth and development
3. Repair and maintenance of body tissues
4. Regulation of metabolic processes
5. Protection against disease

*(3 marks — Definition: 1 mark, Listing: 2 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Atwater factor? State the Atwater values for carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.**

**Model Answer:**
The Atwater factor is a coefficient used to convert the mass of a macronutrient into its energy contribution in kilocalories, based on the heat of combustion corrected for digestive losses.

Atwater values:
- Carbohydrates: **4 kcal/g**
- Fats: **9 kcal/g**
- Proteins: **4 kcal/g**

*(3 marks — Definition: 1.5 marks, Values: 1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define the term "function of food" in the context of human nutrition. Explain in detail the **energy-providing function** of food, with reference to macronutrients and Atwater factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of food in **growth and development** and **repair and maintenance** of body tissues. Highlight the key nutrients involved in each function. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

The **function of food** refers to the specific physiological role that dietary nutrients play in maintaining human health, vitality, and survival. The energy-providing function is the most fundamental, as all bodily activities — from cellular respiration to voluntary movement — require adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

**Macronutrients and their energy contributions (Atwater factors):**

$$
E_{CHO} = 4 \text{ kcal/g}, \quad E_{Fat} = 9 \text{ kcal/g}, \quad E_{Prot} = 4 \text{ kcal/g}
$$

- **Carbohydrates** are the primary and preferred energy source, especially for the brain and central nervous system, which utilize glucose exclusively.
- **Fats** are the most concentrated energy source and are predominantly oxidized during prolonged, low-intensity activity.
- **Proteins** serve primarily as building blocks; they are used as an energy source only when carbohydrates and fats are insufficient (e.g., during starvation).

The daily energy requirement of an average adult ranges from **2000 to 3000 kcal**, depending on basal metabolic rate, physical activity, and thermic effect of food.

**Conclusion:** The energy function of food is essential for sustaining life and is mathematically quantifiable using the Atwater 4-9-4 system. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Growth and Development:**
Food supports the synthesis of new tissues during the formative years of life. The key nutrients include:
- **Proteins** — provide essential amino acids for muscle, organ, and connective tissue formation.
- **Calcium and Phosphorus** — mineralize bones and teeth.
- **Iron and Zinc** — support cognitive development and oxygen transport.
- **Vitamin A and D** — regulate cellular differentiation and skeletal growth.
- The **Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS)** is the modern benchmark for protein quality in growth-supporting foods. *(3.5 marks)*

**Repair and Maintenance:**
The body undergoes continuous cellular turnover; for example, red blood cells have a lifespan of approximately 120 days and must be replenished. Nutrients involved include:
- **Proteins** — replace damaged cells and synthesize new enzymes, hormones, and antibodies.
- **Vitamin C** — essential for collagen synthesis in connective tissue repair.
- **Water** — acts as a transport medium and maintains cellular turgidity.
- **Iron** — regenerates haemoglobin after red cell destruction.

**Conclusion:** Together, the growth and repair functions of food ensure the body can expand during development and maintain structural integrity throughout life. *(3.5 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **regulatory function** of food with suitable examples. How do vitamins and minerals act as regulators in metabolic pathways? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **protective function** of food, including the role of antioxidants, phytochemicals, and functional foods in disease prevention. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

The **regulatory function** of food refers to the role of dietary components in controlling and coordinating biochemical and physiological processes without being oxidized for energy.

**Key regulatory roles include:**
- **Acid-base balance** — blood pH (7.35–7.45) is maintained by buffer systems dependent on protein, phosphate, and bicarbonate.
- **Electrolyte balance** — Sodium (Na⁺), Potassium (K⁺), Calcium (Ca²⁺), and Magnesium (Mg²⁺) maintain osmotic pressure and nerve transmission.
- **Thermoregulation** — water dissipates heat through perspiration.
- **Enzymatic catalysis** — vitamins and minerals act as cofactors and coenzymes.

**Vitamins as regulators (B-complex examples):**
- **Thiamine (B1)** — coenzyme in pyruvate dehydrogenase reaction.
- **Riboflavin (B2)** — coenzyme in FAD-dependent redox reactions.
- **Niacin (B3)** — coenzyme in NAD⁺/NADH electron transfer.

**Minerals as regulators:**
- **Iron** — cofactor in catalase and cytochromes.
- **Iodine** — integral to thyroid hormones (T3, T4) that regulate basal metabolism.
- **Zinc** — cofactor in over 300 enzymes.

**Conclusion:** Without the regulatory function, metabolic pathways would proceed chaotically, leading to homeostatic failure. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

The **protective function** of food refers to its role in defending the body against pathogens, free radicals, and chronic diseases such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes.

**1. Antioxidants:**
- **Vitamin C, Vitamin E, β-carotene, and Selenium** neutralize free radicals (reactive oxygen species).
- These prevent **oxidative stress**, which damages DNA, lipids, and proteins.

**2. Phytochemicals:**
- **Flavonoids** (in citrus, berries) — anti-inflammatory and anti-carcinogenic.
- **Polyphenols** (in green tea, dark chocolate) — reduce LDL oxidation.
- **Carotenoids** (in carrots, tomatoes) — protect vision and skin.

**3. Functional Foods:**
- **Probiotics** (Lactobacillus in curd) — maintain gut flora and immunity.
- **Prebiotics** (inulin, oligosaccharides) — feed beneficial gut bacteria.
- **Fortified foods** (iodized salt, vitamin-D milk) — address micronutrient deficiencies.

**4. Immune-Boosting Nutrients:**
- **Vitamin A, D, and Zinc** strengthen the mucosal barrier and white blood cell function.

**Conclusion:** The protective function of food extends beyond basic nutrition and forms the scientific basis of the modern **nutraceutical and functional food industries**. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "energy function" with "regulation function" — energy means ATP production, while regulation means controlling metabolic rate.
> 2. **Atwater factors are 4-9-4**, NOT 4-4-9. Reversing fat and protein values is the most common numerical error in KTU scripts.
> 3. **BMI formula uses $H^2$** (height in **metres**, not centimetres). Forgetting to convert cm → m is a frequent mistake costing 1 mark.
> 4. In Part B answers, ensure you provide **both nutritional examples AND biochemical mechanisms** — vague answers without specific nutrient names lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. Always **write units** (kcal, g, kg/m²) — units omission is a top reason for mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions of Food**

- ✅ **Food** = any substance providing nutrients for life sustenance.
- ✅ **Five Functions**: Energy, Growth, Repair, Regulation, Protection.
- ✅ **Atwater Rule**: Carbs = **4**, Fats = **9**, Proteins = **4** kcal/g.
- ✅ **Energy nutrients**: Carbohydrates (primary), Fats (concentrated), Proteins (secondary/backup).
- ✅ **Growth nutrients**: Proteins, Calcium, Phosphorus, Iron, Zinc, Vitamin A & D.
- ✅ **Repair nutrients**: Proteins, Vitamin C, Water, Iron.
- ✅ **Regulatory nutrients**: B-complex vitamins, Na, K, Ca, Mg, Water, Fibre.
- ✅ **Protective nutrients**: Vitamin C, E, A, Selenium, Phytochemicals, Probiotics, Prebiotics.
- ✅ **BMI Formula**: $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ (kg/m²).
- ✅ **BMI Categories**: <18.5 Underweight | 18.5–24.9 Normal | 25–29.9 Overweight | ≥30 Obese.
- ✅ **BMR (Harris-Benedict)**: Males = $66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$; Females = $655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$.
- ✅ **Energy Conversion**: $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$.
- ✅ **PDCAAS** = gold standard for protein quality (range 0.0–1.0; egg/milk = 1.0).
- ✅ **SDA** (Specific Dynamic Action): Carbs 5%, Fat 2%, Protein 30%.
- ✅ **RDA** = average daily intake sufficient for 97–98% of healthy individuals.
- ✅ **Functional foods** = foods providing health benefits beyond basic nutrition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food groups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Food Groups

**Food groups** are systematic classifications of foods that share similar nutritional properties, botanical origins, or chemical compositions. They serve as a foundational framework in **dietetics, nutrition science, and public health planning**, allowing nutritionists, dietitians, and food technologists to design balanced diets and communicate dietary recommendations effectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Food groups refer to the categorization of foods based on their predominant nutrient composition, biological origin, and functional role in human nutrition. Standard classifications include the ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research) Five Food Group System and the USDA (United States Department of Agriculture) MyPlate system.*

Formally, a **food group** is defined as a collection of foods that:
- Supply a **similar profile of macronutrients** (carbohydrates, proteins, fats)
- Share a **common biological source** (plant vs. animal)
- Provide **comparable micronutrients** (vitamins and minerals)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **massive warehouse store** where thousands of different products are piled on shelves. To find what you need, the store organizes items into **departments** — Electronics, Groceries, Apparel, Hardware. Food groups work the same way in nutrition: nature provides thousands of edible items, but nutritionists group them into categories so that a "balanced meal" can be built scientifically, just like a balanced shopping cart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of food groups as a "nutritional color palette."** Just as an artist mixes primary colors to get every other shade, a balanced diet mixes foods from each group to obtain every essential nutrient the human body requires.

## 1.3 The Two Major Classification Systems

| Aspect | **ICMR Classification (India)** | **USDA MyPlate (USA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Groups | **5 main groups** | **5 main groups** |
| Primary Basis | **Nutrient composition** | **Plate proportion** |
| Emphasis | Nutritional adequacy | Visual portion guidance |
| Use Case | Indian dietary surveys, public health | Educational campaigns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, the **ICMR classification** is the most frequently tested, as it forms the basis of Indian nutrition policy (the National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad, publishes the *Nutritive Value of Indian Foods*).

## 1.4 ICMR Five Food Groups (2011 Revised)

The Indian Council of Medical Research groups foods into **five primary categories**:

1. **Cereals, Millets, and Pulses** — energy and protein
2. **Vegetables and Fruits** — vitamins, minerals, fiber
3. **Milk and Animal Foods** (meat, fish, egg) — protein, calcium
4. **Oils, Fats, Nuts, and Sugars** — concentrated energy
5. **Condiments and Spices** — flavor and adjuncts

Each group contributes a **distinct nutrient signature**, which is the basis of the "balanced diet plate" concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Food Groups Exist — The Underlying Logic

The human body requires **six major classes of nutrients**:
- **Carbohydrates** (4 kcal/g) — primary energy
- **Proteins** (4 kcal/g) — tissue building
- **Fats** (9 kcal/g) — concentrated energy
- **Vitamins** — metabolic regulation
- **Minerals** — structural and regulatory
- **Water** — solvent and medium

No single food item provides **all six nutrients in the right proportions**. Therefore, foods are classified into groups so that **combining items from complementary groups** yields a complete nutrient profile.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification Logic

The classification uses a **3-tier hierarchy**:

> **Macro-level (Origin):** Plant-based vs. Animal-based
> **Meso-level (Group):** Cereals, Pulses, Vegetables, Fruits, etc.
> **Micro-level (Nutrient Density):** Energy-dense, Protein-dense, Vitamin-dense

### 2.2.1 Group 1 — Cereals, Millets, and Pulses

**Dominant Nutrients:** Carbohydrates (60–75%), Proteins (7–12% in cereals, 20–25% in pulses), B-complex vitamins, fiber.

**Examples:**
- **Cereals:** Rice, wheat, maize, barley, oats, ragi
- **Millets:** Jowar (sorghum), bajra (pearl millet), foxtail millet
- **Pulses:** Bengal gram, red gram (tur), black gram (urad), green gram (moong), lentils (masoor)

**Engineering/Industrial Relevance:** Milling yields flour (refined vs. whole); parboiling; malting; fermentation (idli, dosa batter); pulse processing (dehulling, splitting, dal).

### 2.2.2 Group 2 — Vegetables and Fruits

**Dominant Nutrients:** Vitamins (especially Vitamin A from β-carotene, Vitamin C), minerals (K, Ca, Fe), dietary fiber, antioxidants, water (80–95%).

**Sub-classification:**
- **Green leafy vegetables (GLV):** Spinach, fenugreek (methi), amaranth, drumstick leaves
- **Root and tuber vegetables:** Carrot, beetroot, radish, potato, sweet potato
- **Other vegetables:** Brinjal, okra, beans, gourds
- **Fruits:** Citrus, mango, banana, guava, papaya, apple

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The ICMR recommends **300 g of vegetables + 100 g of fruits daily** for an adult sedentary worker, with **GLV contributing 50 g/day**.

### 2.2.3 Group 3 — Milk, Meat, Fish, and Eggs

**Dominant Nutrients:** High-quality protein (complete amino acid profile), calcium (especially milk), Vitamin B12, heme iron (in meat), omega-3 fatty acids (in fish).

**Examples:**
- **Milk and dairy:** Cow milk, buffalo milk, curd, paneer, ghee, cheese
- **Meat:** Chicken, mutton, pork, beef
- **Fish and seafood:** Rohu, salmon, prawns
- **Eggs:** Chicken egg (a complete protein reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Value (BV) Comparison:**
> - Egg protein BV = **94** (reference standard)
> - Milk protein BV = **91**
> - Fish protein BV = **83**
> - Rice protein BV = **83**
> These values are tested in KTU.

### 2.2.4 Group 4 — Oils, Fats, Nuts, and Sugar

**Dominant Nutrients:** Concentrated energy (9 kcal/g from fats), essential fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic), Vitamin E (from nuts), simple sugars.

**Examples:**
- **Edible oils:** Mustard oil, groundnut oil, coconut oil, sunflower oil, olive oil
- **Nuts and oilseeds:** Almond, cashew, groundnut, sesame, coconut
- **Sugar and jaggery:** Sucrose, jaggery (gur), honey, khandsari

**Engineering Relevance:** Oil extraction (cold press, expeller, solvent extraction); hydrogenation; emulsification.

### 2.2.5 Group 5 — Condiments, Spices, and Beverages

**Dominant Nutrients:** Phytochemicals, essential oils, antioxidants, flavor compounds; negligible macronutrients.

**Examples:**
- **Condiments:** Salt, vinegar, pickles, chutneys
- **Spices:** Turmeric, cumin, coriander, black pepper, cardamom, clove
- **Beverages:** Tea, coffee, cocoa, soft drinks

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

| Concept | Expression / Value | Unit | Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy from carbohydrates | **4 kcal/g** | kcal | RDA calculation |
| Energy from proteins | **4 kcal/g** | kcal | RDA calculation |
| Energy from fats | **9 kcal/g** | kcal | RDA calculation |
| RDA of energy (sedentary adult) | **1900 (F) / 2400 (M)** | kcal/day | ICMR table recall |
| RDA of protein | **0.83 g/kg body weight** | g/kg/day | WHO/ICMR |
| BV of egg protein | **94** | unitless | Comparison question |
| ICMR vegetables recommendation | **300 g/day** | g | Public health question |
| ICMR fruits recommendation | **100 g/day** | g | Public health question |
| Number of ICMR food groups | **5** | — | Direct question |
| Dietary fiber recommendation | **25–40 g/day** | g | Adult health |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calculating Total Energy in a Mixed Meal:**
> If a meal contains $P$ grams protein, $C$ grams carbohydrate, and $F$ grams fat, the total caloric value $E$ is:
>
> $$E = 4P + 4C + 9F \quad \text{(kcal)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Technology

In industrial food formulation, food groups drive the **concept of "Food Fortification"**:
- Iodized **salt** (Group 5) → combats Iodine Deficiency Disorders
- Iron-fortified **wheat flour** (Group 1) → combats anemia
- Vitamin D-fortified **milk** (Group 3) → combats rickets
- Vitamin A-fortified **oil** (Group 4) → combats night blindness

The food group framework is also the basis of **"Food Exchange Lists"** used by dietitians, where foods within a group can be **interchanged** without major nutrient disturbance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Distribution in a Balanced Diet

The ICMR (2010) recommends a specific **macro-energy ratio** for a healthy adult:

> **Protein:** 10–15% of total energy
> **Fat:** 20–30% of total energy
> **Carbohydrate:** 55–75% of total energy

### Step-by-Step Derivation for a 2000 kcal/day Diet

**Step 1:** Identify the total energy requirement.

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2000 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the energy from carbohydrates (60% of total).

$$E_{\text{CHO}} = 0.60 \times E_{\text{total}} = 0.60 \times 2000 = 1200 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3:** Convert carbohydrate energy to grams using the **Atwater factor** of **4 kcal/g**.

$$C = \frac{E_{\text{CHO}}}{4} = \frac{1200}{4} = 300 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the energy from proteins (15% of total).

$$E_{\text{protein}} = 0.15 \times 2000 = 300 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 5:** Convert protein energy to grams.

$$P = \frac{E_{\text{protein}}}{4} = \frac{300}{4} = 75 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the energy from fats (25% of total).

$$E_{\text{fat}} = 0.25 \times 2000 = 500 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 7:** Convert fat energy to grams using the Atwater factor of **9 kcal/g**.

$$F = \frac{E_{\text{fat}}}{9} = \frac{500}{9} \approx 55.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 8:** Verify the energy balance.

$$E_{\text{check}} = 4C + 4P + 9F = 4(300) + 4(75) + 9(55.6)$$

$$E_{\text{check}} = 1200 + 300 + 500.4 \approx 2000 \text{ kcal} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Linking Food Group to Nutrient Deficiency

| Food Group | Key Nutrients Provided | Deficiency If Absent | Public Health Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cereals & millets | Energy, B1, B3, B6 | Beriberi, Pellagra | Wernicke-Korsakoff, dermatitis |
| Pulses & legumes | Protein, iron, B-complex | Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM) | Kwashiorkor, Marasmus |
| Vegetables (GLV) | Folic acid, Fe, Vit A, Vit C | Anemia, scurvy, night blindness | Spina bifida (folate) |
| Fruits (citrus) | Vit C, potassium | Scurvy, gum bleeding | Capillary fragility |
| Milk & dairy | Ca, Vit D, Vit B12, complete protein | Rickets, osteomalacia | Bone deformities |
| Meat, fish, egg | Vit B12, heme Fe, Zn, complete protein | Pernicious anemia, Zn deficiency | Cognitive decline |
| Oils, fats, nuts | EFA (ω-3, ω-6), Vit E, energy | Dermatitis, hair loss | Cardiovascular risk |
| Condiments, spices | Phytochemicals, antioxidants | Reduced immunity | Oxidative stress disorders |
| Water (beverage) | Hydration, electrolytes | Dehydration, heat stroke | Renal calculi |

## 3.3 Worked Example: RDA Calculation Using Food Groups

**Problem:** A 60 kg sedentary adult woman (using ICMR norms) needs to plan her daily food groups. Calculate her daily requirement of pulses, milk, vegetables, fruits, and oil.

**Given:** RDA energy = **1900 kcal/day**, Protein = **0.83 g/kg body weight**.

**Step 1 — Protein requirement:**

$$P = 0.83 \times 60 = 49.8 \approx 50 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 2 — Pulse quantity** (1 g pulse protein per 1.5 g cooked pulse; assume 25% protein in raw pulse):

$$\text{Raw pulse} = \frac{P_{\text{from pulse}} \times 1}{0.25} = \frac{25}{0.25} = 100 \text{ g/day}$$

(Assuming half of daily protein comes from pulses, i.e., 25 g.)

**Step 3 — Milk quantity** (1 g protein per 30 mL cow milk ≈ 3.3% protein):

$$\text{Milk} = \frac{P_{\text{from milk}} \times 1}{0.033} = \frac{25}{0.033} \approx 750 \text{ mL/day}$$

(Assuming 25 g protein from milk.)

**Step 4 — Vegetables and fruits** (ICMR recommendation, independent of body weight):

$$\text{Vegetables} = 300 \text{ g/day}, \quad \text{Fruits} = 100 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 5 — Oil/fat** (energy from fat = 20% of 1900 kcal = 380 kcal):

$$F = \frac{380}{9} \approx 42 \text{ g/day} \approx 45 \text{ g oil/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example is a **direct KTU Part B question pattern** (14 marks). The full meal plan with cooking conversions is expected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define food groups. List the five major food groups recommended by the ICMR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Food groups are a classification of foods based on their nutrient composition, biological origin, and dietary function, used to design balanced diets.

**The five ICMR food groups (2 marks):**
1. **Cereals, millets, and pulses** — energy and protein
2. **Vegetables and fruits** — vitamins, minerals, and fiber
3. **Milk and animal foods** (meat, fish, egg) — complete protein and calcium
4. **Oils, fats, nuts, and sugars** — concentrated energy and essential fatty acids
5. **Condiments and spices** — flavor, antioxidants, and phytochemicals

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State the ICMR recommended daily intake of vegetables and fruits for an adult sedentary worker. Mention one key nutrient contributed by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Vegetables: 300 g/day** (including 50 g green leafy vegetables) — key nutrient: **β-carotene (Vit A precursor) and iron** (1.5 marks)
- **Fruits: 100 g/day** — key nutrient: **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** (1.5 marks)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Classify foods into the ICMR five food groups with two examples and one dominant nutrient from each. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the grams of carbohydrate, protein, and fat required for an adult sedentary male requiring **2400 kcal/day** using ICMR energy distribution norms (CHO 60%, Protein 15%, Fat 25%). Also calculate the grams of oil to be consumed daily. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — ICMR Five Food Group Classification (7 marks)**

| Food Group | Examples (2) | Dominant Nutrient |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Cereals, millets, pulses | Rice, Wheat / Jowar, Bajra | Carbohydrate, B-complex vitamins |
| 2. Vegetables and fruits | Spinach, Carrot / Mango, Orange | Vitamins (A, C), fiber, minerals |
| 3. Milk, meat, fish, egg | Milk, Paneer / Egg, Fish | Complete protein, calcium, B12 |
| 4. Oils, fats, nuts, sugar | Groundnut oil, Ghee / Almond, Sugar | Concentrated energy, EFA |
| 5. Condiments and spices | Turmeric, Pepper / Salt, Vinegar | Phytochemicals, antioxidants |

*(1.4 marks per group = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — Energy Distribution Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Given $E_{\text{total}} = 2400$ kcal/day.

**Step 2:** Energy from carbohydrates (60%):

$$E_{\text{CHO}} = 0.60 \times 2400 = 1440 \text{ kcal}$$

$$C = \frac{1440}{4} = 360 \text{ g/day} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 3:** Energy from proteins (15%):

$$E_{\text{protein}} = 0.15 \times 2400 = 360 \text{ kcal}$$

$$P = \frac{360}{4} = 90 \text{ g/day} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 4:** Energy from fats (25%):

$$E_{\text{fat}} = 0.25 \times 2400 = 600 \text{ kcal}$$

$$F = \frac{600}{9} = 66.7 \text{ g/day} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 5:** Visible oil calculation (visible fat = 50% of total fat):

$$F_{\text{visible}} = \frac{66.7}{2} \approx 33 \text{ g/day} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 6:** Energy balance verification:

$$4(360) + 4(90) + 9(66.7) = 1440 + 360 + 600.3 \approx 2400 \text{ kcal} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 7:** Conclusion — The adult male needs **360 g CHO, 90 g protein, 66.7 g fat, and 33 g visible oil/day** (1 mark for unit clarity and statement).

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the ICMR recommended dietary allowance (RDA) of food groups for a balanced vegetarian diet. List the major nutrients contributed by each group. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the concept of "food exchange" within a group with a suitable example. Explain its role in diet planning. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Vegetarian Balanced Diet (7 marks)**

A balanced vegetarian diet includes the following ICMR food groups with their RDAs:

| Food Group | Daily Intake (Vegetarian Adult) | Key Nutrients |
|---|---|---|
| Cereals and millets | 350 g | Energy, B-complex, fiber |
| Pulses and legumes | 60 g | Protein, iron, B1 |
| Green leafy vegetables | 50 g | Vit A, folate, Fe, Ca |
| Other vegetables | 200 g | Vit C, fiber, K |
| Fruits | 100 g | Vit C, β-carotene, K |
| Milk and curd | 300 mL | Ca, Vit B12, complete protein |
| Fats and oils (visible) | 30 g | EFA, Vit E, energy |
| Sugar and jaggery | 30 g | Concentrated energy |
| Condiments and spices | As per taste | Phytochemicals, antioxidants |

*(1 mark for the table structure + 0.5 marks per correct group entry = 7 marks)*

**Discussion (extra emphasis):** Since vegetarian diets exclude meat/fish/egg, **B12 supplementation or fortified foods** is critical. Pulses combined with cereals provide **complementary proteins** (e.g., rice + dal) with PDCAAS > 0.9.

#### **Part (b) — Food Exchange Concept (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **food exchange list** is a system in which foods within the same group are considered **interchangeable** because they provide approximately the same amount of calories, protein, fat, and carbohydrate per specified serving.

**Example (3 marks):** In the "Cereal Exchange" group, **1 exchange = 30 g raw rice ≈ 100 kcal ≈ 2 g protein ≈ 20 g carbohydrate**, which can be exchanged with:
- 30 g raw wheat
- 35 g raw ragi
- 30 g raw jowar
- 1 medium roti (≈ 30 g wheat flour)

**Role in Diet Planning (2 marks):**
1. **Variety:** Prevents monotony in the diet.
2. **Local availability:** Allows substitution based on regional produce.
3. **Caloric control:** Ensures consistent energy intake.
4. **Cost efficiency:** Patient can choose cheaper alternatives within the same group.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ICMR and USDA** — Always mention **ICMR** for KTU unless the question explicitly says otherwise.
> 2. **Missing the 5-group count** — Listing only 4 groups is a common error (students often merge sugar with fats, but ICMR treats them together in one group, so do not split them).
> 3. **Atwater factor units** — Use **kcal/g**, not kJ/g, unless the question asks for SI units. (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ.)
> 4. **Vegetable sub-classification** — Always mention **GLV separately**; examiners allocate 1 mark for this distinction.
> 5. **RDA references** — When writing 300 g/day vegetables, include the **50 g GLV** detail to score full marks.
> 6. **Calculation errors** — In Part B energy problems, **always verify** with the energy balance equation in the last step.
> 7. **Exchange list confusion** — Exchange is **within group**, not across groups. Do NOT exchange rice for milk.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Food Groups"**

- **Definition:** Systematic classification of foods based on nutrient composition.
- **ICMR classification has exactly 5 groups** (not 4, not 6).
- **Five groups are:** (1) Cereals/millets/pulses, (2) Vegetables/fruits, (3) Milk/animal foods, (4) Oils/fats/nuts/sugar, (5) Condiments/spices.
- **Atwater factors (must memorize):** 4 kcal/g for CHO and protein; **9 kcal/g** for fat.
- **ICMR daily veg + fruit:** 300 g + 100 g (with 50 g GLV).
- **Visible oil recommendation:** 25–30 g/day for sedentary adult.
- **Egg protein BV = 94** (highest biological value, used as reference).
- **Energy distribution norm:** CHO 60%, Protein 15%, Fat 25% (ideal balanced diet).
- **Food exchange** happens **within the same group**, not across groups.
- **Balanced diet = diet that provides all nutrients in correct proportions** from all five groups.
- **Pulses + cereals = complementary protein** (improves PDCAAS for vegetarian diet).
- **Glossaries to remember:** PDCAAS, BV (Biological Value), PER (Protein Efficiency Ratio), NPU (Net Protein Utilization), RDA, EAR.
- **Public health link:** Food group framework is the basis of **mid-day meal schemes, ICDS, PDS, and POSHAN Abhiyaan** in India.
- **For Part B (14 marks):** Always present a **table** for the food group classification; this fetches easy 4–5 marks.
- **Industrial relevance:** Milling (cereals), extraction (oilseeds), pasteurization (milk), canning (fruits/veg), fortification (across all groups).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutrients in food - Carbohydrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nutrients in food - Carbohydrates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbohydrates** are chemically defined as **polyhydroxy aldehydes** (aldoses) or **polyhydroxy ketones** (ketoses), or as substances that upon hydrolysis yield one or both of these compound classes. The simplest structural unit is the **saccharide** (from the Greek *sakkharon*, meaning sugar).

The historical, empirical general formula for carbohydrates is expressed as:

$$C_n(H_2O)_n$$

This formula gave rise to the misleading but commonly used name "hydrates of carbon." Although the empirical formula holds reasonably well for simple sugars, it fails for several derived sugars such as **deoxysugars** (e.g., deoxyribose), **amino sugars** (e.g., glucosamine), and **sugar acids** (e.g., glucuronic acid). Hence, modern biochemistry defines carbohydrates based on their **functional group chemistry** rather than the empirical composition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Fuel Tank of a Car:**
> Imagine your body as a car engine. Just as a petrol/diesel tank supplies energy to run the engine, **carbohydrates are the primary, fast-accessible fuel tank of the human body**. 
> - **Glucose** is the "petrol" that directly powers every cell.
> - **Glycogen** is the "reserve fuel tank" stored in the liver and muscles.
> - **Dietary fiber (cellulose)** is the "rough frame" of the fuel source — it cannot be burnt by human enzymes but keeps the digestive machinery clean and functional.

### 1.3 Standard Energy Metric

> [!TIP]
> The **Atwater energy factor** for carbohydrates is **4 kcal/g** (which equals **17 kJ/g**). This is the universally accepted standard used in nutritional labeling, food composition tables, and dietary planning worldwide.

| Energy Source | Energy Yield per Gram |
|---|---|
| Carbohydrate | **4 kcal (17 kJ)** |
| Protein | 4 kcal (17 kJ) |
| Fat | 9 kcal (37 kJ) |
| Alcohol (Ethanol) | 7 kcal (29 kJ) |

### 1.4 Daily Nutritional Recommendation (RDA Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) Dietary Guidelines for Indians (2020)**, carbohydrates should contribute **45–65 % of total daily caloric intake**, with an emphasis on complex carbohydrates and minimal intake of free sugars (less than **25 g/day** of added/free sugars, ideally less than **5 %** of total energy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Carbohydrates Matter — The "Why" Behind Each Function

Carbohydrates perform four major biological roles in food systems:

1. **Primary Energy Source (Glucose Metabolism):**
   - Through **glycolysis**, the **Krebs (TCA) cycle**, and **oxidative phosphorylation**, glucose yields **38 ATP molecules per molecule** of glucose aerobically.
   - The brain and central nervous system rely almost exclusively on glucose, requiring approximately **120 g of glucose per day**.

2. **Energy Storage Polysaccharides:**
   - In plants: **Starch** (amylose + amylopectin) stored in tubers, seeds, grains.
   - In animals: **Glycogen** stored in liver (~100 g) and skeletal muscle (~400 g).

3. **Structural Polysaccharides:**
   - **Cellulose** in plant cell walls (β-1,4 linkages).
   - **Chitin** in fungal cell walls and arthropod exoskeletons.

4. **Dietary Fiber and Functional Components:**
   - Indigestible carbohydrates that aid gut motility, feed gut microbiota (prebiotic effect), and regulate blood glucose.

### 2.2 Classification of Carbohydrates (Step-by-Step Logic)

Carbohydrates are broadly classified based on **degree of polymerization (DP)** and **chemical complexity**:

**Step 1 → Identify the monomeric units**
- Carbohydrates are built from single units called **monosaccharides**.

**Step 2 → Determine how many units are joined**
- **1 unit** → Monosaccharide
- **2 units** → Disaccharide
- **3–9 units** → Oligosaccharide
- **10 or more units** → Polysaccharide

**Step 3 → Within polysaccharides, distinguish by structure and function**
- **Storage polysaccharides** (starch, glycogen)
- **Structural polysaccharides** (cellulose, chitin)

**Step 4 → Recognize modified sugars**
- Sugar alcohols (sorbitol, mannitol), sugar acids (ascorbic acid), amino sugars (glucosamine).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all essential carbohydrates concepts, equations, and metrics needed for KTU examinations. No vertical pipes are used inside cells to preserve markdown rendering.

| Concept / Parameter | Chemical Symbol / Formula | Value / Definition | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| General empirical formula | $C_n(H_2O)_n$ | Hydrate-of-carbon approximation | Outdated but exam-relevant |
| Actual molecular formula of glucose | $C_6H_{12}O_6$ | Aldose hexose sugar | Primary blood sugar |
| Molecular formula of sucrose | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$ | Disaccharide (Glu + Fru) | Common table sugar |
| Polysaccharide repeat unit | $C_6H_{10}O_5$ | Loss of $H_2O$ per glycosidic bond | Starch/cellulose monomer |
| Energy yield of carbohydrate | — | **4 kcal/g** (17 kJ/g) | Atwater factor |
| Glycemic Index (GI) | $GI$ | Scale 0–100 | Glucose = 100 (reference) |
| Glycemic Load | $GL$ | $GI \times \frac{CHO \text{ (g)}}{100}$ | Real-world glycemic impact |
| RDA (% of energy) | — | **45–65 %** of total kcal | ICMR recommendation |
| Free sugars limit | — | $<25$ g/day (ideal $<5$ %) | WHO/ICMR guideline |
| ATP yield from glucose (aerobic) | — | **38 ATP** | Full oxidation |
| ATP yield (anaerobic) | — | **2 ATP** | Lactic acid fermentation |

### 2.4 Glycemic Index — Concept and Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glycemic Index (GI)** is a ranking system (0–100) for carbohydrate-containing foods based on how they affect blood glucose levels compared to pure glucose (set at 100). 
> - **High GI:** $\geq 70$ (white bread, glucose, potatoes)
> - **Medium GI:** 56–69 (basmati rice, sucrose)
> - **Low GI:** $\leq 55$ (lentils, oats, apples)

**Real-world food technology utility:**
- Designing **diabetic-friendly food products**.
- Formulating **sports nutrition** (high GI for quick recovery, low GI for sustained energy).
- Creating **weight-management foods** with low glycemic load.

### 2.5 Major Carbohydrate Sources (Food Science Perspective)

| Carbohydrate Type | Common Food Sources | Digestibility |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose | Fruits, honey, corn syrup | Fully digestible |
| Fructose | Fruits, honey, high-fructose corn syrup | Fully digestible |
| Sucrose | Sugarcane, sugar beet, fruits | Fully digestible |
| Lactose | Milk and dairy products | Requires lactase enzyme |
| Maltose | Germinating grains, malt products | Fully digestible |
| Starch | Rice, wheat, potato, maize | Fully digestible |
| Glycogen | Animal liver, meat (trace) | Fully digestible |
| Cellulose | Vegetables, whole grains, legumes | **Indigestible** (fiber) |
| Pectin | Apples, citrus fruits | Partially digestible |
| Inulin | Garlic, onion, chicory root | Indigestible (prebiotic) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrolysis Reaction of a Disaccharide

The general hydrolysis of a disaccharide to two monosaccharides follows a condensation-reversal pathway. The glycosidic bond breaks by addition of water, releasing the two monomers.

**General Reaction (Sucrose as example):**

$$\text{Sucrose} + \text{Water} \xrightarrow{\text{Invertase or H}^+} \text{Glucose} + \text{Fructose}$$

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Representation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the molecular formula of sucrose:**
$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the reactant water molecule:**
$$H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of mass (sum of atoms on LHS = RHS):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS atoms:} \quad & C: 12, \quad H: 22 + 2 = 24, \quad O: 11 + 1 = 12 \\
\text{RHS atoms:} \quad & C: 6 + 6 = 12, \quad H: 12 + 12 = 24, \quad O: 6 + 6 = 12
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Confirm mass balance:**
All atom counts match on both sides, confirming a valid hydrolysis equation. The reaction is **endothermic** and energy-neutral under standard conditions; however, the activation energy barrier is reduced significantly by the enzyme **invertase** (β-fructofuranosidase, EC 3.2.1.26).

**Step 5 — Examine the resulting monosaccharide mixture:**
The equimolar mixture of glucose and fructose produced is called **invert sugar**, which is **levorotatory** (rotates plane-polarized light to the left), whereas sucrose is **dextrorotatory**. This optical rotation inversion is the origin of the term "invert sugar," commonly used in confectionery to prevent crystallization (e.g., in honey, fondants).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Caloric Energy from Carbohydrate Oxidation

The complete combustion of glucose inside living cells (via cellular respiration) follows the overall equation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + \text{Energy}$$

**Step 1 — Convert mass of glucose to moles:**
$$n_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1\text{ g}}{180\text{ g/mol}} = 5.56 \times 10^{-3}\text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard enthalpy of combustion:**
$$\Delta H_{\text{comb}} = -2802 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total energy per gram:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E &= n \times \Delta H_{\text{comb}} \\
&= 5.56 \times 10^{-3}\text{ mol} \times 2802\text{ kJ/mol} \\
&= 15.57\text{ kJ/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dietary kcal:**
$$E = \frac{15.57\text{ kJ}}{4.184\text{ kJ/kcal}} \approx 3.72\text{ kcal/g}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Atwater round-off:**
For food-labeling purposes, this is rounded to **4 kcal/g**, which is the value universally reported on nutrition fact panels worldwide.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Glycemic Load Calculation

Glycemic Load (GL) integrates both the **quality (GI)** and **quantity (available carbohydrate)** of a food serving.

$$GL = \frac{GI \times \text{available CHO per serving (g)}}{100}$$

**Worked Example:**
A bowl of cooked white rice contains **45 g** of available carbohydrate per serving and has a **GI of 73**.

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**
$$GL = \frac{73 \times 45}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Perform arithmetic:**
$$GL = 32.85$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result:**
- **GL $< 10$** → Low (safe for daily consumption)
- **GL 11–19** → Medium
- **GL $\geq 20$** → High

Since $32.85 > 20$, this rice serving has a **high glycemic load**, making it less suitable for diabetic patients.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Table of Major Mono- and Disaccharides

| Sugar | Type | Molecular Formula | Sweetness (relative to sucrose = 1.0) | Key Food Source | Digestibility Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose | Aldohexose (monosaccharide) | $C_6H_{12}O_6$ | **0.70** | Blood, fruits, honey | Direct absorption |
| Fructose | Ketohexose (monosaccharide) | $C_6H_{12}O_6$ | **1.70** (sweetest natural sugar) | Fruits, honey, HFCS | Absorbed via GLUT-5 |
| Galactose | Aldohexose (monosaccharide) | $C_6H_{12}O_6$ | 0.30 | Lactose hydrolysis product | Converted to glucose in liver |
| Sucrose | Disaccharide | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$ | **1.00** (reference) | Sugarcane, beet | Hydrolyzed by sucrase |
| Lactose | Disaccharide | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$ | 0.40 | Milk and dairy | Requires lactase |
| Maltose | Disaccharide | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$ | 0.50 | Malt, germinating seeds | Hydrolyzed by maltase |
| Trehalose | Disaccharide | $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$ | 0.45 | Mushrooms, yeast | Hydrolyzed by trehalase |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Flow of Carbohydrates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CARBOHYDRATES] --> B[Simple Carbohydrates]
    A --> C[Complex Carbohydrates]
    
    B --> D[Monosaccharides]
    B --> E[Disaccharides]
    B --> F[Oligosaccharides 3 to 9 units]
    
    D --> D1[Glucose C6H12O6]
    D --> D2[Fructose C6H12O6]
    D --> D3[Galactose C6H12O6]
    D --> D4[Pentoses e.g. Ribose]
    
    E --> E1[Sucrose Glu plus Fru]
    E --> E2[Lactose Glu plus Gal]
    E --> E3[Maltose Glu plus Glu]
    
    C --> G[Storage Polysaccharides]
    C --> H[Structural Polysaccharides]
    
    G --> G1[Starch Amylose plus Amylopectin]
    G --> G2[Glycogen Liver plus Muscle]
    
    H --> H1[Cellulose Plant cell wall]
    H --> H2[Chitin Fungal wall]
    H --> H3[Pectin Fruits]
    
    G1 --> S1[Sourced from Rice Wheat Potato Maize]
    G2 --> S2[Found in Animal Liver and Muscle]
    H1 --> S3[Found in Vegetables Whole Grains Legumes]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Carbohydrate Digestion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INGESTED FOOD] --> P2[MOUTH Salivary Amylase]
    P2 --> P3[STOMACH Acidic pH stops amylase]
    P3 --> P4[SMALL INTESTINE Pancreatic Amylase]
    P4 --> P5[MALTOSE MALTOTRIOSE DEXTRINS]
    P5 --> P6[BRUSH BORDER ENZYMES Maltase Sucrase Lactase]
    P6 --> P7[MONOSACCHARIDES Glucose Fructose Galactose]
    P7 --> P8[ABSORPTION via SGLT1 and GLUT transporters]
    P8 --> P9[PORTAL VEIN to Liver]
    P9 --> P10[BLOOD CIRCULATION Glucose to Cells]
    P10 --> P11[ATP PRODUCTION Glycolysis plus TCA plus ETC]
    
    F1[INDIGESTIBLE FIBER] --> F2[COLON Microbiota Fermentation]
    F2 --> F3[SHORT CHAIN FATTY ACIDS Acetate Propionate Butyrate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Sucrose Hydrolysis (Inversion Process)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SucroseState
    SucroseState: Sucrose Dextrorotatory plus 66.5 degrees
    SucroseState --> EnzymeComplex: plus Invertase enzyme
    EnzymeComplex: Enzyme Substrate Complex
    EnzymeComplex --> Hydrolysis: plus H2O molecule
    Hydrolysis: Hydrolysis Step
    Hydrolysis --> InvertSugar: Glucose plus Fructose
    InvertSugar: Invert Sugar Levorotatory minus 39.9 degrees
    InvertSugar --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Glycemic Index Bar Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[FOOD GLYCEMIC IMPACT] --> R[High GI greater than or equal to 70]
    Q --> S[Medium GI 56 to 69]
    Q --> T[Low GI less than or equal to 55]
    
    R --> R1[White Bread GI 75]
    R --> R2[Glucose GI 100]
    R --> R3[Potato GI 78]
    
    S --> S1[Basmati Rice GI 58]
    S --> S2[Sucrose GI 65]
    S --> S3[Banana GI 62]
    
    T --> T1[Lentils GI 32]
    T --> T2[Oats GI 55]
    T --> T3[Apples GI 36]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define carbohydrates. Write the general empirical formula and the energy value per gram.*

**Model Answer:**
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds that yield such substances on hydrolysis. The empirical general formula is $C_n(H_2O)_n$. They supply **4 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)** of energy as per the Atwater factor. Carbohydrates are the body's primary source of energy, especially for the brain and nervous system.

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between starch and cellulose in terms of structure and digestibility.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Starch | Cellulose |
|---|---|---|
| Glycosidic linkage | α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,6 (amylopectin) | β-1,4 linkages only |
| Function | Energy storage in plants | Structural cell wall component |
| Digestibility in humans | Fully digestible | **Indigestible** (humans lack cellulase) |
| Branching | Highly branched (amylopectin) | Linear, unbranched |
| Solubility | Partially soluble in hot water | Insoluble |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the empirical formula $C_n(H_2O)_n$ with the actual molecular formula of all carbohydrates — it fails for deoxysugars, amino sugars, and uronic acids. Examiners deduct marks if you state it as a universal truth.
> 2. **Always specify** the glycosidic linkage type (α or β) and the carbon numbers involved (e.g., α-1,4 glycosidic bond) when describing disaccharides — generic "bond" answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Glycemic Index vs. Glycemic Load** — students often use these interchangeably. GI measures quality only; GL accounts for both quality AND quantity. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Energy value (4 kcal/g)** is the Atwater factor — do not write 3.75 kcal/g or 17.5 kJ/g; round to standard values.

---

#### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Classify carbohydrates with suitable examples. Add a note on the biological functions of carbohydrates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Carbohydrates:**

1. **Monosaccharides (Single sugar units):** Cannot be hydrolyzed further.
   - Examples: Glucose (aldohexose), Fructose (ketohexose), Galactose, Ribose (aldopentose).
   - General formula: $(CH_2O)_n$ where $n = 3$ to $7$.

2. **Disaccharides (Two monosaccharide units joined by glycosidic bond):**
   - Sucrose (glucose + fructose, α-1,β-2 glycosidic bond)
   - Lactose (glucose + galactose, β-1,4 glycosidic bond)
   - Maltose (glucose + glucose, α-1,4 glycosidic bond)
   - General formula: $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$.

3. **Oligosaccharides (3–9 monosaccharide units):**
   - Examples: Raffinose, Stachyose (found in legumes; cause flatulence due to bacterial fermentation).

4. **Polysaccharides (10 or more units):**
   - **Storage:** Starch (amylose + amylopectin), Glycogen.
   - **Structural:** Cellulose, Chitin, Pectin.

**Biological Functions of Carbohydrates:**

1. **Primary energy source** — 4 kcal/g, especially for brain and RBCs.
2. **Energy storage** — Glycogen in animals; Starch in plants.
3. **Structural role** — Cellulose in plants; chitin in fungi.
4. **Dietary fiber** — Promotes gut health, prevents constipation.
5. **Glycoproteins and glycolipids** — Cell signaling and recognition.
6. **Nucleic acid backbone** — Ribose (RNA), Deoxyribose (DNA).

**(b)** Explain the structure of starch. Differentiate between amylose and amylopectin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Starch Structure:**
Starch is the principal storage polysaccharide in plants, composed of glucose units linked by glycosidic bonds. It exists as a mixture of two polymers:

**1. Amylose (15–20 % of starch):**
- Linear, unbranched chain of glucose units linked by **α-1,4 glycosidic bonds**.
- Molecular weight: ~10⁵ to 10⁶ Da.
- Forms a **helical structure** (6 glucose units per turn).
- Soluble in hot water; gives **blue color** with iodine (I₂/KI) test due to iodine fitting into the helical cavity.
- Lower viscosity in solution.

**2. Amylopectin (80–85 % of starch):**
- Highly **branched** structure with **α-1,4** linkages in chains and **α-1,6** linkages at branch points (every 24–30 glucose units).
- Molecular weight: ~10⁶ to 10⁸ Da (much larger than amylose).
- Insoluble in water; gives **purple-red** color with iodine.
- Higher viscosity; responsible for thickening in food.

**Diagrammatic Distinction (to be drawn in exam):**

- Amylose: → α-1,4 → α-1,4 → α-1,4 → α-1,4 → (linear chain)
- Amylopectin: → α-1,4 → α-1,4 → α-1,6 (branch point) → α-1,4 → α-1,4 (branched)

---

#### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Define glycemic index and glycemic load. Explain their significance in food product formulation with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Glycemic Index (GI):**
GI is a numerical scale (0–100) that ranks carbohydrate-containing foods based on their effect on post-prandial blood glucose levels compared to a reference food (pure glucose = 100).

- **High GI:** $\geq 70$ (white bread, glucose, potatoes)
- **Medium GI:** 56–69 (basmati rice, sucrose)
- **Low GI:** $\leq 55$ (lentils, oats, apples)

**Glycemic Load (GL):**
GL is calculated as $GL = \frac{GI \times \text{available CHO per serving (g)}}{100}$ and accounts for both the quality (GI) and quantity (carbohydrate content) of a serving.

- **GL $< 10$** → Low
- **GL 11–19** → Medium
- **GL $\geq 20$** → High

**Significance in Food Product Formulation:**

1. **Diabetic-friendly foods** — Use low-GI ingredients (oats, barley, legumes) to formulate products with $GL < 10$.
2. **Sports nutrition** — High-GI foods (glucose polymers, maltodextrins) immediately post-workout for rapid glycogen replenishment.
3. **Weight management** — Low-GI/high-fiber formulations promote satiety and steady insulin response.
4. **Functional foods** — Resistant starch and soluble fiber (β-glucan) lower GL while boosting prebiotic activity.

**Example:** A standard 50 g white bread slice has GI ≈ 75 and 25 g available carbohydrate:
$$GL = \frac{75 \times 25}{100} = 18.75 \text{ (Medium)}$$
Replacing white flour with whole-grain oat flour (GI ≈ 55) reduces GL significantly.

**(b)** Discuss the dietary sources, requirements, and deficiency/excess implications of carbohydrates in human nutrition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Dietary Sources:**
- **Simple sugars:** Fruits (fructose), milk (lactose), honey, table sugar (sucrose), sugarcane juice.
- **Complex carbohydrates:** Whole grains (wheat, rice, oats), tubers (potato, yam), legumes (lentils, chickpeas), cereals.
- **Dietary fiber:** Vegetables, fruits with skin, whole grains, nuts.

**2. Requirements (RDA):**
- **ICMR (2020)** recommends **45–65 %** of total daily energy from carbohydrates.
- For a 2000 kcal diet: 225–325 g/day of carbohydrates.
- **Free/added sugars** should be less than **25 g/day** (ideal $<5$ % of energy).

**3. Deficiency Implications:**
- **Hypoglycemia** — dizziness, tremors, fatigue, irritability.
- **Ketosis** — when glucose is severely lacking, the body breaks down fats producing **ketone bodies** (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate), leading to ketoacidosis.
- **Muscle wasting** — protein breakdown for gluconeogenesis.
- **Impaired brain function** — cognitive decline, seizures in extreme cases.

**4. Excess Implications:**
- **Obesity** — excess carbohydrates stored as fat via lipogenesis.
- **Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus** — chronic hyperglycemia, insulin resistance.
- **Dental caries** — fermentable sugars promote cariogenic bacteria (*Streptococcus mutans*).
- **Hypertriglyceridemia** — high sugar intake raises blood triglycerides.
- **Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)** — fructose overload promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Carbohydrates — KTU Module 1:**

- ✅ Carbohydrates are **polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones** — not strictly hydrates of carbon.
- ✅ Empirical formula: **$C_n(H_2O)_n$** — historical, not universal.
- ✅ Atwater energy factor: **4 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)** — memorize for all calculations.
- ✅ RDA: **45–65 %** of total daily calories from carbohydrates (ICMR 2020).
- ✅ Free sugars limit: **$<25$ g/day** (WHO/ICMR).
- ✅ Complete glucose oxidation yields **38 ATP** (aerobic); **2 ATP** (anaerobic glycolysis).
- ✅ Glucose is the **primary blood sugar**; brain consumes **~120 g/day**.
- ✅ Monosaccharides are the **simplest, non-hydrolyzable** sugars (glucose, fructose, galactose).
- ✅ Disaccharides all share the molecular formula **$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$** — what differs is the glycosidic bond and monomers.
- ✅ Sucrose = **Glucose + Fructose** (α-1,β-2 bond); hydrolyzed by **invertase** → forms **invert sugar**.
- ✅ Lactose = **Glucose + Galactose** (β-1,4 bond); requires **lactase** — deficiency causes **lactose intolerance**.
- ✅ Maltose = **Glucose + Glucose** (α-1,4 bond); found in germinating grains.
- ✅ Starch = **Amylose (15–20 %, linear, α-1,4) + Amylopectin (80–85 %, branched, α-1,4 + α-1,6)**.
- ✅ **Glycogen** — animal equivalent of starch, more highly branched (branch every 8–12 units).
- ✅ **Cellulose** — β-1,4 linkages, **indigestible by humans** (we lack cellulase) — important dietary fiber.
- ✅ **Glycemic Index (GI)** measures *quality*; **Glycemic Load (GL)** measures *quality × quantity*.
- ✅ High GI $\geq 70$; Medium GI 56–69; Low GI $\leq 55$.
- ✅ Excess carbohydrate intake → obesity, T2DM, dental caries, NAFLD, hypertriglyceridemia.
- ✅ Deficiency → hypoglycemia, ketosis, muscle wasting, impaired cognition.
- ✅ **Invert sugar** (glucose + fructose from sucrose) is levorotatory and resists crystallization — used in confectionery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteins
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proteins

# MODULE 1 — PROTEINS IN FOOD TECHNOLOGY

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proteins** are high-molecular-weight biological macromolecules (biopolymers) composed of long chains of **α-amino acid residues** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds**, formed via the condensation reaction between the carboxyl (–COOH) group of one amino acid and the amino (–NH₂) group of another, releasing one molecule of water.

Proteins are the fundamental nitrogen-containing macronutrients of living systems and form the structural and functional basis of every biological cell. The word *protein* originates from the Greek word **"proteios"** meaning *of the first rank*, coined by Jöns Jacob Berzelius in 1838 and popularized by Gerardus Johannes Mulder. Proteins contain, on average, **16% nitrogen** by mass — a fact that forms the analytical basis for the classical Kjeldahl method of protein estimation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a protein like a pearl necklace...**
>
> Each **pearl** in the necklace represents an **amino acid**. Twenty different types of pearls (the 20 standard amino acids) can be strung together in any order, in any number, to make a necklace of unique shape, colour, and function. Just as a pearl necklace drapes differently depending on how it's hung (coiled, stretched, or knotted), the protein "necklace" folds itself into precise 3-D shapes — and **those shapes determine its job** in the body (an enzyme, a muscle fibre, a hormone, etc.). Break the necklace (denaturation), and the shape — and function — is destroyed, even though the pearls themselves are still intact.

### 1.3 General Properties of Proteins

- **Elemental Composition:** Carbon (50–55%), Hydrogen (6–7%), Oxygen (20–23%), Nitrogen (15–18%), Sulfur (0–3%); some contain phosphorus, iron, zinc, or copper.
- **Molecular Weight:** Ranging from ~5,000 Da (small peptides) to several million Da (e.g., titin in muscle ~ 3,000,000 Da).
- **Amphoteric Nature:** Contain both acidic (–COOH) and basic (–NH₂) groups → can act as both **acids and bases**, the basis of their buffering capacity.
- **Colloidal Nature:** Form colloidal sols in water due to large molecular size; coagulate upon heating, pH change, or salt addition.
- **Optical Activity:** All amino acids (except glycine) are optically active due to a chiral α-carbon → proteins rotate plane-polarized light.

### 1.4 Standard Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classical conversion factor used worldwide to estimate **crude protein** from measured nitrogen is:
>
> **N × 6.25 = Crude Protein (%)**
>
> This is derived from the fact that proteins contain, on average, **16% nitrogen** (100 ÷ 16 = 6.25). Specific food factors exist (e.g., **5.70 for rice**, **5.18 for milk**, **6.38 for wheat flour**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Amino Acids (The Building Blocks)

Amino acids are classified in **five standard ways** relevant to KTU exams:

#### (a) Based on R-group Polarity (Most Common Classification)
- **Non-polar (Hydrophobic):** Glycine, Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Proline, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Methionine.
- **Polar Uncharged:** Serine, Threonine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Asparagine, Glutamine.
- **Positively Charged (Basic):** Lysine, Arginine, Histidine.
- **Negatively Charged (Acidic):** Aspartate (Asp), Glutamate (Glu).

#### (b) Based on Nutritional Essentiality

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Essential (Indispensable)** | Cannot be synthesized by the human body; **must be supplied via diet** | Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys (8); His (children only) |
| **Non-essential** | Synthesized by the body | Gly, Ala, Ser, Cys, Tyr, Asp, Glu, Asn, Gln, Pro |
| **Conditionally Essential** | Become essential under specific physiological conditions (stress, illness, infancy) | Arg, Cys, Gln, Gly, Tyr, Pro |

#### (c) Based on Metabolic Fate (Glucogenic / Ketogenic)
- **Glucogenic:** Converted to glucose via gluconeogenesis (most amino acids).
- **Ketogenic:** Converted to ketone bodies — **Leucine and Lysine are purely ketogenic**.
- **Both:** Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr.

#### (d) Based on Structure
- **Aliphatic, Aromatic, Heterocyclic, Sulfur-containing.**

#### (e) Based on Charge at Physiological pH (7.4)
- **Neutral, Acidic, Basic.**

### 2.2 The Peptide Bond

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **peptide bond** (–CO–NH–) is a covalent amide linkage formed by **dehydration synthesis (condensation)** between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of the next, releasing **one H₂O molecule per bond**.

**Key Features of the Peptide Bond:**
1. **Partial Double Bond Character:** Due to resonance between C=O and C–N, the C–N bond length (1.32 Å) is shorter than a normal C–N single bond (1.47 Å) but longer than a C=N double bond (1.27 Å).
2. **Planar Geometry:** The six atoms (Cα, C, O, N, H, Cα') lie in a **rigid planar configuration** — restricts rotation.
3. **Trans Configuration:** Preferred over cis (except for proline) to minimize steric clash between adjacent R-groups.
4. **No charge:** At physiological pH, peptide bonds are electrically neutral.

### 2.3 Hierarchical Levels of Protein Structure

| Level | Description | Stabilizing Forces | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (1°)** | Linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds | **Covalent (peptide) bonds** | Insulin A-chain sequence |
| **Secondary (2°)** | Local regular folding into α-helices or β-pleated sheets via H-bonding between backbone atoms | **Hydrogen bonds** (backbone N–H···O=C) | Keratin (α-helix), Silk fibroin (β-sheet) |
| **Tertiary (3°)** | 3-D folding of an entire polypeptide chain | H-bonds, disulphide bridges, hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, van der Waals | Myoglobin |
| **Quaternary (4°)** | Association of two or more folded polypeptide subunits | Same as 3° + subunit interactions | Haemoglobin (4 subunits) |

### 2.4 Types of Secondary Structures

- **α-Helix:** Right-handed helix, **3.6 amino acid residues per turn**, pitch **5.4 Å**, H-bond between C=O of residue *n* and N–H of residue *n+4*. Stabilized by intra-chain H-bonds. Side chains project outward. Disrupted by **proline** (helix breaker) and **glycine** (too flexible).
- **β-Pleated Sheet:** Polypeptide chains (β-strands) aligned side-by-side, H-bonds **between chains** (inter-chain) rather than within. Two types:
  - **Parallel β-sheet** (N→C of strands running in same direction)
  - **Antiparallel β-sheet** (N→C running in opposite directions — more stable due to straighter H-bonds).

### 2.5 Forces Stabilizing Protein Structure

1. **Covalent bonds:** Peptide bonds (1°); Disulphide bridges –S–S– (between two Cys residues) (3°, 4°).
2. **Hydrogen bonds:** Between polar side chains or backbone.
3. **Hydrophobic interactions:** Non-polar R-groups cluster in interior away from water — the major driving force for tertiary folding.
4. **Ionic (electrostatic / salt bridges):** Between –COO⁻ of Asp/Glu and –NH₃⁺ of Lys/Arg.
5. **Van der Waals forces:** Weak, but cumulative in tightly packed interiors.
6. **Coordination bonds:** With metal ions (e.g., Fe in haem, Zn in insulin).

### 2.6 Denaturation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denaturation** is the disruption of the **native (functional) 3-D conformation** of a protein by physical or chemical agents, with **breaking of non-covalent bonds (and sometimes disulphide bonds)** while the **primary sequence remains intact** — peptide bonds are NOT broken.

**Agents of Denaturation:**

| Physical Agents | Chemical Agents |
|---|---|
| Heat | Acids (↓ pH) |
| Cold/freezing | Alkalis (↑ pH) |
| Pressure (high) | Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone) |
| Mechanical agitation (beating, whipping) | Heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺) |
| Radiation (UV, γ-rays) | Urea, guanidine HCl (chaotropic agents) |
| Ultrasonication | Detergents (SDS) |
| | Reducing agents (β-mercaptoethanol — break –S–S–) |

**Examples in Food Science:**
- **Egg-white coagulation** on boiling (ovalbumin denatures at ~62 °C).
- **Milk curdling** by acid (yoghurt, paneer) or rennet (cheese).
- **Whisky haze** — due to precipitation of conalbumin by tannins.
- **Bread baking** — gluten denaturation gives structure.

> **Renaturation** is possible only in some cases (e.g., ribonuclease, Anfinsen's experiment). If disulphide bonds are broken irreversibly, the protein cannot refold correctly.

### 2.7 Functional Properties of Proteins in Foods

| Property | Definition | Food Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Solubility** | Ability to disperse in water | Beverages (whey protein), sauces |
| **Emulsification** | Stabilization of oil-in-water or water-in-oil systems | Mayonnaise, salad dressings (egg yolk, casein) |
| **Foaming/Whipping** | Formation of stable gas-in-liquid dispersions | Meringues, mousses, whipped cream (egg white albumin) |
| **Gelation** | Formation of 3-D network trapping water | Jellies, tofu (soy protein), aspic (gelatin) |
| **Viscosity** | Thickening of aqueous systems | Soups, gravies (sodium caseinate) |
| **Water-holding capacity (WHC)** | Ability to retain water against gravity | Meat products, bakery |
| **Dough formation** | Gluten viscoelastic network | Bread, noodles |
| **Flavour binding** | Adsorption of volatile compounds | Texturized vegetable proteins |

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they are HIGH-YIELD for KTU numericals.**

| # | Formula | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Crude Protein (\%)} = N \times F$ | $F$ = conversion factor (**6.25** standard, **5.70** rice, **6.38** wheat flour, **5.18** milk) |
| 2 | $\text{True Protein (\%)} = (\text{Total N} - \text{NPN}) \times F$ | NPN = Non-Protein Nitrogen (free amino acids, urea, ammonia) |
| 3 | $\text{Digestibility (\%)} = \frac{\text{N intake} - \text{N in faeces}}{\text{N intake}} \times 100$ | Determines available protein |
| 4 | $\text{BV (Biological Value)} = \frac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N absorbed}} \times 100$ | Measure of protein quality |
| 5 | $\text{NPU (Net Protein Utilization)} = \text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}$ | Overall protein quality |
| 6 | $\text{PER (Protein Efficiency Ratio)} = \frac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein intake (g)}}$ | Rat growth assay |
| 7 | $\text{NPR (Net Protein Ratio)} = \frac{\text{Weight gain of test group} + \text{Weight loss of non-protein group}}{\text{Protein intake}}$ | Modified PER |
| 8 | $\text{PDCAAS} = \text{AAS} \times \text{Digestibility} \times \text{Truncation factor (1.00 max)}$ | Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score — **FAO/WHO 1991 standard** |
| 9 | $\text{DIAAS (Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score)} = \frac{\text{mg of digestible dietary indispensable A.A. in 1 g test protein}}{\text{mg of same A.A. in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100$ | **FAO 2013 standard**, replaces PDCAAS |

> ⚠️ All formulas above use **percentage basis (%)**. Always quote BV/NPU in **%**; PER and NPR are **dimensionless ratios**.

### 2.9 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Understanding proteins is fundamental to:
- **Food Processing Industry:** Designing thermal processes (pasteurization, UHT, sterilization), texturized vegetable proteins (TVP), protein concentrates/isolates.
- **Clinical Nutrition:** Treating protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) — Kwashiorkor and Marasmus.
- **Sports Nutrition:** Designing BCAA-enriched supplements.
- **Food Quality Testing:** SDS-PAGE, ELISA, amino acid analysis via HPLC.
- **Allergen Management:** Identifying and labelling food allergens (e.g., gluten, casein, peanut proteins).

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Crude Protein from Kjeldahl Nitrogen

The Kjeldahl method measures **total organic nitrogen** in a food, which is then converted to crude protein using the conversion factor.

**Step 1 — Digestion:**
The food sample is digested with concentrated **H₂SO₄** in the presence of a catalyst (CuSO₄ or Se) at ~400 °C. All organic nitrogen is converted into **ammonium sulphate**.

$$
\text{Protein (organic)} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{catalyst}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Distillation:**
The digest is made alkaline with **NaOH**, liberating ammonia:

$$
(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The liberated NH₃ is distilled over and trapped in a known volume of standard **HCl or H₃BO₃** solution.

**Step 3 — Titration:**
The trapped ammonia is titrated against standard acid. The volume of acid consumed is proportional to nitrogen content.

**Step 4 — Calculation:**

$$
\% N = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N_{acid} \times 14.01 \times 100}{W_{sample}}
$$

where:
- $V_s$ = volume of acid used for sample (mL)
- $V_b$ = volume of acid used for blank (mL)
- $N_{acid}$ = normality of standard acid (eq/L)
- $14.01$ = molecular weight of nitrogen
- $W_{sample}$ = weight of sample (g)

**Step 5 — Conversion to Crude Protein:**

$$
\% \text{ Crude Protein} = \% N \times F
$$

where $F$ is the **nitrogen-to-protein conversion factor**. The standard $F = 6.25$ arises from:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since proteins contain 16\% N by mass,} \\
\Rightarrow F &= \frac{100}{16} = 6.25
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical 1 — Crude Protein Calculation (KTU Standard Style)

> **[Problem — KTU University Exam Model]** A 2.0 g sample of milk powder was digested and distilled into 50 mL of 0.1 N HCl. The excess acid required 25 mL of 0.1 N NaOH for back-titration. A blank consumed 50 mL of 0.1 N NaOH. Calculate the % crude protein in the sample. (Use $F = 6.38$ for milk).

**Solution:**

Volume of acid neutralized by NH₃:

$$
V_s = (V_{blank} - V_{sample}) = (50 - 25) = 25 \text{ mL of 0.1 N NaOH equivalent}
$$

Equivalent to **25 mL of 0.1 N HCl** (since same normality).

Using the formula:

$$
\% N = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N_{acid} \times 14.01 \times 100}{W}
$$

Wait — careful: $V_s$ here represents the volume of acid that reacted with NH₃ directly, so:

$$
\% N = \frac{25 \times 0.1 \times 14.01 \times 100}{2.0 \times 1000}
$$

(Converting mL → L by dividing by 1000 in denominator; or equivalently use $V$ in mL and multiply numerator by 1/1000 — let me re-arrange.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% N &= \frac{25 \times 0.1 \times 1.401}{2.0} \\
&= \frac{3.5025}{2.0} \\
&= 1.7513\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Crude Protein} &= \% N \times F \\
&= 1.7513 \times 6.38 \\
&= \boxed{11.17\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — Biological Value (BV) Calculation

> **[Problem]** A protein test diet supplied 10 g N. Faecal N = 1.5 g; Urinary N = 2.0 g. Calculate **Digestibility, BV, and NPU**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Absorbed N:**

$$
N_{\text{absorbed}} = N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{faeces}} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Retained N:**

$$
N_{\text{retained}} = N_{\text{absorbed}} - N_{\text{urinary}} = 8.5 - 2.0 = 6.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Digestibility:**

$$
\text{Digestibility (\%)} = \frac{10 - 1.5}{10} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

**Step 4 — Biological Value:**

$$
\text{BV} = \frac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100 = \frac{6.5}{8.5} \times 100 = \boxed{76.47\%}
$$

**Step 5 — NPU:**

$$
\text{NPU} = \frac{\text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}}{100} = \frac{76.47 \times 85}{100} = \boxed{65.0\%}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protein Quality of Common Foods

| Food Protein | Limiting Amino Acid(s) | BV (%) | PDCAAS | Digestibility (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Egg (whole)** | None (reference) | 94–100 | 1.00 | 97 |
| **Cow's Milk (casein)** | Methionine + Cystine | 91 | 1.00 | 95 |
| **Whey** | None | 104–110 | 1.00 | 99 |
| **Fish** | Tryptophan (mild) | 83 | ~0.85 | 94 |
| **Beef** | None (complete) | 80 | 0.92 | 98 |
| **Soybean** | Methionine | 74 | 0.91 | 95 |
| **Rice** | Lysine | 83 | 0.50–0.65 | 88 |
| **Wheat gluten** | Lysine | 54–65 | 0.42 | 91 |
| **Maize (Corn)** | Lysine + Tryptophan | 60 | 0.42 | 85 |
| **Pea** | Methionine | 65 | 0.67 | 88 |

### 3.5 Limiting Amino Acid Concept (with Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A protein is like a barrel made of staves of unequal length. The **shortest stave** determines how much water the barrel can hold. So the **limiting amino acid** limits the biological utilization of the entire protein.

**Examples:**
- **Cereal grains (wheat, rice, maize):** Limiting amino acid = **Lysine**.
- **Legumes (soy, peanut, beans):** Limiting amino acid = **Methionine** (and Tryptophan).
- **Complementary Proteins:** Combining cereals + legumes gives a balanced amino acid profile (e.g., rice + dal, bread + peanut butter).

### 3.6 Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) — Bureau of Indian Standards (ICMR)

| Group | Protein RDA (g/kg body weight/day) |
|---|---|
| Adult male / female | **0.8–1.0 g/kg** |
| Pregnant women | +23 g/day |
| Lactating women | +16 g/day (first 6 months) |
| Infants (0–6 months) | 2.0 g/kg |
| Children (1–9 years) | 1.4–1.7 g/kg |
| Adolescents | 1.0 g/kg |

### 3.7 Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM)

| Feature | **Marasmus** | **Kwashiorkor** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Severe deficiency of **both** protein AND energy | Deficiency of protein only (with adequate carbs) |
| Age | Mostly < 18 months | 1–4 years (after weaning) |
| Appearance | "Skin and bone" — extreme wasting | Oedematous (swollen) — "sugar baby" |
| Subcutaneous fat | Absent | Present (sometimes) |
| Hair | Normal | Sparse, red/grey (flag sign), easily pluckable |
| Liver | Fatty infiltration is **less** common | **Fatty liver** (due to failure of apolipoprotein synthesis) |
| Serum albumin | Low | **Very low** (< 2.5 g/dL) → oedema |
| Treatment | Balanced diet + nursing care | High-protein diet + K-Mix supplement |
