## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define proteins. Name any four essential amino acids.**

**Model Answer:**
Proteins are complex nitrogen-containing biopolymers composed of α-amino acids linked together by peptide bonds, formed by condensation between the –COOH group of one amino acid and the –NH₂ group of another, with the release of water. They contain on an average 16% nitrogen.

Four essential amino acids: **Valine, Leucine, Lysine, Tryptophan** (any four from: Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys).

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 for listing amino acids)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
> **What is protein denaturation? List any four factors causing it.**

**Model Answer:**
Denaturation is the process by which a protein loses its native (functional) three-dimensional conformation due to disruption of non-covalent bonds (and sometimes disulphide bonds), while the primary amino acid sequence (peptide bonds) remains intact.

Four factors: **Heat, change in pH (acid/alkali), heavy metals (Pb, Hg), organic solvents (ethanol, acetone)** (any four from the table in §2.6).

*(3 marks: 2 definition + 1 for listing factors)*

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam Model — 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain in detail the **four levels of protein structure** with suitable examples and the bonds/forces stabilizing each level.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Describe the **classification of amino acids** based on (i) polarity of R-group, (ii) nutritional essentiality, and (iii) metabolic fate. Give examples for each class.

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q.A (a)

The four hierarchical levels of protein structure are:

**1. Primary Structure:**
- Linear sequence of amino acids joined by **peptide bonds**.
- The number, type, and order of amino acids determine identity and biological activity.
- Bond stabilizing: **Covalent peptide bonds** (and intra-chain disulphide bonds in some proteins like insulin).
- *Example:* Bovine insulin — 51 amino acids in 2 chains (A: 21 aa; B: 30 aa) connected by 2 disulphide bridges.

**2. Secondary Structure:**
- Regular local folding patterns of the polypeptide backbone stabilized by **H-bonds** between the backbone C=O of one residue and N–H of another.
- **α-Helix:** Right-handed; 3.6 residues/turn; pitch 5.4 Å; H-bond between residue *n* C=O and *n+4* N–H. Side chains project outward. *Example:* α-Keratin (hair, nails, wool).
- **β-Pleated Sheet:** Extended strands; H-bonds **between adjacent strands**; can be parallel or antiparallel. *Example:* Silk fibroin (antiparallel β-sheets).

**3. Tertiary Structure:**
- The **overall 3-D folding** of a single polypeptide chain into its functional (native) conformation.
- Stabilizing forces: H-bonds, disulphide bridges (–S–S–), hydrophobic interactions, ionic (salt) bridges, van der Waals forces.
- *Example:* **Myoglobin** — single chain 153 aa with one haem group; compact globular protein with hydrophobic interior.

**4. Quaternary Structure:**
- Assembly of two or more separately folded polypeptide **subunits** into a functional oligomeric protein.
- Stabilized by the same non-covalent forces as tertiary structure.
- *Example:* **Haemoglobin** — 4 subunits (2 α + 2 β), each carrying one haem; exhibits cooperative O₂ binding.

*(7 marks — 1 each for definition, 2 marks each for 1° and 2°, 2 marks for examples collectively)*

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q.A (b)

**(i) Based on Polarity of R-group:**

| Class | Character | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Non-polar (Hydrophobic) | R-group is hydrocarbon / aromatic | Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Pro, Gly |
| Polar uncharged | R-group has –OH, –SH, –CONH₂ | Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln |
| Positively charged (Basic) | R-group gains H⁺ at physiological pH | Lys, Arg, His |
| Negatively charged (Acidic) | R-group loses H⁺ at physiological pH | Asp, Glu |

**(ii) Based on Nutritional Essentiality:**
- **Essential (Indispensable):** Must be supplied by diet — Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys (His essential for infants).
- **Non-essential:** Synthesized by body — Gly, Ala, Ser, Cys, Tyr, Asp, Glu, Asn, Gln, Pro.
- **Conditionally Essential:** Become essential under stress/disease — Arg, Gln, Cys, Tyr, Gly, Pro.

**(iii) Based on Metabolic Fate:**
- **Glucogenic:** Converted to pyruvate or TCA intermediates → glucose. (All except Leu and Lys).
- **Ketogenic:** Converted to ketone bodies (acetyl-CoA or acetoacetate). **Leucine and Lysine only.**
- **Both Glucogenic + Ketogenic:** Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr.

*(7 marks — 2+3+2 marks distribution)*

---

> **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam Model — 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Define **Biological Value (BV)**, **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)**, and **PDCAAS**. Explain their significance in assessing protein quality.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **functional properties of proteins** in food systems. How do these properties influence food product development?

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q.B (a)

**Biological Value (BV):**
The percentage of absorbed nitrogen that is retained (utilized) by the body for growth and maintenance.

$$
\text{BV (\%)} = \frac{N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{faeces}} - N_{\text{urine}}}{N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{faeces}}} \times 100
$$

Higher BV = higher quality. Egg protein BV = 94–100 (reference).

**Net Protein Utilization (NPU):**
The fraction of ingested nitrogen retained by the body. Combines BV and digestibility.

$$
\text{NPU (\%)} = \text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}
$$

**PDCAAS (Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score):**
The current FAO/WHO standard (1991) for evaluating protein quality, based on the most limiting amino acid:

$$
\text{PDCAAS} = \text{AAS} \times \text{Digestibility} \times \text{Truncation factor (capped at 1.00)}
$$

where AAS = ratio of limiting amino acid in 1 g test protein to that in 1 g reference protein (FAO pattern).

**Significance:** BV, NPU, and PDCAAS together give a comprehensive measure of how well a dietary protein supports human growth, maintenance, and metabolic functions — essential for formulating balanced diets, complementary food products, and clinical nutrition plans. Limitations: BV is determined on rats; PDCAAS has been superseded by **DIAAS** (Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score) by FAO in 2013.

*(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 marks distribution)*

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q.B (b)

Proteins are not only nutrients but also **functional ingredients** that determine the texture, stability, appearance, and sensory quality of foods.

1. **Solubility:** Critical for beverages, infant formulas, and clear soups. Influenced by pH (lowest at isoelectric pI), ionic strength, and temperature. *Example:* Sodium caseinate (soluble) vs. casein (insoluble at pI 4.6).

2. **Emulsification:** Proteins adsorb at oil-water interfaces, lowering interfacial tension and stabilizing emulsions via the **hydrophilic-lipophilic balance** of amphiphilic molecules. *Examples:* Egg yolk in mayonnaise; whey protein in salad dressings; sodium caseinate in coffee creamers.

3. **Foaming:** Proteins unfold at air-water interfaces, forming cohesive films that trap air bubbles. Stable foams need proteins with good surface activity, solubility, and film strength. *Examples:* Egg white albumin in meringues; egg albumin in confectionery.

4. **Gelation:** On heating, proteins unfold and form a continuous 3-D network trapping water → gel. *Examples:* Egg-white coagulation; tofu from soy protein; gelatin desserts; surimi gels.

5. **Viscosity / Thickening:** Globular proteins (e.g., whey, soy) thicken aqueous solutions, used in soups, sauces, and gravies.

6. **Water-Holding Capacity (WHC):** Critical in comminuted meat products (sausages, hams) — prevents moisture loss during cooking.

7. **Dough Formation:** Wheat gluten forms a viscoelastic network giving dough its unique gas-retention and elasticity — basis of bread, noodles, and pasta.

8. **Flavour Binding:** Hydrophobic interactions trap volatile flavour compounds — important in texturized vegetable proteins (TVP) and meat analogues.

**Influence on Product Development:** Modern food product design exploits these functionalities to develop high-protein functional foods, plant-based meat/dairy analogues, and therapeutic clinical nutrition products.

*(7 marks — 6 marks for listing + explaining 6 properties, 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing peptide bond with hydrogen bond.** Peptide bonds hold the **primary** structure; H-bonds hold **secondary** structure. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **Writing "BV = N retained / N intake" instead of / N absorbed.** This is the **most common mistake** in numericals — always use absorbed N in the denominator.
> 3. **Forgetting the truncation factor in PDCAAS.** Many students write AAS × Digestibility only. PDCAAS is **capped at 1.00** (or 100%).
> 4. **Mixing up Marasmus and Kwashiorkor** — remember: Kwashiorkor = protein deficiency + **oedema** + fatty liver.
> 5. **Writing wrong conversion factor** — always use **5.70** for rice, **6.38** for milk/wheat, **6.25** as default.
> 6. **Writing "α-helix is left-handed"** — biologically, α-helices in proteins are **right-handed** (except rare synthetic polyglycine).

---

### TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-minute high-yield points to revise before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ✔ Protein = polymer of α-amino acids linked by **peptide bonds**, contains **~16% N**.
- ✔ **Standard conversion factor N × 6.25 = Crude Protein** (5.70 rice; 6.38 milk/wheat; 5.18 milk per some refs).
- ✔ **20 standard amino acids**, **8 essential** for adults (+ **Histidine** for infants).
- ✔ **Leucine and Lysine are purely ketogenic**; rest are glucogenic or both.
- ✔ Peptide bond has **partial double bond character** → rigid, planar, trans.
- ✔ **α-Helix:** right-handed, 3.6 residues/turn, pitch 5.4 Å, H-bond between *n* and *n+4*.
- ✔ **β-Sheet:** inter-strand H-bonds, parallel vs antiparallel.
- ✔ **Denaturation** disrupts 2°/3°/4° structure only; peptide bonds remain intact.
- ✔ Proline = **helix breaker**; Glycine = too flexible for helices.
- ✔ Disulphide bonds (–S–S– between two Cys) stabilize tertiary/quaternary structure.
- ✔ **Egg protein** = reference standard for BV, PDCAAS = 1.00.
- ✔ Cereal grains are limiting in **Lysine**; legumes in **Methionine** → complementary proteins.
- ✔ **Marasmus:** total calorie + protein deficiency, wasting. **Kwashiorkor:** protein deficiency with oedema + fatty liver.
- ✔ **BV** uses N absorbed in denominator; **NPU** = BV × Digestibility; **PDCAAS** = AAS × Digestibility × Truncation factor (max 1.00).
- ✔ Functional properties to memorize: Solubility, Emulsification, Foaming, Gelation, Viscosity, WHC, Dough formation, Flavour binding.
- ✔ **ICMR RDA for adult:** 0.8–1.0 g protein/kg body weight/day.
- ✔ **Kjeldahl method** — three steps: Digestion → Distillation → Titration.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 1 / PROTEINS**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — GDFTT104 Introduction to Food Technology*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lipids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Lipids):**
> Lipids are a diverse group of naturally occurring organic compounds that are **insoluble in water** but **soluble in non-polar organic solvents** (such as ether, chloroform, benzene, and acetone). They are characterized by a heterogeneous composition of fatty acids, glycerol, and other related bio-molecules, and serve as a major source of energy (≈ **9 kcal/g**), structural components of cell membranes, carriers of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), and precursors for hormones.

In the KTU 2024 Food Technology syllabus context, lipids are studied under three broad molecular families:

1. **Simple Lipids** – Esters of fatty acids with alcohols (e.g., fats, oils, waxes).
2. **Compound (Conjugated) Lipids** – Lipids containing additional chemical groups (e.g., phospholipids, glycolipids, lipoproteins).
3. **Derived Lipids** – Hydrolytic products of simple/compound lipids (e.g., fatty acids, sterols, fat-soluble vitamins).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners specifically test the student's ability to *classify* lipids, *draw/identify* the structure of a triglyceride, *differentiate* saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids, and explain the role of lipids in food functionality (emulsification, shortening, melting behavior).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of lipids as the **"greasy workers"** of the food world.

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen:
- **Water-soluble ingredients** (sugars, salts, proteins) behave like polite guests — they happily mix with each other (water-based broth, sauces).
- **Lipids**, however, behave like the **greasy chef** — they refuse to dissolve in water, stain every spoon they touch, but are absolutely essential for the dish to taste good, carry flavors, and provide that satisfying richness.

A simple real-world analogy:
- A drop of oil in a bowl of water is like trying to mix **two people speaking different languages** — they coexist but never truly blend. This is why emulsifiers (like lecithin in egg yolk) are needed in mayonnaise — they act as the **"translator"**, bridging the oil and water phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for food technology:** Every baked good (shortening), fried snack (heat transfer medium), dairy product (cream, butter), and processed meat (texture) relies fundamentally on lipid chemistry. Understanding lipids = understanding the *mouthfeel*, *shelf life*, and *nutritional quality* of foods.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Energy density of lipids | **9 kcal/g (≈ 37 kJ/g)** |
| Density of typical edible oil | **0.91 – 0.93 g/mL** at 25 °C |
| Smoking point (refined oil) | **190 – 250 °C** |
| Saponification value range | **190 – 210 mg KOH/g** |
| Iodine value range (oils) | **80 – 200 g I₂/100 g** |
| Calorific equivalent (Atwater) | **9.0 kcal/g** |

---

## 1.4 Lipid Composition – Molecular Building Blocks

All lipids are constructed primarily from two molecular units:

- **Glycerol** – A 3-carbon trihydric alcohol ($C_3H_8O_3$)
- **Fatty Acids** – Long-chain carboxylic acids (typically $C_4$ to $C_{24}$)

When **one fatty acid** binds glycerol → **Monoglyceride**
When **two fatty acids** bind glycerol → **Diglyceride**
When **three fatty acids** bind glycerol → **Triglyceride (TAG)** ← *Most abundant in nature*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Over 95% of the lipids we consume in our daily diet are in the form of **triglycerides**. This is why KTU Module-1 places heavy emphasis on the triglyceride structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fatty Acids – The Backbone of Lipid Chemistry

A **fatty acid** is a carboxylic acid with a long aliphatic chain (tail), which is either saturated or unsaturated.

### 2.1.1 General Structural Formula

$$ \text{R} - \text{COOH} $$

Where:
- **R** = Hydrocarbon chain (alkyl group), typically $C_nH_{2n+1}$
- **–COOH** = Carboxyl functional group (the "head")

### 2.1.2 Classification of Fatty Acids

**(A) Based on Chain Length**

| Type | Carbon Atoms | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Short-chain fatty acids (SCFA) | $C_2 - C_6$ | Acetic acid, Butyric acid |
| Medium-chain fatty acids (MCFA) | $C_8 - C_{12}$ | Capric acid, Lauric acid |
| Long-chain fatty acids (LCFA) | $C_{14} - C_{18}$ | Palmitic, Stearic, Oleic |
| Very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFA) | $> C_{20}$ | Erucic acid |

**(B) Based on Degree of Saturation**

| Type | Double Bonds | State at Room Temp | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Saturated (SFA) | 0 | Solid (fat) | Palmitic acid (16:0) |
| Monounsaturated (MUFA) | 1 | Liquid (oil) | Oleic acid (18:1) |
| Polyunsaturated (PUFA) | ≥ 2 | Liquid (oil) | Linoleic (18:2), Linolenic (18:3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Tip for KTU Exams:** The shorthand `18:2 ω-6` means: 18 carbons, 2 double bonds, with the first double bond at the 6th carbon from the methyl (ω) end. This is a frequently asked question.

**(C) Based on Nutritional Essentiality**

- **Essential Fatty Acids (EFA)** – Cannot be synthesized by the human body, must be obtained from diet.
   - **Linoleic acid (LA)** – 18:2 ω-6 (found in sunflower, corn oil)
   - **α-Linolenic acid (ALA)** – 18:3 ω-3 (found in flaxseed, fish oil)

---

## 2.2 Triglyceride Structure – The Most Critical Topic for KTU

A **triglyceride (triacylglycerol, TAG)** is formed by the **esterification** of one glycerol molecule with three fatty acid molecules.

### Step-by-Step Formation Logic

1. **Glycerol backbone** provides three hydroxyl (–OH) groups.
2. Each –OH group reacts with the –COOH of a fatty acid, releasing **3 molecules of water**.
3. The resulting bond is called an **ester bond (–COO–)**.

### Chemical Equation

$$ \text{Glycerol} + 3 \, \text{Fatty Acid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat / Catalyst}} \text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Structural Representation

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{CH}_2 - \text{O} - \text{CO} - \text{R}_1 \\
& \mid \\
& \text{CH} - \text{O} - \text{CO} - \text{R}_2 \\
& \mid \\
& \text{CH}_2 - \text{O} - \text{CO} - \text{R}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ = Hydrocarbon tails of three fatty acids (may be identical → *simple triglyceride*, or different → *mixed triglyceride*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Mixed" Triglycerides Matter:** In nature, most triglycerides are *mixed* (contain more than one type of fatty acid). This mixed composition determines the **melting point**, **texture**, and **crystallization behavior** of fats — a key concept in food product development (e.g., cocoa butter equivalents).

---

## 2.3 Physical & Chemical Properties of Lipids

### 2.3.1 Physical Properties

- **Density:** Lighter than water (oil floats on water).
- **Solubility:** Insoluble in water, soluble in organic solvents (Ether, Chloroform, Benzene).
- **Melting Point:** Increases with chain length; decreases with degree of unsaturation.
- **Cis-Trans Isomerism:** *Cis* unsaturated fatty acids have a "kink" → liquid at room temp. *Trans* fats (formed during partial hydrogenation) are straighter → solid at room temp.

### 2.3.2 Chemical Properties

| Reaction Type | Reagent | Product | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hydrolysis | Water / Acid / Enzyme | Glycerol + Fatty Acids | Soap making, digestion |
| Saponification | NaOH / KOH | Soap (Sodium salt of FA) | Hard/Soft soap |
| Hydrogenation | $H_2$ + Ni catalyst | Saturated fat | Margarine production |
| Rancidity | $O_2$ / Light / Heat | Peroxides, Aldehydes | Shelf-life reduction |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems in KTU Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Energy from lipids | $E = m \times 9 \, \text{kcal}$ | kcal |
| 2 | Saponification Value (SV) | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH/g |
| 3 | Iodine Value (IV) | $IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | g $I_2$/100g |
| 4 | Acid Value (AV) | $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH/g |
| 5 | Peroxide Value (PV) | $PV = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq $O_2$/kg |
| 6 | Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV) | Volatile FA × 0.1 N KOH (mL) | mL |
| 7 | Polenske Value (PV) | Insoluble FA × 0.1 N KOH (mL) | mL |
| 8 | Average Mol. Weight (from SV) | $MW = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$ | g/mol |

*Where:* $V_b$ = Blank titre value, $V_s$ = Sample titre value, $N$ = Normality, $W$ = Sample weight (g), **56.1** = Molecular weight of KOH, **12.69** = Atomic weight of $I_2$ divided by 10.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

1. **Margarine Manufacturing:** Partial hydrogenation of vegetable oils (controlled saturation) converts liquid oils into semi-solid spreads.
2. **Bakery Shortening:** Solid fats create flaky pastry layers by preventing gluten network fusion.
3. **Deep Frying:** Oils act as **heat-transfer media** with high thermal stability.
4. **Emulsification:** Lecithin (phospholipid) stabilizes oil-in-water emulsions in chocolate, ice-cream, mayonnaise.
5. **Cocoa Butter Equivalents (CBE):** Engineered triglycerides mimic cocoa butter's melting profile for chocolate confections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometry of Triglyceride Formation

**Problem Setup:**
Determine the molecular weight of tripalmitin (a simple triglyceride formed from glycerol + 3 palmitic acid molecules).

**Given:**
- Molecular formula of palmitic acid: $C_{16}H_{32}O_2$, MW = **256.4 g/mol**
- Molecular formula of glycerol: $C_3H_8O_3$, MW = **92.1 g/mol**
- Water released: 3 molecules per triglyceride

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total MW of 3 Palmitic Acid} &= 3 \times 256.4 = 769.2 \, \text{g/mol} \\
\text{MW of Glycerol} &= 92.1 \, \text{g/mol} \\
\text{MW of Water Released} &= 3 \times 18 = 54.0 \, \text{g/mol} \\
\text{MW of Tripalmitin} &= 769.2 + 92.1 - 54.0 \\
&= 807.3 \, \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Conversion Explanation:** The esterification reaction loses 3 water molecules (each 18 g/mol), so we subtract 54 g/mol from the sum of reactants. This is a standard stoichiometric balancing technique — *mass of products = mass of reactants*.

**Final Answer:** MW of Tripalmitin = **807.3 g/mol** ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculating Saponification Value (SV)

**Problem:**
A 2.5 g oil sample requires 38.5 mL of 0.5 N KOH for complete saponification. Blank titration consumes 48.0 mL. Calculate the **Saponification Value**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} & \quad W = 2.5 \, \text{g}, \, N = 0.5 \, \text{N}, \, V_b = 48.0 \, \text{mL}, \, V_s = 38.5 \, \text{mL} \\
\text{Formula:} & \quad SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W} \\
\text{Substitute:} & \quad SV = \dfrac{(48.0 - 38.5) \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2.5} \\
& \quad SV = \dfrac{9.5 \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2.5} \\
& \quad SV = \dfrac{266.475}{2.5} \\
& \quad SV = 106.59 \, \text{mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** SV ≈ **106.59 mg KOH/g** ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** A low SV (as here) indicates a **high molecular weight** fatty acid (long-chain fat). Coconut oil has a high SV (~250) due to abundant short-chain fatty acids.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Calculating Iodine Value (IV)

**Problem:**
3.0 g of groundnut oil reacts with iodine monochloride (Wijs solution). Blank titre = 45.2 mL of 0.1 N $Na_2S_2O_3$. Sample titre = 28.7 mL. Calculate the **Iodine Value**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} & \quad W = 3.0 \, \text{g}, \, N = 0.1 \, \text{N}, \, V_b = 45.2 \, \text{mL}, \, V_s = 28.7 \, \text{mL} \\
\text{Formula:} & \quad IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{W} \\
\text{Substitute:} & \quad IV = \dfrac{(45.2 - 28.7) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{3.0} \\
& \quad IV = \dfrac{16.5 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{3.0} \\
& \quad IV = \dfrac{20.9385}{3.0} \\
& \quad IV = 6.98 \, \text{g} \, I_2/100\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** IV ≈ **6.98 g $I_2$/100g** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Is Unusually Low:** Pure groundnut oil IV ranges 85–100 g $I_2$/100g. The low value here suggests either a refined/partially hydrogenated sample or experimental error. **Always sanity-check** your numerical answer against known literature ranges.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Identifying a Lipid Sample

| Property Tested | High Result Indicates | Low Result Indicates |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Saponification Value | Short-chain FA (e.g., coconut oil) | Long-chain FA (e.g., tallow) |
| Iodine Value | High unsaturation (e.g., linseed oil) | Saturated fat (e.g., coconut) |
| Acid Value | Hydrolytic rancidity / poor quality | Fresh, well-stored oil |
| Peroxide Value | Oxidative rancidity in progress | Stable, antioxidant-rich oil |
| Reichert-Meissl Value | High volatile FA (butter, coconut) | Non-volatile FA dominant |
| Melting Point | Long saturated FA chains | Short/unsaturated FA chains |

---

## 3.5 Essential vs. Non-Essential Fatty Acid Distinction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Essential FA} &: \text{Cannot be synthesized by mammals} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Must be supplied in diet} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Linoleic (18:2 ω-6) \& Linolenic (18:3 ω-3)} \\
\\
\textbf{Non-Essential FA} &: \text{Body synthesizes via elongation \& desaturation} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Palmitic, Stearic, Oleic acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Note:** The body can convert Linolenic (ω-3) into EPA and DHA — the long-chain ω-3 fatty acids critical for brain and retinal function. This biochemical pathway is a frequent 3-mark short answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Lipids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lipids] --> B[Simple Lipids]
    A --> C[Compound Lipids]
    A --> D[Derived Lipids]

    B --> B1[Fats and Oils - Triglycerides]
    B --> B2[Waxes - Esters of FA with long-chain alcohols]

    C --> C1[Phospholipids - Lecithin, Cephalin]
    C --> C2[Glycolipids - Cerebrosides]
    C --> C3[Lipoproteins - Chylomicrons, LDL, HDL]
    C --> C4[Sphingolipids - Sphingomyelin]

    D --> D1[Fatty Acids - Saturated and Unsaturated]
    D --> D2[Sterols - Cholesterol, Ergosterol]
    D --> D3[Fat-soluble Vitamins - A, D, E, K]
    D --> D4[Hydrocarbons - Squalene]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333

```

---

## 4.2 Triglyceride Molecular Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Glycerol Backbone C3H5] --> E1[Ester Bond 1]
    G --> E2[Ester Bond 2]
    G --> E3[Ester Bond 3]

    E1 --> F1[Fatty Acid R1 - e.g. Palmitic]
    E2 --> F2[Fatty Acid R2 - e.g. Oleic]
    E3 --> F3[Fatty Acid R3 - e.g. Linoleic]

    F1 --> P1[Hydrogen Tail Saturated]
    F2 --> P2[One Double Bond - MUFA]
    F3 --> P3[Two Double Bonds - PUFA]

    style G fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style E2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style E3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333

    style F3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.3 Saturated vs. Unsaturated Fatty Acid Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fatty Acid] --> B[Saturated]
    A --> C[Unsaturated]

    B --> B1[Straight Chain]
    B --> B2[Tightly Packed]
    B --> B3[SOLID at Room Temp]
    B --> B4[Examples - Palmitic, Stearic]

    C --> C1[Mono-unsaturated - 1 Double Bond]
    C --> C2[Poly-unsaturated - 2 or More Double Bonds]

    C1 --> C1a[One Kink in Chain]
    C1 --> C1b[Liquid Oil e.g. Oleic Acid]

    C2 --> C2a[Multiple Kinks]
    C2 --> C2b[Highly Fluid - Linoleic Linolenic]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333
    style B3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333

    style C2b fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram – Role of Lipids in Food Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Lipids Functions] --> B[Energy Source]
    A --> C[Structural Role]
    A --> D[Flavor Carrier]
    A --> E[Texture Modifier]
    A --> F[Heat Transfer Medium]
    A --> G[Vitamin Carrier]

    B --> B1[9 kcal per gram - Most Concentrated]
    C --> C1[Cell membrane phospholipid bilayer]
    D --> D1[Solvent for aroma compounds]
    E --> E1[Shortening in bakery, creaminess in dairy]
    F --> F1[Deep frying medium 180 to 200 degrees C]
    G --> G1[Carrier of fat-soluble vitamins A D E K]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define lipids. List the three main classes of lipids with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Lipids are a group of naturally occurring organic molecules that are insoluble in water but soluble in non-polar organic solvents such as ether, chloroform, and benzene. They serve as a concentrated source of energy (9 kcal/g) and play vital structural and functional roles in foods.

The three main classes are:
1. **Simple Lipids** – Esters of fatty acids with alcohols. *Example: Triglycerides (fats and oils).*
2. **Compound Lipids** – Lipids with additional groups like phosphate, sugar, or protein. *Example: Phospholipids (lecithin).*
3. **Derived Lipids** – Hydrolytic products of simple/compound lipids. *Example: Cholesterol, fatty acids.*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**What are essential fatty acids? Name two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Essential fatty acids (EFAs) are fatty acids that cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be supplied through the diet. They are required for normal growth, cell membrane integrity, and hormone synthesis.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Linoleic acid (LA)** – 18:2 ω-6, found in sunflower, corn, and safflower oils.
2. **α-Linolenic acid (ALA)** – 18:3 ω-3, found in flaxseed, walnut, and fish oils.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**a)** Classify lipids in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Lipids are broadly classified into three categories:

**(i) Simple Lipids:**
These are esters of fatty acids with various alcohols. They include:
- **Fats and Oils:** Triglycerides (triacylglycerols) — glycerol esterified with three fatty acids. Fats are solid at room temperature (e.g., butter, lard), oils are liquid (e.g., groundnut oil).
- **Waxes:** Esters of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain monohydric alcohols. *Example:* Beeswax, carnauba wax, spermaceti.

**(ii) Compound (Conjugated) Lipids:**
These contain additional chemical groups besides fatty acids and alcohols:
- **Phospholipids:** Contain a phosphate group. *Examples:* Lecithin (in egg yolk), cephalin.
- **Glycolipids:** Contain a carbohydrate moiety. *Examples:* Cerebrosides, gangliosides.
- **Lipoproteins:** Lipids complexed with proteins for transport in blood. *Examples:* LDL, HDL, chylomicrons.

**(iii) Derived Lipids:**
Hydrolytic products of simple and compound lipids, including:
- **Fatty Acids:** Palmitic, oleic, stearic.
- **Sterols:** Cholesterol, ergosterol.
- **Fat-soluble Vitamins:** A, D, E, K.
- **Hydrocarbons:** Squalene.

---

**b)** Explain the structure of a triglyceride with a neat diagram. Discuss the formation reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **triglyceride (triacylglycerol)** is the most abundant lipid in nature, formed by the esterification of one molecule of glycerol (trihydric alcohol) with three fatty acid molecules.

**Structural Representation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{CH}_2-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\text{R}_1 \\
& \mid \\
& \text{CH}-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\text{R}_2 \\
& \mid \\
& \text{CH}_2-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\text{R}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ represent the hydrocarbon tails of three fatty acids, which may be **identical (simple TAG)** or **different (mixed TAG)**.

**Formation Reaction (Esterification):**

$$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3 \, \text{RCOOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

For each ester bond formed, one molecule of water is eliminated. Three ester bonds form three water molecules. The reaction is **reversible** and is catalyzed by enzymes (lipases) in biological systems or by acid/alkali catalysts industrially.

**Significance:** Triglycerides serve as the body's primary energy reserve (stored in adipose tissue) and constitute over 95% of dietary lipids.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`

**a)** Differentiate between saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fatty acids. Give examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Saturated (SFA) | Monounsaturated (MUFA) | Polyunsaturated (PUFA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Double bonds | 0 | 1 | ≥ 2 |
| State at 25 °C | Solid | Liquid | Liquid |
| Source | Animal fats, coconut | Olive, groundnut | Sunflower, fish oil |
| Cholesterol effect | Raises LDL | Lowers LDL | Lowers LDL & raises HDL |
| Examples | Palmitic (16:0), Stearic (18:0) | Oleic (18:1) | Linoleic (18:2), Linolenic (18:3) |
| Hydrogenation need | None | Partial | Extensive for margarine |

**Saturated Fatty Acids:** Have no double bonds in their hydrocarbon chain. They are tightly packed, giving a solid texture at room temperature. Common in animal fats and tropical oils. Excess intake raises serum LDL cholesterol.

**Monounsaturated Fatty Acids:** Contain one cis-double bond, creating a "kink" that prevents tight packing. Liquid at room temperature. *Example:* Oleic acid, dominant in olive oil (60–80%).

**Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids:** Contain two or more double bonds, highly fluid. Include essential fatty acids linoleic (ω-6) and linolenic (ω-3). Highly susceptible to oxidative rancidity.

---

**b)** Define saponification value. Calculate the saponification value of 2 g of oil that requires 28 mL of 0.5 N KOH. Blank titre = 42 mL. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Saponification value (SV) is defined as the number of milligrams of potassium hydroxide (KOH) required to completely saponify 1 gram of fat/oil. It is inversely proportional to the average molecular weight of the fatty acids.

**Formula:**
$$ SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W} $$

**Given:** $V_b = 42 \, \text{mL}$, $V_s = 28 \, \text{mL}$, $N = 0.5 \, \text{N}$, $W = 2 \, \text{g}$

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= \dfrac{(42 - 28) \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{14 \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{392.7}{2} \\
&= 196.35 \, \text{mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** SV = **196.35 mg KOH/g** ✅

**Interpretation:** This moderate-to-high SV suggests the oil contains a mixture of medium and long-chain fatty acids, typical of groundnut or coconut oil blends.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert normality or using grams instead of mg (56.1 is MW of KOH in **g/mol**, so 1000 is already absorbed — do not multiply twice).
> 2. **Blank vs Sample Confusion:** Some students reverse $(V_b - V_s)$ — remember blank is ALWAYS larger because sample consumes $Na_2S_2O_3$ or KOH.
> 3. **Misclassifying Waxes:** Waxes are simple lipids (not compound), since they are just esters of fatty acids with alcohols — no phosphate, sugar, or protein group.
> 4. **Cis-Trans Trap:** Only **cis-unsaturated** fatty acids are natural; **trans fats** are mostly artificial (formed during partial hydrogenation) and are linked to cardiovascular disease.
> 5. **Forgetting "Mixed" Triglycerides:** Always mention that *natural* fats are mostly **mixed triglycerides** — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 6. **Confusing ω-3 with ω-6 numbering:** The ω (omega) number counts from the methyl (CH₃) end, not the carboxyl end.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Revise Before Exam**

- ✅ Lipids = water-insoluble, organic-solvent-soluble; energy = **9 kcal/g**
- ✅ Three classes: **Simple (TAGs, waxes), Compound (phospholipids, lipoproteins), Derived (FA, sterols)**
- ✅ **Triglyceride = glycerol + 3 fatty acids**, joined by ester bonds, releases 3 H₂O
- ✅ **Saturated FA** = no double bonds → solid fat (palmitic, stearic)
- ✅ **MUFA** = one double bond (oleic acid)
- ✅ **PUFA** = multiple double bonds (linoleic, linolenic)
- ✅ **Essential FA** = Linoleic (ω-6) and Linolenic (ω-3); body cannot synthesize
- ✅ **Saponification Value** = mg KOH/g fat; high SV → short-chain FA (coconut)
- ✅ **Iodine Value** = g I₂/100g fat; high IV → high unsaturation (linseed)
- ✅ **Acid Value** indicates hydrolytic rancidity; **Peroxide Value** indicates oxidative rancidity
- ✅ **Hydrogenation** = converts unsaturated oils → solid fats (margarine)
- ✅ **Rancidity** types: hydrolytic (water/enzyme action) and oxidative (O₂, light, heat)
- ✅ **Phospholipids** (lecithin) are natural emulsifiers → stabilize oil-water mixtures
- ✅ **Waxes** have long-chain alcohol (not glycerol) + long-chain FA → water-proofing function
- ✅ **Cis-isomers** have kinks (liquid); **trans-isomers** are straight (solid, harmful)
- ✅ **Atwater Factor** for lipids = 9.0 kcal/g — frequently quoted in KTU nutrition questions

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 – Lipids Notes.** Continue to revise key formulas and practice numerical problems on Saponification & Iodine Values before attempting Part B questions. KTU examiners expect unit-perfect answers with clear labeling of reactions and diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vitamins and minerals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vitamins and minerals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Vitamins?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Vitamins are **organic micronutrients** that are required in very small quantities (micrograms to milligrams) for the normal growth, maintenance, metabolism, and physiological functioning of the human body, but which the body cannot synthesize in adequate amounts. They must, therefore, be supplied through the diet.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the human body as a high-performance car engine. **Carbohydrates, proteins, and fats** are the *fuel* (macronutrients — needed in kilograms). **Vitamins and minerals** are like the *spark plugs, engine oil, and coolant* — you need only tiny amounts, but without them, the engine either sputters, corrodes, or simply stops. A car can have a full tank of petrol, but without a spark plug, it won't start. Similarly, without vitamins, the body's metabolic "combustion" fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Vitamins** = **Organic** compounds (contain Carbon, C)
> - **Minerals** = **Inorganic** elements (no Carbon, derived from earth/soil)

### 1.2 What are Minerals?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Minerals are **inorganic, homogeneous chemical elements** that originate from soil and water, are absorbed by plants (or animals eating plants), and play essential structural and regulatory roles in the human body. They are classified into **macro-minerals (major minerals)** and **micro-minerals (trace elements)** based on the daily requirement exceeding or being less than **100 mg/day**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If vitamins are the spark plugs, then minerals are the *nuts, bolts, and frame* of the engine. **Calcium** builds the chassis (bones and teeth), **Iron** carries the fuel (oxygen in blood), and **Sodium and Potassium** are the electrical wiring (nerve impulses). Without these, the structure collapses even if the fuel is perfect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Textbooks:**
> - **RDA (Recommended Dietary Allowance):** The average daily dietary intake level sufficient to meet the nutrient requirement of nearly all (97–98%) healthy individuals in a particular life stage and gender group.
> - Units: **mg/day** or **µg/day** (micrograms/day).

### 1.3 Classification of Vitamins (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Broad Categories (Always Asked in Part A):**
>
> | Property | Fat-Soluble Vitamins | Water-Soluble Vitamins |
> |---|---|---|
> | Solubility | Soluble in **fats & oils** | Soluble in **water** |
> | Members | A, D, E, K | B-Complex, C |
> | Storage | Stored in **liver & adipose tissue** | **Not stored** (excess excreted in urine) |
> | Deficiency | Takes months to develop | Develops **quickly** (weeks) |
> | Toxicity risk | Higher (hypervitaminosis) | Lower (excess is excreted) |
> | Daily intake | **Essential every day** | **Essential regularly** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fat-Soluble Vitamins — Deep Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Vitamin A (Retinol)
- **Chemical Nature:** Retinoids (retinol, retinal, retinoic acid); the provitamin form is **β-carotene** (a carotenoid pigment).
- **Why fat-soluble?** It has a long hydrocarbon chain and a polar hydroxyl group — it partitions into the lipid phase.
- **Functions (3 critical roles):**
  1. **Vision:** Retinal combines with opsin → **rhodopsin** (in rod cells) for low-light (scotopic) vision.
  2. **Epithelial cell differentiation & mucous secretion** (keeps skin and inner linings healthy).
  3. **Growth, immunity, and reproduction.**
- **Deficiency Manifestations:**
  - **Night blindness** (nyctalopia) — earliest sign.
  - **Xerophthalmia** (dry eyes) → **keratomalacia** (corneal softening → blindness).
  - **Follicular hyperkeratosis** (gooseflesh/bumpy skin).
- **Rich Food Sources:** Liver, fish liver oils, egg yolk, dairy; **provitamin A** from carrots, pumpkin, mango, papaya, spinach, sweet potato.
- **Stability in Food Processing:** **Highly sensitive to oxidation** (unsaturated), destroyed by light, heat, and oxygen. Encapsulation and antioxidant addition are used in food industry.

#### 2.1.2 Vitamin D (Calciferol)
- **Chemical Forms:** **D₂ (Ergocalciferol)** from plants/yeast; **D₃ (Cholecalciferol)** from animal sources and synthesized in skin.
- **The "Sunshine Vitamin":** 7-dehydrocholesterol in skin + **UV-B sunlight (290–315 nm)** → cholecalciferol → hydroxylated in liver (25-OH-D) and kidney (1,25-(OH)₂-D) to active form **calcitriol**.
- **Function:** Increases **intestinal absorption of Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻**; regulates bone mineralization.
- **Deficiency:**
  - **Rickets** (children) — bowed legs, soft skull.
  - **Osteomalacia** (adults) — soft, demineralized bones.
- **Sources:** Fish liver oils, fortified milk, egg yolk; sunlight exposure.

#### 2.1.3 Vitamin E (Tocopherols)
- **Chemical Nature:** Group of **tocopherols (α, β, γ, δ)**; α-tocopherol is the most biologically active.
- **Function:** **Most important natural lipid-soluble antioxidant** — protects cell membranes from oxidative damage by scavenging free radicals (peroxyl radicals). Specifically protects PUFAs (polyunsaturated fatty acids) in membranes.
- **Deficiency:** Rare in humans; causes hemolytic anemia (RBC membrane fragility) and neurological issues.
- **Sources:** Wheat germ oil, sunflower oil, almonds, peanuts, sunflower seeds, spinach.
- **Industrial Significance:** Added to oils and fat-based foods as a **natural antioxidant** to prevent rancidity.

#### 2.1.4 Vitamin K (Phylloquinone)
- **Forms:** **K₁ (Phylloquinone)** — plants; **K₂ (Menaquinone)** — bacterial synthesis in gut; **K₃ (Menadione)** — synthetic.
- **Function:** **Co-factor for γ-carboxylation of glutamic acid residues** in clotting factors **II, VII, IX, X** and proteins C, S. Essential for **blood coagulation**.
- **Deficiency:** **Prolonged clotting time**, hemorrhage; seen in newborns (gut sterile) and fat malabsorption.
- **Sources:** Green leafy vegetables (spinach, cabbage, kale), broccoli; gut microflora.

### 2.2 Water-Soluble Vitamins — Deep Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)
- **Chemical Form:** **L-ascorbic acid** (active form); the L-dehydroascorbic acid is also active.
- **Key Reactions and Functions:**
  1. **Antioxidant** — donates electrons; protects against oxidative stress.
  2. **Collagen synthesis** — cofactor for **prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase** (hydroxylation of proline/lysine residues in collagen) — vital for connective tissue, wound healing, blood vessel integrity.
  3. **Iron absorption** — reduces Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ (the absorbable form) in the gut.
  4. **Carnitine synthesis, neurotransmitter synthesis, immune function.**
- **Deficiency:** **Scurvy** — bleeding gums, subcutaneous hemorrhages, poor wound healing, loose teeth, perifollicular hemorrhages.
- **Sources:** Citrus fruits (lemon, orange), guava, amla (Indian gooseberry — *richest natural source*), kiwi, strawberry, tomato, green chili, sprouted grains.
- **Stability:** **Most labile vitamin** in food processing. Destroyed by **heat, oxygen, light, alkaline pH, and copper/iron catalysts**. Leaches into cooking water (water-soluble).

#### 2.2.2 Vitamin B-Complex (8 vitamins — KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick: "The B-Team" — T.Ri.C.N.P.B.F.P**
> **B₁ Thiamine, B₂ Riboflavin, B₃ Niacin, B₅ Pantothenic acid, B₆ Pyridoxine, B₇ Biotin, B₉ Folic acid, B₁₂ Cyanocobalamin.**

| Vitamin | Chemical Name | Active Coenzyme Form | Key Function | Deficiency Disease | Rich Sources |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B₁** | Thiamine | **TPP** (Thiamine Pyrophosphate) | Carbohydrate metabolism (decarboxylation of α-keto acids) | **Beriberi** (wet/dry) | Whole grains, pork, legumes |
| **B₂** | Riboflavin | **FAD, FMN** | Redox reactions (electron transport) | **Ariboflavinosis** (cheilosis, angular stomatitis) | Milk, eggs, liver, green leafy veg. |
| **B₃** | Niacin (Nicotinic acid) | **NAD⁺, NADP⁺** | Dehydrogenation reactions, energy metabolism | **Pellagra** (4 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia, Death) | Meat, fish, whole grains, legumes |
| **B₅** | Pantothenic Acid | **Coenzyme A (CoA)** | Acyl group transfer, TCA cycle | Burning feet syndrome | Egg yolk, liver, whole grains, mushrooms |
| **B₆** | Pyridoxine | **PLP (Pyridoxal Phosphate)** | Transamination, decarboxylation of amino acids | Microcytic anemia, convulsions, dermatitis | Meat, fish, banana, chickpeas |
| **B₇** | Biotin (Vit. H) | **Biocytin** | Carboxylation reactions (CO₂ fixation) | Dermatitis, alopecia (hair loss) | Egg yolk, liver, soybeans, gut bacteria |
| **B₉** | Folic Acid | **THF (Tetrahydrofolate)** | One-carbon transfer (purine, thymidylate synthesis) | **Megaloblastic anemia**, neural tube defects in fetus | Green leafy vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals |
| **B₁₂** | Cyanocobalamin | **Methylcobalamin, Deoxyadenosylcobalamin** | Methionine synthesis, odd-chain FA metabolism | **Pernicious anemia** (megaloblastic + neurological) | **Animal products only**: liver, meat, fish, dairy |

### 2.3 Classification of Minerals

#### 2.3.1 Macro-Minerals (Required > 100 mg/day)

| Mineral | Major Function | Deficiency | Best Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calcium (Ca)** | Bone & teeth formation, blood clotting, muscle contraction, nerve signaling | Osteoporosis, rickets | Milk, cheese, small fish with bones, dark green vegetables |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | Bone, ATP, nucleic acids, phospholipids | Muscle weakness, rickets | Milk, meat, whole grains, legumes |
| **Potassium (K)** | Intracellular fluid balance, nerve transmission, muscle contraction | Hypokalemia, muscle cramps, cardiac arrhythmia | Banana, potato, oranges, dried fruits |
| **Sodium (Na)** | Extracellular fluid balance, nerve transmission | Hyponatremia (muscle cramps) | Table salt, processed foods |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | Cofactor for 300+ enzymes, ATP stabilization | Muscle spasm, tremor, cardiac arrhythmia | Nuts, whole grains, leafy veg, chocolate |
| **Sulfur (S)** | Amino acids (Cys, Met), thiamine, biotin | Rare; related to protein deficiency | Eggs, meat, onions, garlic |
| **Chlorine (Cl)** | HCl in stomach, fluid balance | Metabolic alkalosis | Table salt |

#### 2.3.2 Trace Minerals (Required < 100 mg/day)

| Mineral | Major Function | Deficiency | Best Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iron (Fe)** | Component of **hemoglobin** (O₂ transport) and myoglobin; cytochrome enzymes | **Iron-deficiency anemia** (microcytic, hypochromic) | Liver, red meat, jaggery, spinach, dates, legumes |
| **Iodine (I)** | Synthesis of **thyroid hormones** (T3, T4) | **Goiter**, cretinism (children) | Iodized salt, seafood, seaweeds |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | 100+ enzymes, wound healing, immunity, taste/smell | Growth retardation, hypogonadism, dermatitis | Oysters, red meat, whole grains, legumes |
| **Copper (Cu)** | Cytochrome oxidase, Fe metabolism, connective tissue | Anemia, Menkes disease (kinky hair) | Liver, shellfish, nuts, seeds |
| **Selenium (Se)** | Antioxidant (part of **glutathione peroxidase**) | Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy) | Brazil nuts, seafood, organ meats |
| **Fluorine (F)** | Tooth enamel, bone strength | Dental caries | Fluoridated water, tea, fish |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas and Conversions for KTU Board Exams**

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Recommended Daily Allowance of a nutrient | $\text{RDA} = \text{EAR} + 2 \times \text{SD}_{\text{EAR}}$ | EAR = Estimated Average Requirement; assumes normal distribution |
| Vitamin A conversion (Carotene → Retinol) | $1 \;\text{µg RE} = 6 \;\text{µg β-carotene} = 12 \;\text{µg other provitamin carotenoids}$ | RE = Retinol Equivalent |
| Vitamin D conversion | $1 \;\text{µg cholecalciferol} = 40 \;\text{IU}$ | IU = International Units |
| Vitamin E conversion | $1 \;\text{mg α-tocopherol} = 1.49 \;\text{IU}$ | Natural vs. synthetic differ by ~1.36 IU/mg |
| Niacin conversion from Tryptophan | $60 \;\text{mg Trp} \rightarrow 1 \;\text{mg Niacin}$ | Conversion is inefficient (~1/60) |
| Energy yield for B-vitamin need | Thiamine ≈ **0.5 mg / 1000 kcal**; Riboflavin ≈ **0.6 mg / 1000 kcal**; Niacin ≈ **6.6 mg / 1000 kcal** | Used in meal planning |
| Iron absorption enhancement | Fe³⁺ $\xrightarrow{\text{Vitamin C / reducing agents}}$ Fe²⁺ (absorbable) | Vitamin C increases non-heme Fe uptake by 3–4× |
| Calcium–Phosphorus ratio (bone health) | Optimal dietary Ca : P = **1 : 1 to 1 : 2** | Imbalance impairs Ca absorption |
| Iodine requirement (adults) | **150 µg/day** (KTU standard) | Pregnant women: 250 µg/day |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Technology

1. **Fortification of Foods:** Addition of Fe + Folic acid to wheat flour, Vitamin A to Vanaspati/margarine, I₂ to salt, Vitamin D to milk.
2. **Stability Engineering:** Vitamin C is encapsulated (lipid-coated) in commercial fruit drinks to prevent oxidation.
3. **Shelf-life decisions:** High-fat foods require added Vitamin E (tocopherol) as an antioxidant; iron-fortified cereals use **NaFeEDTA** to mask iron's metallic taste.
4. **Quality Control:** HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography) is used to quantify vitamins in food products — a KTU viva question.
5. **Nutritional Labeling:** Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (**FSSAI**) mandates RDA-based nutrient labeling on packaged foods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Vitamin Loss During Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of "Retained Vitamin" Percentage in a Processed Food**

The retention of a heat/labile vitamin in food during processing is mathematically given by:

$$
\text{Vitamin Retention (\%)} = \frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{initial}}$ = vitamin content in raw food (mg/100g or µg/100g)
- $V_{\text{final}}$ = vitamin content in processed food (mg/100g or µg/100g)

For a heat-labile vitamin following first-order kinetics of destruction:

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot V
$$

**Step 1: Integrate the first-order rate equation**

$$
\int_{V_0}^{V} \frac{dV}{V} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 2: Apply integration limits**

$$
\ln(V) - \ln(V_0) = -k \cdot t
$$

**Step 3: Solve for V**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{V}{V_0}\right) = -k \cdot t
$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate to obtain the retention equation**

$$
V = V_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5: Express as a percentage of retention**

$$
\text{Retention (\%)} = e^{-k \cdot t} \times 100
$$

Where $k$ = destruction rate constant (min⁻¹) and $t$ = processing time (min).

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic in plain text:** The greater the processing time ($t$) and the higher the temperature (which increases $k$), the lower the vitamin retention. This is why quick blanching and minimal heat exposure are key strategies in food technology.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A sample of fresh orange juice contains **50 mg/100 mL** of vitamin C. After pasteurization at 80 °C for 15 minutes, the vitamin C content drops to **30 mg/100 mL**. Calculate the **% retention** of vitamin C and the **rate constant $k$** at this temperature.

**Step 1: Write down the given data**
- $V_0 = 50$ mg/100 mL
- $V = 30$ mg/100 mL
- $t = 15$ min

**Step 2: Apply the retention formula**

$$
\text{Retention (\%)} = \frac{V}{V_0} \times 100 = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

So, **40% of vitamin C was destroyed.**

**Step 3: Use the integrated first-order equation to find $k$**

From $\ln(V/V_0) = -k \cdot t$:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{30}{50}\right) = -k \times 15
$$

**Step 4: Compute the log value**

$$
\ln(0.6) = -0.5108
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $k$**

$$
-0.5108 = -k \times 15
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.5108}{15} = 0.0341 \;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Vitamin C Retention = 60%**
- **Rate constant $k = 0.0341 \;\text{min}^{-1}$**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mineral Bioavailability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Bioavailability is the fraction of an ingested mineral that is absorbed and available for physiological functions.

$$
\text{Bioavailability (\%)} = \frac{\text{Amount absorbed}}{\text{Amount ingested}} \times 100
$$

**Three-Class Bioavailability Model (Used by FAO/WHO):**

| Class | Bioavailability | Common Diet Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | **5–8 %** | High in phytates, oxalates, polyphenols (e.g., vegetarian, cereal-based diet) |
| **Medium** | **10–12 %** | Mixed diet (cereals + vegetables + some animal protein) |
| **High** | **14–18 %** | Western diet rich in meat, fish, vitamin C |

**Example Calculation (Iron Bioavailability):**

A vegetarian woman consumes **15 mg of iron/day** from a phytate-rich cereal diet. If bioavailability is **6%**, how much iron is actually absorbed?

$$
\text{Absorbed Fe} = 15 \;\text{mg} \times 0.06 = 0.9 \;\text{mg/day}
$$

This is well below the RDA of **18 mg/day** for adult women, explaining the high prevalence of iron-deficiency anemia in vegetarian populations.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Enhancers and Inhibitors of Mineral Absorption

| Mineral | **Enhancers** (↑ absorption) | **Inhibitors** (↓ absorption) |
|---|---|---|
| **Iron (non-heme)** | Vitamin C, meat/fish factor (MFP factor), organic acids (citric acid) | **Phytates** (in bran, whole grains), **polyphenols** (tea, coffee), **oxalates** (spinach), Ca²⁺ in high doses |
| **Calcium** | Vitamin D, lactose, lysine, acidic pH in gut | **Oxalates** (rhubarb, spinach), **phytates**, excess fiber, high phosphorus |
| **Zinc** | Animal protein, citric acid, EDTA | Phytates, high calcium, copper (antagonistic) |
| **Iodine** | Selenium | Goitrogens (cabbage, cauliflower — raw) |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Vitamin A Calculation in Food Labeling

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A serving of carrot soup contains **3 mg of β-carotene**. How much Retinol Equivalent (RE) does this provide? Given: $1 \;\text{µg RE} = 6 \;\text{µg β-carotene}$.

**Step 1:** Convert mg → µg

$$
3 \;\text{mg} = 3000 \;\text{µg β-carotene}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply conversion factor

$$
\text{RE} = \frac{3000 \;\text{µg}}{6 \;\text{µg/µg RE}} = 500 \;\text{µg RE}
$$

**Final Answer: 500 µg RE of vitamin A per serving.** This is ≈ **62.5% of the adult RDA of 800 µg RE**, making the product a good source.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify vitamins. Differentiate between fat-soluble and water-soluble vitamins with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vitamins are organic micronutrients classified into two categories:

**1. Fat-Soluble Vitamins:**
- Soluble in fats and oils, insoluble in water.
- Stored in liver and adipose tissue; toxicity risk is high.
- Members: **A, D, E, K**.
- Deficiency develops slowly.

**2. Water-Soluble Vitamins:**
- Soluble in water, insoluble in organic solvents.
- Not stored in the body; excess is excreted in urine.
- Members: **Vitamin C and Vitamin B-complex (B₁, B₂, B₃, B₅, B₆, B₇, B₉, B₁₂)**.
- Deficiency develops rapidly.

> Differentiating clearly with examples and the storage point fetches all 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Name any three fat-soluble vitamins and state their functions. List one rich food source for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Vitamin | Function | Rich Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Vitamin A** | Vision (rhodopsin), epithelial cell differentiation, immunity | Carrot, liver, fish liver oil |
| **Vitamin D** | Calcium and phosphorus absorption, bone mineralization | Sunlight, fortified milk, fish liver oil |
| **Vitamin E** | Antioxidant, protects cell membrane lipids from oxidation | Wheat germ oil, sunflower seeds, almonds |
| **Vitamin K** | Blood coagulation (cofactor for clotting factors II, VII, IX, X) | Green leafy vegetables, broccoli |

*(Student needs to mention any 3 with function + source for full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Module-end ESE Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice between Question A and Question B** within the same module. Each 14-mark question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** *Explain in detail the chemistry, functions, and deficiency manifestations of Vitamin A. Add a note on the role of β-carotene as a provitamin.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Chemistry of Vitamin A (2 marks):**
- Vitamin A belongs to the **retinoid** family. The active forms are **retinol, retinal, and retinoic acid**.
- Retinol has a **β-ionone ring** attached to a conjugated side chain with four double bonds and a terminal hydroxyl group.
- **β-carotene** (a carotenoid, C₄₀H₅₆) is the provitamin form. It is cleaved in the intestinal mucosa by **β-carotene oxygenase (15,15′-dioxygenase)** to yield two molecules of retinal. Retinal is then reduced to retinol by retinaldehyde reductase in the intestine.

**2. Functions of Vitamin A (2.5 marks):**
- **Vision:** Retinal combines with opsin (a protein in rod cells of the retina) to form **rhodopsin** (visual purple). Rhodopsin absorbs light in the rod cells, triggering a nerve impulse. This is essential for **scotopic (dim-light) vision**.
- **Epithelial cell differentiation:** Retinoic acid regulates gene transcription via **RAR (Retinoic Acid Receptors)** and **RXR**, promoting mucous secretion and maintaining the integrity of skin and mucosal linings.
- **Growth, immunity, and reproduction:** Required for proper embryonic development, spermatogenesis, and immune cell function.

**3. Deficiency Manifestations (2 marks):**
- **Night blindness (Nyctalopia):** Earliest sign — due to failure of rhodopsin regeneration.
- **Xerophthalmia:** Dryness of the conjunctiva and cornea.
- **Keratomalacia:** Softening of the cornea, leading to permanent blindness.
- **Follicular hyperkeratosis:** Bumpy "toad-skin" appearance due to keratinization of hair follicles.
- Increased susceptibility to infections (impaired immunity).

**4. Role of β-carotene (0.5 mark):**
- β-carotene is the provitamin A precursor found in **yellow, orange, and dark green plant foods** (carrot, pumpkin, mango, spinach).
- The conversion efficiency: $1 \;\text{µg RE} = 6 \;\text{µg β-carotene}$ — meaning 6 µg of β-carotene is needed to produce 1 µg of retinol.
- β-carotene also functions as an **antioxidant** by quenching singlet oxygen.

> **Summary Table for Viva:**
> | Property | β-carotene | Retinol |
> |---|---|---|
> | Source | Plant pigments | Animal tissues |
> | Activity | Provitamin (1/6th) | Active form |
> | Stability | Stable | Sensitive to O₂, light |

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** *Discuss the classification of minerals with suitable examples. Explain the bioavailability of iron and the factors that enhance or inhibit its absorption.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Classification of Minerals (3 marks):**

Minerals are inorganic elements required by the body. They are classified based on the **daily requirement** and **total body content** into two main groups:

| Type | Daily Requirement | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Macro / Major minerals** | > 100 mg/day | Ca, P, K, Na, Mg, S, Cl |
| **Micro / Trace minerals** | < 100 mg/day | Fe, Zn, I, Cu, Se, Mn, F, Cr, Mo, Co |

**2. Bioavailability of Iron (2 marks):**

Iron exists in two dietary forms:
- **Heme iron:** from animal sources (haemoglobin/myoglobin) — absorbed efficiently (~**15–35%**).
- **Non-heme iron:** from plant sources (ferric Fe³⁺) — poorly absorbed (~**2–20%** depending on meal composition).

Bioavailability is calculated as:

$$
\text{Bioavailability (\%)} = \frac{\text{Absorbed Fe}}{\text{Ingested Fe}} \times 100
$$

In a typical **vegetarian Indian diet** rich in phytates, the bioavailability of non-heme iron is only **5–8%**, leading to widespread iron-deficiency anemia.

**3. Enhancers and Inhibitors of Iron Absorption (2 marks):**

| **Enhancers (↑)** | **Inhibitors (↓)** |
|---|---|
| **Vitamin C** (reduces Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺) | **Phytates** (in bran, whole grains) |
| **Meat–Fish–Poultry (MFP) factor** | **Polyphenols** (tannins in tea, coffee) |
| Organic acids (citric, lactic acid) | **Oxalates** (in spinach, beet greens) |
| Gastric HCl (acidic pH) | High dose of calcium, zinc (competition) |
| | Phosphoproteins in egg yolk (phosvitin) |

**Industrial Application (0.5 mark):** Many food industries fortify **wheat flour with NaFeEDTA** or **elemental iron** (a low-cost, non-reactive form) to combat anemia — directly relevant to the FSSAI food fortification program in India.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** *Describe the chemistry, functions, and deficiency of Vitamin C. Why is Vitamin C considered the most labile vitamin in food processing?*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Chemistry (1.5 marks):**
- Vitamin C is **L-ascorbic acid** (C₆H₈O₆), a six-carbon α-ketolactone with an enediol group.
- The **enediol group (C2–C3 with –OH groups)** is responsible for its acidity and reducing property.
- The oxidized form **L-dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA)** is also biologically active; further oxidation to **diketogulonic acid** destroys activity.

**2. Functions (3 marks):**
- **Collagen synthesis:** Cofactor for **prolyl hydroxylase** and **lysyl hydroxylase**, which hydroxylate proline and lysine in collagen — required for connective tissue, wound healing, and blood vessel integrity.
- **Antioxidant:** Scavenges free radicals; regenerates Vitamin E from its radical form.
- **Iron absorption:** Reduces Fe³⁺ (ferric) to Fe²⁺ (ferrous) — the only absorbable form in the duodenum.
- **Carnitine and neurotransmitter synthesis:** Cofactor for dopamine β-hydroxylase (converts dopamine → norepinephrine).
- **Immune function:** Enhances neutrophil and lymphocyte activity.

**3. Deficiency — Scurvy (1.5 marks):**
- **Perifollicular hemorrhages**, **bleeding gums**, **subcutaneous bleeding**, **impaired wound healing**, **loose teeth**, **bone pain**, **anemia** (due to poor iron absorption).
- **Infantile scurvy (Barlow's disease):** seen in babies fed boiled cow's milk (heat destroys Vit C).

**4. Why Vitamin C is the Most Labile Vitamin (1 mark):**
Vitamin C is destroyed by:
- **Heat** (thermal degradation above 70 °C)
- **Oxygen** (oxidation to DHAA → diketogulonic acid)
- **Light** (UV photodegradation)
- **Alkaline pH** (stable at pH 4–5; unstable above pH 7)
- **Catalysts:** Cu²⁺, Fe²⁺ accelerate oxidation.
- **Water:** Leaches into cooking water (water-soluble losses 50–80%).

**Industrial Mitigation:** Encapsulation (lipid-walled), addition of chelators (EDTA), and **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)** with low O₂.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** *What is a deficiency disease? List the major vitamin deficiency diseases and their causative vitamins. Mention the role of fortification in preventing these deficiencies.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **deficiency disease** is a clinical condition caused by an inadequate intake, absorption, or utilization of one or more essential nutrients over a prolonged period.

**2. Major Vitamin Deficiency Diseases (3 marks):**

| Disease | Causative Vitamin | Key Symptoms |
|---|---|---|
| **Night blindness / Xerophthalmia** | Vitamin A | Poor vision in dim light, dry eyes |
| **Rickets** | Vitamin D | Bowed legs, soft bones (children) |
| **Osteomalacia** | Vitamin D | Soft, demineralized bones (adults) |
| **Scurvy** | Vitamin C | Bleeding gums, poor wound healing |
| **Beriberi** (wet/dry) | Vitamin B₁ (Thiamine) | Nerve damage, heart failure |
| **Pellagra** | Vitamin B₃ (Niacin) | 4 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia, Death |
| **Ariboflavinosis** | Vitamin B₂ (Riboflavin) | Cheilosis, angular stomatitis, glossitis |
| **Megaloblastic anemia** | B₉ (Folic acid) / B₁₂ (Cobalamin) | Large RBCs, fatigue |
| **Pernicious anemia** | Vitamin B₁₂ | Megaloblastic anemia + neurological signs |
| **Hemorrhagic disease (newborn)** | Vitamin K | Excessive bleeding |

**3. Role of Food Fortification (3 marks):**
Fortification is the deliberate addition of vitamins/minerals to a food at levels above its natural state, to prevent public health deficiencies.

**Examples of FSSAI-Mandated Fortification in India:**
- **Salt → Iodine** (Universal Salt Iodization, USI, 1983).
- **Wheat flour → Iron + Folic acid + Vitamin B₁₂** (used in Mid-Day Meal Scheme).
- **Vanaspati/Margarine → Vitamin A** (legally mandatory in India).
- **Milk → Vitamin D** (fortified milk, 400 IU/L).
- **Rice → Iron, Folic acid, Vitamin B₁₂** (fortified rice kernels, used in PDS).

**Public Health Impact:** A landmark example is the **"Golden Rice"** project — genetically modified rice containing **β-carotene** to combat vitamin A deficiency in Southeast Asia.

> **Examiner's Quick Note:** Writing real-time Indian examples (PDS, Mid-Day Meal, FSSAI) fetches higher marks than generic answers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "provitamin" with "vitamin".** β-carotene is a *provitamin* — write the conversion formula.
> 2. **Always mention the active coenzyme form** for B-vitamins (TPP, FAD, NAD⁺, CoA, PLP, THF) — students often skip this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Avoid generic answers** like "Calcium is good for bones." Be specific — *"Calcium forms hydroxyapatite [Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂] crystals in the bone matrix and is required for neurotransmitter release at synaptic terminals."*
> 4. **Do not mix up "Xerophthalmia" and "Xerosis"** — both are vitamin A related but different stages.
> 5. **Spell "Beriberi" correctly** — written as "Beri-Beri" or "BB" loses one mark.
> 6. **For numericals**, always show the unit and convert mg ↔ µg carefully. Off-by-1000 errors are common.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam (Vitamins and Minerals)**

- ☐ **Vitamins = organic, micronutrients; Minerals = inorganic elements.**
- ☐ **Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K):** stored in liver; toxicity possible; deficiency slow.
- ☐ **Water-soluble (B-complex + C):** not stored; excess in urine; deficiency fast.
- ☐ **Vitamin A:** Retinol (active), β-carotene (provitamin). Deficiency → night blindness, xerophthalmia, keratomalacia. Converted: $1 \;\text{µg RE} = 6 \;\text{µg β-carotene}$.
- ☐ **Vitamin D:** "Sunshine Vitamin"; Cholecalciferol (D₃). Deficiency → rickets (children) and osteomalacia (adults). $1 \;\text{µg} = 40 \;\text{IU}$.
- ☐ **Vitamin E:** α-tocopherol; antioxidant protecting PUFAs. Sources: wheat germ oil.
- ☐ **Vitamin K:** Phylloquinone; γ-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X. Deficiency → hemorrhage.
- ☐ **Vitamin C:** Ascorbic acid; cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase (collagen); reduces Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺. Deficiency → scurvy. **Most labile vitamin.**
- ☐ **B₁ (Thiamine):** TPP; coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase; deficiency → beriberi.
- ☐ **B₂ (Riboflavin):** FAD, FMN; electron transport; deficiency → ariboflavinosis.
- ☐ **B₃ (Niacin):** NAD⁺/NADP⁺; **4 D's of Pellagra** (Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia, Death). 60 mg Trp = 1 mg Niacin.
- ☐ **B₉ (Folic acid):** THF; one-carbon transfer; deficiency → neural tube defects, megaloblastic anemia.
- ☐ **B₁₂ (Cobalamin):** only from **animal sources**; intrinsic factor for absorption; deficiency → pernicious anemia.
- ☐ **Calcium (macro):** hydroxyapatite in bones; clotting; nerve conduction. Ca : P ratio = 1 : 1 to 1 : 2.
- ☐ **Iron (trace):** heme (animal) vs. non-heme (plant); Vit C enhances absorption; phytates inhibit.
- ☐ **Iodine (trace):** T3, T4 synthesis; deficiency → goiter, cretinism. **Iodized salt = 150 µg/day.**
- ☐ **Zinc (trace):** 100+ enzymes; growth, immunity, taste. Oysters are the richest source.
- ☐ **Selenium (trace):** part of **glutathione peroxidase**; antioxidant. Deficiency → Keshan disease.
- ☐ **Fluorine (trace):** tooth enamel; deficiency → dental caries.
- ☐ **Bioavailability formula:** $\text{Bioavailability (\%)} = \frac{\text{Absorbed}}{\text{Ingested}} \times 100$.
- ☐ **First-order vitamin destruction:** $V = V_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$.
- ☐ **FSSAI fortification examples:** Salt + I₂, Flour + Fe/Folic acid/B₁₂, Milk + Vit D, Oil + Vit A, Rice + Fe/FA/B₁₂.
- ☐ **Always write the active coenzyme form** for B-vitamins — this is the most-missed KTU point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook (KTU 2024 Hot Trick):**
> *"**A DEK**" — **A**lways **D**rink **E**nriched **K**ombucha — A, D, E, K = fat-soluble.
> *"**B-Team plays C-leverly in water"** — B-complex + C = water-soluble.

**END OF TOPIC NOTES — VITAMINS AND MINERALS (MODULE 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Their classification # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification of Foods** is the systematic arrangement of food materials into distinct groups or categories based on shared characteristics such as origin, composition, perishability, nutritional content, acidity (pH), and water activity ($a_w$). This systematic grouping is essential for applying uniform processing, preservation, packaging, storage, and regulatory standards across the food industry. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (GDFTT104 – Introduction to Food Technology), Module 1 introduces food classification as a foundational concept that enables engineers and technologists to: - Select appropriate **preservation techniques** (thermal, chemical, biological). - Determine **shelf-life** requirements. - Apply correct **food safety** and **quality assurance** protocols (FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius). - Design **packaging materials** and **cold chain logistics**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Hospital Triage System** > Imagine a hospital emergency room. Patients arriving are **classified** into categories: critical (red), urgent (yellow), and non-urgent (green). This classification is **not random** — it is based on shared characteristics (severity, vital signs). Once classified, each patient receives **tailored treatment** specific to their group. Similarly, in food technology, we classify foods so that each group receives the **right preservation method, packaging, and storage conditions**. For example, highly perishable foods (like fresh fish) are treated very differently from shelf-stable foods (like sugar or salt). Without classification, food technology would be chaotic and inefficient. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **pH Scale Range**: **0 to 14** (dimensionless logarithmic scale). - **Water Activity ($a_w$) Range**: **0 to 1.00** (dimensionless ratio). - **Standard Reference Temperature**: **25°C** (for most food analysis). - **FSSAI Definition of "Food"**: Any substance, whether processed, partially processed, or unprocessed, which is intended for human consumption (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006). ## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Food Technology > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme** > Classification forms the **basis for understanding**: > 1. Why milk needs pasteurization but sugar does not. > 2. Why meat is refrigerated but cereals are stored dry. > 3. Why canned tomatoes (acidic) require less severe heat treatment than canned vegetables (low-acid). > 4. How regulatory bodies frame food laws using categories. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Major Classification Systems for Foods Foods can be classified using **several overlapping systems**. A competent food technologist must understand all major systems because each one serves a different industrial or scientific purpose. ### 2.1.1 Classification by Origin (Source) This is the **most fundamental** classification system. | Category | Sub-types | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Plant Origin** | Cereals, Pulses, Fruits, Vegetables, Nuts, Oilseeds, Spices, Beverages | Rice, Wheat, Apple, Spinach, Almond, Mustard, Tea | | **Animal Origin** | Meat, Poultry, Fish, Dairy, Eggs | Chicken, Mutton, Rohu, Milk, Curd, Hen's egg | | **Microbial Origin** | Fermented foods, Mushrooms | Yoghurt, Cheese, Tempeh, Button mushroom | | **Mineral Origin** | Water, Salt | Drinking water, Table salt, Mineral water | | **Synthetic / Processed** | Artificial sweeteners, Food additives | Saccharin, Aspartame, Preservatives | ### 2.1.2 Classification by Perishability (Most Critical for Preservation) This classification **directly determines the preservation strategy**. > [!NOTE] > **Perishability** refers to the tendency of a food to undergo spoilage — chemical, microbiological, or physical deterioration — over time. | Class | Shelf-life at Ambient Temp | Characteristics | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Highly Perishable** | 1–7 days | High moisture, high $a_w$, neutral pH | Fresh fish, milk, meat, cooked foods | | **Semi-Perishable** | Weeks to months | Reduced moisture or natural protective barriers | Potatoes, onions, apples, eggs | | **Shelf-Stable (Non-Perishable)** | Months to years | Low $a_w$ (< 0.6) or antimicrobial constituents | Sugar, salt, honey, dried grains, ghee | ### 2.1.3 Classification by pH (Acidity) This is **the single most important classification for thermal processing** (canning). > [!IMPORTANT] > The **pH of food determines**: > - The type of microorganisms that can grow. > - The intensity of heat treatment required (e.g., in retort processing). > - The choice of container (tin vs. glass). | pH Range | Classification | Spoilage Organisms | Heat Process | Examples | |---|---|---|---|---| | **pH < 4.5** | **Acid Foods** | Moulds, yeasts, aciduric bacteria | Boiling water bath (100°C) | Tomatoes, citrus fruits, pickles, vinegar | | **pH 4.5 – 7.0** | **Medium-Acid Foods** | More resistant bacteria | Requires higher heat | Bananas, figs, meat, milk, soups | | **pH > 7.0** | **Alkaline Foods** | Wide range | Specialized treatment | Eggs (pH ~ 7.6–9.5), baking soda solutions | ### 2.1.4 Classification by Water Activity ($a_w$) > [!NOTE] > **Water activity** is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the partial vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature. $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$ | $a_w$ Range | Classification | Microbial Growth | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **0.00 – 0.20** | **Bone-dry** | No growth | Dried spices, milk powder | | **0.20 – 0.60** | **Intermediate moisture** | Only osmophilic yeasts | Honey, jams, dried fruits | | **0.60 – 0.85** | **Low moisture** | Moulds, some yeasts | Cake, bread, aged cheese | | **0.85 – 0.91** | **High moisture** | Most bacteria inhibited, some grow | Dry sausage, concentrated juices | | **0.91 – 1.00** | **Very high moisture** | All microorganisms grow | Fresh meat, milk, fish, fruits | ### 2.1.5 Classification by Nutritional Content (Dietary Function) | Class | Primary Function | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Energy-yielding** | Provide calories (Carbohydrates, Fats) | Rice, wheat, oils, sugar | | **Body-building** | Protein for growth and repair | Milk, eggs, pulses, meat, fish | | **Protective / Regulatory** | Vitamins, minerals, fibre | Fruits, vegetables, green leafy items | | **Roughage** | Dietary fibre, aids digestion | Whole grains, salads, bran | ### 2.1.6 Classification by Predominant Chemical Constituent | Class | Predominant Nutrient | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Protein-rich** | > 20% protein | Meat, fish, eggs, soyabean, paneer | | **Carbohydrate-rich** | > 60% carbohydrate | Rice, wheat, potato, sugar | | **Fat-rich** | > 30% fat | Ghee, butter, oils, nuts | | **Vitamin/Mineral-rich** | Micronutrient-dense | Fruits, vegetables, milk | | **Water-rich** | > 80% water | Cucumber, watermelon, milk | ### 2.1.7 Other Specialized Classifications - **By Degree of Processing**: Raw → Minimally processed → Processed → Ultra-processed (NOVA classification). - **By Storage Temperature**: Frozen (-18°C), Chilled (0–4°C), Ambient (20–30°C). - **By Regulatory Category** (FSSAI): Proprietary food, Functional food, Novel food, GM food. - **By Genetic Status**: Conventional, Organic, Genetically Modified (GM). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Dimensionless; range $\vert 0 \to 1 \vert$ | | 2 | pH Definition | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Henderson-Hasselbalch | $pH = pK_a + \log \dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Used in buffered/acid foods | | 4 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** | | 5 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100$ | Used in drying calculations | | 6 | Centigrade to Fahrenheit | $°F = \dfrac{9}{5} °C + 32$ | — | | 7 | Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | For foods like ice cream brines | | 8 | Isotherm Relation (BET) | $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w)v} = \dfrac{1}{v_m c} + \dfrac{(c-1)a_w}{v_m c}$ | Sorption isotherms | | 9 | Specific Heat (above freezing) | $C_p = 1.424 m_c + 1.549 m_p + 1.675 m_f + 0.837 m_a + 4.187 m_w$ | kJ/(kg·K) | | 10 | Lower bound of $a_w$ for bacterial growth | $a_w \approx 0.86$ | — | | 11 | Lower bound of $a_w$ for mould growth | $a_w \approx 0.70$ | — | |
