> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Material Density

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Technology** and **Food Process Engineering**, **Material Density** is defined as the mass of a food substance contained per unit of its physical volume. It is a fundamental **physical property** (also called an *intrinsic property*) that governs the design of hoppers, silos, packaging lines, and downstream unit operations such as drying, conveying, and size reduction.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density, measured in **kg/m³** (SI unit) or **g/cm³** (CGS unit)
- $m$ = mass of the food material (kg or g)
- $V$ = volume occupied (m³ or cm³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In food technology, density is **not a single value** for particulate/solid foods. It is a *family* of related parameters — **True Density, Particle Density, Bulk Density, Tapped Density, and Apparent Density** — each measuring a different physical reality of the same material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine filling a cardboard box with **popcorn kernels** for a movie night.

- If you **measure the box from the outside**, the volume includes the air gaps *between* the kernels. This is the **Bulk Volume**.
- If you take one kernel and measure *just* the solid material (no air gaps), that is the **True Volume**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** 
> **Bulk density** = "How heavy is the box of popcorn I can carry?"
> **True density** = "How heavy is a single kernel if I could crush out all the air?"

For a **liquid** (like milk or fruit juice), there is only one density because the molecules pack tightly with no internal air gaps. For **powders, grains, flakes, and porous solids** (like sugar, flour, cornflakes, freeze-dried coffee), multiple densities exist because air pockets are *part of the material system*.

## 1.3 The Five Densities — At a Glance

| # | Density Type | What It Measures | Typical Food Example |
|---|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | True Density ($\rho_t$) | Solid material alone (no pores, no inter-particle voids) | Crystalline sucrose |
| 2 | Particle Density ($\rho_p$) | Single particle including its internal pores | Wheat grain, bread |
| 3 | Apparent Density ($\rho_a$) | Particle plus sealed surface pores | Spray-dried milk powder |
| 4 | Bulk Density ($\rho_b$) | Powder bed including inter-particle voids | Flour in a bag |
| 5 | Tapped Density ($\rho_T$) | Bulk density after mechanical compaction | Packed sugar in a sack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Density of water at **4 °C = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³** (the reference standard)
> - Standard gravity used in pycnometry: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

To measure density *correctly* in a food laboratory, the engineer must answer **three sequential questions**:

### Step 1: What *kind* of material am I holding?
- **Liquid** → Use a **pycnometer** (specific gravity bottle) or **hydrometer**.
- **Free-flowing powder** (salt, sugar) → Use a **bulk density tester** (graduated cylinder method).
- **Porous / irregular solid** (cereal, snack) → Use a **gas (helium) pycnometer** for true density.
- **Granular cohesive powder** (cocoa, milk powder) → Use a **tapped density apparatus**.

### Step 2: Which *volume* definition applies?
- The **true volume** excludes every pore and void.
- The **apparent volume** includes *open* pores but excludes *closed* pores.
- The **bulk volume** includes *all* voids between and within particles.

### Step 3: What *external conditions* must be controlled?
- **Temperature** (most food densities are reported at 20 °C or 25 °C)
- **Moisture content** (water changes volume and mass)
- **Compaction state** (loose, packed, tapped)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete high-yield formula set** for Material Density as required by KTU Module 2 of GDFTT104.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | True Density | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid}}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit solid volume |
| 2 | Particle Density | $\rho_p = \dfrac{m}{V_{particle+closed\ pores}}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit particle volume |
| 3 | Apparent Density | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m}{V_{particle+open\ pores}}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit apparent volume |
| 4 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}} = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid} + V_{voids}}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit bulk volume |
| 5 | Tapped Density | $\rho_T = \dfrac{m}{V_{tapped}}$ | kg/m³ | Bulk density after tapping |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | m³/kg | Volume per unit mass |
| 7 | Specific Gravity (Relative Density) | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water\ at\ 4°C}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio to water |
| 8 | Porosity (Void Fraction) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of void space |
| 9 | Carr Index (Compressibility) | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_T - \rho_b}{\rho_T} \times 100$ | % | Flowability indicator |
| 10 | Hausner Ratio | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_T}{\rho_b}$ | dimensionless | Flowability indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** $HR < 1.2$ → free-flowing powder; $HR > 1.4$ → cohesive, problematic powder.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Engineering Application | Density Used | Reason |
|--------------------------|--------------|--------|
| Sizing storage silos | **Bulk density** | Silo sees total mass of settled powder |
| Air classification (separating husk from grain) | **Particle density** | Air stream acts on single kernels |
| Extruder screw design | **Bulk density** of feed | Throughput depends on feed density |
| Spray drying chamber sizing | **True density** of solute | Heat transfer uses solid mass |
| Powder packaging line | **Tapped density** | Final settled pack density |
| Quality control of milk | **Apparent density** (hydrometer) | Detects adulteration with water |

## 2.4 Why Porosity Matters

Porosity $\varepsilon$ is the *hidden* link between true and bulk density. It quantifies the air fraction in a powder bed and directly affects:

- **Rehydration rate** of instant coffee, milk powder
- **Heat transfer** during drying and baking
- **Shelf life** (oxygen in voids accelerates lipid oxidation)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linking True Density and Bulk Density through Porosity

**Starting Point (Definition of Bulk Density):**

The bulk volume $V_{bulk}$ of a powder bed is the sum of the solid volume $V_{solid}$ and the void volume $V_{voids}$:

$$V_{bulk} = V_{solid} + V_{voids}$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{bulk}$:

$$\frac{V_{bulk}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} + \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

$$1 = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} + \varepsilon$$

Rearranging for the solid volume ratio:

$$\frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = 1 - \varepsilon$$

**Substituting the density definitions:**

$$\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_{bulk}} \quad \text{and} \quad \rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{solid}}$$

Dividing bulk density by true density:

$$\frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = \frac{m \,/\, V_{bulk}}{m \,/\, V_{solid}} = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = 1 - \varepsilon$$

**Final Derived Relation (KTP / Exam Favorite):**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}}$$

This equation is the **standard KTU relationship** between bulk density, true density, and porosity. It is dimensionally consistent because both densities carry the same units (kg/m³), and $\varepsilon$ emerges as a pure ratio (dimensionless).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation between Tapped Density, Bulk Density, and Carr Index

**Step 1 — Define the Carr Index (Compressibility Index):**

The Carr Index measures the *percentage increase* in density when a powder is mechanically tapped from a loose to a packed state:

$$CI = \frac{\rho_T - \rho_b}{\rho_T} \times 100\ \%$$

**Step 2 — Algebraically rearrange to express bulk density in terms of tapped density and CI:**

$$CI = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_T}\right) \times 100$$

Dividing both sides by 100:

$$\frac{CI}{100} = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_T}$$

Solving for $\rho_b$:

$$\boxed{\rho_b = \rho_T \left(1 - \frac{CI}{100}\right)}$$

This is useful in **hopper design** — given the tapped density (worst-case packing) and the material's compressibility (from datasheet), the engineer can compute the *loose* bulk density in the feed hopper.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of corn flour has:
- True density $\rho_t = 1450\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Bulk density $\rho_b = 520\ \text{kg/m}^3$

Calculate:
1. The porosity $\varepsilon$ of the powder bed.
2. The void volume $V_{voids}$ in a 1 m³ container filled with this flour.
3. The specific gravity (take $\rho_{water} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$).

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Porosity:**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{520}{1450}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - 0.3586$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.6414\ \text{(or 64.14 \%)}}$$

**Interpretation:** 64.14 % of the 1 m³ container is *air*; only 35.86 % is *solid corn flour*.

**Part 2 — Void Volume in 1 m³ Container:**

The solid volume fraction is:

$$\frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = 1 - \varepsilon = 0.3586$$

$$V_{solid} = 0.3586 \times 1\ \text{m}^3 = 0.3586\ \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{voids} = V_{bulk} - V_{solid} = 1.0000 - 0.3586$$

$$\boxed{V_{voids} = 0.6414\ \text{m}^3}$$

**Part 3 — Specific Gravity:**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{flour}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{520}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{SG = 0.52\ \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

Since $SG < 1$, the corn flour is *lighter* than water and would float — useful information for designing hydro-transport or cleaning systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Hierarchy of Densities in a Food Powder System

The following block-level functional architecture map shows how the five densities of food materials are *nested* — each one a subset of the previous volume definition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Volume VB] --> B[Solid Volume Vs]
    A --> C[Inter-particle Void Volume Vi]
    B --> D[Particle Volume Vp]
    B --> E[Internal Closed Pore Volume Vcp]
    D --> F[True Solid Volume Vt]
    D --> G[Open Pore Volume Vop]

    A -.-> H[Bulk Density rhob = m / VB]
    B -.-> I[True Density rhot = m / Vs]
    D -.-> J[Particle Density rhop = m / Vp]
    D -.-> K[Apparent Density rhoa = m / Vp]
    A -.-> L[Tapped Density rhoT = m / Vtapped]
    A -.-> M[Porosity epsilon = 1 - rhob / rhot]
    L -.--> N[Carr Index CI = rhoT - rhob / rhoT x 100]
    L -.--> O[Hausner Ratio HR = rhoT / rhob]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top-left (`VB` = Bulk Volume). Trace downward to see how this total volume *fragments* into solid + voids, then how the solid further fragments into particle-level + pore-level volumes. The density formulas (lower rows) are *attached* to their corresponding volume definitions.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Which Density Should I Measure?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Food Material] --> Q{State of matter}
    Q -->|Liquid| R[Use Pycnometer or Hydrometer]
    R --> S[Report Apparent Density at 20 C]
    Q -->|Solid powder| T{Particle behaviour}
    T -->|Free flowing| U[Measure Bulk Density]
    T -->|Cohesive clumpy| V[Measure Tapped Density]
    T -->|Porous flake| W[Use Gas Pycnometer]
    W --> X[Report True Density]
    U --> Y[Apply Carr Index]
    V --> Y
    Y --> Z[Classify Flowability]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This decision logic is exactly the kind of *practical question* asked in KTU lab viva and Part A. Memorize the *order*: first identify the state of matter, then the particle behaviour, then pick the *right* instrument and density.

## 4.3 Mass-Volume Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mass m] --> B[Divide by Volume V]
    B --> C[Density rho]
    C --> D{Volume type}
    D --> E[Solid only]
    D --> F[Solid plus pores]
    D --> G[Solid plus pores plus inter voids]
    E --> H[True Density]
    F --> I[Particle Density]
    G --> J[Bulk Density]
    H --> K[Highest value]
    I --> L[Intermediate value]
    J --> M[Lowest value]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GDFTT104, Module 2]**
**Define the following terms with one food example each:**
(a) True density
(b) Bulk density
(c) Porosity

**Model Answer:**

(a) **True Density** is the mass of the food material per unit volume of the *solid* material alone, excluding all internal pores and inter-particle voids. *Example:* Crystalline sucrose has $\rho_t \approx 1587\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

(b) **Bulk Density** is the mass of the food powder per unit *total* volume occupied, including the voids between particles. *Example:* Wheat flour has $\rho_b \approx 500\text{–}600\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

(c) **Porosity** is the ratio of void volume to total bulk volume of a powder bed. It is dimensionless. *Example:* Freeze-dried instant coffee has $\varepsilon \approx 0.75$ (highly porous).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern, GDFTT104]**
**Distinguish between tapped density and bulk density. Why is tapped density important in packaging industry?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bulk Density | Tapped Density |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Definition | Mass per unit volume in *loose* powder state | Mass per unit volume after *mechanical tapping* |
| Volume | Larger (more air) | Smaller (air expelled) |
| Numerical value | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Method | Graduated cylinder, gentle pour | Cylinder tapped 100–1250 times (USP method) |

**Importance in packaging:** Tapped density represents the *worst-case* settled density of a powder inside a sealed pouch or sachet. Packaging line engineers use it to compute:
- The **minimum headspace** required in the pouch
- The **maximum number of sachets** per kg of powder
- **Shelf-life oxygen exposure** (smaller headspace → less O₂ → longer life)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, every Part B question carries an *internal choice*. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define true density, particle density, apparent density, and bulk density. Explain the role of gas pycnometer in determining true density. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of milk powder has a true density of $1320\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and a bulk density of $480\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
  (i) Calculate the porosity of the powder bed.
  (ii) Calculate the void volume present in 500 g of this powder.
  (iii) The Carr Index is determined experimentally as 28 %. Find the tapped density and Hausner Ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Definitions (3 marks):**

- **True Density ($\rho_t$):** Mass per unit volume of *solid* material only, excluding pores and inter-particle voids.
- **Particle Density ($\rho_p$):** Mass per unit volume of a *single particle*, including its internal pores.
- **Apparent Density ($\rho_a$):** Mass per unit volume of a single particle, including sealed surface pores but excluding inter-particle voids.
- **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$):** Mass per unit *bulk* volume of the powder bed, including all voids.

**Gas Pycnometer — Role (4 marks):**

A gas (helium) pycnometer determines *true volume* of a powder by:
1. Placing the sample in a sealed chamber of known volume $V_1$.
2. Filling the chamber with **helium gas** at pressure $P_1$.
3. Helium — being a *small inert atom* — penetrates **every open pore and crevice** but does *not* adsorb onto the solid.
4. Expanding the gas into a second known chamber $V_2$, the new pressure $P_2$ is recorded.
5. Applying **Boyle's Law**: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ → the *undead volume* (filled by helium) is the *true solid volume* of the powder.
6. Dividing sample mass by this true volume gives $\rho_t$ with **±0.1 % accuracy**.

> Advantages: non-destructive, works on hygroscopic powders, superior to liquid pycnometry (no swelling/dissolution).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\rho_t = 1320\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_b = 480\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $m = 0.500\ \text{kg}$, $CI = 28\ \%$.

**(i) Porosity (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = 1 - \frac{480}{1320} = 1 - 0.3636$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.6364\ \text{(or 63.64 \%)}}$$

**(ii) Void volume in 500 g of powder (3 marks):**

First, compute the bulk volume of 500 g of powder:

$$V_{bulk} = \frac{m}{\rho_b} = \frac{0.500\ \text{kg}}{480\ \text{kg/m}^3} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$$

The void volume is:

$$V_{voids} = \varepsilon \times V_{bulk} = 0.6364 \times 1.0417 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{voids} = 6.629 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 = 662.9\ \text{cm}^3}$$

**(iii) Tapped Density and Hausner Ratio (2 marks):**

From Carr Index definition:

$$CI = \frac{\rho_T - \rho_b}{\rho_T} \times 100$$

Solving for $\rho_T$:

$$\rho_T = \frac{\rho_b \times 100}{100 - CI} = \frac{480 \times 100}{100 - 28} = \frac{48000}{72}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_T = 666.67\ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

Hausner Ratio:

$$HR = \frac{\rho_T}{\rho_b} = \frac{666.67}{480} = 1.389$$

$$\boxed{HR = 1.389}$$

> **Interpretation:** $HR > 1.4$ borderline, $CI = 28\%$ indicates **poor flowability** → milk powder is cohesive, may need a flow aid (e.g., added lecithin).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the principle and working of a **pycnometer** for liquid density measurement. Why is density measurement of milk important in dairy industry? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice concentrate has a true density of $1380\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and is filled into cans. A quality test on a 250 mL can gives a measured mass of concentrate as 280 g. The concentrate contains 12 % air by volume.
  (i) Compute the bulk density of the juice concentrate as filled.
  (ii) Calculate the specific gravity of the concentrate.
  (iii) Comment on the implications of the air fraction on the shelf life of the product. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Pycnometer Principle (4 marks) + Milk Density Importance (3 marks):**

**Pycnometer Working:**
1. A pycnometer (specific gravity bottle) is a glass flask of *precisely known volume* $V_p$ (typically 25, 50, or 100 mL), sealed with a ground-glass stopper having a capillary hole.
2. Steps:
   - Weigh *empty, dry* pycnometer: $W_1$
   - Weigh pycnometer *filled with water* at 20 °C: $W_2$
   - Weigh pycnometer *filled with sample* (e.g., milk) at 20 °C: $W_3$
3. Sample density: $\rho_s = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1} \times \rho_{water}$

**Diagram (described in text):**

```
    ┌──────────────┐
    │ Capillary    │  ← Stopper with fine hole
    │     ┌───┐    │     to release overflow
    │     │   │    │
    │     │ ● │    │  ← Liquid sample
    │     │   │    │
    │     └───┘    │
    │  Pycnometer  │
    │   (known V)  │
    └──────────────┘
```

**Why milk density matters:**
- **Adulteration detection:** Genuine cow milk at 20 °C has $\rho \approx 1.028\text{–}1.033\ \text{g/cm}^3$. Added water drops density; added urea or starch raises it.
- **Total solids (TS) estimation:** $TS\% \approx 250 \times (1.033 - \rho_{sample})$ for cow milk — quick quality test.
- **Standardization** of fat and SNF (solids-not-fat) in dairy plants.
- **Pricing** — payment to farmers is based on fat + SNF, both correlated to density.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\rho_t = 1380\ \text{kg/m}^3$, can volume = 250 mL = $250 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3$, $m_{concentrate} = 280\ \text{g} = 0.280\ \text{kg}$, air volume fraction = 12 %.

**(i) Bulk density of juice concentrate as filled (3 marks):**

Bulk density (mass per total can volume):

$$\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_{can}} = \frac{0.280\ \text{kg}}{250 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3} = 1120\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_b = 1120\ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Specific gravity (2 marks):**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{concentrate}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{1120}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{SG = 1.12\ \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

> Note: Using true density instead (since liquid has negligible pores), $SG_{true} = 1380/1000 = 1.38$.

**(iii) Shelf-life implications (2 marks):**

The 12 % air fraction in the can:
- Provides **headspace oxygen** that drives **lipid oxidation**, browning reactions, vitamin C (ascorbic acid) degradation.
- Shortens shelf life of vitamin C by ~30–50 %.
- Allows **microbial aerobic growth** in the headspace.
- **Engineering fix:** vacuum can filling, hot-fill-hold-cool, or nitrogen flush to reduce residual O₂ to < 2 %.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do not confuse $\rho_t$ with $\rho_b$ in numerical substitution.** Many students divide bulk mass by true volume — *wrong!* Use the formula with the *matching* volume.
> 2. **Always include units in the final answer** and the working. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **The relationship $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$ is valid only when $\rho_t$ is the *true* density** — not particle density or apparent density. This is a classic KTU trap.
> 4. **Carr Index requires multiplication by 100** to express as a percentage. Forgetting this gives wrong CI value.
> 5. **Hausner Ratio is dimensionless** — do not write kg/m³.
> 6. **Specific gravity is dimensionless** — never write units for SG.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ Density = **mass / volume**; SI unit = **kg/m³**; water reference = **1000 kg/m³ at 4 °C**.
- ✅ **Five densities to remember:** True → Particle → Apparent → Bulk → Tapped (in decreasing magnitude of solid-only volume used).
- ✅ **Always:** $\rho_t \ge \rho_p \ge \rho_a \ge \rho_b$ for the same powder.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ — derives the porosity from two density measurements.
- ✅ **Carr Index:** $CI = \dfrac{\rho_T - \rho_b}{\rho_T} \times 100\ \%$ — measures flowability.
- ✅ **Hausner Ratio:** $HR = \dfrac{\rho_T}{\rho_b}$ — companion to CI; $HR < 1.2$ free-flowing.
- ✅ **Gas (He) pycnometer** → true volume; **Liquid pycnometer** → apparent volume; **Bulk density tester** → bulk volume; **Tapped density apparatus** → tapped volume.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity** is *dimensionless*; $SG = \rho_{substance} / \rho_{water\ at\ 4°C}$.
- ✅ **Specific Volume** $v = 1/\rho$ — used in thermodynamics and drying calculations.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** silo sizing (bulk), air-classification (particle), packaging (tapped), shelf life (porosity).
- ✅ **Milk density range:** $1.028\text{–}1.033\ \text{g/cm}^3$ at 20 °C — outside this → adulteration suspected.
- ✅ **Numerical check:** always verify $\varepsilon$ lies between 0 and 1; if not, you've swapped $\rho_b$ and $\rho_t$.
- ✅ **3-mark questions** ask for *definitions + example*; **14-mark questions** always combine *theory + numerical* — never attempt the numerical without showing the formula first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Particle density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particle Density ($\rho_p$):** Particle density (also called **true density** or **solid density**) of a food material is defined as the **mass of the solid particles per unit true volume of the solid material**, excluding all inter-particle voids, inter-particle spaces, and the volume of internal pores that are accessible to the fluid used in measurement.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\rho_p = \frac{m_s}{V_s}$$

Where:
- $m_s$ = Mass of the solid particles (in **kg** or **g**)
- $V_s$ = True volume of the solid material only, excluding voids (in **m³** or **cm³**)
- $\rho_p$ = Particle density (in **kg/m³** or **g/cm³**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sponge in a Cup:**
> Imagine a dry kitchen sponge with tiny air pockets inside it. 
> - **Particle density** measures the sponge material *itself* (ignoring all the tiny holes inside the sponge and the gaps between multiple sponges).
> - **Bulk density** would weigh a whole stack of sponges including all the air between them.
> - So, particle density is essentially: "How heavy is the *actual food stuff* for the *space it truly occupies*?" This is critical in food powders like milk powder, flour, spices, and instant coffee.

### 1.3 Relationship with Other Densities

In food technology, three primary densities are distinguished:

| Density Type | Volume Considered | Use Case |
|--------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Particle Density ($\rho_p$)** | Volume of solid only (no pores/vacuum) | Powder characterization |
| **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)** | Volume including inter-particle voids | Packaging & transport |
| **Apparent Density** | Volume including internal pores | Whole fruits/vegetables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** $\rho_p \geq \rho_{apparent} \geq \rho_{bulk}$

### 1.4 Standard Units

The SI unit of particle density is **kg/m³**, but the commonly used practical unit in food labs is **g/cm³** (numerically equal to **g/mL**).

Standard reference value: **Density of water at 4°C = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Significance

Particle density is a **fundamental physical property** that:
- Reflects the **chemical composition** (fat, protein, carbohydrate content) of the food.
- Indicates the **porosity** when compared with apparent or bulk density.
- Determines **flow behavior**, **mixing**, and **segregation** of powders.
- Affects **heat transfer**, **drying rate**, and **storage stability** in powdered foods.

### 2.2 Porosity Linkage

The relationship between densities is used to derive **porosity ($\varepsilon$)**:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$$

Where:
- $\rho_b$ = Bulk density
- $\rho_p$ = Particle density
- $\varepsilon$ = Porosity (dimensionless, 0 to 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high porosity means the food powder is "fluffy" and traps more air — useful for instant dissolving products (e.g., coffee, cocoa), but increases oxidation risk.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho_p = \dfrac{m_s}{V_s}$ | Particle density definition | g/cm³ |
| 2 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_s}{V_b}$ | Bulk density definition | g/cm³ |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$ | Porosity from density ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_s = \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_p}$ | True solid volume | cm³ |
| 5 | $RR = \dfrac{\rho_{tapped} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tapped}}$ | Hausner Ratio related concept | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_p^{food}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Relative (specific) density | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - For a non-porous, defect-free solid: $\rho_p = \rho_{true} = \rho_{theoretical}$
> - For a porous food particle: $\rho_p > \rho_{apparent}$ always.
> - Particle density can **never be less than bulk density**: $\rho_p \geq \rho_b$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, particle density data is used for:
- **Process design** of spray dryers, fluidized beds, and pneumatic conveyors.
- **Quality control** of milk powder, sugar, salt, spices, and starch.
- **Formulation** of beverage mixes to ensure proper dispersion.
- **Packaging design** — to calculate fill weight per unit volume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Porosity from Densities

**Starting point:** Bulk density is always less than or equal to particle density because the bulk volume contains voids.

$$\rho_b = \frac{m_s}{V_b} \quad \text{and} \quad \rho_p = \frac{m_s}{V_s}$$

**Step 1:** Write the volume of voids as the difference between bulk volume and solid volume.

$$V_{void} = V_b - V_s$$

**Step 2:** Express porosity as the ratio of void volume to total bulk volume.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{void}}{V_b} = \frac{V_b - V_s}{V_b}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $V_b$.

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{V_s}{V_b}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the inverse density ratios. Since $V_s = \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_p}$ and $V_b = \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_b}$:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{m_s / \rho_p}{m_s / \rho_b}$$

**Step 5:** The $m_s$ cancels out cleanly.

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by limits:** If $\rho_b = \rho_p$ (no voids), then $\varepsilon = 0$ ✓. If $\rho_b \rightarrow 0$ (all air), then $\varepsilon \rightarrow 1$ ✓. The derivation is dimensionally and physically consistent.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A sample of milk powder has a mass of **50 g**. Its true volume (measured by air pycnometer) is found to be **62.5 cm³**. The bulk volume occupied in a measuring cylinder is **100 cm³**. Calculate:
1. Particle density
2. Bulk density
3. Porosity

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Particle Density**

$$\rho_p = \frac{m_s}{V_s} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{62.5 \text{ cm}^3}$$

$$\rho_p = 0.8 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (b): Bulk Density**

$$\rho_b = \frac{m_s}{V_b} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ cm}^3}$$

$$\rho_b = 0.5 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (c): Porosity**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p} = 1 - \frac{0.5}{0.8}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375$$

$$\varepsilon = 37.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** 37.5% of the bulk milk powder volume is empty air space. This indicates a moderately porous structure — typical for skim milk powder.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Diagram — Particle vs Bulk vs Apparent Density

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Food Sample Mass m_s"] --> B["Solid Particles Only"]
    A --> C["Stack of Particles in Container"]
    A --> D["Whole Food with Internal Pores"]
    B --> E["True Volume V_s (no pores, no voids)"]
    C --> F["Bulk Volume V_b (includes air gaps)"]
    D --> G["Apparent Volume V_app (includes internal pores)"]
    E --> H["Particle Density rho_p = m_s / V_s"]
    F --> I["Bulk Density rho_b = m_s / V_b"]
    G --> J["Apparent Density rho_app = m_s / V_app"]
    H --> K["Highest density value"]
    I --> L["Lowest density value"]
    J --> M["Intermediate density value"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Measurement of Particle Density (Air Pycnometer Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Weigh empty sample cell"] --> S2["Step 2: Place known mass of food powder in cell"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Seal cell in pycnometer chamber"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Introduce gas Helium or Nitrogen at known pressure P1"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Allow gas to expand into reference chamber P2"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Apply Boyle Law to compute solid volume V_s"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Calculate particle density rho_p = m_s / V_s"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compare with bulk density to get porosity"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Role of Particle Density in Food Engineering

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input: Food Powder"] --> B["Measure Particle Density"]
    B --> C["Calculate Porosity"]
    C --> D["Design Packaging Volume"]
    C --> E["Select Conveyor System"]
    C --> F["Predict Flow Behavior"]
    B --> G["Quality Control Release"]
    D --> H["Finished Packaged Product"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use gas (not liquid) in pycnometer?** Many food powders (milk, flour) absorb water or swell in liquids, distorting the true volume measurement. Inert gases like helium penetrate the smallest pores without reacting with the food.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define particle density. How is it different from bulk density?**

**Model Answer:**
Particle density ($\rho_p$) is the mass of solid food particles per unit true volume of the solid, excluding inter-particle voids and internal pores. Bulk density ($\rho_b$) includes the volume of voids between particles.

$$\rho_p = \frac{m_s}{V_s} \quad ; \quad \rho_b = \frac{m_s}{V_b}$$

Since $V_s \leq V_b$, we have $\rho_p \geq \rho_b$. Particle density is used in powder characterization, while bulk density is used in packaging and transport calculations. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Mention any two methods for determining particle density of food powders.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Gas (Air) Pycnometer Method** — uses Boyle's law with an inert gas; most accurate for porous powders.
2. **Liquid Displacement Method** — uses a non-solvent liquid (e.g., toluene) whose density is known; volume of liquid displaced gives solid volume.
3. **Density Bottle (Pycnometer) Method** — measures mass and uses a calibrated bottle for volume determination. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)**
*Module Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE full question.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure of determining particle density of a food powder using a **gas pycnometer**. Discuss its advantages over the liquid displacement method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food powder sample weighs **75 g**. Its true volume measured by air pycnometer is **60 cm³**, and its bulk volume in a 100 mL cylinder is **120 cm³**. Calculate the particle density, bulk density, and porosity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Principle:** A gas pycnometer operates on **Boyle's Law** ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$). An inert gas (helium/nitrogen) is allowed to fill the void space *around* the particles but not penetrate the solid itself. The change in pressure between two known volumes gives the solid volume of the sample.

**Procedure:**
1. Calibrate the empty sample cell volume $V_c$.
2. Weigh the sample mass $m_s$ accurately.
3. Place the sample in the cell; seal it in the pycnometer chamber.
4. Fill the reference chamber to a known pressure $P_1$.
5. Open the valve to allow gas expansion into the sample chamber.
6. Record the new equilibrium pressure $P_2$.
7. Calculate solid volume: $V_s = V_c - \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)}{P_2} \times V_r$ (where $V_r$ is reference volume).
8. Compute $\rho_p = m_s / V_s$.

**Advantages over liquid method:**
- Suitable for **porous, hygroscopic, or water-soluble** foods.
- No swelling or dissolution of sample.
- Higher **accuracy** (down to 0.001 cm³).
- **Rapid** and non-destructive. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $m_s = 75$ g, $V_s = 60$ cm³, $V_b = 120$ cm³.

**Step 1: Particle Density**

$$\rho_p = \frac{75}{60} = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Bulk Density**

$$\rho_b = \frac{75}{120} = 0.625 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Porosity**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{0.625}{1.25} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**Result:** The powder has 50% porosity, indicating a highly porous structure suitable for instant dissolution applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define particle density, bulk density, and porosity. Derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **gas pycnometer** and **liquid displacement** methods of particle density determination. A starch sample of mass **40 g** occupies a bulk volume of **80 cm³** and has a particle density of **1.5 g/cm³**. Find its porosity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definitions:**
- **Particle density ($\rho_p$):** Mass of solid per unit true solid volume.
- **Bulk density ($\rho_b$):** Mass of solid per unit bulk volume (including voids).
- **Porosity ($\varepsilon$):** Fraction of bulk volume occupied by voids.

**Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.1 above)

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Gas Pycnometer | Liquid Displacement |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Fluid used | Inert gas (He, N₂) | Non-solvent liquid |
| Suitable for | Porous, hygroscopic powders | Non-porous, insoluble solids |
| Accuracy | Very high (0.1% error) | Moderate |
| Time | Fast | Slow |
| Cost | High | Low |

**Calculation:**

Given: $m_s = 40$ g, $V_b = 80$ cm³, $\rho_p = 1.5$ g/cm³.

**Step 1: Calculate solid volume**

$$V_s = \frac{m_s}{\rho_p} = \frac{40}{1.5} = 26.67 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Porosity**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_b - V_s}{V_b} = \frac{80 - 26.67}{80} = \frac{53.33}{80} = 0.667$$

$$\varepsilon = 66.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kg with cm³ — always convert mass to g when volume is in cm³.
> 2. **Inverted Formula:** Writing $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_b}$ (wrong) instead of $1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$ (correct).
> 3. **Confusing True Volume with Bulk Volume:** Using cylinder volume for particle density calculation — leads to artificially low density.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** in the final answer — KTU awards half-mark deductions for missing units.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Particle density** = mass of solid ÷ true solid volume (no pores, no voids).
- ✅ **Bulk density** = mass of solid ÷ bulk volume (includes inter-particle voids).
- ✅ **Always:** $\rho_p \geq \rho_b$.
- ✅ **Porosity formula:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$.
- ✅ **Gas pycnometer** uses **Boyle's Law** — best for porous/hygroscopic foods.
- ✅ **Liquid displacement** uses a **non-solvent liquid** like toluene.
- ✅ **SI unit:** kg/m³; **Lab unit:** g/cm³ (= g/mL).
- ✅ **Reference:** Density of water at 4°C = **1 g/cm³**.
- ✅ Particle density depends on **chemical composition** (fat, protein, moisture).
- ✅ High porosity → good for **instant products**; low porosity → good for **compact storage**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulk density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bulk Density (ρ_b)** of a food material is defined as the **mass of a bulk particulate solid per unit volume it occupies**, where the volume includes the solid particles themselves **plus** all the **inter-particle voids, pores, and empty spaces** trapped between the particles. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\rho_b = \frac{M}{V_{bulk}}$$

where:
- $M$ = Mass of the particulate food material (in **grams (g)** or **kilograms (kg)**)
- $V_{bulk}$ = Total bulk volume occupied (in **cm³**, **mL**, or **m³**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk density is a **fundamental powder/granular property** in Food Technology. It governs **packaging volume calculations, storage silo design, hopper angles, conveying systems, and quality control** of powders such as milk powder, wheat flour, spices, sugar, coffee, and instant food mixes.

The standard SI unit of bulk density is **kg/m³**, although in food science it is commonly expressed as **g/cm³** or **g/mL** for convenience.

### 1.2 Types of Bulk Density

There are **two standard types** recognized by KTU syllabus and pharmacopoeial/food standards (USP, ASTM, BIS):

| Type | Synonym | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Loose Bulk Density (LBD)** | Aerated bulk density, Poured density | Density measured when powder is freely poured into a container **without any tapping or vibration**. |
| **Tapped Bulk Density (TBD)** | Packed density, Consolidated density | Density measured after the container is mechanically **tapped/compacted** a fixed number of times (e.g., 100, 500, 1250 taps) until volume becomes constant. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🍚 **Think of a Glass of Rice:** Imagine filling a transparent glass with rice grains. You will notice that there are visible air gaps between the grains. If you weigh the rice and divide by the **whole volume** of the glass (rice + air gaps), you get the **bulk density**. Now, if you **tap the glass firmly** on a table several times, the rice settles down, the air gaps shrink, the volume reduces, and the **mass stays the same**. Dividing the same mass by this **smaller tapped volume** gives the **tapped bulk density** (which is always higher than loose bulk density).

This simple analogy captures the essence: **bulk density = how tightly or loosely particles pack together in a real-world container**, including all the trapped air.

### 1.4 Distinction from True (Particle) Density

A **critical conceptual distinction** for KTU exams:

- **Bulk Density (ρ_b)**: Mass / Volume (solid **+ internal pores** **+ inter-particle voids**)
- **True Density / Particle Density (ρ_p)**: Mass / Volume (solid **+ internal pores only**)
- **Apparent Density**: Mass / Volume (solid **+ closed internal pores**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship is always: **True Density ≥ Bulk Density**, because bulk volume always contains extra air gaps that are excluded from the true solid volume.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Bulk Density — Engineering Significance

In food technology, almost all raw materials in powdered or granular form (flour, milk powder, salt, sugar, instant coffee, soup mixes, spice powders, protein isolates) are handled, stored, transported, and packaged in **bulk form**, not as individual particles. Therefore, the **engineer must know how much mass fits into a given volume of container, silo, or bag** — this is precisely what bulk density answers.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Measurement

The standardized procedure (referenced in USP, ASTM D7481, and BIS standards) is:

1. **Weigh an empty graduated measuring cylinder or container** of known volume → record $W_1$.
2. **Pour the food powder freely** through a funnel from a fixed height into the cylinder until it overflows. Level the top surface carefully.
3. **Read the bulk volume** $V_{bulk}$ directly from the cylinder graduation.
4. **Weigh the cylinder + powder** → record $W_2$.
5. **Calculate Loose Bulk Density** using $\rho_{LBD} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{V_{bulk}}$.
6. For **Tapped Density**: mechanically tap the cylinder (e.g., using a tap density apparatus) for a standardized number of taps (commonly 100, 500, or until volume change is < 2%).
7. **Read the new (reduced) tapped volume** $V_{tapped}$.
8. **Calculate Tapped Bulk Density** using $\rho_{TBD} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{V_{tapped}}$.

### 2.3 Derived Indices from Bulk Density

These derived parameters are **high-yield KTU topics** because they appear frequently in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

#### (a) Carr Index (Compressibility Index, CI)

The **Carr Index** quantifies the **flowability** of a powder based on how much it compresses upon tapping:

$$CI = \frac{\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{TBD}} \times 100$$

- **Lower CI** → Better flowability
- **Higher CI** → Poor flowability (cohesive/sticky powder)

#### (b) Hausner Ratio (HR)

$$HR = \frac{\rho_{TBD}}{\rho_{LBD}}$$

- $HR < 1.25$ → Good flow
- $HR > 1.5$ → Poor flow

#### (c) Porosity (ε)

The fraction of bulk volume occupied by **air voids**:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{p}}$$

where $\rho_p$ is the **true/particle density** (measured by gas pycnometer or liquid displacement).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note for Examiners:** All equations are universally applicable to any food powder. Always retain **units** in the final answer.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Loose Bulk Density | $\rho_{LBD} = \dfrac{M}{V_{bulk}}$ | **g/cm³** or **kg/m³** | Packaging, bag sizing |
| 2 | Tapped Bulk Density | $\rho_{TBD} = \dfrac{M}{V_{tapped}}$ | **g/cm³** or **kg/m³** | Storage silo design |
| 3 | Carr Index | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{TBD}} \times 100$ | **%** | Powder flowability |
| 4 | Hausner Ratio | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{TBD}}{\rho_{LBD}}$ | Dimensionless | Powder flow classification |
| 5 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{p}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Aeration, wetting studies |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $V_{sp} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{LBD}}$ | **cm³/g** | Inverse density for volume-fill problems |
| 7 | % Voids | $\%V = \varepsilon \times 100$ | **%** | Air fraction in pack |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all equations, **ρ_bulk is always LESS than ρ_tapped is always LESS than or EQUAL to ρ_particle (true density)**. The hierarchy is: $\rho_{LBD} \le \rho_{TBD} \le \rho_{particle}$.

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Bulk Density (KTU Theory Question)

1. **Particle size** — Smaller particles → lower bulk density (more fine inter-particle voids).
2. **Particle size distribution** — Wide distribution → higher bulk density (small particles fill gaps between large ones).
3. **Particle shape** — Spherical/regular → higher bulk density; flaky/irregular → lower.
4. **Moisture content** — High moisture → particles stick together → lower bulk density.
5. **Method of packing / tapping** — More tapping → higher tapped density.
6. **Presence of fat/oil coating** — Coated particles are more cohesive → lower bulk density.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Packaging line design**: Determines bag/sachet dimensions for a target mass.
- **Silo/Hopper capacity**: Storage volume × bulk density = storable mass.
- **Conveyor & auger sizing**: Throughput (kg/hr) depends on bulk density.
- **Quality control**: Batch-to-batch consistency in instant foods, milk powder.
- **Mixing and blending**: Powders of different bulk densities segregate (demix).
- **Reconstitution behavior**: Wettability and sinkability depend on porosity derived from bulk density.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Porosity Equation

We start from the fundamental concept that the **bulk volume of a powder bed is the sum of solid volume and void volume**:

**Step 1 — Volumetric Statement**

$$V_{bulk} = V_{solid} + V_{voids}$$

**Step 2 — Divide throughout by $V_{bulk}$**

$$\frac{V_{bulk}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} + \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

This simplifies to:

$$1 = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} + \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Solid Volume Fraction**

The solid volume fraction can be expressed using densities. Since mass $M$ is conserved:

$$V_{solid} = \frac{M}{\rho_p} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{bulk} = \frac{M}{\rho_{LBD}}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{M / \rho_p}{M / \rho_{LBD}} = \frac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_p}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back into Step 2**

$$1 = \frac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_p} + \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

**Step 5 — Define Porosity**

Porosity $\varepsilon$ is defined as the void volume fraction:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Equation**

Rearranging the previous expression:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_p}}$$

This is the **master porosity equation** derivable directly from the volume-additivity principle.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **[Sample Problem]**
> A food company packages **instant coffee powder**. A **100 mL graduated cylinder** has an empty weight of **145.0 g**. When coffee powder is **freely poured** into it, the volume reads **78 mL** and the filled cylinder weighs **200.5 g**. After **500 mechanical taps**, the volume reduces to **65 mL**.
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Loose Bulk Density
> (b) Tapped Bulk Density
> (c) Carr Index
> (d) Hausner Ratio
> (e) Comment on the powder flowability

**Given Data:**

$$W_1 = 145.0 \text{ g (empty cylinder)} \quad W_2 = 200.5 \text{ g (filled cylinder)}$$

$$M = W_2 - W_1 = 200.5 - 145.0 = 55.5 \text{ g}$$

$$V_{bulk} = 78 \text{ mL} \quad V_{tapped} = 65 \text{ mL}$$

#### (a) Loose Bulk Density

$$\rho_{LBD} = \frac{M}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{55.5 \text{ g}}{78 \text{ mL}} = 0.7115 \text{ g/mL}$$

Rounded to **3 significant figures**:

$$\boxed{\rho_{LBD} \approx 0.712 \text{ g/mL} = 712 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

#### (b) Tapped Bulk Density

$$\rho_{TBD} = \frac{M}{V_{tapped}} = \frac{55.5 \text{ g}}{65 \text{ mL}} = 0.8538 \text{ g/mL}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{TBD} \approx 0.854 \text{ g/mL} = 854 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

#### (c) Carr Index

$$CI = \frac{\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{TBD}} \times 100$$

$$CI = \frac{0.854 - 0.712}{0.854} \times 100 = \frac{0.142}{0.854} \times 100$$

$$CI = 0.1663 \times 100 = 16.63 \text{ \%}$$

$$\boxed{CI \approx 16.63 \text{ \%}}$$

#### (d) Hausner Ratio

$$HR = \frac{\rho_{TBD}}{\rho_{LBD}} = \frac{0.854}{0.712} = 1.199$$

$$\boxed{HR \approx 1.20}$$

#### (e) Flowability Assessment (Standard Reference Table)

| Carr Index (%) | Flow Character | Hausner Ratio |
|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| **≤ 10** | Excellent | 1.00 – 1.11 |
| **11 – 15** | Good | 1.12 – 1.18 |
| **16 – 20** | Fair | 1.19 – 1.25 |
| **21 – 25** | Passable | 1.26 – 1.32 |
| **26 – 31** | Poor | 1.33 – 1.45 |
| **32 – 37** | Very Poor | 1.46 – 1.59 |
| **> 38** | Very Very Poor | > 1.60 |

Since $CI = 16.63\%$ and $HR = 1.20$:

$$\boxed{\text{The instant coffee powder exhibits FAIR flowability.}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bulk vs. Tapped vs. True Density

| Property | Loose Bulk Density | Tapped Bulk Density | True (Particle) Density |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Volume considered** | Solid + internal pores + inter-particle voids | Solid + internal pores + minimal voids (after tapping) | Solid + internal pores (no inter-particle voids) |
| **Measurement method** | Free pouring | Mechanical tapping | Gas pycnometer, liquid displacement |
| **Magnitude** | **Lowest** | Intermediate | **Highest** |
| **Reflects** | Storage in loosely filled state | Storage after settling/vibration | Intrinsic material property |
| **Use** | Packaging, fill volume | Silo design, conveying | Material identification, porosity |
| **Typical value (wheat flour)** | ~0.45 g/mL | ~0.65 g/mL | ~1.45 g/mL |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Relationship of Density Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Bulk Volume Vb of Food Powder] --> B[Solid Particle Volume Vp]
    A --> C[Inter-particle Void Volume Vv]
    B --> D[Solid Material Volume Vm]
    B --> E[Internal Closed Pore Volume Vip]
    A --> F[Loose Bulk Density rho LBD = M slash Vb loose]
    A --> G[Tapped Bulk Density rho TBD = M slash Vb tapped]
    D --> H[True Density rho t = M slash Vm]
    B --> I[Particle Density rho p = M slash Vm plus Vip]
    F --> J[Carr Index and Hausner Ratio]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Powder Flowability Classification]
    F --> L[Porosity epsilon = 1 minus rho LBD slash rho p]
    I --> L
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bulk Density Workflow in Food Industry

```mermaid

    end

    subgraph CALC[Calculation Stage]
        C1[Mass M = W2 minus W1]
        C2[rho LBD = M slash Vb loose]
        C3[rho TBD = M slash Vb tapped]
        C4[CI = rho TBD minus rho LBD slash rho TBD times 100]
        C5[HR = rho TBD slash rho LBD]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Engineering Output Stage]
        D1[Packaging Volume Design]
        D2[Silo Storage Capacity]
        D3[Hopper and Conveyor Sizing]
        D4[Quality Control Decision]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Flowability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Calculate CI and HR] --> S1{CI percent}
    S1 --> S1A[CI less than or equal to 10]
    S1 --> S1B[CI 11 to 15]
    S1 --> S1C[CI 16 to 20]
    S1 --> S1D[CI 21 to 25]
    S1 --> S1E[CI 26 to 31]
    S1 --> S1F[CI 32 to 37]
    S1 --> S1G[CI greater than 38]
    S1A --> R1[Excellent Flow]
    S1B --> R2[Good Flow]
    S1C --> R3[Fair Flow]
    S1D --> R4[Passable Flow Add Glidant]
```
