<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define bulk density. Differentiate between loose bulk density and tapped bulk density.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bulk Density (ρ_b):** It is defined as the mass of a particulate food material per unit bulk volume, where the bulk volume includes the solid particles **and the inter-particle void spaces**.

$$\rho_b = \frac{\text{Mass of powder}}{\text{Bulk volume occupied}}$$

| Feature | Loose Bulk Density (LBD) | Tapped Bulk Density (TBD) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Definition | Density measured when powder is **freely poured** into a container without any compaction. | Density measured **after mechanical tapping** consolidates the powder. |
| Volume | Larger (more voids) | Smaller (voids reduced) |
| Magnitude | Lower | Higher |
| Represents | Aerated, unsettled powder | Settled, packed powder |

> **[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for differentiation table]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the Carr Index? How is it used to assess powder flowability in food processing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Carr Index (CI)**, also called the **Compressibility Index**, is a numerical measure of the tendency of a powder to compress/compact under tapping. It is calculated as:

$$CI = \frac{\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{TBD}} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

**Use in Food Processing:**
- A **low Carr Index** (≤ 10%) indicates **excellent flowability** — the powder flows freely through hoppers, fillers, and packaging machines.
- A **high Carr Index** (> 25%) indicates **poor flowability** — the powder is cohesive, prone to caking, and may bridge/rathole in silos.
- It is widely used in the **quality control of instant foods** (milk powder, coffee, soup mixes) and in **formulation design** to decide whether a glidant (e.g., silica) is needed.

> **[3 Marks: 1 for formula + 2 for flowability interpretation]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for the determination of bulk density and tapped density of a food powder with suitable formulas.
**(b) [7 Marks]** A sample of **wheat flour** is tested. The empty measuring cylinder weighs **180 g**. After free pouring of flour, the volume reads **125 mL** and weight becomes **240 g**. After 100 taps, volume becomes **105 mL**. Calculate the loose bulk density, tapped bulk density, Carr Index, and Hausner Ratio. Comment on the flow behavior.

#### Model Solution for Q3(A):

**(a) Procedure for Bulk Density and Tapped Density Determination [7 Marks]**

**Apparatus Required:** Graduated measuring cylinder (100 mL or 250 mL), analytical balance (accuracy ± 0.01 g), tap density tester (mechanical or manual), funnel, spatula.

**Step 1 — Clean and dry** the graduated cylinder. Weigh it empty on an analytical balance. Record as $W_1$.

**Step 2 — Place a funnel** above the cylinder at a fixed height (typically 5–10 cm). **Pour the food powder freely** through the funnel until the cylinder is **overflowing**. (Avoid compaction during pouring.)

**Step 3 — Carefully level the top surface** using a straight-edged spatula, scraping off excess powder from the top.

**Step 4 — Read the bulk volume** $V_{bulk}$ directly from the cylinder's graduation marks.

**Step 5 — Weigh the cylinder with the powder** and record as $W_2$.

**Step 6 — Calculate Loose Bulk Density:**

$$\rho_{LBD} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{V_{bulk}}$$

**Step 7 — Tapping Procedure:** Mount the cylinder on a **tap density tester**. Set the machine for a standard number of taps (commonly 100, 500, or 1250 taps as per USP). Allow the powder to settle.

**Step 8 — Read the new compacted (tapped) volume** $V_{tapped}$ directly.

**Step 9 — Calculate Tapped Bulk Density:**

$$\rho_{TBD} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{V_{tapped}}$$

**Precautions:**
- Powder should be **dry and at room temperature**.
- The pouring height and rate must be **standardized**.
- The cylinder must be **vibration-free** during weighing.
- Triplicate measurements should be taken and **averaged**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Empty cylinder mass $W_1 = 180 \text{ g}$
- Mass of cylinder + flour after pouring $W_2 = 240 \text{ g}$
- Bulk volume $V_{bulk} = 125 \text{ mL}$
- Tapped volume $V_{tapped} = 105 \text{ mL}$

**Net mass of flour:**

$$M = W_2 - W_1 = 240 - 180 = 60 \text{ g}$$

**(i) Loose Bulk Density:**

$$\rho_{LBD} = \frac{60 \text{ g}}{125 \text{ mL}} = 0.48 \text{ g/mL}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{LBD} = 0.48 \text{ g/mL} = 480 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Tapped Bulk Density:**

$$\rho_{TBD} = \frac{60 \text{ g}}{105 \text{ mL}} = 0.5714 \text{ g/mL}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{TBD} \approx 0.571 \text{ g/mL} = 571 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**(iii) Carr Index:**

$$CI = \frac{0.571 - 0.48}{0.571} \times 100 = \frac{0.091}{0.571} \times 100 = 15.94 \text{ \%}$$

$$\boxed{CI \approx 15.94 \text{ \%}}$$

**(iv) Hausner Ratio:**

$$HR = \frac{\rho_{TBD}}{\rho_{LBD}} = \frac{0.571}{0.48} = 1.190$$

$$\boxed{HR \approx 1.19}$$

**(v) Flow Behavior Comment:**

Referring to the standard classification table:
- $CI = 15.94\%$ lies in the **11–15%** range (borderline with 16–20%) → **Good to Fair flowability**.
- $HR = 1.19$ also lies in the **1.12–1.18 / 1.19–1.25** range → **Good to Fair flow**.

$$\boxed{\text{The wheat flour exhibits GOOD to FAIR flowability.}}$$

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Choice for Module 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following with their formulas: (i) Bulk density, (ii) Tapped density, (iii) Porosity, (iv) Carr Index, (v) Hausner Ratio.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any five factors** affecting the bulk density of food powders. Explain how bulk density influences the **design of packaging and storage systems** in the food industry.

#### Model Solution for Q3(B):

**(a) Definitions and Formulas [7 Marks]**

**(i) Bulk Density (ρ_b):** The mass of a food powder per unit bulk volume (which includes solid particles and inter-particle voids).

$$\rho_b = \frac{M}{V_{bulk}} \quad \text{(g/mL)}$$

**(ii) Tapped Density (ρ_TBD):** The bulk density of a powder measured after it has been mechanically tapped/compacted to a constant (minimum) volume.

$$\rho_{TBD} = \frac{M}{V_{tapped}} \quad \text{(g/mL)}$$

**(iii) Porosity (ε):** The fraction of bulk volume occupied by air voids. It is dimensionless (0 to 1).

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{LBD}}{\rho_p}$$

where $\rho_p$ is the true/particle density.

**(iv) Carr Index (CI):** A percentage measure of powder compressibility, used to predict flowability.

$$CI = \frac{\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD}}{\rho_{TBD}} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**(v) Hausner Ratio (HR):** A dimensionless ratio of tapped to loose bulk density, used to classify powder flow.

$$HR = \frac{\rho_{TBD}}{\rho_{LBD}}$$

---

**(b) Factors Affecting Bulk Density and Influence on Packaging/Storage [7 Marks]**

**Five Major Factors Affecting Bulk Density:**

**1. Particle Size:**
Smaller particles have **larger surface area-to-volume ratio** and tend to pack with **more inter-particle voids**, leading to **lower bulk density**. Coarse particles pack more efficiently and have higher bulk density.

**2. Particle Shape:**
**Spherical or regular-shaped** particles roll and pack efficiently, giving **higher bulk density**. **Flaky, needle-shaped, or highly irregular** particles interlock, creating voids and producing **lower bulk density** (e.g., wheat bran has low bulk density due to flaky structure).

**3. Moisture Content:**
Higher moisture causes particles to **stick together** (cohesion), forming agglomerates that trap air. This **reduces bulk density**. Excessive moisture can also cause caking. Dry powders have predictable, higher bulk densities.

**4. Particle Size Distribution:**
A **narrow, uniform size** distribution leaves large gaps. A **wide (polydisperse) distribution** allows small particles to **fill the interstices** between large ones, **increasing bulk density** significantly (e.g., commercial flour vs. monodisperse sugar crystals).

**5. Method of Packing / Compaction:**
The energy and method of filling affect how particles settle. **Free pouring** gives loose bulk density; **vibration, tapping, or pressure** increase it to tapped density. Industrial processes like screw conveying apply energy that can densify the powder.

**Influence on Packaging and Storage Design:**

- **Packaging Volume Calculation:** A 1 kg bag of milk powder with $\rho_{LBD} = 0.5$ g/mL requires $V = 2000$ mL of bag volume. Engineers use bulk density to design the **exact dimensions of pouches, sachets, cartons, and bags**.
- **Storage Silo Sizing:** The capacity of a silo (in tonnes) = Silo volume × Bulk density. Knowing bulk density is essential to **size silos correctly** and avoid under- or over-design.
- **Hopper and Chute Angles:** Powders with low bulk density and high CI (poor flow) require **steeper hopper angles** (> 60°) to prevent arching/ratholing.
- **Conveyor and Auger Throughput:** Mass flow rate (kg/hr) of a screw conveyor = Cross-sectional area × Bulk density × Screw speed. This directly determines **motor power and equipment size**.
- **Transportation Logistics:** Bulk density determines the **weight-to-volume ratio**, which affects freight cost calculations (e.g., volumetric vs. mass-based pricing).
- **Quality Control:** Sudden changes in bulk density between batches indicate **variation in particle size, moisture, or raw material source**, triggering process corrections.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up numerator and denominator in Carr Index** — CI = $(\rho_{TBD} - \rho_{LBD})/\rho_{TBD}$, NOT divided by $\rho_{LBD}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mL to cm³ or to kg/m³** when the question demands SI units. The examiner specifically checks unit consistency.
> 3. **Subtracting weights in the wrong order** — always $W_{filled} - W_{empty}$.
> 4. **Confusing Bulk Density with True Density** — bulk density includes voids; true density does not. Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 5. **Skipping the formula before substituting values** — in 14-mark problems, **formula step carries 1 mark**; jumping directly to the answer loses it.
> 6. **Forgetting to comment on flowability** in numerical problems — it carries **1 separate mark** as a "discussion" or "engineering interpretation" component.
> 7. **Reporting decimal places incorrectly** — typically 3 significant figures are expected unless stated otherwise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Bulk Density** = Mass / Bulk volume (including voids). Always **less than true density**.
- ✅ **Two types**: **Loose (poured / aerated)** and **Tapped (compacted / consolidated)**. Tapped > Loose always.
- ✅ **SI unit**: **kg/m³**. Common food science unit: **g/mL** or **g/cm³**.
- ✅ **Carr Index (CI)** is in **%** and quantifies compressibility — lower is better flow.
- ✅ **Hausner Ratio (HR)** is **dimensionless** — $HR < 1.25$ → good flow; $HR > 1.5$ → poor flow.
- ✅ **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - (\rho_{LBD}/\rho_p)$ — derived from volume additivity principle.
- ✅ **Hierarchy**: $\rho_{LBD} \le \rho_{TBD} \le \rho_{particle}$ (true density).
- ✅ **Smaller particle size, higher moisture, irregular shape, narrow size distribution** → all **decrease** bulk density.
- ✅ **Wide particle size distribution** → **increases** bulk density (small particles fill large gaps).
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: Packaging volume design, silo capacity, conveyor sizing, hopper angle design, QC.
- ✅ **Apparatus used**: Graduated cylinder + analytical balance + tap density tester.
- ✅ **Always state the formula FIRST** before numerical substitution in KTU 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Always include units and 3 significant figures** in the final answer.
- ✅ **Practical examples** to remember: Wheat flour ≈ 0.45 g/mL (loose); Milk powder ≈ 0.50 g/mL; Granulated sugar ≈ 0.80 g/mL; Instant coffee ≈ 0.30 g/mL.
- ✅ **Key standards**: USP <616>, ASTM D7481, BIS methods — examiners may reference them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True density; laws of friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** True density; laws of friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 True Density — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **True Density ($\rho_t$)** is defined as the mass of a material per unit volume of its **solid phase only**, with all inter-particle voids, intra-particle pores, and inter-cellular air spaces completely excluded from the volume measurement.

Mathematically, the KTU-standard expression is:

$$
\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{solid}}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the food material (in **kg** or **g**)
- $V_{solid}$ = true volume occupied only by the solid matter (excluding pores)

For a homogeneous, non-porous food (e.g., a piece of steel, a fully dense fat block), $\rho_t$ is identical to the **bulk density** of a monolithic piece. However, for powders, flakes, granules, porous snack pellets, puffed cereals, freeze-dried fruits, and porous bakery products, $\rho_t$ is **always greater** than the apparent and bulk densities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners expect students to clearly differentiate True Density, Bulk Density, and Apparent (Particle) Density, and to identify at least one standard method of measurement (Gas Pycnometer being the KTU-recommended method).

## 1.2 Laws of Friction — Formal KTU Definition

The classical description of dry friction between contacting surfaces is governed by **Amontons' Laws of Friction (1699)**, later formalized mathematically by **Coulomb (1785)**. These are collectively referred to in engineering mechanics as the **Laws of Dry Friction** or **Coulomb's Laws of Friction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coulomb's Law of Friction (Mathematical Form):**
> The limiting static frictional force ($F_s$) is directly proportional to the normal reaction ($N$) between the two contacting surfaces, and is independent of the apparent area of contact.

$$
F_s \leq \mu_s N
$$

Where $\mu_s$ is the **coefficient of static friction** (dimensionless).

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogies

### True Density — The Sponge Analogy

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** soaked in water. 
- If you weigh the *entire* sponge (water + sponge material + air inside the holes), you get one value.
- If you crush the sponge into a tiny, dense ball where **all the holes have vanished**, and weigh only the sponge fibers themselves, the volume used is *only* the fiber material's volume.
- That second, intrinsic value is the **True Density** — it is a property of the *material itself*, not the shape it is in.

For food powders (e.g., milk powder, coffee, sugar, salt), true density helps formulators understand how the material will pack, dissolve, and behave during mixing.

### Friction — The Shoe on the Floor Analogy

Picture yourself walking on a **tiled floor** versus **wet marble**.
- The harder you press your shoe down (more normal force), the more friction you feel.
- Whether your shoe is a *narrow stiletto* or a *wide sneaker* (different contact area) makes **no major difference** in friction (for hard surfaces) — only the normal force matters.
- The instant your shoe *starts to slip* (kinetic friction), the friction force suddenly *drops slightly* because $\mu_k < \mu_s$.

In food factories, friction determines whether grain will flow smoothly out of a **hopper**, whether chocolate will *stick* to or *release* from a mould, and how much power a **belt conveyor** needs to move sacks of flour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Key Terms to Memorize:**
> - **True Density** ($\rho_t$) — solid phase only.
> - **Apparent/Particle Density** ($\rho_p$) — particle including closed pores, excluding inter-particle voids.
> - **Bulk Density** ($\rho_b$) — includes inter-particle voids.
> - **Coefficient of Friction** ($\mu$) — dimensionless ratio of frictional to normal force.
> - **Angle of Friction** ($\phi$) — $\tan \phi = \mu$.
> - **Laws of Friction** — Coulomb's three laws (proportionality, independence of area, kinetic vs static).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Densities in Particulate Foods

For a powdery or granular food material, three densities exist in a strict hierarchy:

> [!NOTE]
> $$\rho_t \; > \; \rho_p \; > \; \rho_b$$
> (True density is the maximum; bulk density is the minimum.)

The reason is the successive inclusion of void spaces:

| Density Type | Volume Considered | Pores Inside Particle | Voids Between Particles |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **True Density** ($\rho_t$) | Solid matter only | Excluded | Excluded |
| **Particle / Apparent Density** ($\rho_p$) | Solid + closed pores | Included (if gas penetrates) | Excluded |
| **Bulk Density** ($\rho_b$) | Solid + closed pores + inter-particle voids | Included | Included |

## 2.2 Methods of Measuring True Density

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) emphasizes the following standard methods:

### (a) Gas (Helium) Pycnometer — Most Accurate
- Helium gas is used because its small atomic size penetrates **all open pores** down to atomic-scale voids.
- Volume displaced by the solid = volume calculated from pressure drop (Boyle's Law).
- The solid mass divided by this displaced volume = $\rho_t$.

### (b) Liquid Pycnometer / Displacement Method
- Uses a non-wetting, non-solvent liquid (e.g., toluene for fats, kerosene for powders).
- The volume of liquid displaced equals the volume of the solid.
- **Limitation:** Liquid cannot enter ultra-fine pores, so the volume is sometimes slightly over-estimated.

### (c) Pycnometer Equation (Standard Lab Formula)

$$
\rho_t = \frac{m_{sample}}{V_{pycnometer} - \frac{(m_{pyc+liquid+sample} - m_{pyc+liquid})}{\rho_{liquid}}}
$$

## 2.3 Porosity — The Companion Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Porosity ($\varepsilon$)** is the fraction of total bulk volume occupied by air/void space. KTU commonly tests the relationship between true and bulk density via porosity.

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\varepsilon \, (\%) = \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} \right) \times 100
$$

A high porosity indicates a light, fluffy product (e.g., puffed rice, aerated chocolate, freeze-dried strawberries). A low porosity indicates a dense product (e.g., compacted sugar cubes, hard cheese).

## 2.4 Laws of Friction — Expanded Analysis

The **three classical Coulomb's Laws of Friction** (universally tested in KTU) are:

1. **Law 1 — Proportionality:**  
   The limiting frictional force is directly proportional to the normal reaction between the surfaces in contact.  
   $$F \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \mu N$$

2. **Law 2 — Independence of Apparent Contact Area:**  
   For dry, rigid surfaces, the limiting frictional force is **independent of the apparent area of contact** — it depends only on the normal force.

3. **Law 3 — Kinetic Friction is Less than Limiting Static Friction:**  
   Once motion begins, kinetic friction $\mu_k N$ is **smaller** in magnitude than the maximum static friction $\mu_s N$.

$$
\mu_s > \mu_k
$$

## 2.5 Angle of Friction & Angle of Repose

The **angle of friction** ($\phi$) is the angle made by the resultant reaction force with the normal direction when limiting friction is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> $$\tan \phi = \mu$$

The **angle of repose** ($\theta$) is particularly important in food powder handling — it is the steepest angle at which a heap of granular material (e.g., flour, sugar, salt) remains stable without sliding.

For most food powders: $\theta \approx \phi$ (i.e., the angle of repose approximates the angle of internal friction).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | True Density | $\rho_t = m / V_{solid}$ | $m$ in kg, $V$ in m³ → kg/m³ | Excludes all pores & voids |
| 2 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = m / V_{bulk}$ | kg/m³ | Includes inter-particle voids |
| 3 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b / \rho_t$ | dimensionless (or ×100 for %) | $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Specific Volume | $v_t = 1 / \rho_t$ | m³/kg | Inverse of true density |
| 5 | Coulomb's Friction Law | $F = \mu N$ | $F$ in N, $N$ in N, $\mu$ dimensionless | $\mu_s > \mu_k$ |
| 6 | Limiting Static Friction | $F_s^{max} = \mu_s N$ | N | Block just begins to move |
| 7 | Kinetic Friction | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | N | Block in steady motion |
| 8 | Angle of Friction | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | $\phi$ in degrees/radians | $0^\circ \leq \phi < 90^\circ$ |
| 9 | Force on Inclined Plane (limiting) | $F = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | $W$ = weight (N) | $\alpha$ = plane inclination |
| 10 | Force to Hold on Inclined Plane | $F = W \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | N | When $\alpha > \phi$ and motion is impending upward |
| 11 | Angle of Repose (Powder) | $\tan \theta = h / r$ | $h$ = heap height, $r$ = heap radius | Empirical; equals internal friction angle |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of True Density | Application of Friction |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Powdered milk / coffee packaging** | Determines packaging volume & shelf stability | Hopper wall angle must exceed angle of repose for gravity discharge |
| **Conveyor belts in bakeries** | — | Belt friction coefficient determines required motor torque |
| **Cereal puffing / aeration** | Lower $\rho_t$ → more porous product quality check | Friction in extruder barrel affects throughput |
| **Silo design for grain storage** | Porosity controls aeration / drying rate | Wall friction governs lateral pressure on silo walls (Janssen's equation) |
| **Tabletting of food supplements** | True density needed for die-fill volume calculation | Die-wall friction affects ejection force |
| **Belt conveyors for sugar sacks** | — | Determines required drive power $P = F v = \mu N v$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship between Porosity, Bulk Density, and True Density

Let a particulate food sample occupy a total bulk volume $V_b$. This bulk volume comprises two parts:

$$
V_b = V_{solid} + V_{voids}
$$

Where $V_{voids}$ includes all air spaces inside the particles (open + closed pores) and the air gaps between particles.

The bulk density is therefore:

$$
\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_b} = \frac{m}{V_{solid} + V_{voids}}
$$

The true density uses only the solid volume:

$$
\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{solid}}
$$

By definition of porosity (fraction of bulk volume that is voids):

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{V_{voids}}{V_b}
$$

This gives us $V_{voids} = \varepsilon V_b$, and substituting back:

$$
V_{solid} = V_b - V_{voids} = V_b (1 - \varepsilon)
$$

Now compute $\rho_b / \rho_t$:

$$
\frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = \frac{m / V_b}{m / V_{solid}} = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_b} = \frac{V_b (1 - \varepsilon)}{V_b} = 1 - \varepsilon
$$

Rearranging for $\varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If $\rho_b = 0.4 \, g/cm^3$ and $\rho_t = 1.5 \, g/cm^3$, then $\varepsilon = 1 - 0.267 = 0.733$, i.e., **73.3% of the bulk volume is air** — a very porous, fluffy food (typical of puffed cereals).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Angle and Inclined Plane Limiting Condition

Consider a block of mass $m$ resting on an inclined plane that makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The block is on the verge of sliding down.

**Free body forces acting on the block:**

1. Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.
2. Normal reaction $N$ acting perpendicular to the inclined surface.
3. Limiting static frictional force $F_s$ acting up the plane (opposing impending motion).

**Step 1 — Resolve $W$ along and perpendicular to the inclined plane:**

Component of $W$ along the plane (down-slope): $W \sin \alpha$
Component of $W$ perpendicular to the plane (into surface): $W \cos \alpha$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium conditions perpendicular to the plane:**

$$
N = W \cos \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium condition along the plane (limiting case):**

$$
F_s = W \sin \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Coulomb's law of friction at the limit:**

$$
F_s = \mu_s N
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
W \sin \alpha = \mu_s (W \cos \alpha)
$$

Cancel $W$ from both sides:

$$
\sin \alpha = \mu_s \cos \alpha
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\mu_s$:**

$$
\mu_s = \frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha} = \tan \alpha
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the angle of friction:** When the block is just on the verge of sliding, the angle $\alpha$ at this instant is, by definition, the **angle of friction** $\phi$.

$$
\boxed{\tan \phi = \mu_s}
$$

This is a fundamental KTU result and is tested repeatedly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force Required to Impede Motion Up an Inclined Plane

This is the **classic KTU "Two-Block" derivation** that appears in nearly every university exam.

**Scenario:** A block of weight $W$ is being pulled up an inclined plane (inclination $\alpha$) by a horizontal force $P$. The coefficient of friction is $\mu$. Derive the expression for $P$ at the verge of motion.

**Step 1 — Resolve the applied horizontal force $P$ along and perpendicular to the plane:**

Component along plane (up-slope): $P \cos \alpha$
Component perpendicular to plane (into surface): $P \sin \alpha$

**Step 2 — Resolve the weight $W$ along and perpendicular to the plane:**

Component along plane (down-slope): $W \sin \alpha$
Component perpendicular to plane (into surface): $W \cos \alpha$

**Step 3 — Sum forces perpendicular to the plane (for equilibrium):**

The total normal reaction $N$ must balance all perpendicular components pushing into the surface:

$$
N = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Sum forces parallel to the plane (impending motion upward):**

Driving forces up the plane must overcome weight component down-slope and limiting friction (which acts down-slope, opposing motion):

$$
P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu N
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expression for $N$:**

$$
P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu (W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha)
$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$
P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha + \mu P \sin \alpha
$$

**Step 7 — Collect $P$ terms on the left:**

$$
P \cos \alpha - \mu P \sin \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha
$$

$$
P (\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha) = W (\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha)
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $P$:**

$$
\boxed{P = W \, \frac{\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha}{\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha}}
$$

**Step 9 — Express in tangent form (using $\tan \phi = \mu$, i.e., $\mu = \tan \phi$):**

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos \alpha$:

$$
P = W \cdot \frac{\tan \alpha + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha} = W \cdot \frac{\tan \alpha + \tan \phi}{1 - \tan \alpha \tan \phi}
$$

By the tangent addition formula $\tan(A + B) = \frac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}$:

$$
\boxed{P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)}
$$

This elegant closed form is the **KTU benchmark formula** for this derivation and carries 7–8 marks on its own.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — True Density & Porosity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A sample of instant coffee powder has a mass of **45 g**. When placed in a 100 mL gas pycnometer, the displaced helium gas volume reads **30 mL**. The same powder, when freely poured into a 250 mL graduated cylinder, occupies a volume of **150 mL**. Calculate the true density, bulk density, and porosity of the powder.

**Given:**
- $m = 45 \, g$
- $V_{solid} = 30 \, mL = 30 \, cm^3$
- $V_{bulk} = 150 \, mL = 150 \, cm^3$

**Step 1 — True Density:**

$$
\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{solid}} = \frac{45 \, g}{30 \, cm^3} = 1.5 \, g/cm^3
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk Density:**

$$
\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{45 \, g}{150 \, cm^3} = 0.30 \, g/cm^3
$$

**Step 3 — Porosity:**

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = 1 - \frac{0.30}{1.50} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80
$$

$$
\varepsilon \, (\%) = 0.80 \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The coffee powder is **80% air** by volume. This is typical for freeze-dried or spray-dried instant coffee, which is why a "heaped spoonful" feels so light. This high porosity is desirable because it gives **rapid solubility** in hot water.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Friction on Inclined Plane (Sacks on a Ramp)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 20 kg sack of flour rests on a wooden ramp inclined at $25^\circ$ to the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the sack and the ramp is $\mu_s = 0.45$. Will the sack slide? If yes, find the minimum force $P$ applied parallel to the ramp to keep it stationary.

**Step 1 — Identify the weight and limiting angle of friction:**

$$
W = mg = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \, N
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \tan^{-1}(0.45) = 24.23^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Compare inclination with angle of friction:**

Since $\alpha = 25^\circ > \phi = 24.23^\circ$, the sack **will slide down** when released.

**Step 3 — Minimum force $P$ parallel to the ramp (up-slope) to hold it stationary:**

At impending motion, friction acts **down-slope** (since block tends to slide down). Equilibrium along the plane:

$$
P + F_s = W \sin \alpha
$$

With $F_s = \mu_s N$ and $N = W \cos \alpha$:

$$
P + \mu_s W \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha
$$

$$
P = W \sin \alpha - \mu_s W \cos \alpha = W (\sin \alpha - \mu_s \cos \alpha)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\sin 25^\circ = 0.4226, \quad \cos 25^\circ = 0.9063
$$

$$
P = 196.2 \times (0.4226 - 0.45 \times 0.9063)
$$

$$
P = 196.2 \times (0.4226 - 0.4078) = 196.2 \times 0.0148
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 2.90 \, N}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Only ~3 N (the weight of a small apple) is required to hold a 20 kg sack in place — a critical safety insight for warehouse ramp design. If a worker leans on the sack or the ramp angle increases even slightly, the system becomes dangerously unstable.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — True Density Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Sample Intake] --> B[Pre-conditioning Oven Drying 105 C for 3 hr]
    B --> C[Weigh Sample on Analytical Balance]
    C --> D{Select Measurement Method}
    D --> E1[Gas Pycnometer Helium]
    D --> E2[Liquid Pycnometer Toluene]
    D --> E3[Mercury Displacement Porosimetry]
    E1 --> F[Compute True Density rho t equals m over V solid]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Validate rho t within range 0.3 to 1.8 g per cm cubed]
    G --> H[Store Result in Density Database]
    H --> I[Cross-check with Bulk Density]
    I --> J[Compute Porosity epsilon equals 1 minus rho b over rho t]
    J --> K[Final Quality Report to QA Lab]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Free-Body Diagram — Block on Inclined Plane (Friction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inclined Plane at angle alpha] --> B[Block of mass m on plane]
    B --> C1[Weight W acts vertically downward]
    B --> C2[Normal N perpendicular to plane]
    B --> C3[Friction Fs along plane opposing motion]
    B --> C4[Applied Force P if any]
    subgraph Equilibrium
        D1[Perpendicular to plane N equals W cos alpha]
        D2[Along plane Fs equals W sin alpha at limit]
    end
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2
    D1 --> E[Solve for Fs equals mu N]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Tan phi equals mu s equals tan alpha at limit]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Logic — Will a Food Block Slide?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Plane Angle alpha and Surface mu s] --> B[Compute Angle of Friction phi equals arctan mu s]
    B --> C{Compare alpha and phi}
    C -->|alpha less than phi| D[Block remains STATIONARY Self-locking]
    C -->|alpha equals phi| E[Block is at LIMITING EQUILIBRIUM On the verge of motion]
    C -->|alpha greater than phi| F[Block SLIDES DOWN External force required to hold]
    D --> G[No action needed]
    E --> H[Infinitesimal force disturbs the system]
    F --> I[Compute required force P equals W sin alpha minus mu W cos alpha]
    I --> J[Output to design specification]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Density Hierarchy in Food Powders

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Total Bulk Volume V bulk] --> A2[Solid Volume V solid]
    A1 --> A3[Void Volume V voids]
    A2 --> B1[True Density rho t equals m over V solid]
    A3 --> B2[Porosity epsilon equals V voids over V bulk]
    A1 --> B3[Bulk Density rho b equals m over V bulk]
    A2 --> B4[Particle Density rho p equals m over V solid plus closed pores]
    B1 --> C1[Reference Property for Material]
    B2 --> C2[Quality Indicator for Puffed Foods]
    B3 --> C3[Packaging and Storage Volume]
    B4 --> C4[Intermediate for Porous Materials]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram walks you through a real food-industry protocol for measuring true density. The second represents the free-body analysis (mathematical model, not a physical picture). The third is a quick decision tool used by warehouse and process engineers. The fourth maps the conceptual hierarchy of the three densities.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Define true density and bulk density. How are they different from each other? Give one example where the difference is significant.**

**Model Answer:**

True density ($\rho_t$) is the mass of a food material per unit volume of its solid phase only, with all pores and inter-particle voids completely excluded. Bulk density ($\rho_b$) is the mass per unit volume of the material when loosely packed in a container, including all the air spaces between particles.

For a non-porous, monolithic material (e.g., a slab of butter, a piece of cheese), the two values are nearly identical. However, for a powder such as **spray-dried milk powder**, true density is around $1.30 \, g/cm^3$ while bulk density is only $0.45 \, g/cm^3$ — a three-fold difference due to the high porosity of the powder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point:** True density is an intrinsic material property; bulk density is a packing-dependent property.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **State Coulomb's laws of dry friction.**

**Model Answer:**

The three laws of dry friction (Amontons' Laws, formalized by Coulomb) are:

1. **First Law (Proportionality):** The limiting frictional force between two dry surfaces is directly proportional to the normal reaction between them, i.e., $F = \mu N$.

2. **Second Law (Independence of Area):** For dry and rigid surfaces, the limiting frictional force is independent of the apparent area of contact; it depends only on the normal load.

3. **Third Law (Direction & Magnitude of Kinetic Friction):** Kinetic friction is independent of the relative velocity of sliding, and is always less than the maximum static friction, i.e., $\mu_k < \mu_s$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — True Density & Powder Porosity

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice A | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define true density, bulk density, and porosity of a food powder. Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **gas pycnometer** for true density measurement.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A free-flowing food powder has a true density of $1.45 \, g/cm^3$ and a bulk density of $0.58 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate its **porosity** and discuss the practical significance of this porosity in food packaging and dissolution behaviour.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **True Density** ($\rho_t$): Mass per unit volume of the actual solid material, with all pores and inter-particle voids excluded. Units: $g/cm^3$ or $kg/m^3$.

- **Bulk Density** ($\rho_b$): Mass per unit volume of the material in its loosely packed state, including inter-particle voids. Units: same as $\rho_t$.

- **Porosity** ($\varepsilon$): Volume fraction of voids in the bulk material; dimensionless or expressed as a percentage.

**Gas Pycnometer — Working Principle (4 marks):**

A gas pycnometer (typically using **helium gas**) measures the true volume of a solid by exploiting Boyle's Law ($PV = \text{constant}$ at constant temperature).

- **Step 1:** A precisely known mass of the food sample is placed in the sample chamber of known volume $V_s$.
- **Step 2:** Helium is admitted to a reference chamber of volume $V_r$ at pressure $P_1$.
- **Step 3:** The valve between the chambers is opened; helium expands into the sample chamber. The equilibrium pressure $P_2$ is recorded.
- **Step 4:** By Boyle's Law:
$$
P_1 V_r = P_2 (V_r + V_s - V_{sample})
$$
- **Step 5:** Solving for $V_{sample}$:
$$
V_{sample} = V_r + V_s - \frac{P_1 V_r}{P_2}
$$
- **Step 6:** True density:
$$
\rho_t = \frac{m_{sample}}{V_{sample}}
$$

Helium is preferred because its small atomic size penetrates the finest open pores, giving the closest estimate of the *true* solid volume.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$
\rho_t = 1.45 \, g/cm^3, \quad \rho_b = 0.58 \, g/cm^3
$$

**Step 2 — Porosity Formula:**

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation:**

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{0.58}{1.45} = 1 - 0.4000 = 0.6000
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 60\%
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Significance (4 marks):**

- **Packaging:** A 60% porous powder occupies far more volume per unit mass than its true solid would suggest. Packaging machines must be sized accordingly; 1 kg of such a powder requires about $1 / 0.58 = 1.72 \, L$ of container space, not $1 / 1.45 = 0.69 \, L$.

- **Dissolution Behaviour:** High porosity means water can rapidly penetrate the powder particles, leading to **instant solubility** — a desirable trait for products like instant coffee, cocoa, soup mixes, and milk powder.

- **Storage Stability:** Highly porous powders also adsorb more moisture and oxygen, so packaging under nitrogen or in moisture-barrier laminates is essential.

- **Flow Properties:** Very high porosity often correlates with poor flowability; anti-caking agents may be required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse bulk density with particle density. Remember: **particle density includes closed pores, bulk density includes ALL voids**.
> - Forgetting to convert units (e.g., mixing $g/cm^3$ with $kg/m^3$) is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> - In the gas pycnometer explanation, do **not** write "measures volume of gas" — write "measures the volume of solid by gas displacement using Boyle's Law".

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Laws of Friction & Inclined Plane

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice B | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] State and explain Coulomb's laws of dry friction. Derive the relationship between the angle of friction $\phi$ and the coefficient of static friction $\mu_s$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A wooden crate of mass **50 kg** is pulled up an inclined plane at $30^\circ$ to the horizontal by a **horizontal force $P$**. The coefficient of friction between the crate and the plane is $\mu = 0.25$. Find the magnitude of $P$ required to just move the crate up the plane. Use $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Coulomb's Laws of Friction (3 marks):** (As stated in Question 2 above.)

**Derivation of $\tan \phi = \mu_s$ (4 marks):**

Consider a block of weight $W$ in **limiting equilibrium** on a plane inclined at angle $\alpha$.

Forces acting:
- $W$ vertically downward
- $N$ normal to the inclined surface
- $F_s$ up the plane (limiting static friction)

Resolving forces:

$$
N = W \cos \alpha
$$

$$
F_s = W \sin \alpha
$$

By Coulomb's first law at limiting equilibrium:

$$
F_s = \mu_s N
$$

Substituting:

$$
W \sin \alpha = \mu_s W \cos \alpha
$$

Cancelling $W$ and rearranging:

$$
\mu_s = \frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha} = \tan \alpha
$$

At the **limiting angle of inclination**, $\alpha$ is, by definition, the **angle of friction** $\phi$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mu_s = \tan \phi}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $m = 50 \, kg$, $W = mg = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5 \, N$
- $\alpha = 30^\circ$
- $\mu = 0.25$
- Horizontal force $P$ (to be found)

**Step 1 — Resolve $P$ and $W$ along the plane:**

| Force | Component along plane (up-slope) | Component perpendicular to plane (into surface) |
|-------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| $P$ (horizontal) | $P \cos \alpha$ | $P \sin \alpha$ |
| $W$ (vertical) | $W \sin \alpha$ (down-slope) | $W \cos \alpha$ |

**Step 2 — Equilibrium perpendicular to the plane:**

$$
N = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium along the plane at impending motion (crate about to move up):**

The crate is about to move **up** the plane, so friction acts **down the plane** to oppose impending motion.

$$
P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu N
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N$:**

$$
P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu (W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $P$ terms:**

$$
P \cos \alpha - \mu P \sin \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha
$$

$$
P (\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha) = W (\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $P$:**

$$
P = W \cdot \frac{\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha}{\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\sin 30^\circ = 0.5, \quad \cos 30^\circ = 0.8660
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.5 + 0.25 \times 0.8660 = 0.5 + 0.2165 = 0.7165
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.8660 - 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.8660 - 0.125 = 0.7410
$$

$$
P = 490.5 \times \frac{0.7165}{0.7410} = 490.5 \times 0.9670
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 474.3 \, N}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification using $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ form:**

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^\circ
$$

$$
\alpha + \phi = 30^\circ + 14.04^\circ = 44.04^\circ
$$

$$
P = W \tan(44.04^\circ) = 490.5 \times 0.9670 \approx 474.3 \, N \quad \checkmark
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A horizontal pull of ~474 N is required to start moving a 490.5 N crate up a 30° ramp. This is less than the weight of the crate, demonstrating how an inclined plane reduces the effective effort needed for lifting — a fundamental principle in conveyor and ramp design in food factories.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Friction Problems:**
> - **Direction of friction is the #1 mistake.** Always check the *impending direction of motion* and place friction **opposite** to it. In this problem, the crate is being pulled *up*, so friction acts *down* the plane.
> - **Do not forget the perpendicular component of the horizontal force** $P$. Many students write $N = W \cos \alpha$ alone, missing the $P \sin \alpha$ term — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - **Always state which law you are applying** (Coulomb's first / second / third) for full marks on derivations.
> - When $\alpha + \phi$ exceeds $90^\circ$, the formula $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ gives an infinite or negative value — recognize this as the **self-locking** condition (block cannot be pushed up no matter how large the force is). Such edge cases are bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: True Density & Laws of Friction**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- $\rho_t = m / V_{solid}$ — **true density**, pores excluded.
- $\rho_b = m / V_{bulk}$ — **bulk density**, all voids included.
- $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b / \rho_t$ — **porosity**, dimensionless.
- $F = \mu N$ — **Coulomb's friction law**, $\mu$ dimensionless.
- $\mu_s > \mu_k$ — static > kinetic.
- $\tan \phi = \mu$ — **angle of friction relationship**.
- $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — pulling force up an inclined plane (horizontal pull).
- $P = W \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ — holding force when motion is impending *downward* and force is applied *parallel* to the plane.

### Standard Methods (KTU-Favorite)
- **True density:** Gas (Helium) Pycnometer, Liquid Pycnometer, Mercury Displacement.
- **Bulk density:** Scott Volumeter, Graduated Cylinder Tap Method, ASTM D7481.
- **Porosity:** Computed from $\rho_t$ and $\rho_b$, or directly from image analysis (cross-section).

### Critical Engineering Applications in Food Technology
- **Hopper & silo design:** Wall friction angle + angle of repose determine minimum hopper slope for mass flow.
- **Powder packaging:** True density for quality check; bulk density for package volume calculation.
- **Belt conveyors:** $\mu$ between belt and product dictates required drive power.
- **Tabletting:** Die-wall friction affects ejection force and tablet hardness.
- **Extrusion:** Friction in barrel affects SME (Specific Mechanical Energy) input.
- **Aerated products:** Porosity correlates with crispness, dissolution rate, and crunch perception.

### Numerical Problem Triggers to Watch For
- A question mentioning **"powder" + "two different volumes"** → true density, bulk density, porosity.
- A question mentioning **"block on inclined plane" + "limiting equilibrium"** → angle of friction derivation.
- A question mentioning **"horizontal force" + "inclined plane"** → use $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$.
- A question mentioning **"angle of repose" + "heap"** → $\tan \theta = h / r$, equal to internal friction angle.

### Common Examiner Traps
1. Mixing up particle density and bulk density.
2. Forgetting to convert $cm^3 \leftrightarrow m^3$ when SI units are required.
3. Wrong direction of friction force in inclined plane problems.
4. Missing the $P \sin \alpha$ term in horizontal-pull inclined-plane problems.
5. Using $\mu_k$ when the problem specifies *just begins to move* (use $\mu_s$).
6. Confusing *angle of friction* with *angle of repose* — they are equal only for cohesionless, dry granular materials.
7. Reporting porosity as a number >1 (must always lie in $[0, 1]$).

### One-Line Memory Hooks
- **"True density is the truth — the real, dense, no-pore truth."**
- **"Coulomb said: Force = mu times Normal — that's the law."**
- **"Inclined plane with horizontal pull? Remember: tan(alpha plus phi)."**
- **"Porosity = 1 minus bulk over true — keep it under 1."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, examiners allocate ~3 marks for diagrams or free-body sketches and ~3 marks for stating assumptions and the relevant law. Always **state the law** (e.g., "Applying Coulomb's law of friction: $F = \mu N$") before plugging in values. This single habit can recover 1–2 marks per answer.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **food processing machinery and tribology**, every operation involving size reduction, mixing, conveying, extrusion, pumping, or packaging involves **contacting surfaces in relative motion**. The performance, efficiency, energy consumption, contamination risk, and service life of such equipment are dictated by the **frictional and wear behaviour** at these contacts.

**Tribology** is the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion, encompassing the study of **friction, wear, and lubrication**. The four classical independent variables governing tribological response are:

1. **Load (Normal Force, $W$ or $N$):** The force pressing the two bodies together per unit area.
2. **Properties of the contacting bodies:** Hardness, elastic modulus, surface roughness, microstructure, and chemical affinity.
3. **Sliding velocity ($v$):** The relative tangential speed between the two surfaces.
4. **Contact surface temperature ($T_s$):** The temperature reached at the real (asperity) contact zone, often far above the bulk temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of GDFTT104 requires the student to *qualitatively and quantitatively* explain how the above four parameters influence the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** and the **wear rate ($W_r$)** in food machinery contacts (e.g., stainless-steel rolls in roller mills, screw–barrel interfaces in extruders, blade–hopper contacts in grinders, conveyor belt–pulley contacts).

## 1.2 The Coefficient of Friction — A Refresher

By **Amontons' First Law of Friction (1699)**:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot W$$

where:
- $F_f$ = frictional force resisting motion (in **N**)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $W$ = normal load pressing the surfaces together (in **N**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Amontons' Second Law** states that $\mu$ is **independent of the apparent (nominal) area of contact**. This is paradoxical on a macroscopic scale but is explained by the **asperity-contact theory**: real contact occurs only at microscopic peaks, and the *real* contact area is proportional to the normal load.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sled-on-Snow Intuition

Imagine dragging a wooden **sled over snow**.

- **If you load the sled with bricks (load ↑):** The sled digs deeper, the rope pulls harder, and the wear on the runners increases. This is the *effect of load*.
- **If you replace the wooden runners with Teflon-coated runners (property change):** The rope pulls much more easily. This is the *effect of properties of contacting bodies*.
- **If you drag faster (velocity ↑):** The snow melts momentarily under the runners, creating a thin water film — the friction drops sharply, then rises again as you slide even faster. This is the *effect of sliding velocity* (the Stribeck effect).
- **If you drag for a long time (temperature ↑):** The runners get warm, the snow layer becomes slushy, the contact zone softens. This is the *effect of contact surface temperature*.

In a **food processing context**, the same four factors govern:
- Power drawn by a hammer mill,
- Barrel wear in a cooking extruder,
- Frictional heat in dough kneading,
- Energy lost in food pumping through pipelines,
- Contamination of food by metallic wear debris.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Load | $W$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Contact (Nominal) Pressure | $p$ | **Pa or MPa** |
| Sliding Velocity | $v$ | **m/s** |
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | dimensionless |
| Wear Rate | $W_r$ | **mm³/N·m** or **mm³/m** |
| Hardness | $H$ | **HV, HB, or Pa** |
| PV Product | $pv$ | **MPa · m/s** |
| Contact Surface (Flash) Temperature | $T_f$ | **°C or K** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Archard's Wear Constant ($K$)** is **dimensionless** and typically lies between $10^{-2}$ (severe wear) and $10^{-6}$ (mild wear). For food-grade stainless-steel contacts under boundary lubrication, $K \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Normal Load ($W$)

When two surfaces are pressed together, only the tips of asperities (microscopic peaks) carry the load. The **real area of contact** $A_r$ grows linearly with the normal load:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

where $H$ is the **hardness of the softer material** (in Pa).

The **frictional force** is the shear strength $\tau$ of the junction times the real contact area:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A_r = \tau \cdot \frac{W}{H}$$

Therefore:

$$\mu = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{\tau}{H}$$

### Key Load–Friction–Wear Relationships

- **Friction force** is **directly proportional** to the normal load (Amontons' Law). Doubling the load doubles the friction force, *but* the *coefficient* $\mu$ remains roughly constant.
- **Wear volume** is **directly proportional** to the normal load (Archard, 1953):

  $$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

  where $L$ is the sliding distance (in m) and $K$ is the dimensionless wear coefficient.
- **Wear rate** (volume per unit sliding distance):

  $$W_r = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{K \cdot W}{H}$$

- **Critical Load Failure:** Beyond a threshold contact pressure, the surface undergoes **plastic deformation, scuffing, or seizure**. In food extruders, exceeding the **PV-limit** of the barrel liner causes catastrophic wear and product contamination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** In food processing, increasing the throughput by raising the normal load (e.g., higher roll gap force in a roller mill) **quadruples the power loss** if friction is the dominant resistance, and **doubles the wear rate** — directly reducing equipment life and increasing metallic contamination of the food product.

## 2.2 Effect of Properties of the Contacting Bodies

The tribological response depends on the following material properties:

### 2.2.1 Hardness ($H$)
- Harder surfaces produce **smaller real contact area** for the same load → **lower friction and lower wear**.
- In Archard's equation, wear is **inversely proportional** to hardness. Doubling the surface hardness halves the wear volume.
- For food machinery, **hard-chrome plated** or **nitrided steel** rolls are used to resist abrasive wear from food particles.

### 2.2.2 Elastic Modulus ($E$)
- A higher elastic modulus → less deformation under load → smaller real contact area (combined effect via $H/E$).
- The **plasticity index** $\psi$ (Greenwood & Williamson, 1966) determines whether contact is elastic or plastic:

  $$\psi = \frac{E^*}{H} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^*}{r^*}}$$

  where $E^*$ is the reduced elastic modulus, $\sigma^*$ is the composite RMS roughness, and $r^*$ is the composite mean asperity radius.
- If $\psi < 0.6$, contact is mostly **elastic**; if $\psi > 1$, contact is **plastic** and wear is severe.

### 2.2.3 Surface Roughness ($R_a$)
- For **smooth surfaces** ($R_a < 0.1\,\mu m$), contact is largely elastic and friction is low.
- For **rough surfaces** ($R_a > 1\,\mu m$), asperity interlocking dominates and friction is high.
- Surface roughness has an *optimum*: too smooth causes high adhesion; too rough causes high ploughing. This optimum is the **minimum of the Stribeck curve**.

### 2.2.4 Chemical Affinity / Compatibility
- Similar metals (e.g., steel-on-steel) tend to **cold-weld** (adhere) → high adhesion, galling.
- Dissimilar pairs (e.g., steel-on-bronze) form weaker junctions → lower friction.
- In food applications, **stainless steel 304/316** sliding against **food-grade polymer** (UHMWPE, PTFE) is common for hygienic, low-wear contacts.

### 2.2.5 Thermal Conductivity ($k$) and Specific Heat ($c_p$)
- Materials with high $k$ and $c_p$ can **dissipate frictional heat** faster, limiting flash-temperature rise.
- Polymers (used as bushings in mixers) have low $k$ → prone to thermal failure.

## 2.3 Effect of Sliding Velocity ($v$)

The sliding velocity affects friction through **three simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Stribeck Effect (Hydrodynamic Lubrication Entry):** As velocity increases from zero, a fluid film begins to build up at the interface, partially separating the asperities. Friction first **decreases**.
2. **Viscous Shearing:** At higher velocity, the lubricant's viscous resistance grows, so friction **increases**.
3. **Flash Temperature Rise:** At still higher velocity, surface temperature rises sharply, reducing lubricant viscosity and possibly causing surface oxidation or softening, causing friction to **decrease again** or fluctuate.

The classical representation is the **Stribeck Curve** (1902):

$$\mu = f\!\left(\frac{\eta \cdot v}{p}\right)$$

where $\eta$ is the dynamic viscosity of the lubricant, $v$ is the sliding speed, and $p$ is the mean contact pressure.

| Regime | Friction Trend | Governing Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** (low $\eta v / p$) | High, $\mu \approx 0.1$–$0.4$ | Asperity–asperity contact |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | Decreasing | Partial fluid film, partial asperity contact |
| **Hydrodynamic / Full-Film** | Lowest, $\mu \approx 0.001$–$0.01$ | Complete fluid film separation |
| **High-Speed / Thermal Failure** | Rises, then becomes erratic | Flash temperature, lubricant breakdown |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Engineering Insight:** In **high-shear mixers and extruders**, increasing screw RPM (sliding velocity) shifts the metal–polymer contact from boundary to mixed lubrication. This *reduces* friction at first, but the **barrel wall temperature** rises by tens of degrees, cooking the food prematurely and reducing viscosity — which then *further* shifts the lubrication regime. This coupling is central to extruder design.

### Wear–Velocity Relationship

For **sliding wear** in the boundary regime, the wear rate is generally **proportional to velocity**:

$$W_r \propto v$$

However, in the **oxidative wear regime**, the wear rate may actually *decrease* with velocity because oxide films form faster than they are removed.

## 2.4 Effect of Contact Surface Temperature ($T_s$)

Frictional energy dissipated as heat at asperity junctions raises the local temperature — called the **flash temperature** $T_f$. **Bloch (1937)** derived the classic flash-temperature formula for a circular asperity contact:

$$T_f = T_b + \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot v}{4 \cdot r_c \cdot k_1 + 4 \cdot r_c \cdot k_2}$$

Simplified form (Jaeger, 1942) for a moving heat source on a semi-infinite body:

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu \cdot p \cdot v \cdot \sqrt{\pi \cdot r_c \cdot a / v}}{2 \cdot k}$$

where:
- $T_b$ = bulk (ambient) temperature
- $r_c$ = radius of real contact spot
- $k_1, k_2$ = thermal conductivities of the two bodies
- $a$ = thermal diffusivity of the contact material

### Effects of Rising Contact Temperature

1. **Lubricant viscosity drops** → thinner fluid film → regime shifts from hydrodynamic → mixed → boundary.
2. **Surface hardness drops** → larger real contact area → friction and wear both rise.
3. **Thermal expansion** → tighter clearances → risk of seizure in bearings and screw–barrel gaps.
4. **Phase changes in food product** — protein denaturation, starch gelatinization, sugar caramelization — affecting both product quality and machine behaviour.
5. **Oxidative wear acceleration** — oxide layer growth rate $r_{ox} \propto \exp(-E_a / R T)$.
6. **Material softening / phase change** — polymers (used in food-grade bushings) lose strength rapidly above their glass-transition temperature $T_g$.

### The PV-Limit Concept

Every bearing or sliding contact material has a maximum allowable **PV product** (pressure × velocity), beyond which thermal runaway occurs:

$$PV_{limit} = p_{max} \cdot v_{max}$$

For food-grade PTFE on steel: $PV_{limit} \approx 0.5\,\text{MPa} \cdot \text{m/s}$.
For oil-lubricated steel-on-bronze: $PV_{limit} \approx 100\,\text{MPa} \cdot \text{m/s}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often confuse **flash temperature** (the transient, microscopic, millisecond-scale temperature at a single asperity junction) with **bulk temperature** (the macroscopic, steady-state temperature of the bulk material). The two can differ by **hundreds of degrees Celsius**.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_f = \mu W$ | Amontons' Law of Friction | N |
| 2 | $\mu = \tau / H$ | Friction coefficient = shear strength / hardness | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_r = W / H$ | Real contact area (plastic contact) | m² |
| 4 | $V = K W L / H$ | Archard's Wear Equation | m³ |
| 5 | $W_r = K W / H$ | Wear rate (volumetric) | m³/m |

| 7 | $T_f - T_b = \mu p v \sqrt{\pi r_c a / v} / (2k)$ | Jaeger's Flash Temperature Rise | K |
| 8 | $PV \le PV_{limit}$ | Thermal-runaway criterion | MPa·m/s |
| 9 | $\psi = (E^*/H)\sqrt{\sigma^*/r^*}$ | Greenwood–Williamson plasticity index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W_{friction} = \mu W v$ | Frictional Power Dissipation | W |
| 11 | $Q_{heat} = \mu W v$ | Heat generated by sliding | W |
| 12 | $\Delta T = Q_{heat} / (m c_p)$ | Bulk temperature rise | K |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Technology

| Food Equipment | Key Tribological Pair | Dominant Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Roller Mill** (flour, spices) | Hard-chrome steel roll × roll | Load, surface roughness |
| **Hammer Mill** | Steel hammer × screen | Velocity, impact load |
| **Screw Extruder** | Steel screw × barrel liner | Velocity, temperature, PV-limit |
| **Dough Kneader** | Stainless bowl × helical arm | Load (dough mass), velocity |
| **Belt Conveyor** | Rubber belt × steel pulley | Load, belt property |
| **Centrifugal Pump** | Stainless impeller × casing | Velocity, hydrodynamic regime |
| **Piston Filler** | UHMWPE seal × piston | Velocity, surface temperature |
| **Rolling Pin / Sheeter** | Polymer roll × steel | Pressure, sliding speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit Insight:** In a typical food processing plant, **frictional losses account for 8–15 % of total energy input**. Tribology-driven design improvements can save millions of units of electricity annually in a large plant.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Archard's Wear Equation

**Starting Premise:** Wear volume is proportional to the **real contact area** and the **sliding distance**.

**Step 1 — Real contact area in plastic deformation regime:**

When asperities deform plastically, the real area of contact $A_r$ equals the load divided by the hardness of the softer material.

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of asperity producing a wear particle:**

Not every asperity encounter produces a detached wear particle. Let $K$ be the dimensionless probability (and shape factor) of a wear particle forming — the **Archard wear coefficient**.

$$dV = K \cdot A_r \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A_r$:**

$$dV = K \cdot \frac{W}{H} \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the sliding distance $L$:**

$$\int_0^V dV = \int_0^L \frac{K W}{H} dx$$

$$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

**Step 5 — Wear rate (per unit sliding distance):**

$$W_r = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{K \cdot W}{H}$$

**Step 6 — Dimensionless form using dimensionless wear coefficient:**

In the literature, the **specific wear rate** $k$ is often expressed in $\text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}$:

$$k = \frac{V}{W \cdot L}$$

For metals in boundary lubrication, $k$ typically lies between $10^{-6}$ and $10^{-2}\,\text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **gold standard** in tribology. KTU examiners frequently ask students to state and apply Archard's equation, often in the context of a food-machinery wear problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flash Temperature (Simplified Jaeger Model)

**Starting Premise:** A circular heat source of radius $r_c$ moves with velocity $v$ over a semi-infinite solid. The source dissipates heat at rate $q = \mu W v$ per unit time.

**Step 1 — Total heat dissipated per unit time:**

$$\dot{Q} = \mu \cdot W \cdot v \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

**Step 2 — Heat flux at the contact (uniformly distributed over area $\pi r_c^2$):**

$$q = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\pi r_c^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — For a moving point source on a semi-infinite body, the steady-state surface temperature rise is (Jaeger, 1942):**

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{q \cdot r_c}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $q = \dot{Q}/(\pi r_c^2)$:**

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\pi r_c^2} \cdot \frac{r_c}{k \sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{r_c \cdot k \cdot \pi^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\dot{Q} = \mu W v$ and combine constants into a numerical factor $C$ (experimentally $C \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ for circular contacts):**

$$\Delta T_f = C \cdot \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot v}{r_c \cdot k}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (engineering use):**

$$\boxed{\Delta T_f \approx \frac{\mu \cdot p \cdot v \cdot \sqrt{r_c}}{2 \cdot k}}$$

where $p = W / A_n$ is the nominal contact pressure and $A_n$ is the nominal (apparent) contact area.

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**
- Flash temperature is **proportional to** $\mu$, $p$, and $v$.
- Flash temperature is **inversely proportional to** thermal conductivity $k$.
- This is why **brass and copper** (high $k$) contacts run cooler than **stainless steel** (low $k$) contacts at the same PV.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power and Heat from Sliding Friction

**Step 1 — Power dissipated as friction:**

$$P_f = F_f \cdot v = (\mu W) \cdot v = \mu W v \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 2 — All frictional power (in the absence of other dissipation) is converted to heat:**

$$\dot{Q}_{friction} = P_f = \mu W v \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 3 — Bulk temperature rise of the contacting body (mass $m$, specific heat $c_p$):**

$$\Delta T_{bulk} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{friction} \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{\mu W v \cdot t}{m c_p} \quad \text{[K]}$$

**Step 4 — In steady state, the heat generated equals the heat removed by conduction, convection, and radiation:**

$$\mu W v = h A_s (T_s - T_\infty) + \epsilon \sigma A_s (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient, $A_s$ is the surface area, $\epsilon$ is emissivity, and $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant.

**Step 5 — Solving for the steady-state bulk temperature $T_s$:**

$$T_s = T_\infty + \frac{\mu W v}{h A_s} \quad \text{(convection-dominated, neglecting radiation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the basis for designing **cooling jackets** on extruder barrels and ball-mill trunnions in food processing.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Coupling of Velocity and Temperature in the Stribeck Curve

**Step 1 — Define the dimensionless Hersey number:**

$$H_e = \frac{\eta \cdot v}{p} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

**Step 2 — Friction coefficient as a function of Hersey number (one common analytical form, the $\gamma$-form):**

$$\mu(H_e) = \mu_{bd} \cdot H_e^{-\gamma} + \mu_{hd} \cdot (1 - e^{-\alpha H_e})$$

where:
- $\mu_{bd}$ = boundary-lubrication friction component
- $\mu_{hd}$ = hydrodynamic-lubrication friction component
- $\gamma$ = typically 0.5 to 0.7
- $\alpha$ = a fitting constant

**Step 3 — Differentiate to find the minimum (transition velocity $v^*$):**

$$\frac{d\mu}{dH_e} = -\gamma \mu_{bd} H_e^{-(\gamma+1)} + \alpha \mu_{hd} e^{-\alpha H_e} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve numerically for $H_e^*$:**

$$H_e^* = \left(\frac{\gamma \mu_{bd}}{\alpha \mu_{hd} e^{-\alpha H_e^*}}\right)^{1/(\gamma+1)}$$

**Step 5 — Express the transition sliding velocity:**

$$v^* = \frac{p \cdot H_e^*}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- $v^*$ increases with load $p$ (heavier-loaded contacts need more speed to enter full-film lubrication).
- $v^*$ decreases with viscosity $\eta$ (thicker oils achieve full-film lubrication at lower speeds).
- This is why **high-viscosity food-grade lubricants** (e.g., food-grade silicone oils) are used in slow-moving sugar or chocolate mixers.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A food-grade stainless-steel roll (AISI 304) slides against a hard-chrome plated steel roll under a normal load of $W = 2000$ N and a sliding speed of $v = 2$ m/s. The real contact spot has radius $r_c = 50\,\mu\text{m}$. Hardness of the chrome layer $H = 8$ GPa. Archard's wear coefficient $K = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. Thermal conductivity of steel $k = 16$ W/m·K. Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.3$.

**Find:**
- (a) Real area of contact.
- (b) Wear volume per metre of sliding.
- (c) Flash temperature rise.
- (d) Comment on the result.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Real area of contact:**

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{2000\,\text{N}}{8 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2 = 0.25\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Part (b) — Wear volume per metre of sliding:**

Archard's equation:

$$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 2000 \cdot 1}{8 \times 10^9}$$

$$V = 1.25 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^3/\text{m} = 0.125\,\text{mm}^3/\text{m}$$

**Part (c) — Flash temperature rise:**

Using the simplified Jaeger equation:

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot v}{r_c \cdot k} = \frac{0.3 \cdot 2000 \cdot 2}{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 16}$$

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{1200}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{K}$$

This value is **unphysically high** because the simple formula overestimates; the more accurate Jaeger form gives:

$$\Delta T_f = C \cdot \frac{\mu \cdot p \cdot v \cdot \sqrt{r_c}}{2 k}$$

With nominal contact pressure $p = W / A_n$ (assuming nominal area $A_n = 100\,\text{mm}^2 = 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$):

$$p = \frac{2000}{10^{-4}} = 2 \times 10^7\,\text{Pa} = 20\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.3 \cdot 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 \cdot \sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6}}}{2 \cdot 16}$$

$$\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.07 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.3 \cdot 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 \cdot 7.07 \times 10^{-3}}{32}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 0.5 \cdot \frac{84{,}840}{32} = 0.5 \cdot 2651 = 1326\,\text{K}$$

Adding ambient (say $T_b = 25\,°\text{C}$):

$$T_f = 25 + 1326 \approx 1351\,°\text{C}$$

**Part (d) — Comment:**
- The flash temperature is **above the melting point of AISI 304 stainless steel** (≈ $1400\,°\text{C}$).
- This indicates that under these conditions, the contact is in the **severe wear / scuffing regime**.
- In practice, the contact would either be **lubricated** (reducing $\mu$ to $\approx 0.05$) or run at a **lower load/speed**, otherwise the rolls would fail rapidly.
- With lubrication $\mu = 0.05$:

  $$\Delta T_f \approx 221\,°\text{C}$$

  This is still high but acceptable for short-duration asperity flashes (the bulk material remains cool).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Causal Flow: Variables → Tribological Response

This diagram maps how the four key variables feed into friction, wear, and lubrication regime in a food-machinery contact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs from Food Machinery Operation] --> B[Normal Load W]
    A --> C[Material Properties H, E, Ra]
    A --> D[Sliding Velocity v]
    A --> E[Ambient / Bulk Temperature Tb]

    B --> F[Real Contact Area Ar = W / H]
    C --> F
    C --> G[Plasticity Index psi]
    F --> H[Friction Force Ff = mu W]
    F --> I[Wear Volume V = KWL / H]

    D --> J[Stribeck Number He = eta v / p]
    E --> K[Viscosity eta drops with T]
    D --> K
    K --> J
    J --> L{Lubrication Regime}
    L -->|He low| M[Boundary Lubrication]
    L -->|He medium| N[Mixed Lubrication]
    L -->|He high| O[Hydrodynamic Lubrication]

    D --> P[Flash Temperature Tf]
    H --> P
    E --> P
    P --> Q[Surface Softening]
    P --> R[Oxidative Wear]
    P --> S[Lubricant Breakdown]

    M --> T[High mu, High Wear]
    N --> U[Moderate mu, Moderate Wear]
    O --> V[Low mu, Low Wear]

    T --> W[Final Tribological Response]
    U --> W
    V --> W
    Q --> W
    R --> W
    S --> W

    W --> X[Equipment Life]
    W --> Y[Energy Efficiency]
    W --> Z[Food Product Contamination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The four input variables (load, material, velocity, temperature) all converge on the friction/wear response, but they are **coupled** — for example, velocity affects temperature, which in turn affects viscosity, which then affects the lubrication regime. This is the heart of tribological complexity.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Stribeck Curve (Schematic)

A schematic representation of how $\mu$ varies with the Hersey number $\eta v / p$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: At Rest v=0] --> B[B Boundary Lubrication]
    B --> C[Mixed Lubrication]
    C --> D[H Hydrodynamic Full Film]
    D --> E[Thermal Runaway Region]

    subgraph ZONES
        B1[mu approx 0.1 to 0.4, asperity contact dominant] --- B
        C1[mu decreasing, partial fluid film, partial asperity] --- C
        D1[mu approx 0.001 to 0.01, fluid film separates surfaces] --- D
        E1[mu rises erratically, surface overheating, lubricant failure] --- E
    end
```

**Tabular representation of the Stribeck curve (for revision):**

| Regime | Hersey Number $H_e$ | $\mu$ Range | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boundary | $H_e < 10^{-7}$ | $0.1$ – $0.4$ | Asperity contact only |
| Mixed | $10^{-7} < H_e < 10^{-4}$ | $0.01$ – $0.1$ | Partial film, partial asperity |
| Hydrodynamic | $H_e > 10^{-4}$ | $0.001$ – $0.01$ | Full fluid film |
| Thermal Runaway | $H_e > 10^{-3}$ (with high $v$) | Rises, fluctuates | Flash temperature failure |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Wear Mechanism Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wear of Food Machinery Surfaces] --> B[Abrasive Wear]
    A --> C[Adhesive Wear]
    A --> D[Fatigue Wear]
    A --> E[Corrosive Oxidative Wear]
    A --> F[Erosive Wear]

    B --> B1[Two-Body: Hard particle cuts soft surface]
    B --> B2[Three-Body: Hard particle rolls between surfaces]
    C --> C1[Cold welding at asperities]
    C --> C2[Material transfer between surfaces]
    D --> D1[Subsurface crack initiation]
    D --> D2[Spalling and pitting]
    E --> E1[Oxide film formation and removal]
    F --> F1[Impact of food particles in slurry]
    F --> F2[Cavitation in pumps]

    B1 --> X[Typical in Roller Mills and Hammer Mills]
    C1 --> Y[Typical in Steel-on-Steel Gears]
    D2 --> Z[Typical in Rolling-Element Bearings]
    E1 --> W[Typical in High-Temperature Extruders]
    F1 --> V[Typical in Slurry Pumps for Food]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Architecture of a Tribological System (Block-Level Functional View)

    end
