# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State Amontons' Laws of Friction. Why is the independence of friction from apparent area explainable by asperity contact theory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Amontons' First Law:** The friction force $F_f$ between two dry sliding surfaces is **directly proportional** to the normal load $W$ pressing them together, i.e., $F_f = \mu W$.

**Amontons' Second Law:** The friction force is **independent of the apparent (nominal) area of contact**, i.e., the coefficient of friction $\mu$ is a constant for a given pair of materials.

**Explanation via asperity theory (1 mark):** All surfaces, however polished, have microscopic peaks and valleys (asperities). The real area of contact $A_r$ occurs only at the asperity tips. Since $A_r \propto W / H$ (plastic contact) and $F_f = \tau A_r$, both $F_f$ and $A_r$ scale linearly with $W$, so $\mu = F_f / W$ is independent of the apparent area but depends on the material's shear strength $\tau$ and hardness $H$.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for stating each law, 1 mark for asperity explanation. KTU expects a labelled sketch or verbal description of asperities.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Stribeck curve. What does it depict about the relationship between sliding velocity and coefficient of friction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stribeck curve** is a plot of the **coefficient of friction $\mu$** as a function of the **Hersey number** $H_e = \eta v / p$, where $\eta$ is the lubricant viscosity, $v$ is the sliding velocity, and $p$ is the contact pressure.

The curve depicts three lubrication regimes (1 mark each):

1. **Boundary lubrication** at low $H_e$: $\mu$ is high (0.1–0.4) and roughly constant; asperities are in direct contact.
2. **Mixed lubrication** at intermediate $H_e$: $\mu$ decreases rapidly as a partial fluid film begins to form.
3. **Hydrodynamic (full-film) lubrication** at high $H_e$: $\mu$ is at a minimum (0.001–0.01); the surfaces are fully separated by a fluid film.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark axes/labels, 1 mark the three regimes. A quick sketch is highly recommended.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Choice A) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: With the help of a derivation, explain Archard's wear equation. Discuss the effect of (a) load, (b) hardness, and (c) sliding distance on wear volume, in the context of a roller mill used in a flour processing plant.** $(7 + 7 = 14\text{ marks})$

**Or**

**Q: What is flash temperature? Derive the simplified Jaeger expression for flash-temperature rise at a sliding contact. Explain how this rise influences the choice of food-machinery materials for an extruder barrel.** $(7 + 7 = 14\text{ marks})$

---

### Model Answer for Question B Choice A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Archard's Wear Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the basic premise — wear volume is proportional to the real contact area and the sliding distance.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the real area of contact in the plastic deformation regime:
$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Introduce the wear coefficient $K$ (Archard wear constant, dimensionless), representing the probability of asperity junction producing a detached wear particle:
$$dV = K \cdot A_r \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute $A_r = W/H$:
$$dV = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot dx}{H}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = L$ (sliding distance):
$$V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Derive the wear rate (volume per unit distance):
$$W_r = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{K \cdot W}{H}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the typical range of $K$ (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻²) and the engineering meaning of each term.

#### Part (b) — Effect of Load, Hardness, and Sliding Distance on Wear in a Roller Mill (7 marks)

**(i) Effect of Load $W$ (2 marks):**
From Archard's equation, $V \propto W$. Doubling the roll gap force **doubles** the wear volume per unit time. In a flour mill, this means the rolls (typically hardened cast iron or chrome steel) must be **re-ground or replaced more frequently** when processing hard wheat varieties (high vitreousness) or when the throughput is increased. Excessive load also causes **plastic deformation of roll surfaces**, leading to corrugation and loss of grinding accuracy.

**(ii) Effect of Hardness $H$ (2 marks):**
$V \propto 1/H$. Doubling the surface hardness halves the wear volume. This is why:
- Rolls are made of **chilled cast iron** (HV ≈ 400–600) or **chromium-coated steel** (HV ≈ 800–1000).
- Hard-chrome plating extends roll life by 3–5× compared to bare cast iron.
- However, excessively hard rolls become **brittle** and prone to spalling under impact loading from wheat kernels.

**(iii) Effect of Sliding Distance $L$ (3 marks):**
$V \propto L$. For a roll rotating at $N$ RPM with radius $R$, the sliding distance per unit time is $L = 2 \pi R N$ (per revolution). Over an operating day of $t$ hours:

$$V_{day} = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot 2 \pi R N \cdot t}{H}$$

**Implications for the flour mill:**
- **Higher roll speeds** (used in high-throughput mills) proportionally increase wear.
- **Roll differential** (different speeds of the two rolls, e.g., 1.5:1) introduces sliding in addition to crushing, increasing $L$ by 20–30 %.
- **Abrasive contamination** (sand, soil on wheat) increases the effective $K$ by 10–100×, drastically shortening roll life.
- The **specific wear rate** $k = K/H$ is the key design parameter; for the rolls to last one year, the maximum allowable $k$ can be back-calculated from the required roll life.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Tribological design of a roller mill balances throughput (high $W$ and $v$) against roll life (low wear), with material selection ($H$) and operating conditions ($K$) being the key decision variables.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often write "wear is proportional to load" without specifying that this is **Archard's volumetric wear law** under **plastic contact conditions**. For elastic contacts, the dependence is different (proportional to $W^2$). Always state the regime!

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** The Archard wear coefficient $K$ is **not the same** as the **specific wear rate** $k = V/(WL)$. Confusing these two will cost 1 mark.

---

### Model Answer for Question B Choice B (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a) — Definition and Derivation of Flash Temperature (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define flash temperature $T_f$ as the transient, very-localized temperature rise at a single asperity junction during sliding, lasting microseconds to milliseconds.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the heat-generation rate due to friction:
$$\dot{Q} = \mu W v \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the moving-heat-source model of Jaeger (1942) for a circular contact of radius $r_c$ on a semi-infinite solid of thermal conductivity $k$ and diffusivity $a$:

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu W v}{\pi r_c k} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{v}{r_c a}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi}$$

Simplified to:

$$\Delta T_f = C \cdot \frac{\mu \cdot p \cdot v \cdot \sqrt{r_c}}{2 k}$$

where $C \approx 0.5$ for circular contacts, $p = W / A_n$ is the nominal pressure, and $A_n$ is the apparent area.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final flash temperature:
$$T_f = T_b + \Delta T_f$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Interpretation:
- $T_f \propto \mu, p, v$ (linearly with friction, pressure, velocity).
- $T_f \propto 1/k$ (inversely with thermal conductivity).
- $T_f$ can reach **hundreds of degrees** above the bulk, even with modest $\mu, p, v$.

#### Part (b) — Implications for Extruder Barrel Material Selection (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the contact: a rotating steel screw inside a barrel liner, with food dough in between (cooking extruder, screw diameter 100 mm, length 2 m).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Typical operating conditions:
- $p \approx 5$–$15$ MPa
- $v \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ m/s (peripheral screw speed)
- $\mu \approx 0.05$–$0.2$ (with food dough as a viscous "lubricant")
- $T_b \approx 150\,°\text{C}$ (extrusion temperature)

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate flash temperature:

$$\Delta T_f = 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.1 \cdot 10 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.75 \cdot \sqrt{50 \times 10^{-6}}}{2 \cdot 16}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.1 \cdot 10^7 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 7.07 \times 10^{-3}}{32} \approx 8.3\,°\text{C}$$

So $T_f \approx 158\,°\text{C}$. For higher $p$ or $v$, $\Delta T_f$ can reach 50–100 °C, putting $T_f$ near or above 250 °C, where:
- **Polymer barrel liners** (e.g., nitrided steel) soften and lose dimensional accuracy.
- **Food proteins** denature and adhere to the barrel, creating **adhesive wear** and product contamination.
- **Lubricating dough** carbonises, creating abrasive debris.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Material selection implications:
- **Bore-hardened nitrided steel** (e.g., EN41B, hardness HV ≈ 900) — high $H$ reduces wear, decent $k$ (~30 W/m·K) keeps $T_f$ moderate.
- **Bimetallic barrels** with a hard, wear-resistant inner liner (e.g., Colmonoy, hardness HV ≈ 600) bonded to a tough outer body.
- **Ceramic-lined barrels** (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, HV ≈ 1500) — very high $H$, very low wear, but **low thermal conductivity** can *raise* $T_f$ unless carefully designed. Risk of thermal-shock cracking.
- **Food-grade coatings** (e.g., PTFE, DLC) reduce $\mu$ to 0.05, cutting $T_f$ by 5–10×.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Flash temperature drives barrel material selection toward **high-hardness, high-thermal-conductivity** materials with low coefficient of friction against the food/cooking medium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** Students often confuse **bulk (average) barrel temperature** with **flash temperature**. Bulk temperature is uniform and steady; flash temperature is localized, transient, and can be 10–1000× higher in severity. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4:** The **PV-limit** of a material is *not* a constant — it depends on the cooling arrangement. Effective cooling can raise the practical PV-limit by 3–10×. State this when discussing extruder design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Key Definitions
- **Friction coefficient** $\mu = F_f / W$ — dimensionless, Amontons' law.
- **Real contact area** $A_r = W / H$ — only at asperity tips, not the apparent area.
- **Wear volume** (Archard) $V = K W L / H$ — linear in load and distance, inverse in hardness.
- **Specific wear rate** $k = V / (W L)$ — units $\text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}$.
- **Hersey number** $H_e = \eta v / p$ — dimensionless, drives the Stribeck curve.
- **Flash temperature** $T_f = T_b + \Delta T_f$ — transient local asperity temperature.

### Effect of Load ($W$)
- Friction force $\propto W$, but $\mu$ is **independent** of $W$ (Amontons).
- Wear volume $\propto W$ (Archard).
- Real contact area $\propto W / H$.

### Effect of Properties
- Harder material → smaller $A_r$ → **lower friction and wear**.
- Higher $E/H$ ratio → more elastic (less wear) for the same load.
- Smoother surfaces (lower $R_a$) → less asperity interlocking → lower $\mu$.
- Dissimilar materials → less cold welding → lower adhesive wear.
- Higher $k$ (thermal conductivity) → better heat dissipation → lower $T_f$.

### Effect of Sliding Velocity ($v$)
- Frictional power $P_f = \mu W v$ grows **linearly** with velocity.
- Friction coefficient follows the **Stribeck curve** — boundary → mixed → hydrodynamic.
- Wear rate generally $\propto v$ in the boundary regime.
- At very high $v$, flash temperature may rise enough to **soften** surfaces and accelerate wear.

### Effect of Contact Surface Temperature ($T_s$)
- **Lubricant viscosity drops** with rising $T_s$ → thinner film → shifts to boundary.
- **Surface hardness drops** with rising $T_s$ → larger real contact area → higher friction.
- **Oxidative wear** rate grows exponentially with $T_s$: $r_{ox} \propto \exp(-E_a / RT)$.
- **PV-limit** must be respected: $p \cdot v \le PV_{limit}$, else thermal runaway.

### Stribeck Regimes — Memorize
1. **Boundary** (low $H_e$): $\mu$ high, asperity contact.
2. **Mixed** (intermediate $H_e$): $\mu$ decreasing, partial film.
3. **Hydrodynamic** (high $H_e$): $\mu$ minimum, full film.
4. **Thermal runaway** (very high $H_e$ with high $v$): $\mu$ rises, surface damage.

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- $F_f = \mu W$ — Amontons' law.
- $V = K W L / H$ — Archard.
- $\mu = f(\eta v / p)$ — Stribeck.
- $\Delta T_f \approx \mu p v \sqrt{r_c} / (2 k)$ — Jaeger.
- $PV \le PV_{limit}$ — Failure criterion.

### Common Food-Engineering Applications
- **Roller mills:** Load-hardness-sliding distance trade-off.
- **Hammer mills:** Velocity-dominated impact wear.
- **Extruders:** PV-limit and flash temperature.
- **Dough mixers:** Load-velocity-temperature coupling.
- **Pumps:** Velocity-lubrication regime.

### Examiner Traps to Avoid
- Confusing $K$ (Archard wear coefficient) with $k$ (specific wear rate).
- Confusing flash temperature with bulk temperature.
- Forgetting that $\mu$ is independent of apparent area (only of real area).
- Omitting the effect of temperature on viscosity when discussing the Stribeck curve.
- Stating "wear is proportional to load" without specifying the plastic-contact regime.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In any 14-mark question, structure your answer as **(i) Statement of principle, (ii) Derivation, (iii) Numerical example, (iv) Engineering application, (v) Conclusion**. This maps directly to the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels** (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyse → Evaluate) that KTU 2024 examiners use for CO mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of water film and surface roughness; rolling resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of water film and surface roughness; rolling resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Tribological Interactions in Food Processing

In the context of food technology and the engineering of food processing equipment, the interaction between surfaces — whether metallic, polymeric, or food-borne — is governed by the science of **tribology** (from the Greek *tribein*, meaning "to rub"). Tribology encompasses the study of **friction, wear, and lubrication**. The performance, energy efficiency, hygiene, and shelf-life of food products are heavily influenced by the microscopic and macroscopic behavior of the surfaces involved during processing, transport, and packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Tribology Module)**
> *Tribology is the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion. It includes the study and application of the principles of friction, lubrication, and wear. Tribology is highly relevant to food technology as it governs the efficiency of conveyors, extruders, mixers, and packaging lines.*

Within this domain, the **effect of water film** and **surface roughness** dictate the regime of friction, while **rolling resistance** determines the energy losses in rotating and rolling machinery (e.g., rollers in grain mills, ball bearings in mixers, wheels of transport trolleys).

---

## 1.2 Surface Roughness — Formal Definition

**Surface Roughness** is formally defined as the measure of the finely spaced micro-irregularities on a surface, typically quantified by the parameter **Ra (Arithmetic Average Roughness)**, measured in micrometers (µm) or microinches (µin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ra Definition:**
> Ra is the arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the ordinates of the surface profile from the mean line, measured over a sampling length.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx
$$

Where:
- **L** = Sampling length (in mm or µm)
- **z(x)** = Vertical deviation from the mean line at position *x*

Other key roughness parameters include:
- **Rz** = Mean peak-to-valley height (10-point height)
- **Rq (RMS)** = Root mean square roughness
- **Rt** = Maximum peak-to-valley height

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Range:**
> Imagine a metal surface as a vast mountain range seen from above. The "peaks" are the asperities (tiny bumps), and the "valleys" are the micro-cavities. A *very rough* surface is like the rugged Himalayas; a *polished* surface is like a calm lake's flat bottom. When two such surfaces touch, only the peaks collide first — this is the essence of friction at the microscopic level.

In food technology, surface roughness is critical:
- **Polished stainless steel (Ra < 0.8 µm)**: Used in hygienic food contact surfaces (CIP — Clean-In-Place systems).
- **Rough surfaces (Ra > 3.2 µm)**: Harbor bacteria, food residues, and are difficult to sanitize.

---

## 1.3 Water Film Effect — Formal Definition

The **water film effect** refers to the presence of a thin layer of water (or moisture from food products) between two contacting surfaces. This water film can act as a **lubricant** (reducing friction) or, in excess, as a **contaminant** (causing hydroplaning, accelerated wear, or corrosion).

In the context of friction regimes, the water film thickness is characterized by the **Lambda ratio (λ)**:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}
$$

Where:
- **h_min** = Minimum lubricant (water) film thickness
- **R_q1, R_q2** = RMS roughness of the two contacting surfaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda Ratio Interpretation (Stribeck Curve Zones):**
> - **λ < 1** → Boundary Lubrication (asperities in direct contact)
> -1 **≤ λ ≤ 3** → Mixed Lubrication (partial film)
> - **λ > 3** → Full-Film (Hydrodynamic) Lubrication (surfaces fully separated)

---

## 1.4 Rolling Resistance — Formal Definition

**Rolling resistance** (also called **rolling friction** or **rolling drag**) is the force resisting the motion when a body (such as a ball, wheel, or cylinder) rolls on a surface. It is primarily caused by:
1. **Deformation of the rolling body**
2. **Deformation of the supporting surface**
3. **Surface roughness and micro-slip** at the contact patch
4. **Internal friction** within the materials

Unlike sliding friction, rolling resistance is generally **100 to 1000 times smaller**, which is why wheels and ball bearings revolutionized machinery.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Tire:**
> When you ride a bicycle on a smooth, hard road, the bike rolls easily because the tire and road deform very little. Now, ride the same bike on soft sand or a muddy field. The tire sinks in, deforms heavily, and you must pedal much harder. The "extra effort" you feel is the **rolling resistance**. Food products like dough, wet grains, and pastes behave similarly — they deform and create high rolling resistance in rollers and conveyors.

In food processing, rolling resistance is observed in:
- **Roller mills** for grain crushing
- **Conveyor rollers** moving packaged goods
- **Ball bearings** in centrifuges and mixers

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tribological Constants & Units (BOLD for emphasis):**
> - **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance (μr or Crr)**: Dimensionless, typically **0.001 to 0.01** for steel on steel.
> - **Coefficient of Static Friction (μs)**: Typically **0.5 to 0.8** for dry metals.
> - **Coefficient of Kinetic Friction (μk)**: Typically **0.3 to 0.6** for dry metals.
> - **Water's Dynamic Viscosity (η) at 20°C**: **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (or 1.002 cP).
> - **Standard Surface Roughness Grades (ISO 1302)**: N1 (Ra 0.025 µm) to N12 (Ra 50 µm).
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure**: **101.325 kPa** (affects water film boiling point and cavitation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Friction Regimes (Stribeck Curve)

The **Stribeck Curve** is the foundational diagram in tribology that relates the **coefficient of friction (μ)** to a dimensionless combination of speed, viscosity, and load — known as the **Hersey number**.

### The Hersey Number

$$
\text{Hersey Number} = \frac{\eta \cdot v}{P}
$$

Where:
- **η** = Dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s)
- **v** = Relative sliding velocity (m/s)
- **P** = Normal load per unit length (N/m)

### The Three Regimes Explained

**1. Boundary Lubrication Regime (Low Speed / High Load / Dry Surfaces)**
- The water (or oil) film is thinner than the combined surface roughness.
- Asperities of the two surfaces are in **direct metal-to-metal contact**.
- Friction is high and governed by **surface roughness** and the **shear strength of the asperity junctions**.
- In food tech: dry grain transport, dry cereal processing.

**2. Mixed Lubrication Regime (Intermediate Speed / Load)**
- The film is partially developed; some asperities still contact, while others are separated by the water film.
- Friction **decreases** as speed increases or load decreases.
- In food tech: moist dough in rollers, wet food mixing.

**3. Full-Film (Hydrodynamic / Elastohydrodynamic) Lubrication Regime (High Speed / Low Load / Thick Film)**
- The water film completely separates the two surfaces.
- Friction is governed by the **viscous shear of the fluid**.
- Friction **increases** with speed (because more fluid must be sheared).
- In food tech: high-speed centrifuge bearings, hydrodynamic seals in pumps handling milk or juice.

---

## 2.2 Why Surface Roughness Matters — Step by Step

### Step 1: Real Contact Area vs. Apparent Contact Area
When two nominally flat surfaces are pressed together, the **apparent contact area** (the geometric overlap) is large, but the **real contact area** (the sum of all microscopic asperity contacts) is **very small** — often less than **1%** of the apparent area.

### Step 2: Asperity Pressure
Because the real area is tiny, the **local pressure** on each asperity can be enormous — often near the **yield strength** of the material.

### Step 3: Asperity Deformation and Cold Welding
Under this high pressure, asperities deform plastically and may even **cold-weld** to each other. Sliding one surface over the other requires shearing these micro-junctions, which is the fundamental origin of **friction force**.

### Step 4: Effect of Roughness on Friction
- **Higher Ra** → More asperity interactions → Higher friction (in boundary regime)
- **Lower Ra** → Fewer asperity interactions → Lower friction (in boundary regime)
- **Exception**: In hydrodynamic regime, ultra-smooth surfaces can actually **increase** friction by reducing the lubricant's ability to generate a pressure wedge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> In food-grade stainless steel (AISI 304/316), the standard finish is **2B (Ra ≈ 0.3–0.5 µm)** or **No. 4 (Ra ≈ 0.4–0.8 µm)**. This range provides an optimal balance between cleanability and tribological performance.

---

## 2.3 The Mechanics of Rolling Resistance

When a rigid cylinder of weight **W** rolls on a flat elastic surface, the contact patch is not a single point but a small **contact area** (a line for a cylinder, an ellipse for a sphere). Within this area, the normal pressure distribution is asymmetric:
- The pressure peak is **shifted slightly ahead** of the geometric center of the contact patch.
- This asymmetry creates a **moment** that opposes the rolling motion.

This resisting moment is quantified as:

$$
M_{rr} = W \cdot a
$$

Where:
- **M_rr** = Rolling resistance moment (N·m)
- **W** = Normal load (N)
- **a** = Offset distance of pressure peak from contact center (m), also called the **rolling resistance arm**

The **coefficient of rolling resistance** is defined as:

$$
\mu_r = \frac{a}{r}
$$

Where **r** is the radius of the rolling body. Thus, the **rolling resistance force** is:

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W = \frac{a}{r} \cdot W
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The rolling resistance force is **inversely proportional to the radius** of the rolling body. Larger wheels roll more easily — this is why trucks have large wheels and why food processing rollers are designed with generous diameters.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Surface Roughness (Ra) | $R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert z(x)\vert \, dx$ | L in mm, z in µm | Surface finish measurement |
| 2 | Lambda Ratio | $\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | h in µm, Rq in µm | Lubrication regime identification |
| 3 | Hersey Number | $H = \frac{\eta \cdot v}{P}$ | η in Pa·s, v in m/s, P in N/m | Stribeck curve input |
| 4 | Coefficient of Rolling Resistance | $\mu_r = \frac{a}{r}$ | a in m, r in m | Rolling friction analysis |
| 5 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W$ | F in N, W in N | Energy loss in rollers |
| 6 | Rolling Resistance Moment | $M_{rr} = W \cdot a$ | M in N·m | Torque on roller shafts |
| 7 | Sliding Friction Force | $F_s = \mu_k \cdot W$ | F in N, W in N | Dry sliding contact |
| 8 | Reynolds Equation (1D) | $\frac{dp}{dx} = 6\eta v \frac{(h - h^*)}{h^3}$ | p in Pa, h in m | Hydrodynamic film pressure |
| 9 | Hertzian Contact Stress (Cylinder) | $p_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot L \cdot r}}$ | F in N, E* in Pa, L in m, r in m | Maximum contact pressure |
| 10 | Energy Loss per Roll Cycle | $E_{loss} = F_{rr} \cdot \pi \cdot d$ | E in J, d in m | Per-revolution energy dissipation |

> [!TIP]
> **Avoid Markdown Table Breaks:**
> Notice that in the table above, all absolute values and conditional signs use **\vert** or **\mid** syntax, NOT the vertical pipe `|`. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking the row.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food processing industries, these tribological principles are applied in:
- **Roller mills for wheat and grain**: Optimized roller surface roughness (often fluted or corrugated) ensures controlled crushing with minimal flour dust.
- **Belt conveyors in dairies**: Smooth stainless steel rollers with low Ra reduce friction, while food-grade lubricants (or water films from cleaning) provide boundary lubrication.
- **Centrifugal separators for milk**: High-speed rotating bowls (10,000+ rpm) operate in the **full-film hydrodynamic regime**; any transition to mixed or boundary regime can cause catastrophic failure.
- **Packaging machinery**: Rollers for films and laminates are designed with rubber coatings to maximize friction (intentionally high μ) for traction.
- **Food texture analysis**: Instruments like the **Texture Analyzer (TA)** measure the rolling and sliding resistance of food gels, doughs, and pastes to quantify mouthfeel.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coefficient of Rolling Resistance from First Principles

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a rigid cylinder of radius **r** and weight **W** resting on a flat, elastic horizontal surface. The cylinder is about to be pulled by a horizontal force **F**.

**Conversion logic:** We assume quasi-static motion (very slow roll) so that inertial effects are negligible.

### Step 2: Identify the Contact Patch
Due to elastic deformation, the cylinder contacts the surface over a small patch of length **2b** (where **b** is the half-width of contact). Within this patch, the pressure distribution is **asymmetric**: it rises sharply at the leading edge (entry side) and decays gradually at the trailing edge (exit side).

### Step 3: Write the Vertical Force Equilibrium
Summing vertical forces:

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \int_{-b}^{+b} p(x) \, dx
$$

**Conversion logic:** The total upward pressure force from the surface must exactly balance the downward weight.

### Step 4: Write the Moment Equilibrium About the Center of the Cylinder
Summing moments about the cylinder's center (clockwise positive, in the direction of impending roll):

$$
\sum M_{center} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F \cdot r - \int_{-b}^{+b} p(x) \cdot x \, dx = 0
$$

**Conversion logic:** The applied force F creates a driving moment F·r about the center. The pressure distribution creates a resisting moment equal to the integral of p(x)·x (since x is the lever arm from the center).

### Step 5: Introduce the Concept of Pressure Peak Offset
The pressure distribution can be approximated as symmetric about a point **+a** (ahead of the geometric center). Substituting:

$$
F \cdot r = W \cdot a
$$

**Conversion logic:** Since the total vertical force is W, the centroid of the pressure distribution is at x = +a. Therefore, the moment of the pressure distribution about the center is simply W·a.

### Step 6: Solve for the Rolling Resistance Force
Rearranging the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot r &= W \cdot a \\[6pt]
F &= W \cdot \frac{a}{r} \\[6pt]
F_{rr} &= \mu_r \cdot W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The applied force F required to maintain rolling equals the rolling resistance force. Defining **μr = a/r**, we obtain the canonical form.

### Step 7: Final Result and Physical Interpretation

$$
\boxed{\; F_{rr} = \frac{a}{r} \cdot W = \mu_r \cdot W \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The offset **a** is a function of material elasticity and surface roughness.
> - For **steel on steel**, a ≈ 0.0005 m (0.5 mm), giving μr ≈ 0.0005/r.
> - For **rubber tire on asphalt**, a ≈ 0.01 m, giving μr ≈ 0.01/r.
> - For a 0.3 m radius tire: μr ≈ 0.033; for a 0.05 m radius caster: μr ≈ 0.2 (caster wheels are hard to push!).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Loss Per Revolution in a Rolling Element

### Step 1: Distance Traveled Per Revolution
For a cylinder of diameter **d** rolling without slipping, one revolution covers a linear distance:

$$
L_{rev} = \pi \cdot d
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is simply the circumference of the cylinder.

### Step 2: Force Required to Overcome Rolling Resistance
The rolling resistance force is constant (in the simplest model):

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W
$$

### Step 3: Work Done Per Revolution
Work = Force × Distance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{rev} &= F_{rr} \cdot L_{rev} \\[6pt]
W_{rev} &= \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \pi \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the energy lost to hysteresis, asperity deformation, and micro-slip per full revolution.

### Step 4: Power Required at Rotational Speed **N** (rev/s)

$$
P_{rr} = W_{rev} \cdot N = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot N
$$

**Conversion logic:** Power is energy per unit time, and at N revolutions per second, the cylinder completes N revolutions each second.

### Step 5: Substitute N = ω/(2π) where ω is angular velocity in rad/s

$$
P_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\mu_r \cdot W \cdot d \cdot \omega}{2}
$$

### Step 6: Final Power Loss Expression

$$
\boxed{\; P_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_r \cdot W \cdot d \cdot \omega \;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Engineering Insight:**
> This equation shows that power loss scales **linearly with rotational speed, load, and rolling resistance coefficient**, but also with **diameter**. Designers minimize power loss by reducing μr (better surface finish, harder materials) and choosing optimal roller diameters.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Friction Types

| Parameter | Static Friction (μs) | Kinetic/Sliding Friction (μk) | Rolling Friction (μr) | Hydrodynamic Friction (μh) |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Contact Type | At rest, no motion | Sliding contact | Rolling contact | Separated by fluid film |
| Typical Magnitude | **0.5 – 0.8** | **0.3 – 0.6** | **0.001 – 0.01** | **0.001 – 0.05** (effective) |
| Velocity Dependence | None (until breakaway) | Slightly decreases with v | Increases with v | Strongly increases with v |
| Surface Roughness Effect | Major | Major | Moderate | Minor |
| Water Film Effect | N/A | Boundary regime dominant | Reduces μr | Essential (enables regime) |
| Energy Loss | Highest | High | Very Low | Lowest (no contact) |
| Food Tech Example | Grain resting on chute | Flour sliding in pipe | Roller mill rolls | Milk in centrifuge |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Roller Mill in a Flour Plant

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A roller mill in a flour processing plant has two steel rollers, each of diameter **d = 250 mm**, supporting a total normal load of **W = 5000 N**. The rollers run at **N = 300 rpm**. The combined surface roughness is **Ra = 1.6 µm** for both rollers, and a thin water film of thickness **h = 2.0 µm** is present. Calculate:
(a) The Hersey number (assume η_water = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s).
(b) The lambda ratio and identify the lubrication regime.
(c) The rolling resistance force and power loss, assuming μr = 0.002.

### Part (a): Hersey Number

**Step 1:** Compute the linear velocity at the roller's surface:

$$
v = \pi \cdot d \cdot N = \pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 5 = 3.927 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the normal load per unit length (assuming contact length L = 0.05 m):

$$
P = \frac{W}{L} = \frac{5000}{0.05} = 100{,}000 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Hersey number formula:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot v}{P} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 3.927}{100{,}000} = 3.927 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is an extremely small Hersey number, indicating operation **far from hydrodynamic regime** — typical of slow, heavily loaded roller mills.

### Part (b): Lambda Ratio

**Step 1:** Assume Rq ≈ 1.3 × Ra (approximate industry relation):

$$
R_{q1} = R_{q2} = 1.3 \cdot 1.6 = 2.08 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the lambda ratio formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{2.08^2 + 2.08^2}} = \frac{2.0}{2.942} = 0.68
$$

**Conversion logic:** Since **λ < 1**, the rollers operate in the **boundary lubrication regime**. The water film is too thin to fully separate the asperities — direct asperity contact dominates.

### Part (c): Rolling Resistance and Power Loss

**Step 1:** Compute rolling resistance force:

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.002 \cdot 5000 = 10 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 300}{60} = 31.42 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the power loss formula:

$$
P_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_r \cdot W \cdot d \cdot \omega = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.002 \cdot 5000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 31.42 = 39.27 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answer:** The roller mill dissipates approximately **39.3 W** as rolling friction heat. While this seems small, across hundreds of rollers in a commercial flour mill, the cumulative loss becomes significant for energy auditing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Surface Roughness" and explain the significance of the parameter Ra in food processing equipment.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Surface roughness refers to the finely spaced micro-irregularities present on a manufactured surface. It is a measure of the texture of the surface at the microscopic level.

**Parameter Ra (1 Mark):** Ra, the **Arithmetic Average Roughness**, is the most commonly used parameter to quantify surface roughness. It is defined as the arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the vertical deviations of the surface profile from the mean line over a specified sampling length. It is measured in micrometers (µm).

**Significance in Food Processing (0.5 Mark):** In food processing, Ra governs (a) cleanability — smoother surfaces (low Ra) harbor fewer bacteria and are easier to CIP-sanitize; (b) friction and wear — controlled Ra in rollers and conveyors ensures consistent product flow; (c) hygiene compliance — regulatory standards (e.g., 3-A Sanitary Standards) specify maximum Ra values for food contact surfaces, typically Ra ≤ 0.8 µm.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is rolling resistance? State the factors affecting it.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Rolling resistance is the force that opposes the motion of a body (such as a ball, wheel, or cylinder) when it rolls over a surface. It arises primarily from the deformation of the rolling body and the supporting surface at the contact patch, as well as from micro-slip and surface roughness effects.

**Factors (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Material properties** — Elastic modulus and hardness of both the rolling body and the surface (softer materials → higher deformation → higher rolling resistance).
2. **Radius of the rolling body** — Larger radius reduces rolling resistance (inversely proportional).
3. **Normal load** — Higher load increases the contact area and deformation, raising rolling resistance linearly.
4. **Surface roughness** — Rougher surfaces increase micro-slip and asperity interaction losses.
5. **Presence of lubrication (water film)** — Adequate lubrication can reduce rolling resistance by separating asperities.
6. **Rolling speed** — At low speeds, rolling resistance is approximately constant; at higher speeds, viscoelastic losses (hysteresis) increase it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A: (a) Explain with neat sketches the Stribeck curve and the different lubrication regimes. Discuss how the water film and surface roughness influence the friction coefficient in each regime. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the expression for the rolling resistance force of a cylinder rolling on a flat elastic surface. A steel roller of diameter 200 mm supports a load of 3000 N. If the coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.003, calculate (i) the rolling resistance force, and (ii) the power loss when the roller rotates at 200 rpm. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part A(a) — 7 Marks:

**1. Stribeck Curve Description (3 Marks):**
The Stribeck curve is a plot of the **coefficient of friction (μ)** on the Y-axis against the **Hersey number (ηv/P)** on the X-axis (logarithmic scale). The curve exhibits three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Boundary Lubrication:** At very low Hersey numbers (low speed or high load), the lubricant film is absent or extremely thin. The friction coefficient is high and approximately constant, governed by the **shear strength of the asperity junctions** and the **surface roughness** of the contacting bodies. The lambda ratio λ < 1.

- **Region II — Mixed Lubrication:** As speed increases or load decreases, a partial lubricant film begins to form. The friction coefficient **drops sharply** as asperity contact is progressively replaced by fluid film contact. The lambda ratio is between 1 and 3.

- **Region III — Full-Film (Hydrodynamic/Elastohydrodynamic) Lubrication:** At high Hersey numbers, the lubricant film completely separates the surfaces. The friction coefficient **rises again** with increasing speed, governed by the **viscous shear of the fluid film**. The lambda ratio λ > 3.

**2. Influence of Water Film and Surface Roughness (4 Marks):**

- **Boundary Regime:** Surface roughness is the **dominant** factor. Asperity contact determines friction. The water film is too thin (λ < 1) to be effective. Reducing Ra lowers friction.

- **Mixed Regime:** Both surface roughness and water film thickness matter. Increasing water film thickness (better wetting, higher viscosity, or faster speed) pushes the system toward full-film lubrication, reducing friction. A **smoother surface** also helps because the required film thickness to achieve full separation is smaller.

- **Hydrodynamic Regime:** Surface roughness has **minor** influence. The water film's viscosity and the speed of relative motion determine friction. Friction **increases** with speed because more fluid must be sheared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch Description (Verbal, since complex graphs are difficult in text):**
> The student should draw a graph with μ on Y-axis and Hersey number on X-axis (log scale), showing the three regions as described, marking the transition points and the minimum friction point.

#### Model Solution for Part A(b) — 7 Marks:

**1. Derivation of Rolling Resistance Force (3.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a rigid cylinder of radius **r** and weight **W** rolling on a flat elastic surface under quasi-static conditions.

**Step 2:** At the contact patch, the pressure distribution **p(x)** is asymmetric, with its centroid shifted by a distance **a** ahead of the geometric center of the contact.

**Step 3:** Vertical equilibrium:

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \int p(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Moment equilibrium about the cylinder center:

$$
F_{applied} \cdot r = \int p(x) \cdot x \, dx = W \cdot a
$$

**Step 5:** Defining the **coefficient of rolling resistance** as:

$$
\mu_r = \frac{a}{r}
$$

**Step 6:** The rolling resistance force equals the applied force at impending roll:

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W
$$

**2. Numerical Calculation (3.5 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 200 mm → r = 0.1 m
- Load W = 3000 N
- μr = 0.003
- Speed N = 200 rpm

**Part (i): Rolling Resistance Force**

$$
F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.003 \cdot 3000 = 9 \text{ N}
$$

**Part (ii): Power Loss**

**Step 1:** Angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 200}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the power loss formula:

$$
P_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu_r \cdot W \cdot d \cdot \omega
$$

$$
P_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.003 \cdot 3000 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 20.944 = 18.85 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answer:** F_rr = **9 N**; P_loss = **18.85 W**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B: (a) Discuss the effect of surface roughness on friction in food processing equipment. Explain the parameters Ra, Rz, and Rq with their physical significance. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the Stribeck curve in detail. A conveyor roller in a dairy plant has a water film of 1.5 µm between the bearing and the journal. The RMS roughness of the bearing surface is 0.4 µm and that of the journal is 0.5 µm. Determine the lambda ratio and identify the lubrication regime. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part B(a) — 7 Marks:

**1. Effect of Surface Roughness on Friction (3 Marks):**
In food processing equipment (roller mills, mixers, conveyors, extruders, packaging lines), surface roughness directly affects the real area of contact between sliding or rolling surfaces. Rougher surfaces have taller asperities that interlock and cold-weld, increasing the friction force. In the **boundary lubrication regime**, the friction coefficient can be approximated as:

$$
\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y}
$$

Where **τs** is the shear strength of the asperity junction and **py** is the yield pressure of the softer material. Smoother surfaces (low Ra) have fewer and smaller asperity junctions, reducing μ. However, in the **hydrodynamic regime**, excessive smoothness can reduce the ability of the fluid to generate a pressure wedge, slightly **increasing** friction.

**2. Surface Roughness Parameters (4 Marks):**

- **Ra (Arithmetic Average Roughness):** The arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the surface profile deviations from the mean line over the sampling length. It is the most widely used international parameter. It does not distinguish between peaks and valleys.

- **Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height / 10-Point Height):** The average of the **five highest peaks** and the **five lowest valleys** over the sampling length. Rz is more sensitive to extreme features and is commonly used in European standards (DIN/ISO). It provides information about the **maximum height** of roughness.

- **Rq (Root Mean Square Roughness):** The geometric mean of the squared deviations from the mean line, then square-rooted. Rq gives **more weight to large deviations** than Ra, making it a more sensitive statistical measure.

**Physical Significance:** All three parameters characterize the same fundamental property — surface micro-geometry — but with different statistical treatments. In food-grade stainless steel, Ra is most commonly specified because it correlates well with bacterial cleanability (the "Biofilm Retention Threshold" is typically Ra ≈ 0.8 µm).

#### Model Solution for Part B(b) — 7 Marks:

**1. Detailed Explanation of Stribeck Curve (3.5 Marks):**
*(Same content as Part A(a), Region I, II, III description with Hersey number formula)*

The Stribeck curve plots the coefficient of friction **μ** versus the Hersey number **ηv/P**. It identifies three regimes:
- **Boundary Lubrication** (low Hersey number, λ < 1): Asperity contact, high μ, surface roughness dominant.
- **Mixed Lubrication** (intermediate Hersey, 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3): Partial film, decreasing μ.
- **Full-Film Hydrodynamic Lubrication** (high Hersey, λ > 3): Complete separation, μ rises with speed.

The transition between regimes is governed by the **lambda ratio** λ.

**2. Numerical Problem — Lambda Ratio (3.5 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Water film thickness: h_min = 1.5 µm
- Bearing RMS roughness: R_q1 = 0.4 µm
- Journal RMS roughness: R_q2 = 0.5 µm

**Step 1:** Compute the combined RMS roughness:

$$
\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2} = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (0.5)^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.41} = 0.6403 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the lambda ratio formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}} = \frac{1.5}{0.6403} = 2.342
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result:
Since **1 < λ < 3** (specifically λ ≈ 2.34), the bearing operates in the **Mixed Lubrication Regime**. There is partial asperity contact, and the friction coefficient is in the declining portion of the Stribeck curve. To move to full-film lubrication, the water film thickness should be increased to h > 3 × 0.6403 = **1.92 µm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students often mix µm and mm. Always convert to a common unit (preferably µm for roughness and h, mm for dimensions like roller diameter) BEFORE computing. (Penalty: up to 1 mark deduction)
> 2. **Confusing Ra with Rq:** Ra ≈ 1.1 × Rq for typical surfaces, but they are NOT the same. Using Ra when the problem gives Rq is a common error. (Penalty: full marks lost on the lambda calculation)
> 3. **Forgetting the Square Root in the Denominator:** The lambda ratio uses the **RMS combination** (square root of sum of squares), NOT a simple sum. Writing λ = h/(Rq1 + Rq2) is wrong. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Not Identifying the Regime:** Even with the correct λ, students sometimes forget to explicitly state the regime. Always write: "Since λ = X, the system is in the [Boundary/Mixed/Full-Film] regime." (Penalty: 0.5 mark)
> 5. **Rolling Resistance Units:** μr is **dimensionless**. Forgetting to write "dimensionless" or "no units" in numerical answers is penalized under strict KTU valuation. (Penalty: 0.5 mark)
> 6. **Power vs. Energy:** Students often confuse "energy loss per revolution" (J) with "power loss" (W = J/s). Ensure the correct formula is used based on the question. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read This Before Every KTU Exam**

- [x] **Tribology** = Study of friction, wear, and lubrication. Relevant to food processing machinery efficiency and hygiene.

- [x] **Surface Roughness Ra** = Arithmetic mean of absolute profile deviations. Unit: **µm**. Most common in food-grade specs.

- [x] **Rq (RMS Roughness)** = Square root of mean squared deviations. More sensitive to large features than Ra. Used in lambda ratio.

- [x] **Rz** = Mean of 5 highest peaks + 5 lowest valleys. Used in DIN/ISO standards.

- [x] **Lambda Ratio λ** = h_min / √(Rq1² + Rq2²). **NOT** a simple sum in the denominator!

- [x] **Lubrication Regimes:** λ < 1 → Boundary; 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 → Mixed; λ > 3 → Full-Film (Hydrodynamic).

- [x] **Stribeck Curve** = μ vs. Hersey number (ηv/P). Shows three regimes with a minimum friction point at the boundary-to-mixed transition.

- [x] **Hersey Number** = η·v / P. Dimensionless (or units of 1/m depending on convention).

- [x] **Rolling Resistance Force** = **F_rr = μr · W = (a/r) · W**. Dimensionless μr typically **0.001 to 0.01**.

- [x] **Rolling Resistance is Inversely Proportional to Radius:** Larger rollers → less resistance. Critical in food conveyor and mill design.

- [x] **Power Loss Due to Rolling** = **P_rr = (1/2) · μr · W · d · ω** (in Watts).

- [x] **Energy Loss Per Revolution** = F_rr · π · d (in Joules).

- [x] **Water film reduces friction** by separating asperities (in mixed and full-film regimes). In boundary regime, water film is too thin to help.

- [x] **Surface roughness is a double-edged sword:** In boundary regime, smoother is better; in hydrodynamic regime, some roughness helps generate the pressure wedge.

- [x] **Food-grade stainless steel (AISI 304/316)** is typically specified at **2B finish (Ra 0.3–0.5 µm)** or **No. 4 finish (Ra 0.4–0.8 µm)** for hygienic food contact.

- [x] **Rolling resistance is ~100–1000× smaller than sliding friction.** This is why ball bearings and rollers are used in food machinery.

- [x] **Real contact area << Apparent contact area** (often <1%). This is the key to understanding friction at the microscopic level.

- [x] **Cold welding of asperities** is the fundamental atomic-level origin of dry friction.

- [x] **In food texture analyzers (TA instruments)**, rolling and sliding resistance of gels, doughs, and pastes are measured to correlate with sensory mouthfeel properties.

- [x] **Stribeck curve minimum** occurs at the transition from boundary to mixed regime — this is the **design target** for efficient food processing machinery.

- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** Always check units — convert all dimensions to consistent units (SI preferred) before substitution.

- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** The offset **a** in the rolling resistance formula is in **meters**, not mm or µm, when used in the SI equation F_rr = (a/r)·W.

- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** When the problem says "smooth" or "lubricated," it implies a transition from boundary to mixed/full-film regime on the Stribeck curve — sketch this in your answer for full marks.

- [x] **KTU 2024 Relevance:** This topic is typically examined in Module 2 of Engineering Mechanics / Tribology courses. Expect 3-mark direct questions and 7-mark derivations with a numerical component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of repose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Angle of repose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angle of Repose ($\theta_r$):** The angle of repose is the **steepest angle measured from the horizontal plane** at which a pile of granular or powdered food material remains stable and does not collapse under the influence of gravity. Mathematically, it is the angle of the free surface of the piled bulk solid with respect to the horizontal plane when the pile is formed by carefully pouring the material from a fixed height onto a flat horizontal base.

In the context of food technology, the angle of repose is a **critical rheological and bulk property** that governs:
- The **flowability** of powders, granules, and crystalline food materials.
- The **design of storage silos, hoppers, conveyors, and packaging lines**.
- The **prediction of caking, segregation, and discharge problems** in bulk food handling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Notation:** The angle of repose is universally denoted by the Greek letter $\theta$ (theta), and its typical range for food materials is **20° – 60°**. Lower values indicate free-flowing materials; higher values indicate cohesive, sticky, or cohesive materials.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Real-World Picture

> [!TIP]
> **The "Sandcastle Slope" Analogy:** Imagine you are building a sandcastle on the beach. You can pile wet sand upward only up to a certain steepness before the sides start sliding off. That critical slope — the one your pile naturally "wants" to maintain — is exactly the **angle of repose**. Dry, rounded grains (like polished rice) form very shallow piles (low angle), while rough, moist, or irregular grains (like crushed jaggery powder) form steeper, taller piles (high angle).

### Real-World Food Examples Visualized

| Food Material | Approx. Angle of Repose | Flow Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Refined Wheat Flour | 45° – 55° | Moderately free-flowing |
| Granulated White Sugar | 36° – 42° | Excellent flow |
| Polished Rice Grains | 30° – 38° | Highly free-flowing |
| Cocoa Powder | 50° – 65° | Cohesive, sticky |
| Coarse Salt Crystals | 35° – 40° | Excellent flow |
| Icing Sugar (Confectioner's) | 60° – 70° | Highly cohesive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** A KTU examiner will often test whether the student understands that the angle of repose is a **bulk property** (depends on the whole pile), not an individual particle property. It emerges from the **collective interaction** of thousands of particles through inter-particle friction, cohesion, and gravity.

---

## 1.3 Physical Interpretation in the KTU Framework

The angle of repose is essentially a **macroscopic manifestation of inter-particle friction and cohesion**. When a granular food material is poured:

- Individual particles experience **gravitational force** (pulling them down).
- Particles in contact experience **normal forces** and **frictional forces** from neighboring particles.
- When the slope exceeds $\theta_r$, the **gravitational shear component** overcomes the **static friction** between particles, and the pile avalanches down to a new, stable angle.

> **SI Unit:** Dimensionless (since it is the ratio of two lengths, or simply an angle in degrees/radians).
>
> **Standard Range for Food Powders:** $\mathbf{20^\circ \leq \theta_r \leq 70^\circ}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a stable pile at the angle of repose obeys the following equilibrium sequence:

1. **Pouring Phase:** The granular food material falls from a fixed height through a funnel onto a flat, horizontal circular base plate.
2. **Pile Growth:** Particles accumulate at the apex and roll down the sides due to gravity.
3. **Critical Angle Reached:** The slope of the pile steepens until it reaches a **critical angle** $\theta_r$. Beyond this, particles can no longer maintain static equilibrium.
4. **Avalanche Equilibrium:** Particles roll off when the **down-slope gravitational force component** equals the **static frictional resistance** between particles.
5. **Stable Cone Formation:** The pile settles into a stable conical heap with its base diameter $D$ and height $h$ fixed.

> The geometry of the stable cone directly yields the angle of repose through the relation: $\tan(\theta_r) = \dfrac{h}{r} = \dfrac{2h}{D}$

---

## 2.2 Standardized Methods of Measurement (KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlights)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies the following measurement methods. Each yields slightly different values of $\theta_r$ for the same material, so the **method must always be reported** alongside the numerical value.

### Method 1 — Poured (Static) Heap Method (Fixed Funnel)
- A funnel is fixed at a specific height $H$ above a flat circular base.
- Material is poured through the funnel until a stable cone is formed.
- **Equation:** $\tan(\theta_r) = \dfrac{h}{r}$
- *Most commonly used in KTU labs and Indian food industry.*

### Method 2 — Drained (Hollow) Heap Method
- A circular dish with a central hole is filled with the material.
- Material drains out through the hole, leaving a hollow conical pile.
- $\tan(\theta_r) = \dfrac{h}{r}$ where $h$ and $r$ are measured after drainage.

### Method 3 — Tilting Box Method
- A rectangular box is filled with the material and slowly tilted.
- The material surface tilts with the box; when the surface starts to slide, the angle of tilt is the angle of repose.

### Method 4 — Revolving Cylinder Method
- A horizontal cylinder is half-filled with material and rotated slowly.
- The surface forms an angle as the cylinder rotates; this is the dynamic angle of repose.

### Method 5 — Fixed Cone Method
- A cone is placed in the center of a flat plate covered with material.
- The cone is lifted, and the resulting pile's angle is measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** "Mention the method used" carries 1 mark in most KTU answers. A student who simply writes the formula without stating the method loses that mark.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\theta_r) = \dfrac{h}{r}$ | Height $h$ over base radius $r$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\tan(\theta_r) = \dfrac{2h}{D}$ | Height $h$ over base diameter $D$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_r = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{r}\right)$ | Direct computation of angle | **Degrees** (20°–70°) |
| 4 | $\mu_s = \tan(\theta_r)$ | Static coefficient of friction (approx.) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_r \approx 45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | Empirical — where $\phi$ is internal friction angle | Degrees |
| 6 | $V_{\text{cone}} = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ | Volume of the conical pile | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | $M_{\text{cone}} = \rho_b \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ | Mass of the pile ($\rho_b$ = bulk density) | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol was deliberately avoided in the table above (using `\dfrac` and `\vert`-style notation instead) to prevent Markdown table syntax corruption, as mandated by the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 protocol.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting the Angle of Repose (Real-World Utility)

The angle of repose is **not a constant** — it varies with the following engineering and material parameters. KTU questions frequently ask to "list and explain factors" for 5–7 marks.

| Factor | Effect on $\theta_r$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Particle size** | Smaller particles → higher $\theta_r$ | Fine flour has higher $\theta_r$ than coarse sugar |
| **Particle shape** | Angular/irregular → higher $\theta_r$ | Crushed spices pile steeper than spherical salt |
| **Moisture content** | Higher moisture → higher $\theta_r$ (up to a limit) | Wet grains stick and form steeper piles |
| **Surface roughness** | Rougher particles → higher $\theta_r$ | Bran-coated rice has higher $\theta_r$ than polished rice |
| **Bulk density** | Higher density → typically lower $\theta_r$ | Denser material flows more freely |
| **Cohesion** | More cohesive → much higher $\theta_r$ | Cocoa powder vs. sugar |
| **Pour height** | Higher drop → lower $\theta_r$ (impact compaction) | Critical for hopper design |
| **Temperature** | Affects moisture → indirect effect | Warm storage reduces $\theta_r$ of hygroscopic powders |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility (Engineering Field):** Food processing engineers use $\theta_r$ to design **gravity-fed silos** (walls must be steeper than $\theta_r$ to ensure discharge), to size **hopper openings** (smaller than the critical discharge diameter to prevent flooding), and to select appropriate **conveyor angles** (typically $\theta_r + 5°$ for safety margin) in food manufacturing plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Relationship $\tan(\theta_r) = h/r$

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 2 for this topic.

**Given:** A stable conical pile of granular food material with base radius $r$ and height $h$ on a flat horizontal surface.

**Objective:** To derive the relationship between $\theta_r$, $h$, and $r$.

**Setup:** Consider the right-angled triangle formed by the apex of the cone, the center of the base, and a point on the rim of the base.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \theta_r &= \text{ angle of repose} \\
\text{Let } h &= \text{ vertical height of the cone (opposite side)} \\
\text{Let } r &= \text{ radius of the base of the cone (adjacent side)} \\
\text{Let } L &= \text{ slant height of the cone (hypotenuse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

By the definition of a right-angled triangle and basic trigonometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_r) &= \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Adjacent side}} \\
\tan(\theta_r) &= \frac{h}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the angle explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_r &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If the **diameter** $D = 2r$ is used instead of the radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_r) &= \frac{h}{r} = \frac{h}{D/2} = \frac{2h}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume and Mass of the Pile

For mass-balance problems in KTU exams (e.g., "What is the mass of sugar in a conical heap with $h = 30$ cm and $D = 60$ cm?").

The pile is a **right circular cone**, so its volume is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

The mass of food material in the pile, given bulk density $\rho_b$ (in $\text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \rho_b \cdot V \\
M &= \rho_b \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> *A pile of refined wheat flour forms a cone of height $h = 0.40$ m and base diameter $D = 1.20$ m. If the bulk density of the flour is $\rho_b = 550 \text{ kg/m}^3$, find the angle of repose and the mass of flour in the pile.*

**Step 1 — Compute the radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{D}{2} = \frac{1.20}{2} = 0.60 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the angle of repose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_r) &= \frac{h}{r} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.6667 \\
\theta_r &= \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \\
\theta_r &\approx 33.69^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of the cone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h \\
V &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot (0.60)^2 \cdot (0.40) \\
V &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot 0.36 \cdot 0.40 \\
V &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot 0.144 \\
V &\approx 0.1508 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass of flour in the pile:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \rho_b \cdot V \\
M &= 550 \cdot 0.1508 \\
M &\approx 82.94 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\theta_r \approx 33.69^\circ$ and $M \approx 82.94 \text{ kg}$ of flour.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Angle of Repose and Static Friction

For a single particle resting on a slope of granular material, the **limiting equilibrium** condition gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Component of gravity along the slope (downslope)} &= mg \sin(\theta) \\
\text{Normal force on the slope} &= mg \cos(\theta) \\
\text{Maximum static friction force} &= \mu_s \cdot N = \mu_s \cdot mg \cos(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

At the critical angle $\theta_r$, the downslope force equals the maximum static friction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
mg \sin(\theta_r) &= \mu_s \cdot mg \cos(\theta_r) \\
\sin(\theta_r) &= \mu_s \cos(\theta_r) \\
\frac{\sin(\theta_r)}{\cos(\theta_r)} &= \mu_s \\
\tan(\theta_r) &= \mu_s
\end{aligned}
$$

This elegant result shows that **the angle of repose approximates the static angle of internal friction** for dry, non-cohesive granular food materials. For cohesive food powders (like cocoa or starch), this simple relation no longer holds because **inter-particle adhesion forces** become significant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For cohesive food powders**, the modified relation is:
> $$\tan(\theta_r) = \mu_s + \frac{C}{\sigma_n}$$
> where $C$ is the **cohesive strength** and $\sigma_n$ is the **normal stress** on the failure plane. This is the **Jenike's flow function** approach used in advanced silo design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Angle of Repose Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Granular Food Material"] --> B["Selection of Measurement Method"]
    B --> C1["Poured Heap Method"]
    B --> C2["Drained Hollow Method"]
    B --> C3["Tilting Box Method"]
    B --> C4["Revolving Cylinder Method"]
    C1 --> D1["Measure h and D"]
    C2 --> D2["Measure h and D"]
    C3 --> D3["Measure tilt angle"]
    C4 --> D4["Measure dynamic slope"]
    D1 --> E["Apply tan theta equals h over r"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["Compute theta r in degrees"]
    F --> G["Report theta r with method name"]
    G --> H1["Use in Silo Design"]
    G --> H2["Use in Hopper Geometry"]
    G --> H3["Use in Conveyor Slope Selection"]
    G --> H4["Use in Flowability Classification"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Factors Influencing $\theta_r$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Material Properties"] --> OUT["Angle of Repose theta r"]
    F2["Particle Size"] --> F1
    F3["Particle Shape"] --> F1
    F4["Moisture Content"] --> F1
    F5["Surface Roughness"] --> F1
    F6["Bulk Density"] --> F1
    F7["Internal Cohesion"] --> F1
    P1["Process Conditions"] --> OUT
    P2["Pour Height"] --> P1
    P3["Temperature"] --> P1
    P4["Vibration During Handling"] --> P1
    P5["Storage Duration"] --> P1
    OUT --> APP1["Storage Silo Wall Angle Design"]
    OUT --> APP2["Hopper Opening Diameter Sizing"]
    OUT --> APP3["Conveyor Inclination Selection"]
    OUT --> APP4["Powder Flowability Index"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description — The Conical Pile (Text-Based Visualization)

For KTU board-exam purposes, the angle of repose is best depicted as a **right-angled triangle embedded in a 3D cone**:

```
                  APEX
                   /\
                  / |\
                 /  | \
                / θr|h \
               /___|___\
            BASE DIAMETER = D
            RADIUS = D/2 = r
            HEIGHT = h
            θr = tan⁻¹(h/r)
```

- The **apex** of the cone is at the top.
- The **height $h$** is measured vertically from the base center to the apex.
- The **radius $r$** is measured horizontally from the base center to the rim.
- The **angle $\theta_r$** is at the base, between the slant surface and the horizontal base.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "angle of repose" and mention any two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

> The angle of repose is defined as the **maximum angle that the surface of a heap of granular material makes with the horizontal plane** when the heap is formed by pouring the material from a fixed height onto a flat surface.
>
> **Two factors affecting it:**
> 1. **Particle size and shape:** Finer and more irregular particles form steeper piles, increasing the angle of repose. Coarse, rounded particles form flatter piles.
> 2. **Moisture content:** An increase in moisture content (up to a critical level) increases cohesion between particles, leading to a higher angle of repose.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**A conical heap of corn flour has a base diameter of 0.80 m and a height of 0.30 m. Calculate the angle of repose. Given: $\tan^{-1}(0.75) \approx 36.87°$.**

**Model Answer:**

> Given: $D = 0.80$ m, $h = 0.30$ m.
> Radius $r = D/2 = 0.40$ m.
> $$\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{0.30}{0.40} = 0.75$$
> $$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}(0.75) \approx 36.87°$$
> **The angle of repose of corn flour is approximately 36.87°.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**(a)** Describe the **poured heap method** of determining the angle of repose of a granular food material. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pile of ground black pepper forms a cone of height 0.50 m and base diameter 1.00 m. The bulk density of the powder is 480 kg/m³. Calculate:
  1. The angle of repose.
  2. The volume of the pile.
  3. The mass of pepper in the pile. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Poured Heap Method (Fixed Funnel Method):**
>
> 1. A **funnel** is fixed at a specific height (typically 10–20 cm) above a **flat circular base plate** of known diameter.
> 2. The granular food sample is **poured slowly and steadily** through the funnel, allowing it to fall freely and accumulate on the base plate.
> 3. A **conical pile** gradually builds up. The pouring is continued until the pile reaches a stable height (further pouring causes material to roll off without increasing the height).
> 4. The **height $h$** of the pile is measured using a ruler from the apex perpendicular to the base.
> 5. The **base diameter $D$** is measured at two perpendicular directions and averaged to reduce error.
> 6. The angle of repose is calculated using:
>    $$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)$$
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Simple, inexpensive, requires no specialized equipment.
> 2. Suitable for routine quality control in food processing industries.
> 3. Reproducible if pouring rate and funnel height are standardized.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. Results depend on the **operator's pouring technique** (rate, height).
> 2. Not suitable for **highly cohesive** powders that form irregular heaps.
> 3. **Particle segregation** can occur during free fall, skewing measurements.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:** $h = 0.50$ m, $D = 1.00$ m, $\rho_b = 480 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
>
> **1. Angle of Repose:**
> $$\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{2h}{D} = \frac{2 \times 0.50}{1.00} = 1.00$$
> $$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}(1.00) = 45.00°$$
>
> **2. Volume of the Pile:**
> $$V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3} \pi \times (0.50)^2 \times 0.50$$
> $$V = \frac{1}{3} \pi \times 0.25 \times 0.50 = \frac{0.125 \pi}{3} \approx 0.1309 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **3. Mass of Pepper in the Pile:**
> $$M = \rho_b \cdot V = 480 \times 0.1309 \approx 62.83 \text{ kg}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**(a)** Explain the **significance of the angle of repose** in the design of food storage silos and hoppers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, derive the relationship $\tan(\theta_r) = h/r$ for the angle of repose of a granular food material. A sample of turmeric powder forms a heap of height 25 cm and base diameter 70 cm. Comment on the **flowability** of the powder. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Significance in Silo and Hopper Design:**
>
> 1. **Wall Inclination:** The walls of a hopper or silo must be inclined at an angle **steeper than the angle of repose** of the stored food material; otherwise, the material will rest on the walls and fail to discharge by gravity. The recommended design angle is typically $\theta_r + 5°$ to $10°$ as a safety margin.
>
> 2. **Hopper Opening Sizing:** For **mass-flow** discharge (uniform, first-in-first-out), the outlet diameter must exceed a **critical value** that depends on $\theta_r$, the bulk density, and the wall friction angle. An undersized opening causes **arching** (rat-holing) or **bridging**.
>
> 3. **Storage Capacity Estimation:** The angle of repose determines the **shape of the top surface** of the stored material. Engineers use $\theta_r$ to compute the **unused headspace volume** above the bulk material — this headspace must be accounted for in storage capacity calculations.
>
> 4. **Flowability Classification:** Materials with $\theta_r < 35°$ are **free-flowing**; $35° \leq \theta_r < 45°$ are **fairly free-flowing**; $45° \leq \theta_r < 55°$ are **cohesive**; and $\theta_r \geq 55°$ are **very cohesive**. This guides the choice of discharge aids (vibrators, air pads).
>
> 5. **Safety and Segregation:** Steep piles (high $\theta_r$) are more prone to **avalanching**, which can cause dust explosions in flour mills or cocoa processing plants. Proper design using $\theta_r$ mitigates this risk.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Sketch and Derivation:**
>
> (See Section 4.3 above for the schematic.)
>
> For a right circular cone of height $h$ and base radius $r$, the angle of repose $\theta_r$ is the angle between the slant surface and the horizontal base. By right-triangle trigonometry:
> $$\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{h}{r}$$
>
> **Numerical Application:**
> Given: $h = 25$ cm, $D = 70$ cm, so $r = 35$ cm.
> $$\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{25}{35} = 0.7143$$
> $$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}(0.7143) \approx 35.54°$$
>
> **Comment on Flowability:**
> Since $\theta_r \approx 35.54°$ falls in the range $35° \leq \theta_r < 45°$, the turmeric powder is classified as **fairly free-flowing** (moderately flowable). It can be handled in standard silos, but slight design care (slightly steeper hopper walls, perhaps a vibrator aid) is advisable to prevent intermittent arching.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (What KTU Examiners Penalize):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of $\theta_r$:** $\theta_r$ is an **angle in degrees** (or radians), not a length. Students who write "$36.87$" without the degree symbol typically lose **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 2. **Not stating the measurement method:** KTU answers must explicitly state which method is used (poured heap, drained heap, tilting box, etc.). A formula without a method loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing diameter and radius:** Using $D$ where $r$ is required (or vice versa) in the formula $\tan(\theta_r) = h/r$ is a **silly error** that costs full marks on the calculation sub-part.
> 4. **Ignoring bulk density in mass calculations:** For mass-of-pile problems, students sometimes forget to multiply volume by $\rho_b$, computing only the volume — this loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Writing "angle of friction" instead of "angle of repose":** These are related but **distinct** concepts. The angle of repose is for a heap in air; the angle of internal friction is measured by shear-cell tests on confined material. Examiners deduct for this confusion.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch:** For 7-mark derivations, a **labeled diagram** is mandatory. A derivation without a neat sketch loses **1–2 marks** even if the algebra is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Flash Card — Angle of Repose**

- **Definition:** Steepest stable angle of a granular heap with the horizontal. Symbol: $\theta_r$. Unit: degrees.
- **Range for food materials:** $\mathbf{20^\circ \text{ to } 70^\circ}$.
- **Master Formula:** $\tan(\theta_r) = h/r = 2h/D$.
- **Cone volume formula:** $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ (always required for mass problems).
- **Five standard measurement methods** (know at least 3 by name and procedure): Poured heap, Drained hollow, Tilting box, Revolving cylinder, Fixed cone.
- **Flowability rule of thumb:**
  - $\theta_r < 35°$ → free-flowing
  - $35° \leq \theta_r < 45°$ → fairly free-flowing
  - $45° \leq \theta_r < 55°$ → cohesive
  - $\theta_r \geq 55°$ → very cohesive (needs flow aids)
- **Top 5 factors affecting $\theta_r$:** Particle size ↓, particle shape (angular ↑), moisture content ↑, surface roughness ↑, cohesion ↑.
- **Friction linkage:** For dry, non-cohesive powders, $\tan(\theta_r) \approx \mu_s$ (static friction coefficient).
- **Engineering uses:** Silo wall design (use $\theta_r + 5°$ to $10°$ safety margin), hopper outlet sizing, conveyor inclination, flowability classification, headspace volume estimation.
- **Cohesive powder correction:** $\tan(\theta_r) = \mu_s + C/\sigma_n$ (Jenike's flow function) — used in advanced silo design.
- **Always report** the method of measurement alongside the numerical value of $\theta_r$.
- **Always include** a labeled diagram in any 7-mark derivation question.
- **Always specify** the unit of $\theta_r$ in degrees (°).
- **Conversion reminder:** $1$ radian $= 57.296°$ — only relevant for very advanced problems.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Angle of Repose = Heap Geometry + Particle Friction + Cohesion. Measure it, report the method, draw the diagram, and you will score full marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of internal friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Angle of internal friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Angle of Internal Friction** (denoted by the Greek symbol **φ**, *phi*) is a fundamental mechanical property of granular and powdered food materials that quantifies the resistance of the material to shear deformation arising from inter-particle friction. It is formally defined as the angle between the **Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope** and the normal stress axis (σ-axis) in shear stress–normal stress (τ–σ) space.

Mathematically, the angle of internal friction is the angle whose tangent equals the slope of the Mohr-Coulomb yield locus:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\tau - c}{\sigma}$$

where:
- **τ** = Shear stress at failure (Pa or N/m²)
- **c** = Cohesion of the material (Pa or N/m²)
- **σ** = Applied normal stress (Pa or N/m²)
- **φ** = Angle of internal friction (degrees)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In food technology, this property is critical for the design of storage silos, hoppers, conveying systems, and packaging machinery handling dry particulate foods like **flour, sugar, milk powder, spices, instant coffee, and salt**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sandcastle Principle"

Imagine building a sandcastle on a beach. The pile of sand naturally assumes a stable conical shape at a particular steepness. If you try to make the sides steeper, the sand slides down. The maximum slope the pile can sustain before sliding is the **angle of internal friction**. 

Now apply this to a food powder silo:
- When a granular food like **wheat flour** is stored in a hopper, the particles "lock" against each other.
- The angle of internal friction describes how steeply the powder can be piled before particles slide past one another.
- A **high φ** (e.g., 45°) means particles interlock strongly → stable piles but poor flowability.
- A **low φ** (e.g., 20°) means particles slide easily → excellent flowability but unstable heaps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Common Food Powders:**
> - Wheat flour: **40°–45°**
> - Granulated sugar: **35°–40°**
> - Skim milk powder: **40°–50°**
> - Instant coffee: **45°–55°**
> - Fine salt: **35°–45°**
> - Rice grains: **30°–38°**

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Food Engineering

The angle of internal friction is a **dimensionless** quantity expressed in degrees (°) and is independent of particle size distribution, although it is heavily influenced by:
- **Moisture content** (↑ moisture → ↓ φ due to lubrication)
