> 1. **Confusing firmness with hardness:** Many students interchange these terms. Remember: Firmness = slope (N/mm), Hardness = peak (N). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to state units:** Always mention SI units (N, N/mm, Pa, MPa). A numerically correct answer without units loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Using sample area instead of probe area:** The contact area is the **probe's cross-sectional area**, not the sample's. This is a very common error.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up cohesiveness formula:** It is $A_2 / A_1$ (second to first), NOT $A_1 / A_2$. The second area is always smaller because the structure has been disrupted.
> 
> 5. **Not specifying test conditions:** When describing TPA, always mention test speed (1 mm/s), trigger force (5 g), probe type, and percentage strain.
> 
> 6. **Confusing springiness with elasticity:** Springiness is measured on the time axis (T₂/T₁), not as a modulus.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Hardness** = **Peak force** at the **first compression** in a TPA test, measured in **N**.
- ✅ **Firmness** = **Slope** of the **initial linear region** of the force-deformation curve, measured in **N/mm**, OR Young's Modulus in **Pa/MPa**.
- ✅ Firmness is measured *before* rupture; Hardness is measured *at* rupture.
- ✅ TPA uses **two compression cycles** to compute all primary texture parameters.
- ✅ Standard test conditions: **Test speed 1 mm/s, trigger force 5 g, 25% strain** for soft foods.
- ✅ **Modulus of Firmness (Young's Modulus):** $E = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A \cdot \Delta L}$
- ✅ **Hardness formula:** $H = F_{\text{peak, 1st cycle}}$
- ✅ **Cohesiveness formula:** $\text{Coh} = A_2 / A_1$ (always < 1)
- ✅ **Springiness formula:** $S = T_2 / T_1$
- ✅ **Chewiness formula:** $C = H \times \text{Coh} \times S$
- ✅ Probe contact area is used in stress calculation, NOT sample area.
- ✅ Firmness and hardness are both **primary texture attributes** as per Szczesniak's classification.
- ✅ Instrumental (mechanical) methods are objective; sensory methods are subjective but consumer-realistic.
- ✅ Common instruments: **Texture Analyzer, Penetrometer, Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**.
- ✅ Common food applications: **Fruit grading, cheese classification, biscuit quality, meat tenderness**.
- ✅ The unit for stress in SI is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**; the practical unit is often **kPa** or **MPa**.
- ✅ A higher Young's Modulus → **firmer** food; a higher peak force → **harder** food.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanics of hardness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanics of hardness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness (Food Texture Attribute):** Hardness is a *primary mechanical textural attribute* defined as the **force required to deform a food product to a given deformation, or alternatively, the force required to bite through, compress, or penetrate a food sample with the molar teeth (or equivalent mechanical probe).**

In the framework of **Food Material Science**, hardness is a manifestation of a solid food's resistance to localized plastic deformation, indentation, or structural failure. It is a direct outcome of the internal cellular, fibrous, or amorphous matrix structure of the food, governed by the inter-molecular and inter-cellular bonding forces.

According to the **Szczesniak Classification (1963)** of texture, hardness is one of the **five primary texture parameters** evaluated instrumentally in the **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**, alongside cohesiveness, adhesiveness, springiness, and gumminess/chewiness.

From a **rheological and mechanical standpoint**, hardness is expressed as a maximum peak force on a force–deformation (or force–time) curve obtained during the first compression cycle, when a food is subjected to uniaxial compression between parallel plates, or penetration by an indentor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you have two food samples — a slice of soft bread and a piece of raw carrot. When you press your thumb against the bread, it yields easily with little force. When you press against the carrot, you must apply substantial force to deform it. That **resistance to that initial thumb-press** is *hardness*.

A second analogy: Think of hardness as the **"first-bite resistance"** of a food. The harder a food, the more work your jaw muscles must do on the first bite. Engineers translate this human "first-bite" sensation into measurable mechanical units — typically **Newtons (N)** or **grams-force (gf)**.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics for Hardness (board-exam relevant):**
> - **Unit of Force:** Newton (**N**) in SI; gram-force (**gf**) in CGS.
> - **Unit of Stress (σ):** Pascal (**Pa**) = **N/m²** = **kg·m⁻¹·s⁻²**.
> - **Standard Probe Geometries:** Cylindrical, spherical, conical, wedge-shaped.
> - **Standard Crosshead Speeds:** **1 mm/s**, **2 mm/s**, **10 mm/s** (depending on test).
> - **Typical Sample Geometry:** Cylinder of known diameter and height, or a uniform cube.

## 1.4 Syllabus-Highlight Callout

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2 Takeaway:**
> The mechanics of hardness bridges three domains: (1) **Material Science** (stress–strain behaviour), (2) **Instrumental Analysis** (force–deformation curves on a texture analyzer), and (3) **Sensory Science** (correlating the instrumental force with the human perception of hardness). All KTU questions will test at least two of these three domains.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanical Foundation: Stress, Strain & Deformation

When an external force is applied to a food sample, the food undergoes deformation. The mechanics of hardness describe this process through three fundamental quantities.

### 2.1.1 Stress (σ) — *The Internal Resistance Per Unit Area*

When a force **F** is applied perpendicular (normal) to a cross-sectional area **A** of a food sample, the **normal stress (σ)** generated inside the material is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A}
$$

- **σ** = Normal stress (Pa or N/m²)
- **F** = Applied force (N)
- **A** = Original cross-sectional area perpendicular to force (m²)

If the force is applied parallel to the surface (as in a cutting test), the relevant quantity is **shear stress (τ)**:

$$
\tau = \frac{F_{shear}}{A}
$$

### 2.1.2 Strain (ε) — *The Relative Deformation*

Strain is a **dimensionless** measure of how much the food deforms relative to its original dimension:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{0}} = \frac{L - L_{0}}{L_{0}}
$$

- **ΔL** = Change in length (m)
- **L₀** = Original length (m)
- **L** = Length after deformation (m)

### 2.1.3 The Stress–Strain Relationship — Hooke's Law (Elastic Region)

In the **linear elastic region** of a food's stress–strain curve, the material obeys Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

The constant of proportionality **E** is the **Young's Modulus of Elasticity (Pa)** — a direct measure of the *stiffness* of a food. A high E means the food is very rigid (high hardness), while a low E means it is soft.

## 2.2 The Force–Deformation Curve — The Heart of Hardness Testing

The **force–deformation curve** (also called the force–time curve during a controlled-speed compression) is the primary experimental output of a hardness test. A generic curve for a solid food has the following features:

1. **Initial Linear Region (Elastic Zone):** Force rises linearly with deformation; if the load is removed, the food returns to its original shape. Slope = **stiffness**.
2. **Yield Point (σ_y):** The first point where plastic (permanent) deformation begins.
3. **Fracture / Failure Point (σ_f):** The maximum stress the food can withstand. **Hardness in compression tests is typically read as the maximum force on this curve.**
4. **Post-Failure Region:** The food breaks/cracks; force drops sharply (brittle) or gradually (tough/fibrous).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (frequently tested):**
> - **Brittle foods** (e.g., crackers, raw carrots) — fracture with little plastic deformation; failure is sudden.
> - **Plastic/Ductile foods** (e.g., cheese, butter) — deform substantially before fracture.
> - **Elastic foods** (e.g., marshmallow, bread) — show large reversible deformation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table summarises every equation you will need for hardness-related problems in the KTU examination.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = F / A$ | F = force; A = area | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = F_{\text{shear}} / A$ | Force parallel to surface | Pa |
| 3 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_{0}$ | ΔL = change in length | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Hooke's Law (Elastic) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | E = Young's modulus | Pa |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Stiffness of food | Pa |
| 6 | Hardness (Compression) | $H = F_{\max} / A$ | F_max = peak force | Pa (true hardness) |
| 7 | Engineering Hardness | $H_{\text{eng}} = F_{\max}$ | Peak force reading | N (instrumental) |
| 8 | Shear Modulus | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | γ = shear strain | Pa |
| 9 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V \cdot \Delta P / \Delta V$ | Compressibility | Pa |
| 10 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{trans}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Lateral vs axial strain | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Modulus Resilience | $U_{r} = \sigma_{y}^{2} / (2E)$ | Energy to yield point | J/m³ |
| 12 | Toughness (Area) | $T = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{f}} \sigma \cdot d\varepsilon$ | Area under σ-ε curve | J/m³ |

**Useful constraints (rarely needed but examinable):**

- For **isotropic linear elastic materials:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$.
- For **incompressible materials** (most wet foods): $\nu \approx 0.5$, hence $E \approx 3G$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

The mechanics of hardness is foundational in:

1. **Product Development & Reformulation** — replacing sugar/fat in biscuits while maintaining bite-feel.
2. **Quality Control (QC)** — ensuring batch-to-batch uniformity in snack foods, breakfast cereals, and confectionery.
3. **Shelf-Life Studies** — monitoring hardness changes during staling of bread, softening of cookies, or firming of vegetables during post-harvest storage.
4. **Mouth-Feel Engineering** — designing foods for specific consumer groups (e.g., soft foods for the elderly, harder foods for dental development).
5. **Process Optimisation** — correlating extrusion, baking, and cooking parameters to final product hardness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Force–Deformation Data to a Hardness Value

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 board exam. The question is typically phrased as: *"Given a force–deformation curve for a cylindrical food sample, calculate the hardness in Pa."*

### Problem Set-Up (Assumed)

A cylindrical food sample (e.g., a slice of processed cheese) of:
- Initial height: $L_{0} = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- Initial diameter: $d = 25 \text{ mm}$, hence radius $r = 12.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$
- Compressed in a uniaxial compression test (parallel-plate geometry).
- The recorded peak force at fracture: $F_{\max} = 48 \text{ N}$.

**Required:** Compute the **true hardness** $H$ in Pa.

### Step 1 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area

The area of the circular face of the cylinder is:

$$
A = \pi r^{2}
$$

Substituting $r = 0.0125 \text{ m}$:

$$
A = \pi \cdot (0.0125)^{2}
$$

$$
A = \pi \cdot (1.5625 \times 10^{-4}) \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
A = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

> **Logic:** The area is the surface on which the force acts; dividing force by area converts the raw force reading into a material property (stress) that is independent of sample size.

### Step 2 — Apply the Hardness Formula

By definition, the **compressive hardness** is the maximum normal stress the sample can sustain:

$$
H = \frac{F_{\max}}{A}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
H = \frac{48 \text{ N}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}}
$$

$$
H = 97{,}780 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
H \approx 97.8 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Logic:** This number means that each square metre of the cheese face can sustain ~97.8 kN of force before failure. Such a value is typical for soft-to-medium firm cheeses.

## 3.2 Derivation: Young's Modulus from the Force–Deformation Curve

Suppose the linear elastic region of the same sample shows:
- At $F = 10 \text{ N}$, the deformation is $\Delta L = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$.
- Sample area $A = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$ (from above).
- Initial height $L_{0} = 0.020 \text{ m}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Stress

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{10}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma = 20{,}370 \text{ Pa}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Strain

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{0}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.020}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.025
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Young's Modulus

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{20{,}370}{0.025}
$$

$$
E = 814{,}800 \text{ Pa} \approx 815 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Logic:** $E$ quantifies the intrinsic stiffness of the cheese. Note that hardness (H) and stiffness (E) are related but **not identical**: hardness is the stress at *failure*, while stiffness is the slope in the *elastic* region. A food can be stiff (high E) but not hard (low H) if it fractures early — e.g., a crisp cracker.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Relating Force–Deformation Curve Features to Food Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision tool**. Memorise the mapping — examiners frequently ask: *"Which feature of the curve corresponds to hardness?"*

| Curve Feature | Symbol / Reading | Mechanical Meaning | Textural Interpretation |
|---------------|------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| Slope of linear elastic region | $\tan\theta$ | **Stiffness** (related to E) | How "rigid" the food feels |
| Peak height (first bite) | $F_{\max}$ | **Hardness** (compressive) | Resistance to first bite |
| Force at the start of 2nd compression | $F_{2}$ | Cohesiveness-related | How well the food holds together |
| Distance to 2nd peak | $\Delta L_{2}$ | **Springiness** | How much the food springs back |
| Negative force (first cycle) | Area | Adhesiveness | Stickiness to palate/probe |
| Area under curve (1st cycle) | $\int F \, dL$ | Toughness / work of compression | Energy required to chew |

## 3.4 Derivation: Hardness from an Indentation Test (Brinell-type for Foods)

For penetration tests, hardness is sometimes reported as the **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)**, adapted to foods:

$$
\text{BHN}_{\text{food}} = \frac{2 F_{\max}}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \left( D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}} \right)}
$$

Where:
- $F_{\max}$ = applied peak force (N)
- $D$ = indenter ball diameter (m)
- $d$ = diameter of the residual indentation (m)

> **Logic:** The denominator accounts for the curved surface area of the spherical cap indentation. This is rarely computed numerically in KTU exams but the *concept* is frequently asked: *"Why is the Brinell formula more complex than $F/A$?"* — Answer: because the contact area in a spherical indentation grows with load, unlike a flat punch.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Hardness Test Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *complete functional flow* of a hardness measurement — from sample preparation to data interpretation. This is the safest visual representation for a board answer and avoids any risk of malformed drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Sample Preparation] --> B[Step2: Dimensional Measurement L0 and diameter]
    B --> C[Step3: Texture Analyzer Calibration]
    C --> D[Step4: Probe Selection Cylinder, Sphere, or Punch]
    D --> E[Step5: Test Parameter Set Crosshead Speed, Strain Limit]
    E --> F[Step6: Run Uniaxial Compression Test]
    F --> G[Step7: Record Force Deformation Curve]
    G --> H[Step8: Identify Fmax from Peak]
    H --> I[Step9: Compute Area of Cross Section]
    I --> J[Step10: Calculate Hardness H = Fmax over A]
    J --> K[Step11: Report Value in Pa or N with Sample Geometry]
    K --> L[Step12: Compare with Reference and Sensory Panel]
    L --> M[Step13: Conclude on Food Texture Quality]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB
    style F fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B
    style H fill:#FDE8E8,stroke:#DC2626
    style J fill:#E8FDE8,stroke:#16A34A
    style M fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9333EA
```

> **How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
> - **Blue block (Step 1):** Standardisation phase.
> - **Orange block (Step 6):** The actual mechanical test.
> - **Red block (Step 8):** Identification of the peak — the "hardness moment".
> - **Green block (Step 10):** The mathematical definition of hardness.
> - **Purple block (Step 13):** Linking instrument to human perception.

## 4.2 Schematic: Force–Deformation Curve with Annotated Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Origin Zero Force, Zero Deformation] --> P2[Linear Elastic Region Stiffness Equals Slope]
    P2 --> P3[Yield Point Onset of Plastic Deformation]
    P3 --> P4[Fracture Point Fmax Equals Hardness Reading]
    P4 --> P5[Post Failure Region Force Drops]
    P5 --> P6[Complete Failure Sample Fractured]

    style P1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    style P2 fill:#DDEEFF,stroke:#2563EB
    style P3 fill:#FFF1B2,stroke:#CA8A04
    style P4 fill:#FECACA,stroke:#DC2626
    style P5 fill:#E5E7EB,stroke:#6B7280
    style P6 fill:#1F2937,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

> **Reading guide for board answers:**
> - The **slope of P1 → P2** is *stiffness* (Young's modulus).
> - The **height of P4** is *hardness*.
> - The **area under the entire curve** is *work of fracture* or *toughness*.

## 4.3 Schematic: Three Categories of Food Failure Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mechanics of Food Failure] --> B[Brittle Mode]
    M --> P[Plastic Mode]
    M --> V[Viscoelastic Mode]

    B --> B1[Examples: Carrot, Cracker, Popcorn]
    B --> B2[Curve: Sharp peak then sudden drop]

    P --> P1[Examples: Cheese, Butter, Dough]
    P --> P2[Curve: Gradual rise, broad peak, slow drop]

    V --> V1[Examples: Bread, Marshmallow, Gelatin]
    V --> V2[Curve: Large deformation, no clear peak]

    style M fill:#1F2937,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#FECACA,stroke:#DC2626
    style P fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#F59E0B
    style V fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#16A34A
```

> **Why this matters for hardness:**
> - The **shape of the failure curve** determines *how* hardness should be reported.
> - Brittle foods → take $F_{\max}$ directly as hardness.
> - Plastic foods → hardness is the force at a defined strain (e.g., 25% compression).
> - Viscoelastic foods → hardness is poorly defined; springiness and chewiness dominate the TPA.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define hardness as a textural attribute of food. How is it measured instrumentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hardness is a primary textural attribute defined as the **force required to deform a food product to a given deformation, or the force required to bite through or compress a food sample between the molars** (1 mark). It is a measure of the food's resistance to compression, indentation, or cutting (1 mark). Instrumentally, hardness is measured using a **texture analyser** by performing a uniaxial compression test between parallel plates or using a penetrating probe; the **peak force ($F_{\max}$) recorded on the force–deformation curve** is taken as the instrumental hardness, expressed in **Newtons (N)** (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between stiffness and hardness of a food material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Stiffness** is the resistance of a food to **elastic (recoverable) deformation** and is quantified by the **Young's modulus (E)**, which is the slope of the linear elastic region of the stress–strain curve (1.5 marks). **Hardness** is the **force or stress required to cause permanent deformation or fracture** and is read as the **peak force ($F_{\max}$)** on the force–deformation curve, or as the maximum stress $H = F_{\max}/A$ (1.5 marks). A food can be stiff (high E) but not hard (low fracture force) — e.g., a crisp cracker — or hard but not very stiff — e.g., a tough meat.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

*(a)* Define hardness and stiffness. With a neat sketch of a typical force–deformation curve for a solid food, identify the regions that correspond to stiffness, yield point, hardness, and toughness. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A cylindrical sample of processed cheese (diameter 30 mm, height 25 mm) is subjected to a uniaxial compression test. The peak force recorded at fracture is 65 N. The slope of the linear elastic region of the force–deformation curve is 1.2 N/mm. Calculate: (i) the compressive hardness in kPa, and (ii) the Young's modulus of the cheese in kPa. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Hardness (1 mark):** Hardness is the maximum force (or stress) required to deform a food to a specified deformation, or to cause fracture, as measured in a compression or biting test. It is read as the **peak force $F_{\max}$** on the force–deformation curve.

**Stiffness (1 mark):** Stiffness is the resistance of a food to elastic deformation. It is quantified by the **Young's modulus E** (or the slope of the initial linear portion of the force–deformation curve).

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw a force–deformation curve with the following labelled features:

- **Origin O:** Zero force, zero deformation.
- **Linear elastic region (O to A):** Slope = stiffness; ends at yield point A.
- **Yield point A:** Onset of plastic (permanent) deformation.
- **Hardness point B (peak):** Force at this point = $F_{\max}$ = hardness.
- **Post-peak region (B to C):** Force decreases; the food fractures.
- **Area under O–A–B–C:** Represents toughness (energy to fracture).

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Curve Feature | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Stiffness | Slope of O–A | Resistance to elastic deformation |
| Yield point | Tip of O–A | Transition from elastic to plastic |
| Hardness | Height of B | Resistance to fracture |
| Toughness | Area under O–A–B–C | Energy absorbed before failure |

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$, so radius $r = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- Initial height $L_0 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Peak force $F_{\max} = 65 \text{ N}$
- Slope of elastic region = $1.2 \text{ N/mm} = 1200 \text{ N/m}$

**(i) Compressive Hardness in kPa (3.5 marks):**

Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.015)^{2} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 2 — Hardness:

$$
H = \frac{F_{\max}}{A} = \frac{65}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
H = 91{,}950 \text{ Pa} \approx 91.95 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Young's Modulus in kPa (3.5 marks):**

The slope of the force–deformation curve in the elastic region equals:

$$
k = \frac{F}{\Delta L} = 1200 \text{ N/m}
$$

For a cylindrical sample under uniaxial compression:

$$
E = \frac{k \cdot L_0}{A}
$$

Substituting:

$$
E = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.025}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{30}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 42{,}440 \text{ Pa} \approx 42.44 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Cross-check (logical reasoning for partial credit):**
> The Young's modulus (42.44 kPa) is much lower than the hardness (91.95 kPa). This is consistent with a soft, plastic food like processed cheese, where substantial plastic deformation occurs before fracture.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

*(a)* Explain the Szczesniak classification of texture. List the five primary textural attributes and identify which one corresponds to hardness. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A force–deformation test on a carrot cylinder (diameter 20 mm, height 30 mm) yields the following data: at a deformation of 0.5 mm the force is 12 N (still in the elastic region); the peak force at fracture is 95 N. Calculate: (i) the engineering strain at the elastic data point, (ii) the stress at that point, and (iii) the hardness of the carrot in MPa. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Szczesniak Classification (3 marks):** Developed by **Anne D. Szczesniak in 1963**, this is the most widely accepted classification of food texture. She classified textural properties into three groups:

1. **Mechanical characteristics** — properties related to the response of food to stress (e.g., hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, adhesiveness, viscosity, brittleness, chewiness, gumminess).
2. **Geometrical characteristics** — properties related to the size, shape, and arrangement of particles (e.g., graininess, fibrousness, crumbliness).
3. **Other (mouth-feel) characteristics** — related to moisture and fat content (e.g., oiliness, moistness, dryness).

**Five Primary Texture Attributes (3 marks):**

| # | Attribute | Definition |
|---|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **Hardness** | Force required to compress food between molars or teeth |
| 2 | **Cohesiveness** | Degree to which food holds together |
| 3 | **Adhesiveness** | Force required to remove food sticking to mouth/probe |
| 4 | **Springiness** | Degree to which food returns to original shape after partial compression |
| 5 | **Gumminess / Chewiness** | Energy required to chew a solid food to a swallowable state |

**Hardness corresponds to the first attribute (1 mark).**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$, hence $r = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- $L_0 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$
- Elastic data point: $\Delta L = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0005 \text{ m}$, $F = 12 \text{ N}$
- Peak (fracture) force: $F_{\max} = 95 \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (used throughout):**

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.010)^{2} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(i) Engineering Strain (2 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.0005}{0.030}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.01667 \approx 1.667 \times 10^{-2} \;(\text{dimensionless})
$$

**(ii) Stress at the Elastic Point (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{12}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma = 38{,}197 \text{ Pa} \approx 38.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(iii) Hardness in MPa (3 marks):**

$$
H = \frac{F_{\max}}{A} = \frac{95}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
H = 302{,}400 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
H = 0.3024 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Logical interpretation (1 mark, optional):**
> A carrot hardness of ~0.30 MPa classifies it as a **brittle, high-hardness vegetable** — consistent with its crisp, snap-like fracture behaviour. The very small strain (1.67%) confirms the brittleness (no plastic deformation).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Hardness Problems — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):**
>    Students frequently write hardness as $F_{\max}$ in Newtons *without* dividing by area. Remember: **instrumental force** is in **N**, but **hardness as a material property** is in **Pa** (N/m²). Always compute $A$ and divide.
>
> 2. **Confusing Force with Stress (–1 mark):**
>    "Hardness of cheese = 48 N" is **wrong**. "Hardness = 97.8 kPa" is **correct**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Stiffness and Hardness (–2 marks):**
>    Stiffness uses the **slope** of the elastic region; Hardness uses the **peak** of the curve. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Factor of $\pi r^2$ (–1 mark):**
>    A surprising number of students use $A = d^{2}$ or $A = 2\pi r$ (circumference). Use $A = \pi r^{2}$ for circular cross-section.
>
> 5. **Not Reporting Units in kPa or MPa (–0.5 mark):**
>    A bare number without units is incomplete. Always include **Pa / kPa / MPa**.
>
> 6. **Confusing "Young's modulus" formula:**
>    Use $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (not $E = F / \Delta L$). The latter is *stiffness* $k$, not modulus. Convert: $E = k L_0 / A$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mechanics of Hardness (Module 2)**

- **Definition (1 line):** Hardness = peak force (or peak stress) on the force–deformation curve during a uniaxial compression of a food.
- **Core formula:** $H = F_{\max} / A$ with units in **Pa**.
- **Stiffness ≠ Hardness:** Stiffness is the *slope* of the elastic region (related to $E$); Hardness is the *peak* of the curve.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ holds only in the **elastic region**.
- **Young's Modulus (stiffness indicator):** $E = \sigma / \varepsilon = (F/A) / (\Delta L / L_0)$.
- **Three failure modes:** Brittle (sharp peak), Plastic (broad peak), Viscoelastic (no clear peak).
- **Hardness categories:** Soft (< 100 kPa), Medium (100 kPa – 1 MPa), Hard (> 1 MPa).
- **Szczesniak's five primary attributes:** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess/Chewiness.
- **Standard test:** Texture Analyser (e.g., TA-XT2, TA-XT.plus) with parallel-plate or probe geometry.
- **Critical for KTU 2024:** Always (i) compute area first, (ii) apply correct formula, (iii) report answer with units, (iv) state whether stiffness or hardness is being asked.
- **Mnemonic for the four curve features in order:** "**S**lope = **S**tiffness, **P**eak = **P**ain (Hardness), **A**rea = toughness, **B**ounce-back = springiness." — (S-P-A-B)
- **Sensory correlation:** High instrumental hardness ↔ "tough / crunchy / firm / chewy" mouth-feel.
- **Don't confuse:** Force (N) vs Stress (Pa) vs Modulus (Pa). All three have different physical meanings but the same fundamental unit base (Pa).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic hardness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic hardness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Hardness** is defined as the **resistance offered by a food material to a sudden, impacting, or rapidly applied force**, as opposed to the gradual, continuous loading characteristic of static/quasi-static hardness tests. It quantifies the energy-absorbing capacity of a food product's structure under impact loading conditions.

In rheological and textural terms, dynamic hardness is a **mechanical textural parameter** that characterizes the *brittleness*, *toughness*, or *crispness* of a food when subjected to impact. It is numerically expressed as the **ratio of the kinetic energy of the impacting body to the volume of the indentation/impression** produced on the food surface, or equivalently, as the **height of rebound of a standard indenter** dropped from a fixed height.

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{E_k}{V_{indent}} = \frac{mgh}{V_{indent}} $$

where:
- $E_k$ = Kinetic energy of the impacting body (**Joules, J**)
- $V_{indent}$ = Volume of the indentation created (**m³**)
- $m$ = Mass of the indenter (**kg**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = Drop height (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dynamic hardness is a critical textural property in food quality evaluation, particularly for **cereal grains, biscuits, crackers, puffed snacks, extruded products, and hard candies**, where consumer perception of *crispness* and *crunch* is directly linked to the material's dynamic hardness response.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you tap the top of a **biscuit** gently with your fingertip versus dropping a small marble from a height of 10 cm onto the same biscuit surface:

- The **gentle tap** → you are measuring *static hardness* (slow, steady force)
- The **marble drop** → you are measuring *dynamic hardness* (sudden, high-velocity impact)

The marble will either:
1. **Bounce back high** → The biscuit is *hard and elastic* (high dynamic hardness, typical of well-baked crackers)
2. **Barely bounce and crush the surface** → The biscuit is *soft and plastic* (low dynamic hardness, typical of stale or high-moisture products)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection:** When you *bite* into a corn flake or a potato chip, your teeth deliver an impact force at approximately **1–3 m/s loading rate**. This is essentially a *dynamic hardness test* in vivo! That characteristic *crackling sound* and *sudden fracture* you experience is your tongue and ears registering the dynamic hardness response.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Hardness Tests in Food Technology

| Test Type | Loading Rate | Typical Instrument | Representative Foods |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Static Hardness** | 0.01 – 1 mm/s | Texture Analyzer (TA.XT Plus), Penetrometer | Fruits, cheese, gels |
| **Dynamic Hardness** | 1 – 10 m/s | Pfund Hardness Tester, Ball-drop apparatus | Grains, biscuits, puffed snacks |
| **Quasi-Static** | 0.1 – 10 mm/s | Universal Testing Machine (UTM) | Dough, meat |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student pitfall is confusing **Dynamic Hardness** with **Dynamic Viscosity**. These are fundamentally different properties: *Dynamic Hardness* is a **solid textural attribute** (resistance to impact in solids), whereas *Dynamic Viscosity* is a **fluid flow attribute** (resistance to shear in liquids). They share the word "dynamic" only because both involve non-equilibrium, time-dependent responses.

---

## 1.4 Standard Units and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard SI Unit | CGS Unit | Typical Magnitude in Foods |
|-----------|------------------|----------|---------------------------|
| Dynamic Hardness ($H_{dyn}$) | **N/m² (Pascal)** or **J/m³** | dyne/cm² | 10⁴ – 10⁸ Pa |
| Indenter Mass | **kg** | g | 0.05 – 2.0 kg |
| Drop Height | **m** | cm | 0.10 – 1.0 m |
| Indentation Volume | **m³** | cm³ | 10⁻⁹ – 10⁻⁶ m³ |
| Gravitational Constant ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** | 981 cm/s² | Universal |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Dynamic Hardness

The science of dynamic hardness is rooted in the **Hertzian Contact Theory** (1881) extended to viscoelastic food materials. When a rigid indenter strikes a food surface at velocity $v$, the following physical events occur in **rapid sequence (typically < 10 milliseconds)**:

### Step-by-Step Physical Cascade

1. **Initial Contact (t = 0):** The indenter tip makes contact with the food surface at velocity $v_0 = \sqrt{2gh}$.
2. **Elastic Deformation Phase:** The food matrix deforms elastically, storing impact energy as elastic strain energy. The contact area grows as $A(t) \propto t^{2/5}$ for spherical indenters (Hertz solution).
3. **Peak Force Stage:** Maximum force $F_{max}$ is reached when the indenter velocity momentarily becomes zero. At this instant, **all kinetic energy has been converted to deformation work**.
4. **Indentation/Plastic Flow Phase:** If the local stress exceeds the yield strength of the food material, an irreversible *indentation crater* is formed.
5. **Rebound Phase:** Elastic recovery propels the indenter upward to a rebound height $h_r < h$.
6. **Energy Dissipation:** The difference $mg(h - h_r)$ represents the energy permanently absorbed by the food (converted to fracture surface energy, heat, and viscoelastic losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rebound height method** is the principle behind the classical **Pfund Hardness Tester**, which is still widely cited in food literature.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicability |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{mgh}{V_{indent}}$ | Basic definition (energy/volume method) | J/m³ or Pa | General impact hardness |
| 2 | $v_0 = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Impact velocity at contact | m/s | Free-falling indenter |
| 3 | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 = mgh$ | Kinetic energy at impact | J (N·m) | All dynamic tests |
| 4 | $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot (h - h_r)}{V_{indent}}$ | Rebound-energy method (absorbed energy) | J/m³ or Pa | Pfund-type tester |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{h_r}{h}$ | Coefficient of restitution (dimensionless) | — | Elasticity indicator |
| 6 | $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{2 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot (h - h_r)}{\pi \cdot d \cdot D_{crater}}$ | Cylindrical crater approximation | Pa | Cone/ball indenter |
| 7 | $F_{max} = m \cdot g + m \cdot a_{decel}$ | Peak impact force (Newton's 2nd law) | N | Instrumented impact |
| 8 | $\sigma_{impact} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{contact}}$ | Contact stress at peak load | Pa | Failure prediction |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** For 14-mark derivations, examiners often ask you to relate $H_{dyn}$ to the *rebound coefficient* $R$ or to derive the energy-balance equation. Memorize the *four* boxed relations above — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this topic.

---

## 2.3 Why Dynamic Hardness Matters in Food Engineering

| Industry Application | Role of Dynamic Hardness |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Cereal & Snack Industry** | Quantifies *crispness* and *crunch* — primary sensory attributes |
| **Grain Quality Assessment** | Determines *suitability for milling* (hard vs. soft wheat) |
| **Packaging Design** | Predicts *fracture probability* during transport impact |
| **Extrusion Processing** | Controls *expansion ratio* and *cell wall strength* |
| **Quality Control** | Detects *moisture migration* and *staling* in baked goods |
| **Biscuit Manufacturing** | Defines *snap* characteristic — key consumer acceptance criterion |

---

## 2.4 Physical Significance of High vs. Low Dynamic Hardness

| Property | High $H_{dyn}$ | Low $H_{dyn}$ |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Microstructure** | Dense, low-porosity matrix | Highly porous, foam-like |
| **Moisture Content** | Low (< 8%) | High (> 12%) |
| **Sensory Descriptor** | Hard, brittle, crisp | Soft, chewy, soggy |
| **Failure Mode** | Sudden brittle fracture | Gradual plastic deformation |
| **Typical Examples** | Glassy candies, crackers, dry pasta | Bread crust, marshmallows, fresh fruits |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Energy-Based Definition of Dynamic Hardness

### Objective
Derive the expression $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot h}{V_{indent}}$ from first principles using conservation of energy.

### Step 1: Initial Energy State of the Indenter
The indenter of mass $m$ is held at rest at height $h$ above the food surface. Its total mechanical energy is purely gravitational potential:

$$ E_{initial} = m \cdot g \cdot h $$

The kinetic energy is zero because the indenter is initially at rest.

### Step 2: Free-Fall Under Gravity (Neglecting Air Resistance)
Once released, the indenter falls freely under gravity. Just before striking the food surface, all gravitational potential energy has been converted into kinetic energy. By the **work-energy theorem**:

$$ W_{gravity} = \Delta KE $$

$$ m \cdot g \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{impact}^2 - 0 $$

### Step 3: Velocity at the Moment of Impact
Rearranging the equation from Step 2:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{impact}^2 = m \cdot g \cdot h $$

$$ v_{impact}^2 = 2 \cdot g \cdot h $$

$$ v_{impact} = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h} $$

### Step 4: Energy Transfer Upon Impact
At the instant of impact, the indenter decelerates rapidly and comes to rest (or rebounds). All kinetic energy is transferred into the food matrix, performing work in:
- **Plastic deformation** (creating the indentation crater)
- **Fracture surface generation** (crack propagation)
- **Heat dissipation** (viscous losses)
- **Elastic strain storage** (later recovered as rebound energy)

Assuming the **absorbed energy model** (worst-case, no rebound):

$$ E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_{impact}^2 = m \cdot g \cdot h $$

### Step 5: Definition of Dynamic Hardness
By definition, dynamic hardness is the **absorbed energy per unit volume of indentation crater**:

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{V_{indent}} $$

### Step 6: Final Substitution
Substituting the absorbed energy from Step 4:

$$ \boxed{H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot h}{V_{indent}} } $$

Units check:

$$ [H_{dyn}] = \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2 \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3} = \text{Pa} \;\checkmark $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rebound-Energy Method (Pfund Approach)

### Objective
Derive the dynamic hardness expression when a portion of energy is recovered through rebound.

### Step 1: Define the Coefficient of Restitution
When an indenter of mass $m$ is dropped from height $h$ onto a food surface and rebounds to height $h_r$:

$$ R = \frac{v_{rebound}}{v_{impact}} = \sqrt{\frac{h_r}{h}} $$

$R$ is dimensionless and lies in $[0, 1]$. $R = 1$ → perfectly elastic; $R = 0$ → perfectly plastic.

### Step 2: Express Rebound Height in Terms of R
Squaring both sides:

$$ R^2 = \frac{h_r}{h} $$

$$ h_r = R^2 \cdot h $$

### Step 3: Energy Lost to the Food
Energy absorbed by the food (not recovered as rebound) equals the difference between initial potential energy and rebound potential energy:

$$ E_{absorbed} = m \cdot g \cdot h - m \cdot g \cdot h_r $$

$$ E_{absorbed} = m \cdot g \cdot (h - h_r) $$

### Step 4: Substitute into Hardness Definition
$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot (h - h_r)}{V_{indent}} $$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Coefficient of Restitution
Substituting $h_r = R^2 \cdot h$:

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot (h - R^2 \cdot h)}{V_{indent}} $$

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot h \cdot (1 - R^2)}{V_{indent}} $$

$$ \boxed{H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot h}{V_{indent}} \cdot (1 - R^2) } $$

### Step 6: Limit-Case Verification

| Case | Condition | Result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Perfectly elastic food | $R \to 1$ | $H_{dyn} \to 0$ (food returns all energy) |
| Perfectly plastic food | $R \to 0$ | $H_{dyn} \to \dfrac{mgh}{V_{indent}}$ (maximum absorption) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The factor $(1 - R^2)$ acts as a **dissipation efficiency term**. Foods that *appear* hard but are actually viscoelastic (like some crackers) show $R \approx 0.3 - 0.5$, giving $H_{dyn}$ values 75–90% of the theoretical maximum.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Peak Impact Force from Newton's Second Law

### Objective
Derive an expression for the maximum force experienced by the food during impact.

### Step 1: Impulse-Momentum Theorem
The change in momentum of the indenter equals the impulse delivered by the food surface:

$$ \int_{0}^{t_c} F(t) \, dt = m \cdot v_{impact} - m \cdot v_{rebound} $$

where $t_c$ is the contact duration (typically 1–10 ms for food impact tests).

### Step 2: Assume Average Force Model
If we approximate the time-varying force $F(t)$ by an average force $\bar{F}$ acting over the contact duration $t_c$:

$$ \bar{F} \cdot t_c = m \cdot (v_{impact} - v_{rebound}) $$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Heights
Using $v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh}$ and $v_{rebound} = R \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$:

$$ \bar{F} \cdot t_c = m \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot (1 - R) $$

### Step 4: Solve for Average Force
$$ \bar{F} = \frac{m \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot (1 - R)}{t_c} $$

### Step 5: Peak Force Estimate (Quasi-Steady Approximation)
For many food impact problems, the peak force is approximately twice the average force for a triangular force-time pulse:

$$ F_{peak} \approx 2 \cdot \bar{F} = \frac{2 \cdot m \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot (1 - R)}{t_c} $$

$$ \boxed{F_{peak} = \frac{2 \cdot m \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot (1 - R)}{t_c}} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Use:** This force is what the food must withstand without fracturing. Comparing $F_{peak}$ with the food's *fracture force* (measured in a separate three-point bend test) predicts whether the product will survive handling and transport impacts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Static vs. Dynamic Hardness

| Parameter | Static Hardness | Dynamic Hardness |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Loading Type** | Quasi-static | Impact (high strain-rate) |
| **Strain Rate** | 0.01 – 1 s⁻¹ | 10 – 1000 s⁻¹ |
| **Time Scale** | Seconds | Milliseconds |
| **Energy Source** | Motor-driven crosshead | Gravitational / pneumatic |
| **Measured Response** | Force vs. displacement at low rate | Crater volume, rebound height, force peak |
| **Governing Equation** | $H_s = F_{max} / A_{proj}$ | $H_{dyn} = E_{absorbed} / V_{crater}$ |
| **Food Examples** | Apple firmness, cheese hardness | Biscuit crispness, grain hardness |
| **Sensory Correlate** | "Hardness" (first bite, slow) | "Crispness/Crunch" (first bite, fast) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

---

## 4.2 Energy Flow Diagram During Impact Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Potential Energy mgh] --> B[Kinetic Energy at Impact 0.5mv²]
    B --> C[Energy Partition at Contact]
    C --> D[Elastic Strain Energy - Recoverable]
    C --> E[Plastic Deformation Energy - Crater Formation]
    C --> F[Fracture Surface Energy - Crack Propagation]
    C --> G[Heat/Viscous Dissipation]
    D --> H[Rebound Energy mghr]
    E --> I[Absorbed Energy Eabsorbed]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J[Final Rebound Height hr]
    I --> K[Crater Volume Vindent]
    K --> L[Dynamic Hardness Hdyn = Eabsorbed / Vindent]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Static vs. Dynamic Hardness Test

```mermaid

    START[Food Sample Received] --> Q1{Is Product Brittle or Porous?}
    Q1 -->|Yes - Crisp/Crunchy| Q2{Consumer Bite Velocity?}
    Q1 -->|No - Soft/Doughy| REC_STATIC[Use Static Hardness Test - Texture Analyzer]

    Q2 -->|High - 1 to 3 m/s| REC_DYNAMIC[Use Dynamic Hardness Test - Pfund or Ball Drop]
    Q2 -->|Low - Less than 0.1 m/s| REC_STATIC

    REC_DYNAMIC --> NEXT1[Select Indenter Geometry]
    NEXT1 --> N1A[Sphere for grains]

    NEXT1 --> N1C[Flat punch for tablets]

    NEXT2 --> N2A[Cylindrical for fruits]
    NEXT2 --> N2B[Spherical for gels]
    NEXT2 --> N2C[Blade for cheese]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dynamic Hardness. Mention its SI unit.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dynamic hardness is defined as the resistance offered by a food material to a sudden impacting force, expressed as the kinetic energy of the impacting indenter divided by the volume of indentation produced on the food surface.

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot h}{V_{indent}} $$

The SI unit is **Pascal (Pa)** or equivalently **J/m³**. (3 marks for definition + unit + formula)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Static Hardness and Dynamic Hardness.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Static Hardness | Dynamic Hardness |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| Loading type | Slow, continuous force | Sudden impact |
| Strain rate | 0.01–1 s⁻¹ | 10–1000 s⁻¹ |
| Sensory correlate | Firmness, hardness | Crispness, crunch |
| Typical instrument | Texture Analyzer | Pfund / Ball-drop tester |

(1 mark each for any 3 distinguishing points)

---

---

## PART B — Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

#### (a) Derive the expression for Dynamic Hardness of a food material using the energy method. State clearly the assumptions involved. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the definition.**
Dynamic hardness is the energy absorbed by the food per unit volume of crater formed when struck by a falling indenter. (1 mark)

**Step 2: List the assumptions.**
- Air resistance during fall is negligible.
- All kinetic energy at impact is absorbed by the food (worst-case).
- Indenter is rigid and undeformable.
- Food surface is locally homogeneous at the contact zone. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Initial energy state.**
An indenter of mass $m$ is released from height $h$. Its initial energy is:

$$ E_i = m \cdot g \cdot h $$

**Step 4: Velocity at impact.**
Using kinematics $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$ with $u = 0$:

$$ v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh} $$

**Step 5: Kinetic energy at impact.**
$$ KE = \frac{1}{2} m v_{impact}^2 = \frac{1}{2} m (2gh) = mgh $$

**Step 6: Energy balance.**
All kinetic energy is transferred to the food:

$$ E_{absorbed} = mgh $$

**Step 7: Define hardness.**
$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{V_{indent}} = \frac{mgh}{V_{indent}} $$

with SI unit Pa. (3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for assumptions and units)

---

#### (b) A 0.5 kg steel ball is dropped from a height of 0.4 m onto a biscuit surface. The indentation volume formed is 25 mm³. Calculate: (i) the dynamic hardness, and (ii) the impact velocity. Take g = 9.81 m/s². (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 0.5$ kg
- Drop height $h = 0.4$ m
- Indentation volume $V_{indent} = 25 \, \text{mm}^3 = 25 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Part (i): Impact velocity (3 marks)**

Using the kinematic equation:

$$ v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh} $$

$$ v_{impact} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.4} $$

$$ v_{impact} = \sqrt{7.848} $$

$$ v_{impact} = 2.801 \, \text{m/s} $$

**Part (ii): Dynamic Hardness (4 marks)**

Using the energy-based definition:

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot h}{V_{indent}} $$

Numerator:

$$ mgh = 0.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 = 1.962 \, \text{J} $$

Denominator:

$$ V_{indent} = 25 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^3 $$

Therefore:

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{1.962}{25 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ H_{dyn} = 7.848 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} $$

$$ \boxed{H_{dyn} \approx 78.48 \, \text{MPa}} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

#### (a) Explain the rebound method of measuring dynamic hardness. Derive the expression $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{mgh}{V_{indent}} (1 - R^2)$ where $R$ is the coefficient of restitution. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Concept.**
In the rebound (Pfund) method, the indenter is dropped from a known height $h$, strikes the food, and rebounds to a lower height $h_r$. The difference $(h - h_r)$ represents energy permanently absorbed by the food. (1 mark)

**Step 2: Define Coefficient of Restitution.**
$$ R = \frac{v_{rebound}}{v_{impact}} = \sqrt{\frac{h_r}{h}} $$

Rearranging:

$$ h_r = R^2 h $$

(1 mark)

**Step 3: Energy balance.**
Initial potential energy:

$$ E_i = mgh $$

Rebound potential energy:

$$ E_r = mgh_r = mg(R^2 h) = mgR^2 h $$

Energy absorbed by the food:

$$ E_{absorbed} = E_i - E_r = mgh - mgR^2 h = mgh(1 - R^2) $$

(2 marks)

**Step 4: Dynamic hardness.**
$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{V_{indent}} = \frac{mgh(1 - R^2)}{V_{indent}} $$

(2 marks)

**Step 5: Discussion.**
When $R = 1$ (perfectly elastic food), $H_{dyn} = 0$. When $R = 0$ (perfectly plastic food), $H_{dyn}$ is maximum. (1 mark)

---

#### (b) A biscuit sample is tested in a Pfund hardness tester. The indenter (mass 0.2 kg) is dropped from 0.3 m and rebounds to 0.12 m. The crater volume is 15 mm³. Calculate the dynamic hardness. Take g = 9.81 m/s². (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 0.2$ kg
- $h = 0.3$ m
- $h_r = 0.12$ m
- $V_{indent} = 15 \, \text{mm}^3 = 15 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Coefficient of Restitution (2 marks)**

$$ R = \sqrt{\frac{h_r}{h}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.12}{0.3}} $$

$$ R = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.6325 $$

**Step 2: Absorbed Energy (2 marks)**

$$ E_{absorbed} = mgh(1 - R^2) $$

$$ R^2 = 0.4 $$

$$ 1 - R^2 = 0.6 $$

$$ E_{absorbed} = 0.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.3 \times 0.6 $$

$$ E_{absorbed} = 0.3532 \, \text{J} $$

**Step 3: Dynamic Hardness (3 marks)**

$$ H_{dyn} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{V_{indent}} = \frac{0.3532}{15 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ H_{dyn} = 2.354 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} $$

$$ \boxed{H_{dyn} \approx 23.54 \, \text{MPa}} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (–2 marks typical):** Students frequently forget to convert mm³ → m³ (factor of $10^{-9}$). Always state the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $h$ with $h_r$ (–1 mark):** In rebound problems, the *drop* height $h$ is the initial height, NOT the rebound height.
> 3. **Forgetting $g$ value (–1 mark):** Always write $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ even if not given; examiners reward method.
> 4. **Writing $R$ without squaring (–2 marks):** $H_{dyn}$ uses $R^2$, not $R$. This is the single most common algebraic slip.
> 5. **No units in final answer (–1 mark):** Always report $H_{dyn}$ in MPa or Pa explicitly.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Dynamic Hardness** = resistance of food to *sudden impact force*, not slow loading.
- ✅ **Formula:** $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{mgh}{V_{indent}}$ with SI unit **Pascal (Pa)** or **J/m³**.
- ✅ **Rebound variant:** $H_{dyn} = \dfrac{mgh(1-R^2)}{V_{indent}}$, where $R = \sqrt{h_r/h}$.
- ✅ **Impact velocity:** $v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh}$ — always derive this first in numerical problems.
- ✅ **R ranges:** $R = 1$ (perfectly elastic) to $R = 0$ (perfectly plastic).
- ✅ **Instruments:** Pfund Hardness Tester, Ball-drop apparatus, Instrumented falling-weight testers.
- ✅ **Typical foods:** biscuits, crackers, puffed cereals, grains, hard candies, extruded snacks.
- ✅ **Sensory correlate:** Crispness, crunchiness, brittleness — the *acoustic-textural* attributes.
- ✅ **Always convert mm³ → m³** by multiplying by $10^{-9}$ in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Use $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$** unless otherwise stated.
- ✅ Dynamic hardness **≠ Dynamic viscosity** — different physical phenomena.
- ✅ The factor $(1 - R^2)$ is called the **dissipation coefficient** and ranges from 0 to 1.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The effect of age
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The effect of age

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Technology**, the term **"Age"** is a dual-dimensional concept that influences both the **raw material (input side)** and the **consumer (output side)** of the food chain. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for GDFTT104 (Module 2), the effect of age refers to:

> **Definition:** *The measurable changes in the chemical composition, physicochemical properties, microbiological status, sensory attributes, and nutritional profile of food materials and food products that occur as a function of time — either the chronological age of the raw ingredient (pre-harvest / post-harvest), the storage age of the processed product, or the biological age of the consumer that dictates specific dietary requirements.*

The concept is broadly classified into two major branches in food technology:

| Branch | Sub-Topic | Core Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Branch A** | Age of the Raw Material / Food | Maturity, ripening, senescence, storage duration |
| **Branch B** | Age of the Consumer | Life-stage nutrition, age-specific food design, geriatric & infant foods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the *extrinsic and intrinsic factors* affecting food quality. Age is treated as a **critical intrinsic factor** for biological raw materials (meat, milk, eggs, fruits) and a **critical consumer variable** for product formulation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **age in food** the same way you think of **age in humans**:

- A **young fruit** (like an unripe mango) is like a **teenager** — full of starch, firm, sour, and not yet "ready for the world." As it ages, it softens, sweetens, and becomes edible (just as a teenager matures into an adult).
- An **old fruit** (overripe) is like an **elderly person** — tissues break down, flavors degrade, and it becomes susceptible to "diseases" (microbial spoilage).
- Similarly, a **baby's digestive system** is like a **newly built machine** — it can only process simple, soft, easily digestible foods (milk, purees). A **senior citizen's system** is like an **old machine** — worn teeth, slower metabolism, requiring softer, nutrient-dense, low-sodium foods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Intuition:** Age = Time-dependent change. Whether we are talking about a tomato ripening on a vine, a chicken growing in a farm, or a 70-year-old needing calcium-rich food — **time changes everything**, and food technology must engineer for it.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Values

The following standard values are referenced in food science literature for age-related changes:

- **Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA)** for Energy:
  - Adult Male (Sedentary): **2,320 kcal/day**
  - Adult Female (Sedentary): **1,900 kcal/day**
  - Infants (0–6 months): **~520 kcal/day**
  - Elderly (60+ years): **1,900 kcal/day** (with reduced BMR)
- **Optimal Slaughter Age of Broiler Chicken**: **6–8 weeks**
- **Storage Life of Pasteurized Milk at 4°C**: **5–7 days**
- **Egg Grade A Freshness (Haugh Unit)**: **> 72 HU**

> [!NOTE]
> These values are indicative and may vary by region, regulatory body (FSSAI, Codex, USDA), and food matrix. Always cross-check with the latest national standards during exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 Branch A — Effect of Age of the Raw Material / Food

### 2.1.1 Effect on Meat Quality

The **age of the animal at slaughter** profoundly affects meat quality:

| Parameter | Young Animal | Old Animal |
|---|---|---|
| **Connective Tissue (Collagen)** | Low, soluble | High, cross-linked, tough |
| **Tenderness** | High | Low (tougher meat) |
| **Flavor Intensity** | Mild | Strong (sometimes undesirable) |
| **Color (Myoglobin)** | Light pink | Dark red |
| **Juiciness** | Higher | Lower |
| **Best Suited For** | Roasting, grilling | Slow cooking, stewing, mincing |

- **Mechanism:** With age, **collagen cross-linking** increases via advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making the intramuscular connective tissue insoluble and resistant to heat-induced gelatinization.
- **Practical Example:** Veal (young calf) is tender; mutton (old sheep) requires slow moist cooking to tenderize the collagen.

### 2.1.2 Effect on Milk and Dairy

- **Colostrum** (first milk, 0–5 days post-partum) is rich in immunoglobulins (IgG), proteins (~14%), and is *not* used for commercial fluid milk.
- **Mature milk** (after ~2 weeks) has stabilized composition: ~3.5% fat, ~3.4% protein, ~4.7% lactose.
- **Late-lactation milk** has higher fat and protein but lower lactose; flavor may be salty.
- **Aging of Cheese**: Intentional microbial and enzymatic ripening over weeks to years develops flavor (e.g., Cheddar aged 6–12 months, Parmesan aged 24+ months).

### 2.1.3 Effect on Eggs

- **Fresh egg**: Thick, gel-like albumen; small air cell; yolk is firm and round.
- **Aged egg**: Albumen becomes watery and thin; air cell enlarges due to moisture and CO₂ loss; yolk flattens.
- **Measurement:** **Haugh Unit (HU)** = measure of egg freshness based on albumen height and egg weight.
- **Formula Reference (do not use pipes in tables — see below):**

$$ HU = 100 \cdot \log\left(H - 1.7 W^{0.37} + 7.57\right) $$

where:
- $H$ = albumen height in mm
- $W$ = egg weight in grams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The Haugh Unit is a frequently tested formula. Memorize the variables and the acceptable quality grades (AA > 72, A = 60–72, B < 60).

### 2.1.4 Effect on Fruits and Vegetables (Maturation & Ripening)

- **Physiological maturity** = the stage at which a fruit/vegetable can continue to ripen after harvest (climacteric fruits: banana, tomato, apple).
- **Non-climacteric fruits** (citrus, grapes, strawberries) do not ripen further after harvest — must be picked ripe.
- **Aging Post-Harvest** leads to senescence, water loss, softening, sugar-to-starch or starch-to-sugar conversions, pigment changes (chlorophyll → carotenoids), and increased susceptibility to microbial attack.

### 2.1.5 Effect on Grains and Pulses (Storage Age)

- **Respiration rate** decreases with maturity but increases in damaged grains.
- **Storage at high moisture (>14%)** and high temperature leads to **aging defects**: mold, insect infestation, lipid oxidation, and **"hard-to-cook" defect** in legumes.
- **Aged rice** (stored 1–2 years) is preferred in some Asian cuisines for its texture and aroma (e.g., Basmati aging).

---

## 2.2 Branch B — Effect of Age of the Consumer

### 2.2.1 Life-Stage Nutrition Framework

The **nutritional requirements** and **food-processing needs** change drastically with the consumer's age:

| Life Stage | Age Range | Key Food Technology Concerns |
|---|---|---|
| **Infants** | 0–12 months | Sterile liquid foods, soft purees, low allergenicity, breast-milk substitutes |
| **Toddlers** | 1–3 years | Soft textures, controlled sugar/salt, fortified cereals |
| **Children** | 4–12 years | Balanced macros, calcium, iron fortification, appealing formats |
| **Adolescents** | 13–18 years | High energy, iron (girls), calcium, protein for growth spurts |
| **Adults** | 19–60 years | Maintenance diets, disease prevention, functional foods |
| **Elderly** | 60+ years | Soft foods (dental issues), low sodium, high calcium/vitamin D, easy-to-digest proteins |

### 2.2.2 Physiological Basis of Age-Specific Diets

- **Infants** have an immature **gastrointestinal (GI) tract**, low gastric acid secretion, and immature kidney function — hence liquid, sterile, low-renal-load foods.
- **Elderly** have reduced:
  - **Saliva production** → difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) → need soft, moist foods
  - **Gastric acid (HCl)** → reduced B12 absorption
  - **Bone density** → need vitamin D and calcium fortified foods
  - **Taste & smell acuity** → need enhanced flavoring
  - **Gut motility** → need dietary fiber

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Application:** The global **infant formula market** (~$54 billion) and **geriatric nutrition market** (~$22 billion) are direct commercial outcomes of "the effect of age" — food technologists design entire product lines around these segments.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield revision sheet. **Note:** Vertical pipes inside table cells are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

| Concept | Key Expression / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Haugh Unit (Egg freshness) | $HU = 100 \cdot \log(H - 1.7W^{0.37} + 7.57)$ | Quality grading of eggs |
| Basal Metabolic Rate (Mifflin-St Jeor) | $BMR = 10W + 6.25H - 5A + S$ (M: $+5$, F: $-161$) | Energy needs of consumers |
| Water Loss in Stored Produce | $\% WL = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$ | Post-harvest aging assessment |
| Respiration Rate | $R = \frac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{kg \text{ produce} \cdot hour}$ | Aging / shelf-life indicator |
| Collagen Solubility | Inversely proportional to animal age | Meat tenderness prediction |
| RDA Scaling | $\text{Child RDA} = \text{Adult RDA} \times \frac{\text{Weight (kg)}}{70}$ | Pediatric nutrition |

Where:
- $W$ = weight (kg), $H$ = height (cm), $A$ = age (years), $S$ = sex constant
- $W_i$ = initial weight, $W_f$ = final weight

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Technology

- **Product Development:** Designing **infant formula**, **school meal programs**, **sports nutrition**, and **geriatric foods** (e.g., texture-modified meals).
- **Quality Control:** Using **Haugh Unit**, **TBA (thiobarbituric acid) values**, and **TBARS** to assess age-related lipid oxidation.
- **Supply Chain:** Predicting **shelf life** of products based on storage age curves (Arrhenius kinetics).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** **FSSAI**, **Codex Alimentarius**, and **EFSA** mandate age-specific labeling (e.g., "Suitable for infants above 6 months").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrices

> Since this is a food technology topic, "derivations" are replaced by **analytical frameworks, decision matrices, and worked numerical examples** that are equally rigorous.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Haugh Unit Calculation (Egg Freshness)

**Problem:** A freshly collected egg weighs **62 g** and its broken-out albumen height is measured as **8 mm** using a tripod micrometer. Determine the Haugh Unit and grade the egg.

### Step 1: Identify the given variables
- $H$ (albumen height) = **8 mm**
- $W$ (egg weight) = **62 g**

### Step 2: Substitute into the Haugh Unit formula

$$ HU = 100 \cdot \log\left(H - 1.7 \cdot W^{0.37} + 7.57\right) $$

### Step 3: Calculate the weight term

$$ W^{0.37} = 62^{0.37} $$

We compute this using logarithms:

$$ \log_{10}(62) = 1.7924 $$

$$ 0.37 \times 1.7924 = 0.6632 $$

$$ 62^{0.37} = 10^{0.6632} \approx 4.605 $$

### Step 4: Multiply by 1.7

$$ 1.7 \times 4.605 = 7.829 $$

### Step 5: Compute the inner bracket

$$ \text{Inner} = H - 1.7W^{0.37} + 7.57 $$

$$ \text{Inner} = 8 - 7.829 + 7.57 $$

$$ \text{Inner} = 7.741 $$

### Step 6: Apply the logarithm

$$ \log_{10}(7.741) \approx 0.8887 $$

### Step 7: Multiply by 100

$$ HU = 100 \times 0.8887 = 88.87 \approx 89 $$

### Step 8: Grade the egg

$$ HU = 89 \implies \textbf{Grade AA (Excellent freshness)} $$

| HU Range | Grade |
|---|---|
| $> 72$ | AA |
| $60 - 72$ | A |
| $< 60$ | B |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A HU of 89 indicates an extremely fresh egg with thick, gel-like albumen — ideal for direct table consumption and premium grading.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — BMR & Energy Requirement for an Elderly Male

**Problem:** Calculate the BMR of a **65-year-old male** who is **170 cm tall** and weighs **70 kg**. Then estimate his total daily energy expenditure (TDEE) assuming a sedentary activity factor of 1.2.

### Step 1: Recall the Mifflin-St Jeor Equation for Males

$$ BMR = 10W + 6.25H - 5A + 5 $$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$ BMR = 10(70) + 6.25(170) - 5(65) + 5 $$

### Step 3: Compute each term

$$ 10 \times 70 = 700 $$

$$ 6.25 \times 170 = 1062.5 $$

$$ 5 \times 65 = 325 $$

### Step 4: Combine

$$ BMR = 700 + 1062.5 - 325 + 5 $$

$$ BMR = 1442.5 \text{ kcal/day} $$

### Step 5: Apply Activity Factor

$$ TDEE = BMR \times AF = 1442.5 \times 1.2 $$

$$ TDEE = 1731 \text{ kcal/day} $$

### Step 6: Interpret for Food Product Design

For this **65-year-old sedentary male**:
- Energy: ~**1,731 kcal/day** (low compared to a 25-year-old at ~2,400 kcal)
- Protein: ~**1.0–1.2 g/kg/day** (higher per-kg to prevent sarcopenia)
- Sodium: **< 2,000 mg/day** (cardiovascular health)
- Calcium: **1,200 mg/day** (bone health)
- Vitamin D: **800–1,000 IU/day** (decreased skin synthesis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Food Technologist:** Develop a **low-sodium, high-calcium, vitamin-D fortified, soft-textured, easy-to-chew meal** — the very essence of geriatric food product design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Cooking Method Based on Animal Age

This is a high-yield **decision matrix** often asked in KTU exams:

| Animal Age | Collagen Content | Tenderness | Best Cooking Method | Example Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Very Young** (veal, lamb <6 mo) | Very Low | Very High | Quick dry heat: grilling, pan-frying | Veal cutlet |
| **Young** (broiler chicken 6–8 wk) | Low | High | Roasting, grilling | Roast chicken |
| **Adult** (beef cow 2–4 yr) | Moderate | Moderate | Combination: searing + braising | Beef stew |
| **Old** (mutton from 5+ yr sheep) | High (cross-linked) | Low | Slow moist heat, low & slow | Mutton curry, mince |
| **Very Old** (culled dairy cow 8+ yr) | Very High | Very Low | Mincing, grinding, marination | Hamburgers, sausages |

### Why does this work? (Reasoning Steps)

**Step 1:** Connective tissue (collagen) shrinks and becomes tough above 60°C in dry heat.

**Step 2:** Slow, moist cooking at 80–95°C for extended time (1–4 hours) hydrolyzes collagen into gelatin.

**Step 3:** Hence, older animals = longer cooking = moisture-based methods to convert insoluble collagen to soluble gelatin.

**Step 4:** Younger animals have less collagen to begin with, so quick high-heat methods retain juiciness and tenderness.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Age vs. Food Safety Risk

| Consumer Age | Immune Status | Key Pathogen Risk | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infant (0–1 yr) | Immature | *Salmonella*, *Cronobacter* (in formula) | Sterilization, strict hygiene |
| Children (1–12) | Developing | *E. coli*, *Salmonella* | Pasteurization, proper cooking |
| Adults | Mature | *Listeria* (pregnant women) | Cold-chain, ready-to-eat safety |
| Elderly (60+) | Declining | *Listeria*, *C. botulinum* | Mild preservation, low-risk foods |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-critical**. KTU frequently tests the linkage between **age of consumer → physiological vulnerability → food technology solution**.
