> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The food fluids at the top (mercury, glycerine) are **denser** than water and would **sink** in it, while those at the bottom (oil, alcohol, air) are **lighter** than water and would **float**. This physical intuition is what the **hydrometer** exploits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define a fluid. How is it different from a solid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fluid is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows) under the action of an applied shear stress**, however small the stress may be. Unlike a solid, which resists shear up to a certain yield limit and then deforms elastically or plastically, a fluid offers **no resistance to shear at rest** and deforms continuously as long as the shear stress is applied. Examples of fluids relevant to food technology include milk, fruit juices, and edible oils.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Define (a) density, (b) specific gravity, and (c) specific weight. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **(a) Density (ρ):** Mass of the fluid per unit volume; $\rho = m/V$. **Unit:** kg/m³.
- **(b) Specific Gravity (S):** Ratio of the density of the fluid to the density of water at 4 °C; $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$. **Unit:** dimensionless (no unit).
- **(c) Specific Weight (γ):** Weight of the fluid per unit volume; $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$. **Unit:** N/m³.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **density, specific weight, and specific gravity** of a fluid. State the standard values for water at 4 °C. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid food product (say, **tomato ketchup**) has a mass of **820 kg** and occupies a volume of **0.75 m³**. Calculate its **(i)** density, **(ii)** specific gravity, **(iii)** specific weight, and **(iv)** specific volume. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Density (ρ):**
The mass of a fluid element divided by its volume gives density.

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Weight (γ):**
Weight is mass times gravity: $W = m \cdot g$. Dividing by volume:

$$\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{V} = \rho \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Specific Gravity (S):**
Ratio of fluid density to water density at 4 °C:

$$S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^{\circ}\text{C}}}$$

**Step 4 — Standard values for water at 4 °C:**
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- $S_{\text{water}} = 1.0$
- $\hat{v}_{\text{water}} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$

**Step 5 — Combined relation:**
Substituting $\rho = S \times 1000$ into $\gamma = \rho g$:

$$\gamma = S \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \times S \text{ N/m}^3$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 820 \text{ kg}$, $V = 0.75 \text{ m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Density:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{820}{0.75} = 1093.33 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific Gravity:**

$$S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{1093.33}{1000} = 1.0933 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**(iii) Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 1093.33 \times 9.81 = 10725.6 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iv) Specific Volume:**

$$\hat{v} = \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{1093.33} = 9.147 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Verification:** Since S > 1, ketchup is denser than water — physically reasonable (it contains suspended solids like tomato pulp and sugar).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **continuum hypothesis** of a fluid. Why is it important in engineering analysis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tank contains **three immiscible liquid food ingredients** stacked in layers as follows: top layer is **edible oil (ρ = 920 kg/m³, depth = 0.4 m)**, middle layer is **water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, depth = 0.6 m)**, and bottom layer is **sugar syrup (ρ = 1300 kg/m³, depth = 0.5 m)**. Find the **(i)** total mass of liquids and **(ii)** the **specific weight of the combined mixture** treated as a homogeneous average. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Continuum Hypothesis (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The continuum hypothesis assumes that fluid properties such as density, velocity, and pressure are **defined at every point** in the fluid and vary **continuously** in space and time, even though the fluid is actually composed of discrete molecules.

**Why important:**
1. Allows the use of **calculus (differential equations)** to describe fluid behaviour.
2. Valid when the **characteristic length** of the problem (e.g., pipe diameter, tank size) is much greater than the **molecular mean free path** (typically 10⁻⁷ m for liquids, 10⁻⁹ m for gases).
3. Fails in **rarefied gas dynamics** (e.g., high-altitude aerodynamics, MEMS devices).

**Food-tech application:** Valid for all conventional food processing equipment — pipes, tanks, heat exchangers.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Layer 1 (oil): $\rho_1 = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $h_1 = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- Layer 2 (water): $\rho_2 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $h_2 = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- Layer 3 (syrup): $\rho_3 = 1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $h_3 = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Tank cross-sectional area: **Assume $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$** (since not given, standardise per unit area).
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Mass of each layer** (for $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$):

$$m_1 = \rho_1 \cdot A \cdot h_1 = 920 \times 1 \times 0.4 = 368 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_2 = \rho_2 \cdot A \cdot h_2 = 1000 \times 1 \times 0.6 = 600 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_3 = \rho_3 \cdot A \cdot h_3 = 1300 \times 1 \times 0.5 = 650 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = 368 + 600 + 650 = 1618 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total volume:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = A \times (h_1 + h_2 + h_3) = 1 \times (0.4 + 0.6 + 0.5) = 1.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Average density:**

$$\rho_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1618}{1.5} = 1078.67 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Specific weight of the mixture:**

$$\gamma_{\text{avg}} = \rho_{\text{avg}} \cdot g = 1078.67 \times 9.81 = 10581.7 \text{ N/m}^3$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³** specifically at 4 °C. The standard reference temperature is 4 °C, not 25 °C.
> 2. **Mixing up specific weight (γ)** with **weight (W)**. Specific weight is a *fluid property*; weight is a *force*.
> 3. **Omitting units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. **Writing specific gravity with units** (e.g., "S = 1.03 kg/m³") — it is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **In conversion problems**, using $g = 9.8$ or $g = 10$ inconsistently. KTU prefers **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> 6. **In density-variation problems**, students often forget to convert °C to absolute temperature when required for gases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision — Module 3 (Fluid Mechanics: Definition – Properties – Density)**

- ✅ A **fluid** is a substance that **deforms continuously under any applied shear stress**.
- ✅ Fluids include **liquids** (incompressible) and **gases** (compressible).
- ✅ **Density (ρ) = m / V**, SI unit: **kg/m³**; for water at 4 °C, ρ = **1000 kg/m³**.
- ✅ **Specific volume (v̂) = 1 / ρ**, unit: m³/kg.
- ✅ **Specific weight (γ) = ρ × g**, unit: N/m³; for water = **9810 N/m³**.
- ✅ **Specific gravity (S) = ρ_fluid / ρ_water**, **dimensionless**.
- ✅ Conversion shortcuts: $\rho = S \times 1000$ kg/m³ and $\gamma = 9810 \times S$ N/m³.
- ✅ Density of liquids **decreases** with temperature; density of gases **decreases** with temperature and **increases** with pressure.
- ✅ The **continuum hypothesis** is valid when characteristic length ≫ molecular mean free path.
- ✅ **Milk S ≈ 1.03**, **water S = 1.0**, **edible oil S ≈ 0.92**, **mercury S ≈ 13.6** — useful for sanity-checks in numerical problems.
- ✅ Always quote **SI units** in KTU 2024 final answers; mark deductions occur for missing units.
- ✅ Use **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless explicitly told otherwise in the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Specific gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Specific Gravity (SG)**, also called **Relative Density (RD)**, is a dimensionless physical quantity defined as the ratio of the density of a given substance to the density of a reference substance under specified conditions of temperature and pressure.

For liquids and solids, the reference substance is **pure water**.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{substance}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

For gases, the reference is typically dry air at standard conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Specific gravity is a *dimensionless* quantity (no units). It is temperature-dependent and must always be reported with the temperatures of both the substance and the reference (e.g., SG at 20°C/4°C or 20°C/20°C).

The conventional standard reference temperature for water is **4°C**, where water attains its maximum density of exactly **1.000 g/cm³** (or 1000 kg/m³). This makes numerical values of specific gravity numerically equal to the density in g/cm³ at that specific condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparent vs. True Specific Gravity**
> - **Apparent SG**: Measured directly with a hydrometer or pycnometer, includes the buoyancy effect of air displaced by the sample.
> - **True SG**: Corrected for the buoyancy of air, representing the actual ratio in vacuum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two identical sealed jars. One is filled with **honey** and the other with **water**. Even though both jars look the same size, the honey jar feels much heavier when you try to lift it. This "heaviness feel" is exactly what specific gravity captures.

- If a substance is **heavier than water**, its SG > 1 (e.g., honey ≈ 1.42, milk ≈ 1.03).
- If a substance is **lighter than water**, its SG < 1 (e.g., vegetable oil ≈ 0.92, alcohol ≈ 0.79).
- If a substance is **exactly the same weight** as water for its volume, its SG = 1.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Quick Test:** A raw egg sinks in plain water (SG > 1) but floats in a heavily salted brine (brine SG > egg SG ≈ 1.03). This same principle is used to test egg freshness and brine strength in food processing.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Density of water at 4°C** = **1.000 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³** (by definition)
- **Density of water at 20°C** = **0.9982 g/cm³**
- **Density of water at 25°C** = **0.9970 g/cm³**
- **Specific gravity of milk** ≈ 1.028 to 1.035
- **Specific gravity of honey** ≈ 1.38 to 1.45
- **Specific gravity of ethanol** ≈ 0.789
- **Specific gravity of mercury** ≈ 13.6
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown

The concept of specific gravity is built on a simple chain of reasoning:

1. **Density (ρ) is the foundation**: Mass per unit volume of a substance. Two substances with the same mass but different volumes have different densities.
2. **Water is chosen as the universal reference**: Because water is the most abundant, easily purified, and reproducible liquid. Its density is well characterized at all temperatures.
3. **Dimensionless ratio**: By dividing two quantities with the same units, units cancel, leaving a pure number. This is the genius of SG — it allows comparison across any unit system (SI, CGS, Imperial).
4. **Temperature compensation**: Since density varies with temperature, the convention *SG (T₁/T₂)* specifies the temperature of the substance (T₁) and the temperature of water (T₂) used as reference.
5. **Buoyancy correction (apparent vs. true)**: When a sample is weighed in air, the air it displaces exerts an upward buoyancy force. The "true" SG removes this artifact, while "apparent" SG retains it.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{subs}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Basic definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho_{\text{subs}} = SG \times \rho_{\text{water}}$ | Density from SG | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 3 | $SG_{20/4} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{subs at 20°C}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}}}$ | Standard reporting | Subscript indicates temperatures |
| 4 | $SG_{20/20} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{subs at 20°C}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 20°C}}}$ | Common in dairy industry | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $SG_{true} = \dfrac{m_{\text{subs}} / (V_{\text{subs}} - V_{\text{air}})}{m_{\text{water}} / (V_{\text{water}} - V_{\text{air}})}$ | Buoyancy corrected | Very accurate |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Total Solids} = \dfrac{(SG - 1) \times 1000}{4}$ | Quevenne lactometer for milk | Empirical, in % |
| 7 | $SG_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{m_1 + m_2}{\dfrac{m_1}{SG_1 \cdot \rho_w} + \dfrac{m_2}{SG_2 \cdot \rho_w}}$ | Mixing rule for two liquids | Weighted by mass |
| 8 | $\text{°Brix} \approx SG \times \text{(correction factor)}$ | Approximate sugar content | Brix ≈ 261 × (1 - 1/SG) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Quevenne lactometer formula ($TS\% = \frac{(SG-1)\times 1000}{4}$) is a quick empirical way used in **dairy industry** to estimate total solids in milk from its specific gravity measured at 15.6°C.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Quality Control in Dairy**: Detecting adulteration of milk (water addition lowers SG, removal of cream raises SG).
- **Sugar Industry**: Brix (°Bx) is essentially a specific gravity measurement for sucrose solutions.
- **Beverage Industry**: Alcohol by volume (ABV) is calculated from SG before and after fermentation using the *specific gravity method*.
- **Oil Industry**: Purity testing of edible oils; adulteration with cheaper oils alters SG.
- **Salt Brining**: Proper concentration of salt solutions is checked using SG to ensure correct osmotic pressure for food preservation.
- **Egg Grading**: Freshness of eggs correlates with SG (a fresh egg has SG ≈ 1.03, old eggs lose moisture and SG decreases).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Gravity from First Principles

Starting from the fundamental definition of density:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

The density of the substance of interest is:

$$\rho_{\text{subs}} = \frac{m_{\text{subs}}}{V_{\text{subs}}}$$

The density of the reference (water) is:

$$\rho_{\text{water}} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{water}}}$$

By definition, specific gravity is the ratio of these two densities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG &= \frac{\rho_{\text{subs}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{m_{\text{subs}}}{V_{\text{subs}}}}{\dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{water}}}} \\
&= \frac{m_{\text{subs}}}{V_{\text{subs}}} \times \frac{V_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{water}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the same volume is considered for both ($V_{\text{subs}} = V_{\text{water}} = V$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG &= \frac{m_{\text{subs}}}{m_{\text{water}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** *For equal volumes, specific gravity equals the ratio of masses.*

## 3.2 Derivation: Apparent vs. True Specific Gravity

When weighing in air, Archimedes' buoyancy applies. The mass reading $m'$ is reduced by the buoyancy of the displaced air:

$$m' = m_{\text{true}} - \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V$$

Thus the apparent density is:

$$\rho_{\text{app}} = \frac{m'}{V} = \rho_{\text{true}} - \rho_{\text{air}}$$

The true density of the substance is:

$$\rho_{\text{true,s}} = \rho_{\text{app,s}} + \rho_{\text{air}}$$

Similarly for water:

$$\rho_{\text{true,w}} = \rho_{\text{app,w}} + \rho_{\text{air}}$$

Therefore, the true specific gravity becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_{\text{true}} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{true,s}}}{\rho_{\text{true,w}}} \\
&= \frac{\rho_{\text{app,s}} + \rho_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{app,w}} + \rho_{\text{air}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The apparent specific gravity (what a hydrometer reads) is:

$$SG_{\text{app}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{app,s}}}{\rho_{\text{app,w}}}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Milk Adulteration Test

**Problem:** A milk sample weighs **103.2 g** in a 100 mL pycnometer at 20°C. The same pycnometer weighs **100.0 g** when empty and **100.0 g** when filled with water. Calculate the specific gravity of the milk and estimate the total solids using the Quevenne formula.

### Step 1: Determine the volume of the pycnometer

The pycnometer filled with water weighs 100.0 g (with sample = water mass = 100.0 g in the container). The mass of water = 100.0 g (since empty + water = 100.0 + mass of water = observed mass; we are given the water mass in container is 100 g when filled).

Therefore, mass of water filling the pycnometer = **100.0 g**.

Since density of water at 20°C = 0.9982 g/cm³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} \\
&= \frac{100.0 \text{ g}}{0.9982 \text{ g/cm}^3} \\
&= 100.18 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Determine the mass of milk in the pycnometer

Given: Full pycnometer with milk = **200.0 g** (assuming problem data, where empty = 100.0 g, hence milk mass = 100.0 g; or the problem states 103.2 g of milk — re-reading the problem)

Re-interpret: Empty pycnometer mass + milk mass in container = 200.0 g. Milk mass in container = 200.0 − 100.0 = **100.0 g** ... but the problem stated 103.2 g, so:

Mass of milk in pycnometer = **103.2 g** (given directly from weighing).

### Step 3: Calculate density of milk

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{milk}} &= \frac{m_{\text{milk}}}{V} \\
&= \frac{103.2 \text{ g}}{100.18 \text{ cm}^3} \\
&= 1.0301 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Specific Gravity (20/20)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_{20/20} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{milk at 20°C}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 20°C}}} \\
&= \frac{1.0301}{0.9982} \\
&= 1.0319
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Estimate Total Solids (Quevenne)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%TS &= \frac{(SG - 1) \times 1000}{4} \\
&= \frac{(1.0319 - 1) \times 1000}{4} \\
&= \frac{31.9}{4} \\
&= 7.98\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Cow's milk typically contains 12–14% total solids. A reading of 7.98% suggests the milk has been heavily watered down — a strong indicator of adulteration.

## 3.4 Effect of Temperature on Specific Gravity

When temperature rises:
- Most food liquids expand → volume increases → density decreases → **SG decreases**.
- The reference water also expands, but at a different rate.

The thermal coefficient of specific gravity is approximated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_T = SG_{T_0} \times \left[1 - \beta (T - T_0)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\beta$ is the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (for water $\beta \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C near 20°C).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define specific gravity. Why is it a dimensionless quantity?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Specific gravity is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of an equal volume of a reference substance (water for liquids and solids) at specified temperatures. Mathematically:
>
> $$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{subs}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> It is dimensionless because it is a ratio of two quantities that have identical units (e.g., g/cm³ divided by g/cm³). The units cancel out, leaving a pure number. This makes SG a convenient universal property, independent of the unit system used (1 mark: definition, 1 mark: equation, 1 mark: dimensionless justification).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** A fruit juice has a specific gravity of 1.05 at 20°C. Calculate its density in (a) g/cm³ and (b) kg/m³.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Density of water at 20°C = 0.9982 g/cm³ = 998.2 kg/m³.
>
> (a) $\rho_{\text{juice}} = SG \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 1.05 \times 0.9982 = 1.0481$ g/cm³ (1.5 marks)
>
> (b) $\rho_{\text{juice}} = 1.0481 \times 1000 = 1048.1$ kg/m³ (1.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Define specific gravity. Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **hydrometer** used for measuring the specific gravity of liquids. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Specific gravity is the ratio of the density of a liquid to the density of pure water at a specified temperature. $SG = \rho_{\text{liquid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$. It is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.

**Principle (1.5 marks):** Archimedes' principle — a floating body displaces a weight of liquid equal to its own weight. The depth to which the hydrometer sinks is inversely proportional to the density of the liquid.

**Construction (1.5 marks):**
- A hollow glass tube, sealed at both ends.
- **Lower bulb**: weighted with mercury or lead shots to keep it upright.
- **Upper stem**: narrow and graduated with specific gravity markings.
- **Top bulb**: a smaller expansion to prevent complete submersion.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The hydrometer is gently lowered into the liquid at 20°C (or specified temperature).
2. It floats freely; the meniscus is read at the liquid level.
3. The SG value at the marked line is recorded directly.
4. For viscous liquids (syrups), readings take longer — must allow for temperature equilibration.

> **[KTU Examiner's Note]:** For a hydrometer reading on the stem, denser liquids give higher readings (deeper float) and lighter liquids give lower readings. A correction for surface tension is also needed for very accurate work.

---

**Q.A (b)** A dairy plant receives 5000 L of milk. The specific gravity is measured as 1.028 at 20°C. Calculate (i) the mass of milk, (ii) the percentage of total solids using the Quevenne lactometer formula, and (iii) the volume of water that must be added to make the SG drop to 1.025 (an indicator of tampering). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Volume of milk $V = 5000$ L = $5 \times 10^6$ cm³; SG = 1.028; density of water at 20°C = 0.9982 g/cm³.

**(i) Mass of milk (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{milk}} &= SG \times \rho_{\text{water}} \\
&= 1.028 \times 0.9982 \\
&= 1.0262 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{milk}} &= \rho_{\text{milk}} \times V \\
&= 1.0262 \times 5 \times 10^6 \\
&= 5.131 \times 10^6 \text{ g} \\
&= 5131 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total solids (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%TS &= \frac{(SG - 1) \times 1000}{4} \\
&= \frac{(1.028 - 1) \times 1000}{4} \\
&= \frac{28}{4} \\
&= 7.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Normal cow milk should have 12–14% total solids; 7% indicates significant dilution.

**(iii) Volume of water to be added (3 marks):**

Let mass of milk = $M$ = 5131 kg. Adding $x$ kg of water gives new mass = $M + x$ and new volume = $V + x/\rho_{\text{water}}$.

New SG required = 1.025, so new density = $1.025 \times 0.9982 = 1.0231$ g/cm³.

New volume in cm³ = $(M + x) \times 1000 / 1.0231$ (with $M$ in kg, water mass in kg)

Simplifying directly (in kg/L, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 0.9982$ kg/L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New SG} &= \frac{(M + x)}{(V + x/\rho_w) \cdot \rho_w} \\
1.025 &= \frac{M + x}{V \rho_w + x}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $V = 5000$ L, $M = 5131$ kg, $\rho_w = 0.9982$ kg/L:

$V \cdot \rho_w = 5000 \times 0.9982 = 4991$ kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.025 (4991 + x) &= 5131 + x \\
5115.78 + 1.025x &= 5131 + x \\
15.22 &= -0.025x \\
x &= -608.8 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative value indicates that water **must be removed** to *raise* the SG; the question is reversed — to *lower* SG from 1.028 to 1.025, water must be **added**. The algebraic negative sign arose because we set up a target below current SG. Recalculating correctly by inverting the sign: adding water, the new SG must be smaller.

Let $x$ = mass of water added (kg).

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.025 = \frac{5131 + x}{4991 + x}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.025(4991 + x) &= 5131 + x \\
5115.78 + 1.025x - 5131 - x &= 0 \\
0.025x &= 15.22 \\
x &= 608.8 \text{ kg} \approx 609.8 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **610 L of water** must be added to 5000 L of milk to drop SG from 1.028 to 1.025.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** What is meant by apparent and true specific gravity? Derive the relationship between them. State two instruments used to measure specific gravity of food liquids. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Apparent SG (1.5 marks):** The ratio of the *apparent* density of a substance (measured in air, including buoyancy effect) to the *apparent* density of water, both weighed in air.

**True SG (1.5 marks):** The ratio of *true* densities (in vacuum, i.e., corrected for air buoyancy).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

For a substance weighed in air:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{app,s}} &= \rho_{\text{true,s}} - \rho_{\text{air}} \\
\rho_{\text{app,w}} &= \rho_{\text{true,w}} - \rho_{\text{air}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_{\text{true}} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{true,s}}}{\rho_{\text{true,w}}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{app,s}} + \rho_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{app,w}} + \rho_{\text{air}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Instruments (1 mark, 0.5 each):** (i) Pycnometer, (ii) Hydrometer, (iii) Westphal balance, (iv) Digital density meter (any two).

---

**Q.B (b)** A pycnometer weighs 32.50 g empty, 132.50 g when filled with water at 25°C, and 135.40 g when filled with edible oil at 25°C. Calculate (i) the volume of the pycnometer, (ii) the density of the oil, and (iii) the specific gravity of the oil at 25°C/25°C. Density of water at 25°C = 0.9970 g/cm³. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Volume of pycnometer (2 marks):**

Mass of water in pycnometer = 132.50 − 32.50 = 100.0 g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{100.0}{0.9970} = 100.30 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Density of oil (2 marks):**

Mass of oil = 135.40 − 32.50 = 102.90 g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{V} = \frac{102.90}{100.30} = 1.0259 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Specific gravity at 25°C/25°C (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_{25/25} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{oil at 25°C}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 25°C}}} = \frac{1.0259}{0.9970} = 1.0289
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake Alert:**
> 1. **Temperature notation missing**: Always write SG as *T₁/T₂* (substance/reference). SG = 1.029 without subscript loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Density of water at wrong temperature**: Using 1.000 g/cm³ for water at 25°C gives a wrong answer. Always use the correct water density for the stated temperature.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract empty pycnometer mass**: Many students directly take the full mass as the sample mass — this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit mismatch**: Mixing kg/m³ and g/cm³ without conversion is a frequent error.
> 5. **Reporting SG with units**: SG is dimensionless; writing "1.03 g/cm³" is incorrect terminology.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Specific gravity** = density of substance / density of water. **Dimensionless**. **Temperature-dependent**.
- **Standard reference**: water at **4°C** (density = 1.000 g/cm³, the maximum density of water).
- **Notation**: *SG T₁/T₂* — always state both temperatures.
- **Apparent SG**: measured directly using hydrometer/pycnometer (includes air buoyancy).
- **True SG**: corrected for air buoyancy.
- **Common Food SG values to memorize**:
  - Water = 1.000
  - Milk = 1.028 – 1.035
  - Honey = 1.38 – 1.45
  - Edible oil = 0.91 – 0.93
  - Ethanol = 0.789
  - Concentrated fruit juice = 1.05 – 1.30
- **Quevenne Lactometer formula** for milk: $\%TS = (SG - 1) \times 1000 / 4$
- **Brix** (°Bx) ≈ sugar concentration; for sucrose ≈ $261 \times (1 - 1/SG)$.
- **Methods of measurement**: Pycnometer (most accurate lab), Hydrometer (industry), Westphal balance (oils), Digital density meter (modern, fast).
- **Always use the correct water density for the reference temperature** — do not assume 1.000 g/cm³ at all temperatures.
- **Denser liquids → higher SG**; **lighter liquids → lower SG**; substance sinks if SG > liquid, floats if SG < liquid.
- **Adulteration detection in milk**: SG below 1.028 → water added; SG above 1.035 → cream removed or synthetic milk.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compressibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Technology** and food rheology, **Compressibility** is defined as the relative volume change (or deformation) of a food material per unit change in applied compressive stress. It is a fundamental mechanical property that quantifies how a food product responds to a squeezing, crushing, or uniaxial compressive load.

Formally, compressibility ($\beta$) is the reciprocal of the **Bulk Modulus (K)**:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{\partial V}{\partial P}$$

where:
- $V$ = original volume of the food sample
- $\partial V$ = change in volume
- $\partial P$ = change in applied hydrostatic pressure

In **uniaxial compression testing** (the most common method in food texture analysis), compressibility is studied via the force–deformation relationship on a single axis, where a probe or platen pushes down on a food sample (e.g., a fruit, biscuit, cheese cube) and the resulting displacement is recorded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Compressibility is studied under the broader umbrella of **rheological and textural properties of foods**. It directly connects to the **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**, hardness determination, and the design of food processing equipment like extruders, rollers, and cutters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge vs. Marble" Intuition

Imagine two items on your kitchen table: a **fresh kitchen sponge** and a **hard marble**.

- If you press your thumb on the **marble**, it barely changes shape. It is **incompressible**. Even under enormous pressure, its volume stays virtually constant.
- If you press your thumb on the **sponge**, it visibly flattens. Its volume decreases noticeably. It is **highly compressible**.

Now think of a **ripe banana** versus a **raw carrot**:
- The **banana** is soft — it deforms a lot under compression.
- The **raw carrot** is firm and crisp — it deforms only slightly.

**Compressibility** in food technology is the mathematical way of measuring *exactly how much* a food squishes, flattens, or deforms when force is applied, and whether it returns to its original shape (elastic) or stays squished (plastic / viscoelastic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Most foods are **not purely elastic** like a spring. They are **viscoelastic** — they exhibit a mix of viscous (fluid-like, time-dependent) and elastic (solid-like) behavior. So compressibility measurements on foods often depend on **how fast** and **how long** you apply the force. This is what makes food rheology fascinating but also challenging.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa** = **1.01325 bar**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
- **Compressibility of water (approx.):** $\beta \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-10}$ **Pa⁻¹** (very low — water is nearly incompressible)
- **Compressibility of air at STP:** $\beta \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ **Pa⁻¹** (very high)
- **Typical unit of Bulk Modulus (K):** **Pa** or **MPa**
- **Typical unit of compressibility ($\beta$):** **Pa⁻¹** or **MPa⁻¹**

> [!NOTE]
> Foods with high moisture content (like fresh fruits, vegetables) tend to behave like water and are **nearly incompressible**, while **dry porous foods** (cereals, biscuits, freeze-dried products) are **highly compressible** due to trapped air pockets in their structure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Mechanics of Compression in Foods

When a food sample is subjected to a uniaxial compressive load, the following sequence of events occurs:

### Step 1: Application of Force
A known force $F$ is applied to the food sample (of original height $H_0$ and cross-sectional area $A_0$) via a flat platen or probe.

### Step 2: Generation of Stress
The applied force per unit original cross-sectional area is termed **Engineering Stress** (also called nominal stress):

$$\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

### Step 3: Generation of Strain
As the sample deforms, its height reduces from $H_0$ to $H$. The **Engineering Strain** (Cauchy strain) is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_e = \frac{\vert \Delta H \vert}{H_0} = \frac{H_0 - H}{H_0}$$

For compression, $\Delta H$ is negative, so we use absolute value to keep strain positive.

### Step 4: True (Hencky's) Strain Correction
For large deformations (common in soft foods like cheese, dough, gels), the **True Strain** or **Hencky's strain** is more accurate because it accounts for the continuously changing sample geometry:

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{H_0}{H}\right) = -\ln\left(\frac{H}{H_0}\right)$$

### Step 5: True Stress Correction
As the sample compresses, its cross-sectional area increases (volume is nearly conserved for incompressible foods). The **True Stress** accounts for the instantaneous area:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A}$$

Assuming **volume conservation** (valid for water-rich, incompressible foods):

$$A \cdot H = A_0 \cdot H_0 \implies A = A_0 \cdot \frac{H_0}{H}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{H_0}{H} = \sigma_e \cdot \frac{H_0}{H} = \sigma_e \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \varepsilon_e}$$

### Step 6: Bulk Modulus Derivation (Hydrostatic)
For **hydrostatic compression** (uniform pressure from all sides):

$$K = -V \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} = \rho \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the density. A higher $K$ means the material resists compression more strongly.

### Step 7: Force–Deformation Curve Interpretation
The output of a compression test is a **Force vs. Deformation curve** (or Stress vs. Strain curve). For foods, this curve typically shows:

- **Linear elastic region** — initial reversible deformation (Hookean: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$)
- **Yield point** — onset of permanent damage / plastic flow
- **Fracture point** — for brittle foods (carrots, biscuits) — sudden rupture
- **Plastic flow region** — for ductile foods (dough, cheese) — continuous deformation
- **Bioyield point** — the specific point where cell walls rupture in plant tissues (fruits/vegetables)

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why distinguish Engineering vs. True stress/strain?**
  Engineering values are simple to compute but only valid for **small strains (<5%)**. Soft foods often deform 30–80% during texture tests, so true values are mandatory for accurate modelling.

- **How does compressibility relate to food quality?**
  - **Fruits:** Compressibility correlates with **ripeness and firmness** — used in sorting/grading.
  - **Bakery:** Compressibility of dough affects **bread loaf volume and crumb structure**.
  - **Meat:** Compressibility relates to **tenderness** — tougher meat has lower compressibility.
  - **Packaging:** Compressibility of snack foods (chips, cereals) determines **breakage during transport**.

- **Why use $\ln$ in Hencky's strain?**
  Hencky's strain is the **integral of incremental strains** ($\int d\varepsilon = \int dH/H = \ln(H_0/H)$). It is the natural measure of large deformations and is **additive** — you can sum strains in stages.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable For |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma_e = F / A_0$ | Pa | Small strains |
| 2 | True Stress | $\sigma_t = F / A$ | Pa | Large strains |
| 3 | True Stress (incompressible) | $\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot H_0 / H$ | Pa | Water-rich foods |
| 4 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_e = (H_0 - H) / H_0$ | dimensionless | Small strains |
| 5 | True (Hencky's) Strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(H_0 / H)$ | dimensionless | Large strains |
| 6 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V (\partial P / \partial V)$ | Pa | Hydrostatic |
| 7 | Compressibility | $\beta = 1 / K$ | Pa⁻¹ | Hydrostatic |
| 8 | Uniaxial Compression Modulus | $K_{uniaxial} = F \cdot H / (A \cdot \Delta H)$ | Pa | Texture analysis |
| 9 | Poisson's Ratio (incompressible) | $\nu = 0.5$ | dimensionless | Rubber, water-rich foods |
| 10 | Relationship (isotropic linear) | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Pa | Elastic solids |
| 11 | Compressibility Factor | $\beta \cdot P = \Delta V / V$ | dimensionless | Quick estimate |
| 12 | Force–Deformation Slope | $k = F / \Delta H$ | N/m | Stiffness measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In uniaxial food compression problems, unless the problem specifically states "hydrostatic", use the uniaxial formulas (rows 3, 5, 8 above). The hydrostatic Bulk Modulus is reserved for thermodynamic/process engineering questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Sensory Texture Mapping:** Compression tests mimic the **action of teeth (molars) biting food**. The data is correlated with human sensory panels to predict consumer acceptance.

2. **Harvest Timing Decision:** Apple growers use a **Magness-Taylor pressure test** (a compression test with an 8 mm probe) to determine optimal harvest date. Firmness (inverse of compressibility) is the key parameter.

3. **Extrusion Processing:** In the design of extruder screws and dies, the compressibility of the food dough/feed determines the **specific mechanical energy (SME)** input and the expansion ratio of the final product (pasta, snacks).

4. **Cold Storage Quality:** Compressibility of frozen foods changes with ice crystal formation. Tracking this helps in **quality control of frozen vegetables**.

5. **3D Food Printing:** Compressibility of food pastes determines **shape retention** after extrusion of each layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between True Stress and Engineering Stress (Incompressible Food)

We derive the relationship between true stress ($\sigma_t$) and engineering stress ($\sigma_e$) for a food sample undergoing uniaxial compression, assuming **incompressibility** (constant volume — valid for most water-rich foods).

### Given:
- Original height: $H_0$
- Original cross-sectional area: $A_0$
- Instantaneous height: $H$
- Instantaneous cross-sectional area: $A$
- Applied force: $F$

### Assumption: Volume Conservation
For an incompressible food material:

$$V_0 = V$$

$$A_0 \cdot H_0 = A \cdot H$$

Solving for $A$:

$$A = \frac{A_0 \cdot H_0}{H}$$

### Substituting into True Stress definition:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A}$$

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{\left(\dfrac{A_0 \cdot H_0}{H}\right)}$$

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F \cdot H}{A_0 \cdot H_0}$$

### Recognizing $\sigma_e = F / A_0$:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{H}{H_0}$$

Wait — careful. Let me redo this carefully:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F \cdot H}{A_0 \cdot H_0} = \left(\frac{F}{A_0}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{H}{H_0}\right)$$

Now, $\frac{H}{H_0} = 1 - \varepsilon_e$ because $\varepsilon_e = \frac{H_0 - H}{H_0} \implies \frac{H}{H_0} = 1 - \varepsilon_e$.

Therefore:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note (Important!):** For **compression**, the engineering strain is positive, and $H < H_0$, so $(1 - \varepsilon_e) < 1$. This means the true stress is **lower** than engineering stress for compression of incompressible foods — opposite to the case of **tension** (where the cross-section necks down and true stress is higher). This is a key conceptual point KTU examiners love to test.

### Final compact form:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Bulk Modulus from Thermodynamic Definition

The **isothermal compressibility** is defined for a fluid/food system as:

$$\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

The **Bulk Modulus** is its reciprocal:

$$K_T = \frac{1}{\beta_T} = -V\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

Expressing in terms of density $\rho$ (since $\rho = m/V$ and mass $m$ is constant):

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} \implies dV = -\frac{m}{\rho^2} d\rho = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$

Substituting:

$$K_T = -V \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial \left(-\dfrac{V}{\rho} d\rho\right)} = \rho \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}$$

So:

$$K_T = \rho \cdot \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_T$$

This is the form used in **equation of state** work for liquid foods (e.g., Tait equation for juice, milk).

## 3.3 Derivation: Uniaxial Compression Modulus for a Food Cylinder

Consider a cylindrical food sample of height $H_0$ and area $A_0$ compressed by a platen moving down by displacement $\Delta H$. Assuming small linear-elastic deformation:

### Hooke's Law (1D form):

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Substituting definitions:

$$\frac{F}{A_0} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta H}{H_0}$$

### Solving for the apparent modulus $E_{app}$:

$$E_{app} = \frac{F \cdot H_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta H}$$

This $E_{app}$ is the **apparent Young's modulus** under uniaxial compression. For non-cylindrical samples, a **shape factor (correction factor)** is applied (e.g., for spheres, platens, etc.):

$$E_{app} = \frac{F \cdot H_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta H} \cdot f(shape)$$

Common shape factors:
- **Flat platen on flat sample:** $f = 1$
- **Sphere between flat platens:** $f \approx 0.96$ to $1.0$ depending on strain
- **Cylinder between flat platens:** $f = 1$ (if no barreling)

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

### Problem:
A cylindrical sample of **mozzarella cheese** has:
- Original height $H_0 = 20$ mm
- Original diameter $d_0 = 25$ mm
- Force applied $F = 50$ N
- New height after compression $H = 15$ mm

Assume volume is conserved. Calculate: (a) Engineering stress, (b) Engineering strain, (c) True stress, (d) True (Hencky's) strain, (e) Apparent Young's modulus.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the original area.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: (a) Engineering stress.**

$$\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{50}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.019 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 101.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: (b) Engineering strain.**

$$\varepsilon_e = \frac{H_0 - H}{H_0} = \frac{20 - 15}{20} = 0.25 \text{ (or 25\%)}$$

**Step 4: (c) True stress (using incompressibility).**

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e) = 101.9 \times 10^3 \times (1 - 0.25)$$

$$\sigma_t = 101.9 \times 10^3 \times 0.75 = 76.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5: (d) True (Hencky's) strain.**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{H_0}{H}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{20}{15}\right) = \ln(1.3333) = 0.2877$$

**Step 6: (e) Apparent Young's modulus.**

$$E_{app} = \frac{F \cdot H_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta H} = \frac{50 \times 0.020}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.005}$$

$$E_{app} = \frac{1.0}{2.454 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.074 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 407.4 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world check:** Mozzarella cheese has a Young's modulus around **0.1 – 1.0 MPa** in the linear range. Our calculated 407 kPa falls in this range — the calculation is physically reasonable.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Compressibility Behaviour of Common Foods

| Food Type | Approx. K (MPa) | Approx. $\beta$ (MPa⁻¹) | Behaviour | Moisture Content |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Water (reference)** | 2200 | $4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | Nearly incompressible | 100% |
| **Apple (fresh)** | 5 – 15 | $0.07 - 0.2$ | Low compressibility | ~85% |
| **Potato (raw)** | 3 – 10 | $0.1 - 0.33$ | Low compressibility | ~80% |
| **Cheddar cheese** | 0.5 – 2 | $0.5 - 2.0$ | Moderate | ~36% |
| **Bread crumb** | 0.1 – 0.5 | $2 - 10$ | Highly compressible | ~40% |
| **Extruded snack (porous)** | 0.01 – 0.1 | $10 - 100$ | Very highly compressible | <5% |
| **Freeze-dried fruit** | 0.001 – 0.01 | $100 - 1000$ | Extremely compressible | <3% |
| **Cooked rice (grain)** | 50 – 200 | $0.005 - 0.02$ | Low (hard grain) | ~70% |
| **Butter** | 0.1 – 0.5 | $2 - 10$ | Highly compressible | ~80% |
| **Gummy candy** | 0.05 – 0.2 | $5 - 20$ | Highly compressible | ~15% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition:** Compressibility generally **increases** as moisture content decreases for porous foods (because air is much more compressible than water). However, for **dense foods** (candy, hard cheese), the relationship is dominated by the solid matrix structure, not moisture alone.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Uniaxial Compression Test Setup (Food Texture Analyzer)

The following block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a typical uniaxial compression test as performed in a food texture analyzer (e.g., TA-XT2, Instron, Texture Technologies).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Sample Mounted] --> A2[Load Cell Calibration]
    A2 --> A3[Platen Approaches Sample at Test Speed]
    A3 --> A4{Contact Detected}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Record Trigger Force Threshold]
    A4 -- No --> A6[Platen Continues Search]
    A6 --> A4
    A5 --> B1[Apply Compressive Force F]
    B1 --> B2[Measure Displacement DeltaH]
    B2 --> B3[Sample Deforms from H0 to H]
    B3 --> C1[Data Acquisition System Samples F and DeltaH]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Engineering Stress sigmaE]
    C1 --> C3[Compute Engineering Strain epsilonE]
    C1 --> C4[Compute True Stress sigmaT]
    C1 --> C5[Compute True Strain epsilonT]
    C2 --> D1[Plot Force vs Displacement Curve]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Identify Linear Elastic Slope]
    D1 --> E2[Identify Bioyield Point]
    D1 --> E3[Identify Fracture Point]
    D1 --> E4[Identify Maximum Force]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Apparent Young Modulus Eapp]
    E2 --> F2[Compute Hardness Index]
    E3 --> F3[Compute Brittleness Index]
    E4 --> F4[Compute Compressibility Coefficient]
    F1 --> G1[Final Output Report]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[End: Store Data in Texture Database]
```

## 4.2 Force–Deformation Curve Anatomy (Typical Soft Food)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin Point] --> B[Linear Elastic Region]
    B --> C[Bioyield Point]
    C --> D[Plastic Deformation Region]
    D --> E[Fracture Point]
    E --> F[Post Fracture Region]
    F --> G[End of Test]

    subgraph SLOPE
        B
    end

    subgraph PEAKS
        C
        E
    end
```

## 4.3 Compressibility Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Food Sample Selected] --> Q1{Material Contains Air Pockets?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Highly Compressible Category]
    P1 --> P2[Examples: Bread, Extruded Snacks, Freeze Dried Foods]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Material is Water Rich greater than 70 percent?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P3[Low Compressibility Category]
    P3 --> P4[Examples: Fresh Fruits, Vegetables, Milk Based Foods]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the Material Hard and Crystalline?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P5[Very Low Compressibility Category]
    P5 --> P6[Examples: Hard Candy, Ice, Frozen Foods]
    Q3 -- No --> P7[Moderate Compressibility Category]
    P7 --> P8[Examples: Cheese, Cooked Meat, Dough]
```

## 4.4 Bulk Modulus vs. Uniaxial Compression — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hydrostatic
        H1[Pressure Applied Uniformly from All Directions]
        H2[Volume Change Measured]
        H3[Compute K hydrostatic]
    end

    subgraph Uniaxial
        U1[Force Applied Along Single Axis]
        U2[Height Change Measured]
        U3[Compute E uniaxial]
    end

    H1 --> H2 --> H3
    U1 --> U2 --> U3

    H3 --> REL[Relation via Poisson Ratio]
    U3 --> REL

    REL[For Linear Isotropic Material]
    REL --> F[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ only holds for **linear elastic isotropic** materials. Most foods are viscoelastic and anisotropic (especially meat, fruits with skin), so this relationship is approximate. KTU examiners often give partial credit for stating this caveat.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, GDFTT104]**
**Define compressibility. State its SI unit. How is it related to the Bulk Modulus?**

**Model Answer:**
Compressibility is defined as the fractional reduction in volume of a material per unit increase in applied pressure. It quantifies how easily a food material can be compressed (deformed volumetrically) under external stress.

Mathematically:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

The **SI unit of compressibility is Pa⁻¹ (per Pascal)**, though MPa⁻¹ is more practical for food systems.

The relationship to Bulk Modulus is reciprocal:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K}$$

A high Bulk Modulus means the material resists compression strongly, and consequently its compressibility is low (e.g., water). A low Bulk Modulus corresponds to a material that compresses easily, hence has high compressibility (e.g., bread).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, GDFTT104]**
**Distinguish between Engineering Stress and True Stress during a uniaxial compression test on a food sample.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Engineering Stress | True Stress |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Definition | Force divided by **original** cross-sectional area | Force divided by **instantaneous** cross-sectional area |
| Formula | $\sigma_e = F / A_0$ | $\sigma_t = F / A$ |
| Valid for | Small deformations (strain < 5%) | Large deformations |
| For incompressible food (compression) | Always higher than true stress | Lower than engineering stress |
| Usage | Quick calculations, simple reports | Accurate modelling, large strain analysis |

In compression of an incompressible food, the cross-sectional area **increases** as the sample shortens. So $A > A_0$, which means $\sigma_t < \sigma_e$. The exact relationship is:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e)$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, GDFTT104]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between True Stress and Engineering Stress for an incompressible food sample undergoing uniaxial compression. Clearly state the assumption used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical sample of **paneer (Indian cottage cheese)** has an original height of 30 mm and original diameter of 40 mm. It is compressed uniaxially to a final height of 22 mm under a constant force of 80 N. Assuming volume conservation, calculate:
- (i) Engineering stress
- (ii) Engineering strain
- (iii) True stress
- (iv) True (Hencky's) strain
- (v) Apparent Young's modulus in the linear region **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumption:** The food sample is **incompressible** (constant volume during deformation). This is valid for water-rich foods like paneer (moisture ~70%).

**Derivation:**

Start with the incompressibility condition:

$$V_0 = V \implies A_0 \cdot H_0 = A \cdot H$$

Solve for instantaneous area $A$:

$$A = A_0 \cdot \frac{H_0}{H}$$

Write the definitions of true and engineering stress:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A}, \quad \sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

Form the ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_e} = \frac{F/A}{F/A_0} = \frac{A_0}{A}$$

Substitute the area relation:

$$\frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_e} = \frac{A_0}{A_0 \cdot H_0/H} = \frac{H}{H_0}$$

Express in terms of engineering strain. Recall $\varepsilon_e = (H_0 - H)/H_0$, so $H/H_0 = 1 - \varepsilon_e$:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e)$$

This is the required relationship. For compression, $0 < \varepsilon_e < 1$, so $\sigma_t < \sigma_e$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $H_0 = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- $d_0 = 40$ mm $\implies r_0 = 0.020$ m
- $H = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m
- $F = 80$ N

**Step 1: Original area.**

$$A_0 = \pi r_0^2 = \pi (0.020)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: (i) Engineering stress.**

$$\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{80}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} = 63.66 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: (ii) Engineering strain.**

$$\varepsilon_e = \frac{H_0 - H}{H_0} = \frac{0.030 - 0.022}{0.030} = \frac{0.008}{0.030} = 0.2667 \text{ (26.67%)}$$

**Step 4: (iii) True stress.**

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_e) = 63.66 \times (1 - 0.2667) = 63.66 \times 0.7333 = 46.68 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5: (iv) True (Hencky's) strain.**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{H_0}{H}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.030}{0.022}\right) = \ln(1.3636) = 0.3102$$

**Step 6: (v) Apparent Young's modulus.**

$$E_{app} = \frac{F \cdot H_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta H} = \frac{80 \times 0.030}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.008} = \frac{2.40}{1.005 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.387 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 238.7 \text{ kPa}$$

This value is in the typical range for paneer (100–500 kPa), confirming the calculation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, GDFTT104]**

**(a)** Define Bulk Modulus. With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the uniaxial compression test procedure for a food sample using a texture analyzer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of **fresh apple tissue** (cylindrical, $H_0 = 15$ mm, $d_0 = 20$ mm) is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure increase from 0.1 MPa to 5.0 MPa. The volume of the sample decreases from $4.712 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ to $4.680 \times 10^{-6}$ m³. Calculate:
- (i) The bulk modulus of the apple tissue
- (ii) The compressibility of the apple tissue
- (iii) Comment on whether the apple tissue behaves more like water or a porous food **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Bulk Modulus:**
The Bulk Modulus ($K$) is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic pressure change to the relative volume change of a material, with a negative sign to make it a positive quantity:

$$K = -V \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}$$

It has units of **Pascal (Pa)** and represents the resistance of a material to uniform compression from all sides.

**Block Diagram — Uniaxial Compression Test Procedure:**

```
[1. Sample Preparation]
         |
         v
[2. Mount sample on lower platen]
         |
         v
[3. Lower platen / load cell zeroed]
         |
         v
[4. Upper platen moves down at constant test speed v (e.g., 1 mm/s)]
         |
         v
[5. Probe contacts sample surface (trigger detected at 0.05 N)]
         |
         v
[6. Platen continues downward; force F and displacement DeltaH recorded continuously]
         |
         v
[7. Data logged in computer: (time, F, DeltaH)]
         |
         v
[8. Test stops at predefined: (a) % strain, (b) max force, or (c) sample fracture]
         |
         v
[9. Platen retracts; sample removed]
         |
         v
[10. Software computes: sigma_e, epsilon_e, sigma_t, epsilon_t, E_app, hardness, bioyield]
         |
         v
[11. Output: Force vs Displacement curve + report]
```

**Key Components of the Texture Analyzer:**
- Load cell (force measurement)
- Linear actuator (displacement control)
- Platen/probe (geometry depends on food)
- Data acquisition system
- Analysis software

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $H_0 = 15$ mm, $d_0 = 20$ mm
- $P_1 = 0.1$ MPa, $P_2 = 5.0$ MPa
- $V_1 = 4.712 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $V_2 = 4.680 \times 10^{-6}$ m³

**Step 1: (i) Bulk Modulus.**

Pressure change:

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 5.0 - 0.1 = 4.9 \text{ MPa} = 4.9 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

Volume change (magnitude):

$$\vert \Delta V \vert = V_1 - V_2 = 4.712 \times 10^{-6} - 4.680 \times 10^{-6} = 0.032 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 3.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

Bulk Modulus:

$$K = -V_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V} = -\left(4.712 \times 10^{-6}\right) \cdot \frac{4.9 \times 10^{6}}{-3.2 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$K = \frac{4.712 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.9 \times 10^{6}}{3.2 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{23.09}{3.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.215 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 721.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: (ii) Compressibility.**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{7.215 \times 10^{8}} = 1.386 \times 10^{-9} \text{ Pa}^{-1} = 1.386 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: (iii) Comparison with water.**

Water: $K_{water} = 2200$ MPa; $\beta_{water} = 4.5 \times 10^{-10}$ Pa⁻¹ = 0.45 GPa⁻¹

Apple tissue: $K_{apple} = 721.5$ MPa; $\beta_{apple} = 1.386$ GPa⁻¹

Comparison:
- Apple tissue has a **lower Bulk Modulus** (about 3× less) and a **higher compressibility** (about 3× more) than pure water.
- This means apple tissue is **more compressible** than water.
- Reason: Apple tissue has **air-filled intercellular spaces** (~20-25% porosity) and a **pithy/porous cell structure**, unlike pure liquid water. However, because apple is mostly water and has limited porosity, it still behaves **closer to water** than to a highly porous food (e.g., bread, which is ~100× more compressible).

**Conclusion:** The apple tissue behaves more like **water (low compressibility)** than a porous food, but with measurable deviation due to its cellular structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors:** Students often forget the negative sign in the Bulk Modulus formula. Always state $K = -V (\Delta P / \Delta V)$ explicitly.
> 2. **Unit Conversion:** Mixing MPa and Pa is the #1 reason for lost marks. Always convert everything to **SI base units** (Pa, m³) before computing.
> 3. **Confusing Engineering and True Stress in Compression:** Many students assume true stress > engineering stress in compression, copying the formula from tension. Remember: in **incompressible compression**, the cross-section **expands**, so true stress is **lower** than engineering stress.
> 4. **Forgetting to Compute $\ln$ for True Strain:** True strain is $\ln(H_0/H)$, NOT $(H_0 - H)/H_0$. The latter is engineering strain.
> 5. **Not Stating the Incompressibility Assumption:** Whenever using $A = A_0 H_0 / H$, you **must** state the incompressibility assumption to get full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compressibility (GDFTT104, Module 3)**

- ✅ **Compressibility ($\beta$)** is the fractional volume change per unit pressure change; **Bulk Modulus ($K$)** is its reciprocal.
- ✅ SI units: $\beta$ in **Pa⁻¹** (or MPa⁻¹); $K$ in **Pa** (or MPa).
- ✅ **Engineering stress** uses original area $A_0$; **True stress** uses instantaneous area $A$.
- ✅ **Engineering strain** $= (H_0 - H)/H_0$; **True (Hencky's) strain** $= \ln(H_0/H)$.
- ✅ For **incompressible** food in compression: $\sigma_t = \sigma_e (1 - \varepsilon_e)$ and $\sigma_t < \sigma_e$.
- ✅ For **incompressible** food in tension: $\sigma_t = \sigma_e / (1 - \varepsilon_e)$ and $\sigma_t > \sigma_e$.
- ✅ **Water-rich foods** (fruits, vegetables, milk products) have **low compressibility** (high $K$).
- ✅ **Porous/dry foods** (bread, extruded snacks, freeze-dried products) have **high compressibility** (low $K$).
- ✅ The **apparent Young's modulus** in a uniaxial compression test is $E_{app} = F H_0 / (A_0 \Delta H)$.
- ✅ The **bioyield point** on a force–deformation curve corresponds to **cell wall rupture** in plant tissues and is a key quality indicator.
- ✅ The **isotropic linear elastic relation** is $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$; for fully incompressible materials, $\nu = 0.5$ and $E = 3K$.
- ✅ **Tait equation** is a common empirical model for the high-pressure compressibility of liquid foods: $K = K_0 + k \cdot P$.
- ✅ Compressibility of foods depends on **moisture content, porosity, temperature, and strain rate** (because foods are viscoelastic).
- ✅ Real-world applications: **Magness-Taylor firmness test** for apples, **texture profile analysis (TPA)**, **extruder design**, **3D food printing**, **frozen food quality control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** When a question mentions "compression test on a food" without specifying hydrostatic, default to the **uniaxial interpretation** and use $\sigma_e$, $\varepsilon_e$, $E_{app}$. Only use the hydrostatic Bulk Modulus $K$ when the problem explicitly says "uniform pressure from all sides" or "hydrostatic pressure".

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Surface Tension (σ)

**Formal Definition:** Surface tension is the tendency of the surface of a liquid to resist external forces and minimize its surface area. It is defined as the force per unit length acting along an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface, or equivalently, the energy required to increase the surface area by one unit.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a stretched rubber sheet — it pulls inward. Similarly, the surface of a liquid behaves like an elastic membrane, always trying to "shrink" to the smallest possible area. That is why water droplets are spherical — a sphere has the minimum surface area for a given volume. In food technology, surface tension controls how a liquid food (milk, juice, syrup) forms droplets, spreads on a surface, or forms emulsions (mayonnaise, butter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** Surface tension is responsible for **capillarity**, **drop formation**, **foaming**, and **emulsification** in food systems. The standard value of σ for **water at 20 °C is 0.0728 N/m**.

---

## 1.2 Viscosity (μ) — The Internal Friction of a Fluid

**Formal Definition:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that resists the relative motion of its layers. It quantifies the internal resistance to flow (shear stress). Two related forms exist:

**(a) Absolute (Dynamic) Viscosity, μ:**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where τ is shear stress (N/m²), du/dy is the velocity gradient (s⁻¹), and μ has units of **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²** or **Poiseuille (Pl) = 10 Poise = 1 Pa·s**.

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity, ν:**
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

where ρ is fluid density (kg/m³).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of pouring honey vs. water from the same bottle. Honey flows slowly because its internal molecules "grip" each other strongly — high viscosity. Water flows freely — low viscosity. In food processing, viscosity governs **pumping power**, **heat transfer rate**, **mixing efficiency**, and **flow through pipelines**.

> [!NOTE]
> **1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s ; 1 cP (centipoise) = 0.001 Pa·s**. Water at 20 °C has μ ≈ 1.002 cP. Many liquid foods (oils, syrups, sauces) are far more viscous.

---

## 1.3 Rheology of Fluids

**Formal Definition:** Rheology is the science of the deformation and flow of matter. In food technology, it studies how food materials deform and flow under applied stress, going beyond simple Newtonian behavior.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Water always flows the same way regardless of how hard you stir it (Newtonian). But tomato ketchup sits still, then suddenly flows when squeezed hard (non-Newtonian / shear-thinning). Rheology classifies such behaviors systematically.

---

## 1.4 Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids

**Newtonian Fluid:** Obeys Newton's law of viscosity linearly:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Examples: water, air, thin oils, milk (approximately), low-concentration sugar solutions.

**Non-Newtonian Fluid:** Apparent viscosity changes with shear rate (du/dy). Categories include:
- **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** viscosity decreases with shear rate — ketchup, fruit juice concentrates, blood.
- **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** viscosity increases with shear rate — corn starch slurry, wet sand.
- **Bingham Plastic:** behaves as a solid until yield stress τ₀ is exceeded — toothpaste, mayonnaise, margarine.
- **Thixotropic / Rheopectic:** time-dependent behavior — yogurt, gels.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Newtonian fluid = a calm person who reacts predictably. Non-Newtonian = a moody person — react differently depending on how much you push them.

---

## 1.5 Fluid Statics

**Formal Definition:** The study of fluids at rest, where velocity is zero and only pressure forces and body forces (gravity) act. Key principles: Pascal's Law, hydrostatic pressure distribution, and the barometric equation.

---

## 1.6 Pascal's Law

**Formal Definition:** A pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every part of the fluid and the walls of the container.

$$\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h \quad \text{transmitted everywhere}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Squeeze a fully filled water balloon at one point — the pressure rises equally at every other point of the balloon instantly. This is the principle behind hydraulic lifts, hydraulic presses, and food processing equipment like hydraulic juice presses.

---

## 1.7 Hydrostatic Equilibrium

**Formal Definition:** In a static fluid, the pressure at any depth h below a free surface is:
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

In a rotating (centrifugal) field, fluid particles experience a centrifugal acceleration ω²r in addition to gravity, leading to a parabolic free-surface profile.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A swimming pool — pressure increases the deeper you go. In a rotating salad spinner, the water is pushed outward to the wall, not the bottom.

---

## 1.8 Barometric Equation

**Formal Definition:** It describes how atmospheric pressure varies with altitude in a compressible, isothermal atmosphere (or with temperature variation):
$$P = P_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Mgh}{RT}\right)$$

where M = molar mass, g = gravity, h = height, R = universal gas constant, T = absolute temperature.

---

## 1.9 Manometer Principle

**Formal Definition:** A manometer measures fluid pressure by balancing a column of liquid (manometer fluid) against the unknown pressure. For a U-tube:
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$$

---

## 1.10 Gravity and Centrifugal Decanters

**Gravity Decanter:** Uses the density difference between two immiscible liquids; the heavier phase settles down due to gravity, and the lighter phase floats, allowing separation by decantation.
**Centrifugal Decanter:** Uses centrifugal force (g-force > 1000g) to accelerate the phase separation in food applications like milk cream separation, oil clarification, fruit juice clarification.

---

## 1.11 Fluid Kinematics & Reynolds Experiment

**Fluid Kinematics:** Deals with the geometry of fluid motion (velocity field, streamlines, flow types) without considering forces.

**Reynolds Number (Re):**
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

**Reynolds Experiment:** Osborne Reynolds (1883) injected dye into water flowing in a transparent pipe and observed:
- **Re < 2100:** Laminar flow (smooth, parallel streamlines, dye travels in straight line).
- **Re > 4000:** Turbulent flow (chaotic, eddies, dye mixes completely).
- **2100 < Re < 4000:** Transitional flow.

**Equation of Continuity (Statement):** For an incompressible fluid in steady flow through a pipe of varying cross-section:
$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q \quad (\text{constant volumetric flow rate})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Derivation of the continuity equation is **NOT required** — only the statement and application are needed.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Variation of Fluid Properties with Temperature and Pressure

### (a) Viscosity vs Temperature

**For Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** sharply with temperature because intermolecular cohesive forces weaken. Empirical relation (Andrade's equation):
$$\mu = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{T}\right)$$

**For Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with temperature because momentum transfer between molecules increases with random molecular motion. Sutherland's formula:
$$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

where S ≈ 110.4 K for air.

### (b) Density vs Temperature and Pressure
- Liquids: density **decreases** with temperature (thermal expansion); is **almost incompressible** — changes very little with pressure.
- Gases: density is highly sensitive to both T and P (Ideal gas: ρ = PM/RT).

### (c) Surface Tension vs Temperature
Surface tension **decreases** with temperature and becomes zero at the **critical temperature**:
$$\sigma = \sigma_0 \left(1 - \frac{T}{T_c}\right)^n \quad (n \approx 1.2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> In food processing, heating oils/syrups **reduces viscosity** (easier pumping) and **reduces surface tension** (better wetting of solid food surfaces — important in coating and frying).

---

## 2.2 Rheology — Generalized Newton's Law

For all fluid types, the shear stress can be expressed in generalized form:
$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

| Fluid Type | τ₀ | K | n | Behaviour |
|------------|-----|---|----|--------------|
| Newtonian | 0 | μ | 1 | τ = μ(du/dy) |
| Pseudoplastic | 0 | K | n < 1 | Shear-thinning |
| Dilatant | 0 | K | n > 1 | Shear-thickening |
| Bingham Plastic | τ₀ > 0 | K | 1 | Yield stress needed |

Apparent viscosity for non-Newtonian fluids:
$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | σ = F/L | Surface tension | N/m |
| 2 | τ = μ(du/dy) | Newton's law of viscosity | N/m² |
| 3 | ν = μ/ρ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 4 | μ_liq = A exp(B/T) | Liquid viscosity vs T | Pa·s |
| 5 | Re = ρvD/μ = vD/ν | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 6 | P = P₀ + ρgh | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa |
| 7 | P = P₀ exp(–Mgh/RT) | Barometric equation | Pa |
| 8 | P_A – P_B = (ρ_m – ρ)gh | Manometer (differential) | Pa |
| 9 | A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ | Continuity (incompressible) | m³/s |
| 10 | Q = A·v | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 11 | τ = τ₀ + K(γ̇)ⁿ | General fluid model | Pa |
| 12 | F_c = mω²r = m·v²/r | Centrifugal force | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Conversion Must-Know for KTU:**
> 1 Pa·s = 10 Poise = 1000 cP
> 1 m²/s = 10⁴ Stokes = 10⁶ cSt
> 1 atm = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 10.33 m H₂O

---

## 2.4 Real-World Food Engineering Applications

1. **Pump Selection:** Viscosity determines the type and power of pumps used for milk, oils, fruit pulps.
2. **Heat Exchanger Design:** Higher viscosity reduces convective heat transfer coefficient → larger area needed.
3. **Quality Control:** Rheology determines mouthfeel of sauces, pourability of syrups, spreadability of butter.
4. **Centrifugal Separators:** Used in dairy industry to separate cream (lighter) from skim milk (heavier).
5. **Spray Drying:** Surface tension controls droplet size, which controls drying rate.
6. **Beverage Carbonation:** Pressure solubility governed by Henry's law depends on absolute pressure.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (P = P₀ + ρgh)

**Setup:** Consider a static fluid element of cross-sectional area A and small thickness dh at depth h below a free surface. Forces acting on it:

1. Pressure on top face: P (downward on fluid)
2. Pressure on bottom face: (P + dP) (upward on fluid)
3. Weight of element: dW = ρ·g·A·dh (downward)

**Equilibrium condition (ΣF_vertical = 0):**
$$P \cdot A - (P + dP) \cdot A - \rho g A \, dh = 0$$

**Step 1:** Cancel A from all terms:
$$P - P - dP - \rho g \, dh = 0$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:
$$-dP = \rho g \, dh$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from free surface (h = 0, P = P₀) to depth h (P):
$$\int_{P_0}^{P} -dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

**Step 4:** Since ρ and g are constant for an incompressible fluid:
$$-(P - P_0) = \rho g h$$

**Step 5:** Final expression:
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

**Engineering Insight:** This equation is the basis of all manometer and pressure-gauge problems, design of storage tanks, and food packaging under vacuum/pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Barometric Equation (Isothermal Atmosphere)

**Setup:** Consider a thin horizontal slab of air at altitude h, thickness dh, area A, density ρ(h). Hydrostatic equilibrium gives:
$$dP = -\rho g \, dh$$

**Step 1:** For an ideal gas, ρ = PM/RT (where M is molar mass of air = 0.029 kg/mol):
$$dP = -\frac{PM g}{RT} \, dh$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:
$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{RT} \, dh$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from sea level (h = 0, P = P₀) to altitude h (P = P):
$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{RT}\int_{0}^{h} dh$$

**Step 4:** Both integrals are simple:
$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{Mgh}{RT}$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:
$$P = P_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Mgh}{RT}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **isothermal barometric formula**. In food engineering, the same exponential form is used in vacuum drying (P vs. moisture content) and in describing gas solubility in carbonated beverages.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Continuity Equation (Statement + Justification Only, per KTU)

**Setup:** Consider a stream-tube (control volume) between section 1 (area A₁) and section 2 (area A₂) of a pipe carrying an incompressible steady flow.

**Conservation of Mass:** Mass entering per second = Mass leaving per second.

Since ρ is constant:
$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2$$

Cancelling ρ:
$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q \quad (\text{constant volumetric flow rate})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Directive:** "Derivation not needed" — only the statement, definition of stream-tube, and the concept of incompressible steady flow should be explained.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Reynolds Number as a Dimensionless Group

**Starting from dynamic similarity:** The ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flowing fluid defines Re.

Inertial force per unit volume: ρ·v·(dv/dx) ~ ρv²/L
Viscous force per unit volume: μ·(d²v/dy²) ~ μv/L²

**Step 1:** Form the ratio:
$$\frac{\text{Inertial}}{\text{Viscous}} = \frac{\rho v^2 / L}{\mu v / L^2} = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

**Step 2:** For pipe flow, characteristic length L = D (diameter):
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

**Significance:** Re < 2100 → laminar; Re > 4000 → turbulent; 2100 < Re < 4000 → transitional.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Centrifugal vs Gravity Decanter

| Parameter | Gravity Decanter | Centrifugal Decanter |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Driving force | g (9.81 m/s²) | ω²r (can exceed 10,000g) |
| Separation time | Hours | Seconds |
| Equipment size | Large | Compact |
| Separation efficiency | Limited for fine particles | Very high |
| Food applications | Oil–water in settling tanks, wine clarification | Cream separation, juice clarification, milk skimming |
| Limitation | Slow for emulsions | Higher energy cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Centrifugal acceleration** = ω²r = v²/r. If r = 0.1 m and ω = 3000 rpm (= 314 rad/s), then a_c ≈ 9,870 m/s² ≈ 1000 g.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: U-Tube Manometer

**Problem:** A U-tube manometer with mercury (ρ_m = 13,600 kg/m³) is connected to a pipe carrying oil (ρ_oil = 800 kg/m³). The mercury level difference is h = 0.25 m. Find the gauge pressure of oil in the pipe (above atmospheric).

**Solution:** Apply manometer principle at the bottom common level:
$$P_{oil} + \rho_{oil} g h_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

If the oil-mercury interface is at the lower mercury level and the open side is exposed to atmosphere, the simplified manometer equation gives:
$$P_{oil, gauge} = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{oil}) g h$$

$$P_{oil, gauge} = (13600 - 800) \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$

**Step 1:** Calculate difference in densities:
$$\Delta \rho = 13600 - 800 = 12800 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by g:
$$\Delta \rho \cdot g = 12800 \times 9.81 = 125568 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by h:
$$P = 125568 \times 0.25 = 31,392 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer:** P_oil,gauge ≈ **31.4 kPa**
