# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024]`
**Define absolute viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and the relation between them.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Absolute (Dynamic) Viscosity, μ:** It is the ratio of shear stress (τ) to the velocity gradient (du/dy) in a flowing fluid. It represents the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. **SI unit: Pa·s** (also N·s/m² or Poiseuille).
- **Kinematic Viscosity, ν:** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity (μ) to the density (ρ) of the fluid. It represents the resistance to flow under the action of gravity. **SI unit: m²/s** (also Stoke).
- **Relation:** ν = μ / ρ

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024]`
**Explain the principle of a manometer with a neat sketch and derive the pressure difference equation for a U-tube manometer.**

**Model Answer:**
A manometer works on the principle of balancing the unknown pressure against a column of manometric fluid (e.g., mercury) under gravity. Considering a U-tube manometer with manometric fluid of density ρ_m and a fluid of density ρ in the pipe:

**Equation:** P_A − P_B = (ρ_m − ρ) g h

where h is the difference in the levels of the manometric fluid in the two limbs. (Sketch: U-tube with two limbs connected to points A and B; left limb open to atmosphere for gauge pressure measurement.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) Classify fluids based on their rheological behaviour with the help of a stress vs. shear-rate diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The classification is based on the relationship between shear stress (τ) and shear rate (du/dy = γ̇). The flow curve (rheogram) plots τ (y-axis) vs γ̇ (x-axis).

1. **Newtonian Fluid:** The rheogram is a straight line through the origin. Slope = μ.
   $$\tau = \mu \gamma'$$
   *Examples:* water, air, glycerine, low-concentration sugar solutions.

2. **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning):** Curve starts steep (high apparent viscosity) and flattens (viscosity decreases) as shear rate increases. No yield stress. n < 1.
   $$\tau = K (\gamma')^n$$
   *Examples:* ketchup, blood, fruit juice concentrates, polymer solutions, molten chocolate.

3. **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening):** Curve becomes progressively steeper — apparent viscosity increases with shear rate. n > 1.
   *Examples:* corn starch slurry, wet sand, sugar–water mixtures with high solids.

4. **Bingham Plastic:** Behaves as a rigid body until yield stress τ₀ is reached, then flows linearly.
   $$\tau - \tau_0 = \mu_p \gamma'$$
   *Examples:* toothpaste, mayonnaise, drilling mud, margarine.

5. **Thixotropic:** Time-dependent pseudoplastic — viscosity decreases with time of shearing at constant shear rate.

6. **Rheopectic:** Time-dependent dilatant — viscosity increases with time of shearing.

(Sketch required: τ on y-axis; γ̇ on x-axis; show all six curves relative to Newtonian line.)

---

**(b) Describe the Reynolds experiment and the classification of flow based on Reynolds number. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reynolds Experiment (Osborne Reynolds, 1883):**
A transparent horizontal glass pipe is connected to a steady water tank. A dye of the same density as water is injected through a fine needle at the entrance of the pipe. The flow rate is gradually increased by opening a control valve.

**Observations:**
- At **low flow rates**: Dye forms a **smooth, straight, unbroken thread** parallel to the pipe axis → **Laminar Flow**.
- At **moderately increased flow rates**: Dye thread begins to waver and shows small disturbances → **Transitional Flow**.
- At **high flow rates**: Dye thread **breaks up and diffuses** through the entire cross-section, mixing thoroughly → **Turbulent Flow**.

**Classification based on Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

| Flow Type | Reynolds Number Range | Character |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------|
| Laminar | Re < 2100 | Smooth, parallel streamlines |
| Transitional | 2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 | Unstable, intermittent |
| Turbulent | Re > 4000 | Chaotic, eddies, mixing |

**Significance for food industry:**
- Determines whether flow in pipes (milk, juice, oil) is laminar or turbulent.
- Affects heat transfer coefficient, pressure drop calculations, and pump power.
- For non-circular ducts, the **hydraulic diameter** (D_h = 4A/P) is used in place of D.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) State and explain Pascal's law. Describe the working of a hydraulic press with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of Pascal's Law:** "A pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of the containing vessel."

**Explanation:** When an external force is applied on a confined fluid, the resulting pressure increase is the same at all points inside the fluid. This is because fluids cannot sustain shear stress in static condition, so any applied pressure must be transmitted as a normal stress equally.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\Delta P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Working of a Hydraulic Press (Block Diagram below):**
- A small force F₁ is applied on a small piston of area A₁ → pressure P = F₁/A₁.
- This pressure is transmitted undiminished through the fluid to a larger piston of area A₂.
- The output force on the larger piston: F₂ = P × A₂ = F₁ × (A₂/A₁).
- Mechanical advantage = A₂ / A₁.

**Applications in food industry:**
- Hydraulic juice presses for fruit extraction.
- Hydraulic oil presses for cold-pressing vegetable oils.
- Hydraulic lifts in dairy and meat processing plants.

---

**(b) Define hydrostatic equilibrium. Derive the equation for the variation of pressure in a fluid column under gravity. A tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.85. Find the pressure at a depth of 5 m below the free surface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Hydrostatic equilibrium refers to the condition of a fluid at rest (or moving with constant velocity), where the sum of forces on any fluid element is zero. There is no shear stress, only normal pressure and body forces (gravity).

**Derivation of P = P₀ + ρgh:**

Consider a static fluid element of cross-sectional area A, located at depth h below the free surface of an open container. Apply force balance in the vertical direction:

Forces:
- Downward pressure on top face: P × A
- Upward pressure on bottom face: (P + dP) × A
- Downward weight: dW = ρ g A dh

Equilibrium: Sum of forces = 0
$$P A - (P + dP) A - \rho g A dh = 0$$
$$-A \, dP = \rho g A \, dh$$
$$dP = -\rho g \, dh$$

Integrate from h = 0 (P = P₀) to h (P):
$$P - P_0 = -\rho g h$$
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

**Numerical Problem:**

Given:
- Specific gravity of oil = 0.85 → ρ = 0.85 × 1000 = 850 kg/m³
- h = 5 m
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- P₀ = atmospheric (gauge pressure is computed → P₀,gauge = 0)

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic equation:
$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 5$$

**Step 2:** Calculate:
$$P = 850 \times 49.05 = 41,692.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 41.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer:** P ≈ **41.7 kPa (gauge)** or **143.0 kPa (absolute)** at 5 m depth in oil.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing μ in cP without converting to Pa·s in problems — always state and convert units.
> 2. **Mixing τ and τ₀:** Forgetting to include yield stress τ₀ in Bingham plastic equation.
> 3. **Confusing Dynamic vs Kinematic Viscosity:** ν = μ/ρ (not ρ/μ).
> 4. **Barometric Equation Mistake:** Forgetting to use **M (molar mass)** in kg/kmol and **R = 8314 J/(kmol·K)**, NOT 8.314.
> 5. **Continuity Equation Misuse:** Applying A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ to compressible flow without correction.
> 6. **Manometer Direction Sign:** Mixing up P_A and P_B orientation — always start from one side and walk through the manometer to the other side, adding pressure when going down and subtracting when going up.
> 7. **Reynolds Number:** Using kinematic viscosity (ν) with diameter in mm without unit consistency (convert all to SI: m, m²/s, m/s).
> 8. **Rheology Plots:** Drawing flow curves without labeling axes and showing which curve corresponds to which fluid type.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Surface tension (σ)** = F/L [N/m]; water at 20 °C = 0.0728 N/m; **decreases with temperature**.
- ✅ **Absolute (dynamic) viscosity μ** = τ/(du/dy) [Pa·s]; **kinematic viscosity ν = μ/ρ** [m²/s].
- ✅ **1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s = 10⁻³ Pa·s**; water at 20 °C: μ ≈ 1.002 cP, ν ≈ 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s.
- ✅ **Liquid viscosity decreases with T** (Andrade: μ = A exp(B/T)); **gas viscosity increases with T** (Sutherland).
- ✅ **Newtonian fluid:** τ = μ(du/dy); examples: water, air, thin oils.
- ✅ **Pseudoplastic** (n < 1): ketchup, blood, juice concentrates.
- ✅ **Dilatant** (n > 1): corn starch slurry, wet sand.
- ✅ **Bingham plastic** (τ = τ₀ + μ_p γ̇): toothpaste, mayonnaise.
- ✅ **Thixotropic** = time-dependent pseudoplastic (yogurt); **Rheopectic** = time-dependent dilatant.
- ✅ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure change in enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic equation:** P = P₀ + ρgh.
- ✅ **Centrifugal hydrostatics:** Free surface is a paraboloid z = (ω²r²)/(2g); effective g = ω²r.
- ✅ **Barometric equation (isothermal):** P = P₀ exp(–Mgh/RT).
- ✅ **Manometer:** P_A − P_B = (ρ_m − ρ)gh; for open-end gauge manometer: P = (ρ_m − ρ)gh.
- ✅ **Gravity decanter:** separation by density difference under g (~9.81 m/s²); slow.
- ✅ **Centrifugal decanter:** separation under ω²r (1000–10000 g); fast, used in milk cream separation, juice clarification.
- ✅ **Reynolds experiment:** Dye injection; **Re < 2100 laminar, 2100–4000 transitional, > 4000 turbulent**.
- ✅ **Reynolds number:** Re = ρvD/μ = vD/ν (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Continuity equation:** A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = Q (incompressible steady flow) — **derivation not required in KTU**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for Flow Classification:** "**L**aminar is **L**ow Re, **T**urbulent is **T**op Re" → **L < 2100 < T (4000)**. Remember: laminar flow = layered, viscous-dominated, Re small.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Bernoulli's equation and its applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Bernoulli's equation and its applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Equation** is a fundamental statement of the **conservation of mechanical energy** for a flowing fluid. It expresses that, for a **steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow along a streamline**, the sum of the pressure head, the velocity head, and the elevation (potential) head remains **constant**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{constant}
$$

where each term represents a specific form of energy per unit weight of the fluid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Water flowing through a hillside pipe**
> Imagine a pipe carrying water down a hillside from a higher reservoir to a lower tank.
> - At the **top**, water moves **slowly**, but it has **high pressure** and **high elevation**.
> - At the **bottom**, water moves **fast**, but it has **lower pressure** and **lower elevation**.
>
> Bernoulli's equation says: as the water speeds up, it must "give up" either pressure or height. **Energy is neither created nor destroyed — it is simply traded between pressure, velocity, and elevation.**

In food processing plants, this same principle governs how milk, fruit juices, oils, and syrups flow through pipelines, pumps, heat exchangers, and spray nozzles.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density of water at 4 °C:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$
> - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^{3}$
> - **Bernoulli's constant** is expressed in units of **metres (m)** of fluid (energy per unit weight).

### 1.4 Why Bernoulli's Equation Matters in Food Technology

Food technologists frequently handle fluids (milk, oils, fruit pulps, broths, syrups). Understanding how these fluids behave under flow is essential for designing:
- Pasteurizers and heat exchangers
- Pumping systems for liquid foods
- Spray dryers and atomizers
- Mixing and agitation tanks
- Flow metering devices (Venturi, orifice meters)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Behind Bernoulli's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bernoulli's equation is valid only when **ALL** of the following assumptions hold:
> 1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters do not change with time.
> 2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density $\rho$ is constant.
> 3. **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — fluid viscosity is negligible.
> 4. **Flow along a streamline** — the equation applies between two points on the same streamline.
> 5. **No shaft work** — no pump, turbine, or compressor adds or removes energy between the two points.

### 2.2 The Three Energy Terms — Interpretation

Each term in Bernoulli's equation represents a specific form of mechanical energy per unit weight of the fluid:

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|------|------|------------------|------|
| $P / \rho g$ | **Pressure head** | Energy due to fluid pressure | m |
| $v^{2} / 2g$ | **Velocity head** (kinetic head) | Energy due to fluid motion | m |
| $z$ | **Elevation head** (potential head) | Energy due to vertical position | m |
| $P / \rho g + v^{2} / 2g + z$ | **Total head** | Sum of all three energy heads | m |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form the backbone of every Bernoulli-based problem.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy per unit weight) |
| 2 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} + \rho g z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy per unit volume) |
| 3 | $\frac{P}{\rho} + \tfrac{1}{2}v^{2} + g z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy per unit mass) |
| 4 | $\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}$ | Applied between points 1 and 2 |
| 5 | $\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} \pm h_{\text{pump}} - h_{\text{turbine}} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{L}$ | Extended Bernoulli (real fluids with machines and losses) |
| 6 | $Q = A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$ | Continuity equation (conservation of mass) |
| 7 | $v = C_{d} \sqrt{\tfrac{2 g \Delta h}{1 - (A_{2}/A_{1})^{2}}}$ | Orifice / Venturi flow velocity |
| 8 | $h_{f} = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing absolute value of a quantity in any KTU exam answer (e.g. $\vert P_{1} - P_{2} \vert$), do **NOT** use the vertical pipe $\vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\vert x \vert$ or `\lvert x \rvert` instead to avoid table-breaking.

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Term — Engineering Insight

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why $P/\rho g$?** Pressure does work on a fluid column of height equal to $P/\rho g$ — this is the *equivalent height* of fluid that the pressure could support.
> - **Why $v^{2}/2g$?** This is the **Torricelli head** — the height a fluid would have to fall freely to reach velocity $v$. It originates from $v^{2} = 2gh$.
> - **Why $z$?** A fluid element at elevation $z$ has potential energy $\rho g z$ per unit volume; dividing by $\rho g$ gives just $z$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Food Technology

In food plants, Bernoulli's equation is used to:
- **Size pumps** for liquid food transfer (milk, oil, juice).
- **Design Venturi scrubbers** for air-borne dust/spice removal.
- **Calibrate flow meters** measuring milk or beverage flow rates.
- **Compute spray nozzle exit velocities** in spray drying of milk powder.
- **Analyze pipe networks** carrying steam, water, or product in CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Streamline Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Used:** Applying Newton's second law of motion to a small fluid element moving along a streamline, then integrating.

**Setup:** Consider a fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ moving along a streamline that makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.

Let:
- Pressure at the upstream face = $P$
- Pressure at the downstream face = $P + dP$
- Velocity of the fluid element = $v$
- Elevation of the element above a datum = $z$

The geometry gives:

$$
dz = ds \, \sin\theta
$$

---

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the fluid element in the streamline direction $s$:**

1. **Net pressure force** acting in the direction of motion:
   The pressure on the rear face pushes the element forward; the pressure on the front face pushes it backward.
   Net pressure force = $P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA = -dP \cdot dA$

2. **Weight component** along $s$ (opposing upward motion):
   Weight of the element = $\rho g \, dA \, ds$
   Component along $s$ = $-\rho g \, dA \, ds \, \sin\theta = -\rho g \, dA \, dz$

3. **Friction and other forces:** Assumed zero (inviscid flow).

---

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law along the streamline direction $s$:**

$$
\text{Mass} \times \text{Acceleration} = \text{Net force along } s
$$

Mass of the element = $\rho \, dA \, ds$
Acceleration along $s$ = $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = v \dfrac{dv}{ds}$ (for a particle moving along the streamline)

Therefore:

$$
\rho \, dA \, ds \cdot v \frac{dv}{ds} = -dP \cdot dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz
$$

---

**Step 3 — Simplify the equation:**

Divide both sides by $dA$:

$$
\rho \, ds \cdot v \frac{dv}{ds} = -dP - \rho g \, dz
$$

Cancel $ds$:

$$
\rho \, v \, dv = -dP - \rho g \, dz
$$

Rearrange:

$$
dP + \rho g \, dz + \rho v \, dv = 0
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
dP + \rho v \, dv + \rho g \, dz = 0
$$

---

**Step 4 — Integrate between two points (1 and 2) on the streamline:**

$$
\int_{1}^{2} dP + \rho \int_{1}^{2} v \, dv + \rho g \int_{1}^{2} dz = 0
$$

Performing each integration:

$$
(P_{2} - P_{1}) + \rho \left(\frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2} - \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2}\right) + \rho g (z_{2} - z_{1}) = 0
$$

---

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard Bernoulli form:**

$$
P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2} + \rho g z_{1} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2} + \rho g z_{2}
$$

This is **Bernoulli's equation in energy per unit volume form**.

---

**Step 6 — Convert to energy per unit weight (head form):**

Divide throughout by $\rho g$:

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the head form, every term has units of **metres (m)** of fluid — convenient for plotting energy grade lines and hydraulic grade lines in food process pipelines.

---

### 3.2 Alternative Form: Energy Per Unit Mass

Dividing by $\rho$ instead of $\rho g$:

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2} + g z_{1} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2} + g z_{2}
$$

Here each term has units of **J/kg**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Connection of Each Term to Conservation Law

| Conservation Principle | Term in Bernoulli's Equation | Energy Form |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------|
| Work done by pressure forces | $P / \rho g$ | Flow / pressure energy |
| Kinetic energy of the fluid | $v^{2} / 2g$ | Kinetic energy |
| Gravitational potential energy | $z$ | Potential energy |
| **Sum (constant)** | $P/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z = C$ | **Total mechanical energy per unit weight** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fluid Element Along a Streamline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-level schematic illustrates the forces and energy transformations acting on a fluid element moving along a streamline — used as the basis of the Bernoulli derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FLUID ELEMENT dA x ds] --> B1[Moving along streamline at angle theta]
    B1 --> C1[Mass = rho x dA x ds]
    C1 --> D1[Acceleration along s = v dv/ds]

    F1[Pressure force on rear face P dA] --> N1[Net pressure force = -dP x dA]
    F2[Pressure force on front face P + dP times dA] --> N1
    N1 --> SUM[Net force along s]

    G1[Weight W = rho g dA ds] --> G2[Component along s = rho g dA dz]
    G2 --> SUM

    SUM --> EQ1[rho dA ds x v dv/ds = -dP dA - rho g dA dz]

    EQ1 --> EQ2[dP + rho v dv + rho g dz = 0]

    EQ2 --> EQ3[Integrate from 1 to 2]

    EQ3 --> FINAL[Bernoulli Equation: P/rho g + v squared / 2g + z = Constant]
```

### 4.2 Energy Transformations Along a Pipeline (Conceptual Flow)

This diagram illustrates how the three energy heads exchange values along a converging-diverging pipeline carrying a liquid food product.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point 1: Large Area] -->|Velocity LOW| P2[Point 2: Small Area]
    P2 -->|Velocity HIGH| P3[Point 3: Large Area]
    P3 -->|Velocity LOW again| P4[Point 4: Exit]

    P1 --> H1[Pressure head HIGH]
    P2 --> H2[Velocity head HIGH]
    P3 --> H3[Pressure head RECOVERS]
    P4 --> H4[Total head CONSTANT minus losses]

    H1 -.-> TOTAL[Total head constant if inviscid]
    H2 -.-> TOTAL
    H3 -.-> TOTAL
    H4 -.-> TOTAL
```

### 4.3 Real-World Application Schematic — Spray Nozzle in a Milk Powder Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TANK[Liquid milk feed tank] --> PUMP[High-pressure pump]
    PUMP --> PIPE[Pipeline]
    PIPE --> NOZZLE[Atomizer nozzle - small area]
    NOZZLE --> SPRAY[Fine milk droplets for spray drying]
    SPRAY --> DRYER[Hot air chamber - converts to milk powder]

    TANK -.Pressure head HIGH, velocity LOW.-> PIPE
    PIPE -.Continuity increases velocity.-> NOZZLE
    NOZZLE -.Pressure converts to kinetic energy.-> SPRAY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As liquid milk enters the narrow nozzle, the cross-sectional area decreases. By continuity ($A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$), velocity increases, and by Bernoulli's equation, pressure must drop. The high-velocity jet then atomizes into fine droplets — the core operating principle of any **spray dryer** used in dairy plants.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State Bernoulli's equation and list any **four assumptions** made in its derivation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bernoulli's equation states that for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{constant}
$$

**Four assumptions:**
1. Steady flow — properties do not change with time.
2. Incompressible flow — $\rho$ is constant.
3. Inviscid flow — no viscous (friction) losses.
4. Flow is along a streamline — no energy addition/removal between the two points considered.

*(1 mark for equation, 2 marks for assumptions — any four valid assumptions accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Distinguish between the **pressure head**, **velocity head**, and **elevation head** terms of Bernoulli's equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------|------------------|
| Pressure head | $P / \rho g$ | Energy per unit weight stored as fluid pressure |
| Velocity head | $v^{2} / 2g$ | Kinetic energy per unit weight of the moving fluid |
| Elevation head | $z$ | Potential energy per unit weight due to vertical position |

The sum $P/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z$ is called the **total head** and remains constant for ideal flow.

---

### 5.2 PART B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from the principle of conservation of energy for a fluid element moving along a streamline. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe carrying fruit juice contracts from a diameter of **15 cm** at section 1 to **7.5 cm** at section 2. The pressure at section 1 is **120 kPa** and the velocity is **1.5 m/s**. Assuming the pipe is horizontal and the juice has a density of **1050 kg/m³**, find the pressure at section 2. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above. The student must clearly state:

1. **Assumptions** — steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline. (2 marks)
2. **Free body diagram** of fluid element showing pressure forces and weight component. (1 mark)
3. **Application of Newton's second law** along streamline direction. (1 mark)
4. **Integration** yielding the final Bernoulli equation. (2 marks)
5. **Final equation** in all three forms (head, volume, mass). (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_{1} = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $D_{2} = 7.5 \text{ cm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $P_{1} = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $v_{1} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- $z_{1} = z_{2}$ (horizontal pipe)

**Step 1 — Find the velocity $v_{2}$ using continuity:**

$$
A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^{2} \times 1.5 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} \times v_{2}
$$

$$
v_{2} = 1.5 \times \left(\frac{0.15}{0.075}\right)^{2} = 1.5 \times (2)^{2} = 1.5 \times 4 = 6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between sections 1 and 2:**

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}
$$

Since $z_{1} = z_{2}$, the elevation terms cancel:

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g}
$$

Solving for $P_{2}$:

$$
P_{2} = P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
P_{2} = 120{,}000 + \frac{1}{2} \times 1050 \times \left((1.5)^{2} - (6)^{2}\right)
$$

$$
P_{2} = 120{,}000 + 525 \times (2.25 - 36)
$$

$$
P_{2} = 120{,}000 + 525 \times (-33.75)
$$

$$
P_{2} = 120{,}000 - 17{,}718.75
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{2} = 102{,}281.25 \text{ Pa} \approx 102.28 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Interpretation:** As the juice flows through the contraction, its velocity quadruples (from 1.5 to 6 m/s). Consequently, the pressure drops from 120 kPa to about 102.28 kPa — a direct confirmation of Bernoulli's principle.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation using the principle of conservation of energy. Mention the limitations of Bernoulli's equation when applied to real fluid systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A venturi meter is installed in a horizontal pipeline carrying milk. The inlet diameter is **20 cm**, throat diameter is **10 cm**, and the differential pressure head measured is **20 cm of milk**. If the discharge coefficient is **0.98**, calculate the volume flow rate of milk. Take density of milk as **1030 kg/m³** and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation & Limitations (7 marks):**

**Derivation outline:** Consider a fluid element moving from point 1 to point 2 along a streamline. The work done by pressure and gravity forces equals the change in kinetic energy:

$$
(P_{1} - P_{2}) \cdot dV - \rho g \, dV (z_{2} - z_{1}) = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, dV (v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2})
$$

Rearranging yields the standard Bernoulli equation.

**Limitations (3 marks expected):**
1. Valid only for **steady, incompressible, inviscid** flow.
2. Applies only **along a streamline**, not between two different streamlines.
3. **Ignores friction losses** — real fluids always have viscous effects.
4. **No shaft work** — cannot directly handle pumps or turbines.
5. Assumes **no heat transfer** between the fluid and surroundings.

**Part (b) — Venturi Meter Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_{1} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $D_{2} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Differential head $h = 0.20 \text{ m of milk}$
- $C_{d} = 0.98$, $\rho = 1030 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
A_{1} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^{2} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
A_{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^{2} = 0.007854 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} = \left(\frac{0.10}{0.20}\right)^{2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the venturi discharge formula:**

$$
Q = C_{d} \cdot \frac{A_{1} A_{2}}{\sqrt{A_{1}^{2} - A_{2}^{2}}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
$$

First, compute:

$$
A_{1}^{2} - A_{2}^{2} = (0.03142)^{2} - (0.007854)^{2} = 9.87 \times 10^{-4} - 6.17 \times 10^{-5} = 9.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
\sqrt{A_{1}^{2} - A_{2}^{2}} = 0.03042 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
A_{1} \cdot A_{2} = 0.03142 \times 0.007854 = 2.467 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20} = \sqrt{3.924} = 1.981 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Q$:**

$$
Q = 0.98 \times \frac{2.467 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03042} \times 1.981
$$

$$
Q = 0.98 \times 8.111 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.981
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 0.01575 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 15.75 \text{ L/s}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on Bernoulli-based questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always express pressures in Pa, velocities in m/s, and heads in metres. Mixing units is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Omitting assumptions** — A derivation without clearly stated assumptions loses up to **2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention** — When applying Bernoulli's equation, write all terms on **opposite sides** with clear subscripts; examiners deduct marks for unclear algebra.
> 4. **Ignoring elevation difference** — Even if the pipe is "nearly horizontal," do not cancel $z_1$ and $z_2$ unless explicitly told the pipe is horizontal.
> 5. **Using Bernoulli for real fluids without friction correction** — If the problem says "real fluid" or "with losses," you must include the head loss term $h_f$.
> 6. **Plugging into venturi formula without the discharge coefficient** — Always verify if $C_d$ is given; if yes, multiply by it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bernoulli's Equation**

- **Equation (head form):** $\dfrac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \dfrac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}$
- **Equation (volume form):** $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} + \rho g z = \text{constant}$
- **Five assumptions:** steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no shaft work.
- **Three heads:** pressure head ($P/\rho g$), velocity head ($v^{2}/2g$), elevation head ($z$).
- **Continuity equation:** $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$ (must always accompany Bernoulli).
- **Trade-off principle:** as velocity increases, pressure and/or elevation must decrease.
- **Extended Bernoulli (real fluids):** add $h_{\text{pump}}$ and subtract $h_{\text{turbine}}$ and $h_L$.
- **Common applications in food plants:**
  - Venturi meters for measuring milk, juice, or oil flow.
  - Spray nozzles in spray drying (milk powder, coffee, flavors).
  - Pump sizing for CIP and product transfer lines.
  - Atomizers in flavor encapsulation systems.
- **Venturi formula:** $Q = C_{d} \dfrac{A_{1} A_{2}}{\sqrt{A_{1}^{2} - A_{2}^{2}}} \sqrt{2 g h}$
- **Always state units** in your final answer — KTU examiners award partial credit for clearly dimensioned final answers.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Bernoulli's equation gives no information about **friction losses** — use Darcy–Weisbach or Hazen–Williams for that.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of process calculations - units and dimensions - basic and derived quantities - conversion of units - conversion of empirical equations - moisture content - wet basis and dry basis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of process calculations - units and dimensions - basic and derived quantities - conversion of units - conversion of empirical equations - moisture content - wet basis and dry basis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Units and Dimensions — Formal Definition

In the context of process calculations in Food Technology, a **unit** is a standard reference quantity used to express the magnitude of a physical variable, while a **dimension** is the qualitative nature of that physical variable (independent of the numerical value). Every measured or calculated quantity in a food processing unit operation (e.g., pasteurization, drying, evaporation) carries both a numerical magnitude and a unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"A unit is a chosen standard of measurement of a physical quantity, while a dimension represents the fundamental nature of that quantity expressed in terms of basic quantities (M, L, T, θ, …)."*

The **International System of Units (SI)** is the globally accepted standard for engineering process calculations and forms the backbone of KTU numerical problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying rice for a hostel mess. The shopkeeper says "**5**" — but 5 what? 5 kilograms, 5 grams, 5 quintals, or 5 bags? Without the **unit**, the number **5 is meaningless**. Now imagine the shopkeeper says "I need rice measured by **weight**." The word *weight* tells you the *dimension* (mass-type), and kg or g gives you the *unit*. So:

- **Dimension** = the *type* of measurement (mass, length, time, temperature)
- **Unit** = the *chosen standard* to express that dimension (kg, m, s, °C)

In Food Technology, this distinction is critical: a heating calculation for milk pasteurization needs energy (Joules or calories), time (seconds), and temperature (°C or K) — all must be dimensionally consistent.

### 1.3 Basic (Fundamental) vs Derived Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **Basic Quantities** are independent physical quantities that do not depend on other quantities for their definition. **Derived Quantities** are formed by mathematical combinations of two or more basic quantities.

| **Basic Quantity** | **SI Symbol** | **SI Unit** | **SI Symbol (Unit)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length | L | metre | m |
| Mass | M | kilogram | kg |
| Time | T | second | s |
| Temperature | θ | kelvin | K |
| Amount of substance | N | mole | mol |
| Electric current | I | ampere | A |
| Luminous intensity | J | candela | cd |

**Examples of Derived Quantities used in Food Processing:**

- **Velocity** = Length / Time → m/s
- **Density** = Mass / Volume → kg/m³
- **Force** = Mass × Acceleration → kg·m/s² (Newton, N)
- **Energy** = Force × Length → kg·m²/s² (Joule, J)
- **Power** = Energy / Time → kg·m²/s³ (Watt, W)
- **Pressure** = Force / Area → kg/(m·s²) (Pascal, Pa)
- **Heat Transfer Coefficient** = W/(m²·K)
- **Specific Heat** = J/(kg·K)
- **Thermal Conductivity** = W/(m·K)
- **Viscosity** = Pa·s
- **Surface Tension** = N/m
- **Diffusivity** = m²/s
- **Concentration** = mol/m³ or kg/m³

### 1.4 Conversion of Units — Definition

Unit conversion is the multiplicative process of changing a quantity from one unit system to another (e.g., SI to CGS, or English to Metric) using a **conversion factor** that is dimensionless and equal to 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** Unit conversion problems are worth 3–7 marks and test a student's ability to apply the **dimensional homogeneity** principle (every term in a physical equation must have the same dimensions).

### 1.5 Conversion of Empirical Equations — Definition

An empirical equation is a mathematical relationship obtained from experimental data (e.g., a heat transfer correlation). When such an equation is developed in one unit system, it must be **converted to another unit system** by substituting dimensional conversion factors — this process is called **conversion of empirical equations** (also known as **dimensionless substitution** or **dimensional analysis of equations**).

> [!NOTE]
> Example: The Dühring rule, Antoine equation for vapor pressure, and various heat-mass transfer correlations in food drying (e.g., Page's model, Henderson-Pabis model) are empirical and frequently need unit conversion for food process design.

### 1.6 Moisture Content — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Moisture content of a food material is the quantity of water present in it, expressed either on a wet basis (w.b.) or dry basis (d.b.)."*

Moisture content is the single most important parameter in food preservation operations such as drying, dehydration, baking, frying, and storage stability studies. It directly influences:
- Shelf life of food products
- Microbial growth (water activity)
- Texture, color, and nutritional quality
- Energy requirements in drying calculations

There are two ways to express it:
1. **Wet Basis (w.b.)** — moisture expressed per unit mass of *wet* material
2. **Dry Basis (d.b.)** — moisture expressed per unit mass of *bone-dry* (moisture-free) material

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet basis** is the most commonly used in food industry (e.g., "fresh apple contains 84% moisture w.b."), while **dry basis** is preferred in drying engineering calculations because the dry mass remains constant during the drying process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SI System — Dimensional Foundation

The SI system provides **7 base units** and an unlimited number of **derived units** formed by multiplication and division. In food process calculations, we predominantly use:

- **Mass (kg)**, **Length (m)**, **Time (s)**, **Temperature (K)**
- Plus derived units: **Newton (N)**, **Joule (J)**, **Watt (W)**, **Pascal (Pa)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Homogeneity Principle (Buckingham π-theorem foundation):**
> *A valid physical equation must have the same dimensions on both sides of the equality sign.* This is the single most tested concept in unit conversion problems.

### 2.2 Steps in Unit Conversion

1. **Identify** the original unit and the target unit.
2. **Write down** the conversion factor with both units visible.
3. **Multiply** the given quantity by the conversion factor, arranging the units so that unwanted units **cancel out**.
4. **Perform** the arithmetic.

**General Rule:** To convert a quantity from unit $U_1$ to $U_2$:

$$X_{U_2} = X_{U_1} \times \left(\frac{U_1}{U_2}\right)_{\text{conversion factor}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion factor is always **dimensionless = 1**, e.g., $\left(\frac{1 \text{ km}}{1000 \text{ m}}\right) = 1$.

### 2.3 Conversion of Empirical Equations — Stepwise Logic

**Procedure:**

1. Write the empirical equation in its **original units** (developed by experimenter).
2. Express each variable as a product: $\text{Variable} = (\text{Numerical value}) \times (\text{Unit})$.
3. Replace each unit with the corresponding **new unit** in the target system.
4. Simplify to obtain a new constant in the equation.
5. The new equation is **dimensionally consistent** in the new unit system.

> [!NOTE]
> This procedure is essentially the **Rayleigh method** and is the foundation of **dimensional analysis** — converting equations without having to re-run expensive experiments.

### 2.4 Moisture Content — Wet Basis vs Dry Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Wet basis (M.C.$_{w.b.}$)** uses the **total (wet) mass** as the reference.
> - **Dry basis (M.C.$_{d.b.}$)** uses the **dry (moisture-free) mass** as the reference.
> - For the same food sample: **M.C.$_{d.b.}$ > M.C.$_{w.b.}$** (because the denominator is smaller in d.b.)
> - **M.C.$_{w.b.}$** can never exceed 1 (or 100%), but **M.C.$_{d.b.}$** can be greater than 1 (e.g., 200% for very wet foods).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary / Note** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass balance (general) | $\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation}$ | All in kg or kg/h | Steady state → Input = Output |
| Wet basis M.C. | $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{\text{total}}}$ | $m_w$ = mass of water (kg); $m_d$ = mass of dry solid (kg) | $0 \le M_{w.b.} \le 1$ |
| Dry basis M.C. | $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$ | Same as above | $M_{d.b.} \ge 0$ (can exceed 1) |
| Relationship (w.b. to d.b.) | $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}}$ | — | Valid for fractional form |
| Relationship (d.b. to w.b.) | $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$ | — | — |
| Total mass from M.C.$_{d.b.}$ | $m_{\text{total}} = m_d(1 + M_{d.b.})$ | kg | — |
| Dry solid mass | $m_d = m_{\text{total}}(1 - M_{w.b.})$ | kg | — |
| Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | — |
| Specific volume | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | m³/kg | — |
| Specific gravity | $S.G. = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{substance}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | dimensionless | At 4 °C, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³ |
| Force | $F = m \cdot a$ | N (kg·m/s²) | — |
| Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | 1 atm = 101325 Pa |
| Energy / Work | $E = F \cdot d$ | J (N·m) | 1 cal = 4.184 J |
| Power | $P = \dfrac{E}{t}$ | W (J/s) | — |
| Heat required (sensible) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | $c_p$ = J/(kg·K) |
| Heat of vaporization | $Q_{\text{vap}} = m \cdot \lambda$ | J | $\lambda$ = J/kg |
| Flow rate (mass) | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | kg/s | — |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | — |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Pa·s | — |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | — |
| Conversion factor (length) | 1 m = 100 cm = 3.281 ft = 39.37 in | — | — |
| Conversion factor (mass) | 1 kg = 2.205 lb = 1000 g | — | — |
| Conversion factor (energy) | 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ = 3.968 Btu | — | — |
| Conversion factor (force) | 1 N = 10⁵ dyne = 0.2248 lbf | — | — |
| Conversion factor (pressure) | 1 atm = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 psi = 760 mmHg | — | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying calculations:** Dry basis moisture content is used in thin-layer drying models like the **Page equation** ($MR = e^{-kt^n}$) to study the drying kinetics of food materials (rice, grapes, fish, etc.).
- **Energy audits in food industry:** Conversion of units is essential when comparing data from international sources (e.g., a US-built oven rated in BTU/h vs Indian energy consumption in kW).
- **Refrigeration & cold chain:** Heat load calculations require consistent units across compressors, evaporators, and condensers.
- **Quality control:** Moisture determination by oven drying (gravimetric method) uses w.b. moisture content in reporting.
- **Baking & extrusion:** Process calculations use mass balances requiring wet/dry basis conversions for water removal rates.
- **Emulsion and formulation design:** Converting percentages (w/w, w/v, v/v) requires dimensional analysis.

### 2.7 Common Prefixes in SI

| **Prefix** | **Symbol** | **Multiplier** | **Food Engineering Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| kilo | k | 10³ | kJ, kg, kPa |
| mega | M | 10⁶ | MW, MPa |
| giga | G | 10⁹ | GHz (microwave heating) |
| centi | c | 10⁻² | cm (size of food particles) |
| milli | m | 10⁻³ | mm (thickness of food slice) |
| micro | μ | 10⁻⁶ | μm (microbial cell size) |
| nano | n | 10⁻⁹ | nm (nano-encapsulation) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Wet Basis Moisture Content

**Starting point:** A wet food sample has total mass $m_{\text{total}}$, which is the sum of dry solid mass ($m_d$) and water mass ($m_w$).

$$m_{\text{total}} = m_d + m_w$$

**Definition (wet basis):** Moisture content on wet basis is the ratio of water mass to total wet mass.

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{m_w}{m_d + m_w}$$

Since the total mass is the **reference**, $M_{w.b.}$ is always between 0 and 1 (i.e., 0% to 100%).

### 3.2 Derivation of Dry Basis Moisture Content

**Definition (dry basis):** Moisture content on dry basis is the ratio of water mass to dry solid mass.

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d}$$

Since dry mass is the reference (smaller denominator), $M_{d.b.}$ can be **greater than 1** (e.g., 5.0 means 500% moisture on dry basis — very wet food).

### 3.3 Derivation of Interconversion: w.b. ↔ d.b.

**Starting from w.b.:**

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d + m_w}$$

**Divide numerator and denominator by $m_d$:**

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w/m_d}{m_d/m_d + m_w/m_d} = \frac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$$

This gives the **d.b. → w.b. conversion:**

$$\boxed{M_{w.b.} = \frac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}}$$

**Invert both sides:**

$$\frac{1}{M_{w.b.}} = \frac{1 + M_{d.b.}}{M_{d.b.}} = \frac{1}{M_{d.b.}} + 1$$

**Rearrange:**

$$\frac{1}{M_{d.b.}} = \frac{1}{M_{w.b.}} - 1 = \frac{1 - M_{w.b.}}{M_{w.b.}}$$

**Invert again:**

$$\boxed{M_{d.b.} = \frac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Unit Conversion (Energy in Food Heating)

> **Problem:** A food processor uses **75 kcal** of energy to heat milk during pasteurization. Convert this to **kilojoules (kJ)**.

**Given:** $Q = 75$ kcal

**Known conversion:** 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ

**Step 1 — Set up conversion factor:**

$$Q \text{ (kJ)} = 75 \text{ kcal} \times \frac{4.184 \text{ kJ}}{1 \text{ kcal}}$$

**Step 2 — Units cancel (kcal on top and bottom):**

$$Q \text{ (kJ)} = 75 \times 4.184 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Arithmetic:**

$$Q \text{ (kJ)} = 313.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{Q = 313.8 \text{ kJ}}$

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Unit Conversion (Pressure in Retort Processing)

> **Problem:** A retort operates at **15 psig** (pounds per square inch gauge). Convert to **bar**.

**Given:** $P = 15$ psig

**Known conversion:** 1 psi = 0.06895 bar

**Step 1 — Setup:**

$$P \text{ (bar)} = 15 \text{ psi} \times \frac{0.06895 \text{ bar}}{1 \text{ psi}}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel and compute:**

$$P = 15 \times 0.06895 = 1.03425 \text{ bar}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{P \approx 1.034 \text{ bar}}$

### 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Moisture Content (Wet Basis Calculation)

> **Problem:** A sample of fresh fish weighs **250 g** and after oven drying at 105 °C for 24 hours, the dry mass is **90 g**. Calculate the moisture content on **wet basis**.

**Step 1 — Identify masses:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = 250 \text{ g}, \quad m_d = 90 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate water mass:**

$$m_w = m_{\text{total}} - m_d = 250 - 90 = 160 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply w.b. formula:**

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{160}{250} = 0.64$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage:**

$$M_{w.b.} = 64\%$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{M_{w.b.} = 0.64 \text{ or } 64\%}$

### 3.7 Worked Example 4 — Moisture Content (Dry Basis Calculation)

> **Problem:** For the same fish sample above, calculate the moisture content on **dry basis**.

**Step 1 — Apply d.b. formula:**

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d} = \frac{160}{90}$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$M_{d.b.} = 1.7778$$

**Step 3 — Verify using the interconversion formula:**

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}} = \frac{0.64}{1 - 0.64} = \frac{0.64}{0.36} = 1.7778 \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{M_{d.b.} = 1.778 \text{ (i.e., 177.78\% on d.b.)}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that 64% (w.b.) ≠ 177.78% (d.b.). KTU examiners **frequently** test this conversion — the trap is to confuse the two!

### 3.8 Worked Example 5 — Conversion of Empirical Equation (Dimensional Substitution)

> **Problem:** An empirical equation for the thermal conductivity of bread (developed in the CGS system) is:
> $$k = 0.00135 \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85}$$
> where $k$ is in cal/(s·cm·°C), $\rho$ is in g/cm³, and $c_p$ is in cal/(g·°C). Convert the equation so that $k$ is in **W/(m·K)**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³**, and $c_p$ in **kJ/(kg·K)**.

**Step 1 — Identify unit conversions:**

- $1$ cal = $4.184$ J = $4.184 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ
- $1$ s = $1$ s
- $1$ cm = $10^{-2}$ m
- $1$ °C = $1$ K (temperature *interval*)
- $1$ g = $10^{-3}$ kg

**Step 2 — Express each variable as (numerical value) × (unit):**

$$k = 0.00135 \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85} \quad \text{[cal/(s·cm·°C)]}$$

Substitute units:

$$k \cdot \frac{\text{cal}}{\text{s·cm·°C}} = 0.00135 \cdot \left(\rho \cdot \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\right)^{1.65} \cdot \left(c_p \cdot \frac{\text{cal}}{\text{g·°C}}\right)^{0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to target units (substitute new unit definitions):**

$$\frac{\text{cal}}{\text{s·cm·°C}} = \frac{4.184 \text{ J}}{1 \text{ s} \cdot 10^{-2} \text{ m} \cdot 1 \text{ K}} = 418.4 \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m·K}}$$

$$\frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{10^{-6} \text{ m}^3} = 10^3 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

$$\frac{\text{cal}}{\text{g·°C}} = \frac{4.184 \text{ J}}{10^{-3} \text{ kg} \cdot 1 \text{ K}} = 4184 \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg·K}} = 4.184 \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the original equation:**

$$k \cdot 418.4 = 0.00135 \cdot (10^3 \cdot \rho)^{1.65} \cdot (4.184 \cdot c_p)^{0.85}$$

**Step 5 — Expand:**

$$k \cdot 418.4 = 0.00135 \cdot 10^{4.95} \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot 4.184^{0.85} \cdot c_p^{0.85}$$

**Step 6 — Combine constants:**

$$k \cdot 418.4 = 0.00135 \cdot 89125.09 \cdot 3.2247 \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85}$$

$$k \cdot 418.4 = 387.85 \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $k$ in W/(m·K):**

$$k = \frac{387.85}{418.4} \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.927 \cdot \rho^{1.65} \cdot c_p^{0.85} \quad \text{[W/(m·K)]}}$$

This new constant (**0.927**) is what would be used in SI-based food process simulation software.

### 3.9 Worked Example 6 — Drying Mass Balance Using Dry Basis

> **Problem:** 1000 kg of wet food enters a dryer with **80% moisture (w.b.)**. It is dried to a final moisture content of **10% (w.b.)**. Calculate the mass of water removed.

**Step 1 — Initial water mass:**

$$M_{w.b.,1} = 0.80 \Rightarrow m_{w,1} = 0.80 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{d,1} = 1000 - 800 = 200 \text{ kg (dry solid, constant throughout)}$$

**Step 2 — Final water mass:**

$$M_{w.b.,2} = 0.10 \Rightarrow m_{w,2} = 0.10 \times m_{\text{total},2}$$

**Step 3 — Use dry basis for clarity (dry mass is conserved):**

$$M_{d.b.,1} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.0$$

$$M_{d.b.,2} = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.1111$$

**Step 4 — Total mass at the end:**

$$m_{\text{total},2} = m_d(1 + M_{d.b.,2}) = 200 \times (1 + 0.1111) = 222.22 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Water mass at the end:**

$$m_{w,2} = 222.22 - 200 = 22.22 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Water removed:**

$$\Delta m_w = m_{w,1} - m_{w,2} = 800 - 22.22 = 777.78 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\text{Water removed} = 777.78 \text{ kg}}$

### 3.10 Analytical Matrix — Moisture Content Conversions at a Glance

| **Input** | **Output** | **Formula** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m_w$, $m_d$ | $M_{w.b.}$ | $\dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$ | Must be < 1 |
| $m_w$, $m_d$ | $M_{d.b.}$ | $\dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$ | Can be > 1 |
| $M_{w.b.}$ | $M_{d.b.}$ | $\dfrac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}}$ | Standard conversion |
| $M_{d.b.}$ | $M_{w.b.}$ | $\dfrac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$ | — |
| $m_{\text{total}}$, $M_{w.b.}$ | $m_d$ | $m_{\text{total}}(1 - M_{w.b.})$ | — |
| $m_{\text{total}}$, $M_{d.b.}$ | $m_d$ | $\dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$ | — |
| $m_d$, $M_{d.b.}$ | $m_{\text{total}}$ | $m_d(1 + M_{d.b.})$ | Useful in drying |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Classification of Physical Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Physical Quantities in Food Process Calculations"]:::start
    A --> B["Basic or Fundamental Quantities"]
    A --> C["Derived Quantities"]
    B --> B1["Length L (m)"]
    B --> B2["Mass M (kg)"]
    B --> B3["Time T (s)"]
    B --> B4["Temperature theta (K)"]
    B --> B5["Amount of Substance N (mol)"]
    B --> B6["Electric Current I (A)"]
    C --> C1["Velocity L/T m per s"]
    C --> C2["Density M per L cubed kg per m cubed"]
    C --> C3["Force ML per T squared Newton N"]
    C --> C4["Energy ML squared per T squared Joule J"]
    C --> C5["Power ML squared per T cubed Watt W"]
    C --> C6["Pressure M per LT squared Pascal Pa"]
    C --> C7["Viscosity M per LT Pascal second"]
    C --> C8["Thermal Conductivity ML per T cubed theta W per m K"]
    classDef start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Process for Converting Empirical Equations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Start with empirical equation in original units"]:::step
    S2["Step 2 Identify each variable and its unit in original system"]:::step
    S3["Step 3 Write each variable as value multiplied by unit"]:::step
    S4["Step 4 List all unit conversion factors for new system"]:::step
    S5["Step 5 Substitute new units into the equation"]:::step
    S6["Step 6 Collect all constant multipliers together"]:::step
    S7["Step 7 Simplify to obtain new empirical constant"]:::step
    S8["Step 8 Final equation in new unit system"]:::finish
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    classDef step fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef finish fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Moisture Content Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Weigh wet food sample m total in grams"]:::input
    DRY["Oven dry at 105 C for 24 hours"]:::proc
    REW["Reweigh sample to get dry mass m d"]:::proc
    CALC1["Calculate water mass m w = m total minus m d"]:::calc
    CALC2["Apply wet basis formula M wb = m w divided by m total"]:::calc
    CALC3["Apply dry basis formula M db = m w divided by m d"]:::calc
    CONV1["Convert w b to d b using M db = M wb divided by 1 minus M wb"]:::conv
    CONV2["Convert d b to w b using M wb = M db divided by 1 plus M db"]:::conv
    OUT["Report M wb percent for industry M db for engineering"]:::finish
    IN --> DRY --> REW --> CALC1
    CALC1 --> CALC2
    CALC1 --> CALC3
    CALC2 --> CONV1
    CALC3 --> CONV2
    CONV1 --> OUT
    CONV2 --> OUT

```
