## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the terms: (i) Unit, (ii) Dimension, and (iii) Derived quantity, with one food-engineering example for each.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Unit:** A unit is a standard reference quantity used to measure a physical quantity. *Example:* kilogram (kg) for measuring mass of food materials.

**(ii) Dimension:** The qualitative nature of a physical quantity, expressed in terms of basic quantities (M, L, T, θ). *Example:* Force has the dimension $[MLT^{-2}]$.

**(iii) Derived quantity:** A physical quantity that is expressed as a combination of two or more basic quantities. *Example:* Density = Mass / Volume, with SI unit kg/m³ and dimension $[ML^{-3}]$.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
A sample of food weighs 320 g when wet and 80 g after oven drying. Calculate the moisture content in both **wet basis** and **dry basis**.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m_{\text{total}} = 320$ g, $m_d = 80$ g

**Step 1 — Water mass:**

$$m_w = 320 - 80 = 240 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Wet basis:**

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{240}{320} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 3 — Dry basis:**

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d} = \frac{240}{80} = 3.00 = 300\%$$

**Final Answer:** $M_{w.b.} = 75\%$; $M_{d.b.} = 300\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write the d.b. value as **75%** by mistake (forgetting that the *dry mass* is the denominator, not the wet mass). Always double-check whether the question asks for w.b. or d.b. before plugging into the formula.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define the SI system of units. List the seven basic quantities with their SI units and symbols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An empirical correlation for the viscosity of fruit juice developed in the CGS system is:

$$\mu = 0.0042 \cdot e^{(0.018 T)}$$

where $\mu$ is in **poise (g/(cm·s))** and $T$ is in **°C**. Convert the equation to obtain $\mu$ in **Pa·s** with $T$ in **K**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **International System of Units (SI)** is the modern, internationally accepted metric system of measurement, established to provide a coherent, decimal-based, and dimensionally consistent standard for all scientific and engineering calculations. It is built upon **seven well-defined base units**, from which all other (derived) units are mathematically obtained.

The seven SI basic quantities are:

| **Basic Quantity** | **SI Unit** | **Symbol** |
|---|---|---|
| Length | metre | m |
| Mass | kilogram | kg |
| Time | second | s |
| Electric current | ampere | A |
| Temperature | kelvin | K |
| Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| Luminous intensity | candela | cd |

**Advantages of SI:**

- Internationally accepted and unambiguous
- Coherent (derived units are obtained by simple multiplication/division, no extra factors)
- Decimal-based (easy to convert using prefixes)
- Facilitates dimensional analysis and equation conversion

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 0.0042 \cdot e^{0.018 T}$ with $\mu$ in poise, $T$ in °C.

**Step 1 — Write the original equation with explicit units:**

$$\mu \cdot \left(\frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm} \cdot \text{s}}\right) = 0.0042 \cdot e^{0.018 T}$$

**Step 2 — Express $T$ in K (note: temperature interval is the same in K and °C):**

$$T \text{ (in °C)} = T \text{ (in K)} - 273.15$$

So the exponent in terms of $T_K$:

$$0.018 \cdot T_{^\circ C} = 0.018 \cdot (T_K - 273.15) = 0.018 T_K - 4.9167$$

Thus:

$$e^{0.018 T} = e^{0.018 T_K - 4.9167} = e^{-4.9167} \cdot e^{0.018 T_K} = 0.00732 \cdot e^{0.018 T_K}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $\mu$ from poise to Pa·s:**

$$1 \text{ poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$$

So:

$$\mu \text{ (Pa·s)} = \mu \text{ (poise)} \times 0.1 = \mu \text{ (poise)} \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute everything into the original equation:**

$$\frac{\mu_{\text{new}}}{10^{-1}} = 0.0042 \cdot 0.00732 \cdot e^{0.018 T_K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $10$:**

$$\mu_{\text{new}} = 0.0042 \times 0.00732 \times 10 \cdot e^{0.018 T_K}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the new constant:**

$$0.0042 \times 0.00732 \times 10 = 3.0744 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Final converted equation:**

$$\boxed{\mu \text{ (Pa·s)} = 3.0744 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{0.018 T} \quad \text{where } T \text{ is in K}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is meant by the **dimensional homogeneity principle**? Why is it important in food process calculations? Give an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet food material contains **75% moisture (wet basis)**. It is dried in a tray dryer until the moisture content drops to **10% (wet basis)**. If the initial mass is **500 kg**, calculate: (i) the mass of water removed, (ii) the dry basis moisture contents before and after drying, and (iii) the final mass of the dried product. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dimensional Homogeneity Principle:**

The **dimensional homogeneity principle** states that *in any valid physical equation, every term on both sides of the equality sign must have exactly the same dimensions.* In other words, an equation is dimensionally homogeneous if the dimensions of all terms are identical.

**Importance in food process calculations:**

1. **Validation of equations:** It allows engineers to check whether an empirical or derived equation is mathematically and physically correct.
2. **Unit conversion:** It provides a systematic method to convert equations from one unit system to another.
3. **Detection of errors:** If terms do not match dimensionally, the equation is flawed and must be re-derived.
4. **Dimensional analysis:** It forms the basis of the Buckingham π-theorem, used to design experiments and develop correlations.
5. **Engineering design:** It ensures consistent units in process design, scale-up, and cost calculations.

**Example:** The heat balance equation:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

- LHS dimension: $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$ (energy)
- RHS dimension: $[M] \times [L^2 T^{-2} \theta^{-1}] \times [\theta] = [M L^2 T^{-2}]$ ✓

Hence, the equation is dimensionally homogeneous.

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- Initial mass: $m_1 = 500$ kg
- $M_{w.b.,1} = 0.75$
- $M_{w.b.,2} = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Initial water and dry mass:**

$$m_{w,1} = 0.75 \times 500 = 375 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_d = 500 - 375 = 125 \text{ kg (constant during drying)}$$

**Step 2 — Initial moisture content (dry basis):**

$$M_{d.b.,1} = \frac{M_{w.b.,1}}{1 - M_{w.b.,1}} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.00 \text{ or } 300\%$$

**Step 3 — Final moisture content (dry basis):**

$$M_{d.b.,2} = \frac{M_{w.b.,2}}{1 - M_{w.b.,2}} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.1111 \text{ or } 11.11\%$$

**Step 4 — Final water mass:**

$$m_{w,2} = m_d \cdot M_{d.b.,2} = 125 \times 0.1111 = 13.89 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — (i) Water removed:**

$$\Delta m_w = 375 - 13.89 = 361.11 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Final mass:**

$$m_2 = m_d + m_{w,2} = 125 + 13.89 = 138.89 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answers:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| (i) Water removed | **361.11 kg** |
| (ii) $M_{d.b.,1}$ | **3.00 (300%)** |
| (ii) $M_{d.b.,2}$ | **0.1111 (11.11%)** |
| (iii) Final mass | **138.89 kg** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing w.b. and d.b. denominators** — most common mistake. W.b. divides by *total* mass, d.b. divides by *dry* mass.
> 2. **Forgetting that dry mass is conserved** during drying — using $m_{\text{total}} - m_w$ without anchoring the dry mass leads to errors.
> 3. **Mixing units** in empirical equations without proper conversion factors — a single missing factor can change the answer by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Treating temperature intervals as the same in K and °C** is valid (e.g., ΔT), but for absolute T, conversion $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ must be applied.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unit** = numerical standard of measurement; **Dimension** = qualitative nature of quantity.
- **SI has 7 basic quantities** (m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd); all others are derived.
- **Dimensional homogeneity** is the foundation of unit conversion and equation conversion.
- **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ**; **1 cal = 4.184 J**; **1 atm = 101325 Pa = 1.01325 bar**; **1 psi ≈ 0.06895 bar**; **1 ft = 0.3048 m**; **1 lb = 0.4536 kg**; **1 Btu = 1.055 kJ**.
- **Temperature interval** is the same in K and °C; only absolute temperature needs shifting by 273.15.
- **Empirical equation conversion** uses the **dimensional substitution method** — express each variable as (value × unit), substitute new units, simplify, and obtain a new constant.
- **Wet basis** uses total (wet) mass as reference; **dry basis** uses dry mass as reference.
- $M_{w.b.} \in [0, 1]$; $M_{d.b.} \in [0, \infty)$.
- Interconversion: $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}}$ and $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$.
- **Dry mass is conserved** during drying — use d.b. moisture content for drying mass balance.
- In food industry, **w.b. is used for reporting** (e.g., "85% moisture" on label); in **engineering calculations** (drying kinetics, energy balance), d.b. is preferred.
- **Quick conversion check:** A moisture content of **50% (w.b.) = 100% (d.b.)** — a useful mental benchmark.
- **Page equation** and **Henderson-Pabis model** for drying kinetics are typical empirical equations needing unit conversion in food technology.
- Always **check the dimensions** of every term in a physical equation to ensure consistency before solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mole concept - method of expressing composition - mole fraction - weight fraction - volume fraction - concentration of liquid solutions - molarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mole concept - method of expressing composition - mole fraction - weight fraction - volume fraction - concentration of liquid solutions - molarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Mole Concept — Formal Definition

The **mole** is the SI unit for the amount of substance. It is defined as the amount of substance that contains exactly **6.02214076 × 10²³** elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, or any specified particles). This number is known as the **Avogadro Constant (Nₐ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Definition:**
> One mole of any substance contains **Avogadro's number (Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³)** of particles. The mass of one mole of a substance in grams is numerically equal to its **relative molecular mass (M)** expressed in unified atomic mass units (u).

Mathematically, the relationship between mass, molar mass, and number of moles is the cornerstone expression:

$$n = \dfrac{m}{M}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of moles (mol)
- $m$ = mass of the substance (g)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chemist's Dozen"

Imagine you go to a bakery and ask for "a dozen" doughnuts. The baker hands you exactly **12 doughnuts**, regardless of whether they are plain, chocolate, or glazed. The word "dozen" simply counts *how many* — it says nothing about weight or size.

The **mole** is the chemist's "dozen," but scaled astronomically. Just as a dozen always means 12, a mole always means **6.022 × 10²³ particles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy in Food Technology:**
> Suppose a food chemist wants to compare the sweetness of sucrose vs. fructose. Comparing them *per gram* is meaningless, because one molecule of sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) has a different mass than one of fructose (C₆H₁₂O₆). By converting to **moles**, we compare them on an equal "per-particle" basis — exactly what the baker's dozen does for doughnuts.

---

### 1.3 Methods of Expressing Composition — Overview

In food technology and chemistry, a "composition" refers to **how much of each component is present in a mixture or solution**. Since food products are complex mixtures (water, sugars, salts, fats, acids, etc.), we need standardized ways to express these proportions. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following methods:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Composition Expressions:**
> 1. **Mole Fraction (x)** — ratio of moles of a component to total moles of all components (dimensionless).
> 2. **Weight Fraction (w)** — ratio of mass of a component to total mass of the mixture (dimensionless).
> 3. **Volume Fraction (φ)** — ratio of volume of a component to total volume of the mixture (dimensionless).
> 4. **Molarity (M)** — moles of solute per litre of solution (mol/L).
> 5. **Molality (m)** — moles of solute per kilogram of solvent (mol/kg).
> 6. **Normality (N)** — gram-equivalents of solute per litre of solution (eq/L).
> 7. **Mass Concentration (ρ or γ)** — mass of solute per litre of solution (g/L).
> 8. **Percentage Composition** — (w/w), (w/v), or (v/v) percent.

---

### 1.4 Why Are These Definitions Important?

In **food technology**, these expressions are used daily:

- **Soft drink formulation**: sugar concentration in g/100 mL (% w/v)
- **Brine solutions for pickles**: salt content in % w/w
- **Vinegar acidity**: acetic acid content in normality or molarity
- **Alcoholic beverages**: ethanol expressed as volume fraction or proof
- **Dairy products**: fat content in weight fraction
- **Essential oils in flavoring**: volume fraction

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - *Mole fraction* is **temperature-independent** (depends only on count).
> - *Volume fraction* and *molarity* are **temperature-dependent** (volumes expand/contract with heat).
> - *Molality* is **temperature-independent** because it uses mass of solvent (not volume of solution).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mole Fraction (xᵢ) — The Deep Dive

The **mole fraction** of component *i* in a mixture is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the **total number of moles of all components** in the mixture.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Identify each component in the mixture (solute, solvent, additives).
2. Convert the mass of each component into moles using $n = m/M$.
3. Sum all the moles to obtain $n_{total}$.
4. Divide the moles of each component by $n_{total}$ to get its mole fraction.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j}$$

**Properties:**
- Dimensionless (no units)
- $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$
- $\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 1$ (the mole fractions of all components must sum to **exactly 1**)
- Temperature-independent

---

### 2.2 Weight (Mass) Fraction (wᵢ) — The Deep Dive

The **weight fraction** (also called mass fraction) of a component is the ratio of the mass of that component to the total mass of the mixture.

$$w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

**Properties:**
- Dimensionless
- Often expressed as a decimal or percentage (multiply by 100)
- Temperature-independent (mass does not change with temperature)
- $\sum w_i = 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Industry Usage:**
> Nutritional labels display compositions as **% w/w** (e.g., "Fat: 15% w/w" means 15 g of fat per 100 g of product).

---

### 2.3 Volume Fraction (φᵢ) — The Deep Dive

The **volume fraction** is defined for mixtures of liquids (or gases) and represents the ratio of the volume of one component to the total volume of the mixture.

$$\phi_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}}$$

**Important Caveat — Non-Additivity:**

Unlike mole and mass fractions, **volumes are not strictly additive** in real mixtures due to *intermolecular interactions* and *electrostriction*. For example, mixing 50 mL ethanol with 50 mL water gives **~96 mL** (not 100 mL) of solution.

$$\phi_i \approx \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}} \quad \text{(idealized; assumes additive volumes)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Fraction vs. Volume Percent:**
> Volume fraction × 100 = Volume percent (% v/v). In food labels, alcohol content in beverages is commonly expressed as **% v/v** (e.g., "40% alcohol v/v").

---

### 2.4 Concentration of Liquid Solutions

A "concentration" expresses **how much solute is present per unit of solution or solvent**. There are multiple conventions, and the KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of:

#### 2.4.1 Molarity (M)

Molarity is defined as the **number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of solution**.

$$C_M \equiv M = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{V_{solution}} \quad \text{(units: mol/L or M)}$$

**Where:**
- $n_{solute}$ = moles of solute (mol)
- $V_{solution}$ = volume of the **solution** (not solvent!) in litres

#### 2.4.2 Molality (m)

Molality is the **number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent**.

$$m = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{m_{solvent\,(kg)}}$$

**Where:**
- Units: mol/kg (sometimes written as *m* or *molal*)

#### 2.4.3 Normality (N)

Normality is **gram-equivalents of solute per litre of solution**.

$$N = \dfrac{\text{gram-equivalents of solute}}{V_{solution\,(L)}} = \dfrac{n \times \text{n-factor}}{V_{solution\,(L)}}$$

Where *n-factor* depends on the reaction:
- For acids: number of replaceable H⁺ ions
- For bases: number of replaceable OH⁻ ions
- For salts: total positive or negative charge

#### 2.4.4 Mass Concentration (γ)

$$\gamma = \dfrac{m_{solute}}{V_{solution}} \quad \text{(units: g/L)}$$

#### 2.4.5 Percentage Concentrations

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **% w/w** | grams of solute per 100 g of solution |
| **% w/v** | grams of solute per 100 mL of solution |
| **% v/v** | millilitres of solute per 100 mL of solution |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It appears in 80%+ of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Temperature Dependence |
|----------|--------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| Number of moles | $n$ | $n = m/M$ | mol | — |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | particles/mol | — |
| Mole fraction | $x_i$ | $x_i = n_i / n_{total}$ | dimensionless | Independent |
| Weight fraction | $w_i$ | $w_i = m_i / m_{total}$ | dimensionless | Independent |
| Volume fraction | $\phi_i$ | $\phi_i = V_i / V_{total}$ | dimensionless | **Dependent** |
| Molarity | $M$ or $C_M$ | $M = n_{solute} / V_{solution}$ | mol/L | **Dependent** |
| Molality | $m$ | $m = n_{solute} / m_{solvent}$ | mol/kg | Independent |
| Normality | $N$ | $N = (n \times \text{n-factor}) / V_{solution}$ | eq/L | **Dependent** |
| Mass concentration | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = m_{solute} / V_{solution}$ | g/L | **Dependent** |
| Molarity dilution | — | $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$ | — | — |
| Mole → Mass | — | $m = n \times M$ | g | — |
| Mole → Volume (gas) | — | $V = n \times 22.4$ (STP) | L | — |
| Mole → Particles | — | $N = n \times N_A$ | particles | — |

---

### 2.6 Inter-Conversion Master Key

$$\boxed{M = \dfrac{\rho \times 10 \times \%\,w/w}{M_{solute}}} \quad \text{(to find Molarity from \% w/w and density }\rho\text{)}$$

$$\boxed{x_{solute} = \dfrac{m_{solute}/M_{solute}}{m_{solute}/M_{solute} + m_{solvent}/M_{solvent}}}$$

$$\boxed{m = \dfrac{1000 \times M}{(1000 \times \rho) - (M \times M_{solute})}}$$

$$\boxed{N = M \times \text{n-factor}}$$

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food processing calculations**: Determining salt or sugar content for preservation.
- **Quality control**: Checking concentration of preservative solutions (e.g., sodium benzoate in soft drinks).
- **Recipe scaling**: Converting between mass-based and volume-based measurements.
- **Reaction stoichiometry**: Formulating ingredient ratios based on molar proportions (e.g., acid-base neutralization in cheese making).
- **Beverage industry**: Converting between proof, % v/v, and mole fraction for alcohol content.
- **Dairy science**: Fat content (weight fraction) → mass per litre using density.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mole Fraction from Mass Data

> **Problem:** A solution is prepared by dissolving **18 g of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** in **178.5 g of water (H₂O)**. Calculate the mole fraction of glucose and water.

**Given:**
- Mass of glucose, $m_g = 18$ g
- Mass of water, $m_w = 178.5$ g
- Molar mass of glucose, $M_g = 180$ g/mol
- Molar mass of water, $M_w = 18$ g/mol

**Step 1 — Convert mass of glucose to moles:**

$$n_g = \dfrac{m_g}{M_g} = \dfrac{18\text{ g}}{180\text{ g/mol}} = 0.100 \text{ mol}$$

*Logic: Divide mass by molar mass; 180 g of glucose is one mole, so 18 g is 0.1 mole.*

**Step 2 — Convert mass of water to moles:**

$$n_w = \dfrac{m_w}{M_w} = \dfrac{178.5\text{ g}}{18\text{ g/mol}} = 9.917 \text{ mol}$$

*Logic: Water has molar mass 18 g/mol, so 178.5 g / 18 = 9.917 moles.*

**Step 3 — Calculate total moles:**

$$n_{total} = n_g + n_w = 0.100 + 9.917 = 10.017 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate mole fractions:**

$$x_g = \dfrac{n_g}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{0.100}{10.017} = 9.983 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00998$$

$$x_w = \dfrac{n_w}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{9.917}{10.017} = 0.99002$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$x_g + x_w = 0.00998 + 0.99002 = 1.00000 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even though glucose is a relatively small fraction by mass (18/196.5 ≈ 9.16% w/w), its mole fraction is only ~1% because water molecules are 10× lighter. This is why *mole fraction* gives a fundamentally different picture than *mass fraction*.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weight Fraction to Mass

> **Problem:** A brine solution contains **NaCl at 12% w/w**. If you have **2.5 kg** of this solution, how many grams of NaCl are present?

**Given:**
- % w/w = 12%
- Total mass = 2.5 kg = 2500 g

**Step 1 — Apply the definition:**

$$w_{NaCl} = \dfrac{m_{NaCl}}{m_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to find solute mass:**

$$m_{NaCl} = w_{NaCl} \times m_{total} = 0.12 \times 2500 \text{ g} = 300 \text{ g}$$

**Answer:** 300 g of NaCl.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Volume Fraction of Ethanol in Wine

> **Problem:** A wine bottle contains 750 mL of beverage with an ethanol content labelled as **12% v/v**. How many mL of pure ethanol is in one bottle?

**Step 1 — Apply the definition:**

$$\phi_{ethanol} = \dfrac{V_{ethanol}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{12}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$V_{ethanol} = 0.12 \times 750 \text{ mL} = 90 \text{ mL}$$

**Answer:** 90 mL of pure ethanol.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Molarity Calculation from Solute Mass

> **Problem:** Calculate the molarity of a solution prepared by dissolving **5.85 g of NaCl** in water and making the volume up to **500 mL**.

**Given:**
- Mass of NaCl = 5.85 g
- Molar mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 g/mol
- Volume of solution = 500 mL = 0.5 L

**Step 1 — Calculate moles of NaCl:**

$$n_{NaCl} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{5.85\text{ g}}{58.5\text{ g/mol}} = 0.100 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply molarity definition:**

$$M = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{V_{solution}} = \dfrac{0.100\text{ mol}}{0.5\text{ L}} = 0.200 \text{ mol/L} = 0.200 \text{ M}$$

**Answer:** **0.200 M NaCl**

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Molarity from % w/w and Density

> **Problem:** A commercial vinegar has **4.0% w/w acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** and density **1.005 g/mL**. Calculate its molarity.

**Given:**
- % w/w = 4.0%
- $\rho = 1.005$ g/mL = 1005 g/L
- Molar mass of CH₃COOH = 60 g/mol

**Step 1 — Determine mass of acetic acid per litre of solution:**

$$m_{acetic} = \dfrac{4.0}{100} \times 1005 \text{ g/L} = 40.2 \text{ g/L}$$

*Logic: 1 L of solution weighs 1005 g; 4% of that is acetic acid.*

**Step 2 — Convert mass to moles:**

$$n = \dfrac{40.2\text{ g}}{60\text{ g/mol}} = 0.670 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate molarity:**

$$M = \dfrac{0.670\text{ mol}}{1\text{ L}} = 0.670 \text{ M}$$

**Answer:** **0.670 M acetic acid**

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Inter-Conversion: Molarity ↔ Molality

> **Problem:** A solution of NaOH has molarity **2.0 M** and density **1.08 g/mL**. Calculate its molality.

**Given:**
- M = 2.0 mol/L
- $\rho = 1.08$ g/mL = 1080 g/L

**Step 1 — Mass of NaOH (solute) per litre:**

$$m_{solute} = n \times M_{NaOH} = 2.0 \times 40 = 80 \text{ g/L}$$

*Logic: 2.0 moles × 40 g/mol = 80 g of NaOH per litre.*

**Step 2 — Mass of solution per litre:**

$$m_{solution} = \rho \times V = 1.08 \text{ g/mL} \times 1000 \text{ mL} = 1080 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of solvent (water) per litre:**

$$m_{solvent} = m_{solution} - m_{solute} = 1080 - 80 = 1000 \text{ g} = 1.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply molality formula:**

$$m_{molality} = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{m_{solvent\,(kg)}} = \dfrac{2.0\text{ mol}}{1.0\text{ kg}} = 2.0 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Answer:** **2.0 m (molal)**

---

### 3.7 Worked Example 7 — Dilution Calculation (M₁V₁ = M₂V₂)

> **Problem:** You have **250 mL of 1.5 M HCl**. How much water must be added to dilute it to **0.5 M**?

**Step 1 — Apply the dilution equation:**

$$M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$$

$$1.5 \times 250 = 0.5 \times V_2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for V₂:**

$$V_2 = \dfrac{1.5 \times 250}{0.5} = 750 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of water to add:**

$$V_{water} = V_2 - V_1 = 750 - 250 = 500 \text{ mL}$$

**Answer:** **Add 500 mL of water.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Always add the difference (V₂ − V₁), not V₂ itself. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this.

---

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Best Expression | Reason |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| Comparing gas partial pressures | Mole fraction | Dalton's law: $P_i = x_i \times P_{total}$ |
| Boiling-point elevation / Freezing-point depression | Molality | $K_b, K_f$ colligative constants use molality |
| Acid-base titration | Normality | Equivalents react 1:1 ($N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$) |
| Lab reagent preparation | Molarity | Most common in volumetric flasks |
| Food label (nutrition) | Weight fraction (% w/w) | Independent of T; reflects actual mass |
| Alcohol content in beverages | Volume fraction (% v/v) | Easy to measure with hydrometer |
| Very dilute aqueous solutions | Molarity ≈ Molality | Density ≈ 1 g/mL |
| Theoretical thermodynamics | Mole fraction | Pure ratio; no unit ambiguity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define mole fraction. A solution contains 30 g of urea (M = 60 g/mol) dissolved in 470 g of water. Calculate the mole fraction of urea.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The mole fraction of a component in a mixture is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components present in the mixture.

$$x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$$

It is dimensionless, and the sum of mole fractions of all components equals 1.

**Calculation:**

*Moles of urea:*
$$n_{urea} = \dfrac{30\text{ g}}{60\text{ g/mol}} = 0.500\text{ mol}$$

*Moles of water:*
$$n_{water} = \dfrac{470\text{ g}}{18\text{ g/mol}} = 26.111\text{ mol}$$

*Total moles:*
$$n_{total} = 0.500 + 26.111 = 26.611\text{ mol}$$

*Mole fraction of urea:*
$$x_{urea} = \dfrac{0.500}{26.611} = 0.01879 \approx 0.0188$$

**Answer:** $x_{urea} \approx 0.0188$

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between molarity and molality. Why is molality preferred over molarity in colligative property calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Molarity (M) | Molality (m) |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Definition | Moles of solute per litre of **solution** | Moles of solute per kilogram of **solvent** |
| Units | mol/L | mol/kg |
| Temperature dependence | **Depends** on temperature | **Independent** of temperature |

**Reason for preferring molality in colligative properties:**

Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure) depend on the **number of solute particles per unit mass of solvent**. Since mass does not change with temperature (unlike volume), molality provides a temperature-independent measure that gives consistent values regardless of thermal conditions.

In contrast, molarity uses volume of solution, which expands or contracts with temperature, leading to variable concentration values. Therefore, **molality is the preferred concentration unit in thermodynamic and colligative property calculations.**

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define weight fraction and volume fraction. A food product contains 25 g of protein, 8 g of fat, 15 g of carbohydrates, and 52 g of water per 100 g of sample. Calculate (i) the weight fraction of each component, and (ii) the percentage by mass (% w/w) of protein.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is molarity? 250 mL of a Na₂CO₃ solution contains 26.5 g of the salt. Calculate its molarity. If 100 mL of this solution is diluted to 500 mL, what will be the new molarity?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Part (i) — Weight fractions:**

$$w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

Total mass = 25 + 8 + 15 + 52 = **100 g**

$$w_{protein} = \dfrac{25}{100} = 0.25$$

$$w_{fat} = \dfrac{8}{100} = 0.08$$

$$w_{carbs} = \dfrac{15}{100} = 0.15$$

$$w_{water} = \dfrac{52}{100} = 0.52$$

*Verification:* $0.25 + 0.08 + 0.15 + 0.52 = 1.00$ ✓

**(a) Part (ii) — % w/w of protein:**

$$\%\,w/w = w_{protein} \times 100 = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\%$$

**(b) Molarity Definition:**

Molarity is the number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of solution.

$$M = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{V_{solution\,(L)}}$$

**Calculating molarity of Na₂CO₃:**

Molar mass of Na₂CO₃ = 2(23) + 12 + 3(16) = 46 + 12 + 48 = **106 g/mol**

$$n_{Na_2CO_3} = \dfrac{26.5\text{ g}}{106\text{ g/mol}} = 0.250\text{ mol}$$

$$M_1 = \dfrac{0.250\text{ mol}}{0.250\text{ L}} = 1.00\text{ M}$$

**After dilution:**

$$M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$$

$$1.00 \times 100 = M_2 \times 500$$

$$M_2 = \dfrac{100}{500} = 0.200\text{ M}$$

**Final Answer:** Initial M = **1.00 M**; After dilution M₂ = **0.200 M**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the mole concept? Explain Avogadro's number. A food chemist needs to prepare 500 mL of 0.5 M NaCl solution. Calculate (i) the mass of NaCl required, and (ii) the number of NaCl formula units in the solution.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sugar syrup used in confectionery has 60% w/w sucrose (M = 342 g/mol) and density 1.30 g/mL. Calculate (i) the molality of the syrup, and (ii) the mole fraction of sucrose.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) The Mole Concept & Avogadro's Number:**

A mole is the amount of substance containing as many elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions) as there are atoms in exactly 12 g of carbon-12. This number is the **Avogadro constant**, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles/mol.

The mole bridges the gap between the microscopic (atoms, molecules) and macroscopic (grams, kilograms) worlds. One mole of any substance has a mass in grams numerically equal to its molecular weight.

**(i) Mass of NaCl required:**

Molar mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5 = **58.5 g/mol**

$$n_{NaCl} = M \times V = 0.5 \text{ mol/L} \times 0.5 \text{ L} = 0.25 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{NaCl} = n \times M = 0.25 \times 58.5 = \mathbf{14.625 \text{ g}}$$

**(ii) Number of formula units:**

$$N = n \times N_A = 0.25 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = \mathbf{1.5055 \times 10^{23} \text{ formula units}}$$

**(b) Part (i) — Molality of syrup:**

Take 100 g of syrup as basis.
- Mass of sucrose (solute) = 60 g
- Mass of water (solvent) = 100 − 60 = 40 g = 0.040 kg

$$n_{sucrose} = \dfrac{60}{342} = 0.1754 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Molality} = \dfrac{0.1754\text{ mol}}{0.040\text{ kg}} = \mathbf{4.385 \text{ mol/kg}}$$

**(b) Part (ii) — Mole fraction of sucrose:**

Moles of water:

$$n_{water} = \dfrac{40}{18} = 2.222 \text{ mol}$$

Total moles:

$$n_{total} = 0.1754 + 2.222 = 2.398 \text{ mol}$$

$$x_{sucrose} = \dfrac{0.1754}{2.398} = \mathbf{0.0731}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mass of solute ≠ mass of solvent:** Confusing total solution mass with solvent mass costs 2–3 marks in molality problems.
> 2. **Volume unit conversion:** Forgetting to convert mL to L (divide by 1000) before applying $M = n/V$ is the #1 error.
> 3. **Molar mass calculation errors:** Common mistakes include writing Na₂CO₃ as 106 g/mol correctly but miscalculating Ca(OH)₂ or H₃PO₄.
> 4. **"Make up to 500 mL" trap:** The solute is dissolved in less water first, then water is added *up to the mark*. The total volume is 500 mL, not the volume of water added.
> 5. **Mole fraction sum must be 1:** Examiners cross-check this; a sum ≠ 1 (due to rounding) loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Dilution equation misuse:** Writing $V_2 - V_1$ as the water added is correct, but writing just $V_2$ as "water added" is wrong.
> 7. **% v/v vs % w/v confusion:** Alcohol in beverages is % v/v; salt in brine is % w/v. Read the label carefully.
> 8. **Normality's n-factor:** Forgetting to multiply by n-factor in $N = M \times \text{n-factor}$ errors cost full marks in titration problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- **One mole** = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles = mass equal to molecular weight in grams.
- **Mole (n)** is the *bridge* between mass ($m = n \times M$) and number of particles ($N = n \times N_A$).
- **Mole fraction** ($x_i$): dimensionless, temperature-independent; sums to **1**.
- **Weight fraction** ($w_i$): mass-based; used in **food labels (% w/w)**.
- **Volume fraction** ($\phi_i$): liquid mixtures; used for **alcohol (% v/v)**; volumes are *not strictly additive*.
- **Molarity (M)** = mol solute / L solution — temperature-dependent; common in labs.
- **Molality (m)** = mol solute / kg solvent — temperature-independent; used in colligative properties.
- **Normality (N)** = gram-equivalents / L solution — used in acid-base titrations; $N = M \times \text{n-factor}$.
- **Dilution equation**: $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$ — moles of solute remain constant.
- **% w/w** = (mass solute / mass solution) × 100.
- **% w/v** = (mass solute / volume solution in mL) × 100.
- **% v/v** = (volume solute / volume solution) × 100.
- **At STP**, 1 mole of any gas = **22.4 L**.
- **Mole fraction is preferred** for partial pressures (Dalton's Law): $P_i = x_i \times P_{total}$.
- **For dilute aqueous solutions**, molarity ≈ molality (since density ≈ 1 g/mL).
- **Sum of mole fractions of all components = 1**, and similarly for mass fractions and volume fractions.
- **Molarity changes with temperature**; **molality does not** — pick the right one for the problem.
- **Master the conversion triangle:** mass ↔ moles ↔ particles ↔ (gas volume at STP).

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — GDFTT104 Introduction to Food Technology**
> **Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Molality** (denoted by the lowercase letter **m**) is a unit of concentration in chemistry that expresses the number of **moles of solute** dissolved in exactly **one kilogram (1 kg) of solvent**. It is one of the four fundamental ways of expressing the concentration of a solution, alongside Molarity, Normality, and Mole Fraction.

Mathematically, it is defined as:

$$m = \frac{\text{Number of moles of solute}}{\text{Mass of solvent in kilograms}} = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$$

The standard SI unit of molality is **mol/kg**, which is also sometimes written as **molal** (lowercase, to distinguish it from the capitalized **M** of Molarity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Molality is classified as a **mass-based concentration unit**. Unlike Molarity (which depends on solution volume), Molality depends only on the *mass* of the solvent. This makes it **temperature-independent**, a property of immense importance in food processing calculations where temperature varies significantly (pasteurization, sterilization, freezing).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are preparing a **cup of sweet tea**:
- The **solute** is sugar.
- The **solvent** is water (tea base).
- The **solution** is your sweet tea.

If you add **1 tablespoon of sugar to 1 glass of water**, the sweetness is mild. If you add the **same 1 tablespoon of sugar to only a quarter glass of water**, the sweetness becomes intensely strong. The amount of sugar (solute) did not change, but the amount of water (solvent) shrank — and so the concentration rose sharply.

Molality asks the question: *"How many moles of solute are packed into each kilogram of solvent?"* Just like our tea example, when the solvent mass (water) decreases, the molality rises; when solvent mass increases, the molality falls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction for Examiners:** Students frequently confuse **Molarity (M)** with **Molality (m)**. The capital **M** refers to *Molarity* (moles per litre of **solution**), while the small **m** refers to *Molality* (moles per kilogram of **solvent**). The volume term in Molarity is *solution*, not *solvent* — this is the single most-tested point in KTU papers.

## 1.3 Why Molality is Critical in Food Technology

Food matrices (milk, juice, syrups, brines) undergo constant heating and cooling during processing. Since liquids **expand on heating** and **contract on cooling**, the *volume* of a solution changes with temperature. Molality bypasses this issue entirely because it uses only **mass**, which never changes with temperature. This is why it is the preferred unit in:
- **Freezing point depression** calculations (used in ice-cream and frozen food manufacture)
- **Boiling point elevation** calculations (sugar boiling in confectionery)
- **Colligative property** studies of food solutions

> [!TIP]
> The word **"Molality"** has two **"l"s** (m-o-l-a-l-i-t-y). A quick mnemonic: **"Mass Loves Molality"** — both start with **M**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Molality Concept

To fully understand molality, the operational logic is decomposed into four sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the Solute and Solvent**
- *Solute* = the component present in the smaller amount (the one being dissolved).
- *Solvent* = the component present in the larger amount (the one doing the dissolving).
- In food systems, water is almost always the solvent, and salts, sugars, acids, or proteins are the solutes.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Moles of Solute**
- Use the formula $n = \dfrac{W}{M}$, where $W$ is the given mass of solute and $M$ is its molar mass (g/mol).
- This step converts a tangible mass measurement into the abstract SI count of particles.

**Step 3 — Measure the Mass of the Solvent in Kilograms**
- The denominator strictly uses the solvent mass, **NOT** the solution mass.
- If the *solution* mass is given, the solvent mass must be obtained by subtraction: $W_{\text{solvent}} = W_{\text{solution}} - W_{\text{solute}}$.
- The result is then divided by 1000 to convert grams → kilograms.

**Step 4 — Apply the Molality Equation**
- Divide the moles of solute (Step 2) by the kg of solvent (Step 3) to obtain molality in **mol/kg**.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it is Necessary | How it Links to Molality |
|------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Identify Solute/Solvent | Ensures correct assignment of numerator vs. denominator | Avoids sign error in dilution problems |
| Calculate Moles | SI requires amount-of-substance as the base | Aligns with KTU's NEP 2020 unit-based learning |
| Convert Solvent Mass to kg | SI standardizes mass in kg, not grams | Ensures dimensional consistency |
| Final Division | Yields the intensive property 'm' | Gives a single, unambiguous concentration value |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Molality. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions thoroughly.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Notes & Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definition of Molality | $m = \dfrac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent(kg)}}}$ | mol/kg (molal) | Denominator is solvent only, never solution |
| 2 | Mole Calculation | $n = \dfrac{W}{M}$ | mol | W = grams, M = g/mol |
| 3 | Mass Conversion | $W_{(\text{kg})} = \dfrac{W_{(\text{g})}}{1000}$ | kg | Required before applying eq. 1 |
| 4 | Solvent Mass from Solution | $W_{\text{solvent}} = W_{\text{solution}} - W_{\text{solute}}$ | g or kg | Used when only solution mass is given |
| 5 | Molality from Molarity (aq. dilution) | $m = \dfrac{M \times 1000}{1000\rho - M \times M_{\text{solute}}}$ | mol/kg | $\rho$ = solution density in g/mL |
| 6 | Molality ↔ Mole Fraction | $m = \dfrac{x_{\text{solute}}}{x_{\text{solvent}} \times M_{\text{solvent(kg/mol)}}}$ | mol/kg | $x$ = mole fraction |
| 7 | Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \times m$ | °C or K | $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant (kg·K/mol) |
| 8 | Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T_b = K_b \times m$ | °C or K | $K_b$ = ebullioscopic constant |
| 9 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = m \times R \times T$ | Pa or atm | Used in food preservation by osmosis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL ALERT:** In equation (5) above, students frequently write the denominator as "$1000 - M \times M_{\text{solute}}$" — forgetting the **density term $\rho$**. This is one of the top mark-deducting errors flagged by KTU examiners in the valuation key.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Molality is indispensable in the following food technology domains:

1. **Frozen Food Engineering:** Predicting the freezing point of fruit juices, ice-cream mixes, and brine solutions for seafood preservation.
2. **Sugar Boiling in Confectionery:** Calculating the boiling point rise of sucrose syrups during the manufacture of hard candies, jams, and jellies.
3. **Reverse Osmosis & Membrane Filtration:** Determining the osmotic pressure of milk and whey for concentration purposes.
4. **Brine Curing in Meat Processing:** Designing accurate salt concentrations (NaCl molality) for curing hams, bacons, and pickles.
5. **Quality Control:** Standardizing syrup densities in beverage industries where temperature varies along the production line.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Foundational Derivation — From First Principles

The molality formula is not derived in the calculus sense; rather, it is **defined** by a ratio. However, we can demonstrate its origin from the more general concept of *amount concentration* and then connect it to related formulae to satisfy KTU's "show the derivation" requirements.

### Derivation 1: Linking Moles to Mass

The SI unit "amount of substance" (in moles) for a solute is obtained by dividing the given mass of the solute by its molar mass:

$$n_{\text{solute}} = \frac{W_{\text{solute (g)}}}{M_{\text{solute (g/mol)}}}$$

The mass of the solvent in kilograms is:

$$W_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = \frac{W_{\text{solvent (g)}}}{1000}$$

Substituting both into the definition $m = \dfrac{n}{W}$:

$$m = \frac{\dfrac{W_{\text{solute (g)}}}{M_{\text{solute (g/mol)}}}}{\dfrac{W_{\text{solvent (g)}}}{1000}}$$

Simplifying the compound fraction by multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$m = \frac{W_{\text{solute (g)}} \times 1000}{M_{\text{solute (g/mol)}} \times W_{\text{solvent (g)}}}$$

This is the most practical working formula for textbook problems.

### Derivation 2: Relationship Between Molality and Molarity

Molarity is defined as $M = \dfrac{n_{\text{solute}}}{V_{\text{solution (L)}}}$.

The volume of the solution in litres can be written as:

$$V_{\text{solution (L)}} = \frac{W_{\text{solution (g)}}}{1000 \times \rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the solution in g/mL (or equivalently, kg/L). Substituting into the molarity equation:

$$M = \frac{n_{\text{solute}} \times 1000 \times \rho}{W_{\text{solution (g)}}}$$

The solution mass equals the sum of solute mass plus solvent mass:

$$W_{\text{solution}} = W_{\text{solvent}} + W_{\text{solute}}$$

We also know that $n_{\text{solute}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{solute}}}{M_{\text{solute}}}$. Replacing this:

$$M = \frac{\dfrac{W_{\text{solute}}}{M_{\text{solute}}} \times 1000 \times \rho}{W_{\text{solvent}} + W_{\text{solute}}}$$

By cross-multiplication and algebraic manipulation, the standard result is obtained:

$$m = \frac{M \times 1000}{1000\rho - M \times M_{\text{solute}}}$$

where $M_{\text{solute}}$ is the molar mass of the solute in g/mol. This equation is essential when the question gives a molarity value and asks for molality.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** 4.5 g of glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$, molar mass = 180 g/mol) is dissolved in 250 g of water. Calculate the molality of the resulting solution.

### Step 1 — List the Given Data

Given:
- Mass of solute (glucose), $W_{\text{solute}} = 4.5$ g
- Molar mass of solute, $M_{\text{solute}} = 180$ g/mol
- Mass of solvent (water), $W_{\text{solvent}} = 250$ g

### Step 2 — Calculate Moles of Solute

Applying $n = \dfrac{W}{M}$:

$$n_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{4.5 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}}$$

Performing the division:

$$n_{\text{glucose}} = 0.025 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Solvent Mass to kg

$$W_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = \frac{250 \text{ g}}{1000} = 0.250 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Molality Formula

$$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}} = \frac{0.025 \text{ mol}}{0.250 \text{ kg}}$$

$$m = 0.1 \text{ mol/kg}$$

> **Final Answer:** The molality of the glucose solution is **0.1 molal** (or **0.1 m**).

## 3.3 Second Worked Example — Food Technology Context

> **Problem:** A brine solution used for curing meat contains 58.5 g of NaCl (molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) dissolved in 2 kg of water. If the cryoscopic constant of water $K_f$ = 1.86 K·kg/mol, calculate (a) the molality of the solution, and (b) the freezing point depression.

### Step 1 — Compute Moles of NaCl

$$n_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{58.5 \text{ g}}{58.5 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.0 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Molality

$$m = \frac{1.0 \text{ mol}}{2 \text{ kg}} = 0.5 \text{ mol/kg}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Freezing Point Depression Formula

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \times m = 1.86 \times 0.5 = 0.93 \text{ °C}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The brine freezes at **-0.93 °C** instead of 0 °C. This property is exploited in *slush-ice brine refrigeration* of fish in the seafood industry.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Concentration Units

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison table. Memorize it for Part A short-answer questions.

| Property | Molarity (M) | Molality (m) | Normality (N) | Mole Fraction (x) |
|----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Definition | mol solute / L solution | mol solute / kg solvent | eq. solute / L solution | mol component / total mol |
| Denominator basis | Volume of solution | Mass of solvent | Volume of solution | Total moles |
| Temperature dependent? | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** | **No** |
| Symbol notation | Capital M | Lowercase m | Capital N | Lowercase x |
| Preferred in | Wet-lab titrations | Colligative property work | Acid-base chemistry | Thermodynamics |
| Unit | mol/L | mol/kg | eq/L | Dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — How to Calculate Molality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read the Problem] --> B{Is the solute mass given?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Convert solute mass to moles using n = W / M]
    B -- No --> D[Convert given moles directly]
    C --> E{Is solvent mass in kg?}
    D --> E
    E -- No, in grams --> F[Divide solvent mass by 1000 to get kg]
    E -- Yes, already in kg --> G[Use solvent mass directly]
    F --> H[Apply formula: m = n_solute / W_solvent_kg]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Report answer in mol/kg or molal]
    I --> J{Does problem ask for a colligative property?}
    J -- Yes, freezing point --> K[Compute delta_Tf = Kf x m]
    J -- Yes, boiling point --> L[Compute delta_Tb = Kb x m]
    J -- Yes, osmotic pressure --> M[Compute pi = m x R x T]
    J -- No --> N[END: Final answer is m]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Molality vs Molarity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solution Concentration Problem] --> B{Is the solution heated or cooled?}
    B -- Yes, temperature varies --> C[USE MOLALITY m]
    B -- No, temperature constant --> D{Is the solute a non-volatile molecule?}
    D -- Yes, need boiling/freezing data --> C
    D -- No --> E[Use Molarity M if volume data given]
    C --> F[Formula: m = n / W_solvent_kg]
    E --> G[Formula: M = n / V_solution_L]
    F --> H[Final result: mol/kg]
    G --> I[Final result: mol/L]
```

## 4.3 Molarity–Molality Interconversion Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Molarity M] --> B[Multiply by 1000]
    B --> C[Compute 1000 x density minus M x M_solute]
    C --> D[Divide result to get Molality m]
    D --> E[Verify: m less than or equal to M for dilute solutions]
    E --> F[Report m in mol/kg]
```

## 4.4 Application Domain Map in Food Technology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Molality as a Concept] --> B[Colligative Properties]
    A --> C[Process Calculations]
    B --> B1[Freezing Point Depression]
    B --> B2[Boiling Point Elevation]
    B --> B3[Osmotic Pressure]
    C --> C1[Brine Curing of Meat]
    C --> C2[Sugar Boiling in Confectionery]
    C --> C3[Ice-Cream Mix Design]
    C --> C4[Reverse Osmosis of Milk]
    B1 --> D[Quality Control Lab]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> E[Industrial Food Plant]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define molality. Why is it preferred over molarity in colligative property determinations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Molality (m) is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one kilogram (1 kg) of solvent. Its SI unit is mol/kg, also written as *molal*.
$$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$$
Molality is preferred over molarity in colligative property studies because it uses **mass of the solvent** in the denominator, which is **independent of temperature**. In contrast, molarity uses the volume of the solution, which changes with temperature due to thermal expansion or contraction. Since colligative properties (freezing point depression, boiling point elevation, osmotic pressure) are measured across temperature changes, the temperature-independence of molality makes it the mathematically rigorous choice.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** A solution contains 12 g of urea (molar mass = 60 g/mol) dissolved in 500 g of water. Calculate the molality of the solution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Step 1 — Moles of urea:**
$$n = \frac{W}{M} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.20 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of solvent in kg:**
$$W_{\text{solvent}} = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.500 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Molality:**
$$m = \frac{0.20}{0.500} = 0.40 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $m = \mathbf{0.40 \text{ molal}}$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 3, May 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between molality (m) and molarity (M) of a solution. State clearly all the variables used in your derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solution of NaOH has a molarity of 0.5 M and density 1.05 g/mL. Calculate the molality of the solution. (Molar mass of NaOH = 40 g/mol) **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

By definition:
$$M = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{V_{\text{solution (L)}}}$$

The volume of the solution can be written using density $\rho$ (g/mL):
$$V_{\text{solution (L)}} = \frac{W_{\text{solution (g)}}}{1000 \times \rho}$$

Substituting:
$$M = \frac{n_{\text{solute}} \times 1000 \times \rho}{W_{\text{solution (g)}}}$$

We also have:
$$W_{\text{solution}} = W_{\text{solvent}} + W_{\text{solute}}$$

And $n_{\text{solute}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{solute}}}{M_{\text{solute}}}$, so:
$$M = \frac{W_{\text{solute}} \times 1000 \times \rho}{M_{\text{solute}} \times (W_{\text{solvent}} + W_{\text{solute}})}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $W_{\text{solvent}}$ and recognizing that the molality is $m = \dfrac{W_{\text{solute}} \times 1000}{M_{\text{solute}} \times W_{\text{solvent (g)}}}$, we obtain after algebraic simplification:

$$\boxed{m = \frac{M \times 1000}{1000\rho - M \times M_{\text{solute}}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $M = 0.5$ mol/L
- $\rho = 1.05$ g/mL
- $M_{\text{solute (NaOH)}} = 40$ g/mol

**Substitute into the derived formula:**

Denominator calculation:
$$1000 \rho = 1000 \times 1.05 = 1050$$

$$M \times M_{\text{solute}} = 0.5 \times 40 = 20$$

$$1000\rho - M \times M_{\text{solute}} = 1050 - 20 = 1030$$

Now the molality:
$$m = \frac{0.5 \times 1000}{1030} = \frac{500}{1030} = 0.4854 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $m \approx \mathbf{0.485 \text{ mol/kg}}$

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define molality. Explain why it is a temperature-independent concentration unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food technologist prepares a sugar syrup by dissolving 171 g of sucrose ($C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$, molar mass = 342 g/mol) in 1.5 kg of water. Calculate the molality of the syrup and predict its freezing point depression. (For water, $K_f = 1.86$ K·kg/mol) **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

Molality is the number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent:
$$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$$

It is temperature-independent because:
1. **Mass is invariant with temperature** — a kilogram of water remains a kilogram whether at 4 °C or 90 °C.
2. **Moles are a count of particles** — unaffected by temperature, as long as no chemical reaction occurs.
3. Therefore, the *ratio* of moles to kilograms cannot change due to thermal expansion.

This is fundamentally different from molarity, which uses the *volume* of the solution, and volume changes with temperature according to:
$$V_T = V_0 (1 + \gamma \Delta T)$$
where $\gamma$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Moles of sucrose:**
$$n = \frac{171}{342} = 0.5 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Molality:**
$$m = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Freezing Point Depression:**
$$\Delta T_f = K_f \times m = 1.86 \times 0.333 = 0.620 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answer:** $m = 0.333$ molal; $\Delta T_f = 0.620$ °C. The syrup freezes at approximately **-0.62 °C**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert grams to kilograms** in the denominator. A student who writes $\dfrac{0.025}{250}$ instead of $\dfrac{0.025}{0.250}$ loses **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Confusing "solvent" and "solution"** in the denominator. KTU examiners strictly check this — losing 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the units in the final answer.** A bare number like "0.1" without "mol/kg" is incomplete. Always write the unit.
> 4. **Dropping the molar mass symbol $M$** in derivations. The variable $M$ (molar mass) and $M$ (molarity) are different — use distinct symbols like $M_{\text{solute}}$ and $M_{\text{molarity}}$ to avoid losing clarity marks.
> 5. **Not showing the algebraic transition steps** in Part B (a) derivations. KTU awards step-wise marks; skipping a step loses 1 mark per skipped logical transition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Molality (m) = moles of solute ÷ kg of solvent. Unit = **mol/kg** or **molal**.
- ✅ **Case-sensitive symbol:** lowercase **m** = molality; capital **M** = molarity.
- ✅ **Working Formula:** $m = \dfrac{W_{\text{solute}} \times 1000}{M_{\text{solute}} \times W_{\text{solvent(g)}}}$
- ✅ **Denominator is solvent, NEVER solution.** If only solution mass is given, subtract solute mass first.
- ✅ **Temperature-independent** because it uses mass, not volume.
- ✅ **Used in colligative property calculations:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$, $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$, $\pi = mRT$.
- ✅ **Food technology applications:** brine curing, sugar boiling, ice-cream freezing point, osmotic pressure of milk/whey.
- ✅ **Molarity-Molality link:** $m = \dfrac{M \times 1000}{1000\rho - M \times M_{\text{solute}}}$.
- ✅ **Conversion to mole fraction:** $m = \dfrac{x_{\text{solute}}}{x_{\text{solvent}} \times M_{\text{solvent(kg/mol)}}}$.
- ✅ **Always show units in the final numerical answer.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTT104 - INTRODUCTION TO FOOD TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Normality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normality (N)** is defined as the **number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution**. It is a unit of concentration that expresses the reactive capacity of a solution in terms of the number of equivalents per litre.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$N = \dfrac{\text{Number of gram equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}$$

The **SI unit** of normality is **equivalents per litre (eq/L)**, often denoted simply as **N**.

In the context of food technology, normality is critical because most analytical procedures — including **acid-base titrations, Kjeldahl nitrogen estimation, and acidity determination in food products** — depend on equivalent-based stoichiometry rather than molecular concentrations.

## 1.2 The Concept of Gram Equivalent Weight

> [!NOTE]
> **Gram Equivalent Weight (Eq. wt)** is the mass of a substance that will react with or supply **1 mole of electrons**, **1 mole of H⁺ ions**, or **1 mole of OH⁻ ions** in a chemical reaction.

It is calculated as:

$$\text{Equivalent Weight} = \dfrac{\text{Molecular Weight (M)}}{\text{n-factor}}$$

Where the **n-factor** is also called the **valence factor** or **replacing power** of the species. The value of n depends entirely on the type of reaction:

| Species Type | n-factor (Valence Factor) |
|---|---|
| **Acids** (HCl, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) | Basicity = number of replaceable H⁺ ions |
| **Bases** (NaOH, Ca(OH)₂) | Acidity = number of replaceable OH⁻ ions |
| **Salts** (Na₂CO₃, KMnO₄ in neutralization) | Total positive or negative charge |
| **Oxidizing/Reducing agents** (KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇) | Number of electrons gained or lost per molecule |
| **Ions in precipitation reactions** | Magnitude of charge on the ion |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hands-On" Approach 🤝

Imagine each chemical species as a **worker** whose job is to react with a partner. Each worker has a specific number of "hands" available to grasp reaction partners:

- **HCl** has **1 hand** (1 replaceable H⁺) — it can grab only **1 partner**.
- **H₂SO₄** has **2 hands** (2 replaceable H⁺) — it can grab **2 partners**.
- **H₃PO₄** has **3 hands** (3 replaceable H⁺) — it can grab **3 partners**.

Now, normality is like counting **"how many workers are actively working per litre"** in terms of *hand capacity* — not just how many heads are present. A **1 L solution of 1 N HCl** has the *same reactive capacity* (1 equivalent of H⁺) as a **1 L solution of 0.5 N H₂SO₄**, because both deliver exactly **1 mole of H⁺ ions** for neutralization.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Quick Memory Trick:** *Normality = Molarity × n-factor* — so it's essentially "molarity scaled up by the reactive hands each molecule carries."

## 1.4 Why Normality Matters in Food Technology

Food analysis laboratories routinely use normality for:

- **Titratable Acidity (TA) determination** in fruit juices, wines, vinegar, and dairy products.
- **Protein estimation** via the Kjeldahl method, where the normality of standardized HCl or H₂SO₄ is used to back-calculate nitrogen content.
- **Salt (NaCl) determination** in processed foods by Mohr's or Volhard's titration.
- **Standardization of reagents** used in quality control (QC) labs.
- **Determination of preservatives** like benzoic acid, sorbic acid, and sulphites.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving Normality from First Principles

Starting from the definition:

$$N = \dfrac{\text{Gram equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution (L)}}$$

Since **1 gram equivalent = (Mass of solute in grams) / (Equivalent weight)**:

$$N = \dfrac{W \, / \, \text{Eq. wt}}{V_{L}}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Mass of solute in grams
- Eq. wt = Equivalent weight of solute
- $V_L$ = Volume in litres

To express volume in **millilitres** (which is more common in lab practice):

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{\text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}}$$

## 2.2 The Foundational Formula Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet covers **every normality-based equation** you need for Module 4 problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Application in Food Tech |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Eq. wt} = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight from molecular weight & n-factor | General reagent preparation |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{\text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}$ | Normality from mass & volume | Standardizing solutions |
| 3 | $N = M \times n$ | Normality ↔ Molarity conversion | Switching concentration units |
| 4 | $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ | Dilution / neutralization equation | Acid-base titrations |
| 5 | $\text{% Purity} = \dfrac{N \times \text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}{W_{sample} \times 1000} \times 100$ | Purity of a solid sample | Assay of food-grade chemicals |
| 6 | $\text{Acidity (\%)} = \dfrac{N \times V \times \text{Eq. wt of acid}}{W_{sample} \times 1000} \times 100$ | Titratable acidity in foods | Juice, wine, vinegar analysis |
| 7 | $\text{Nitrogen (\%)} = \dfrac{(N_1 V_1 - N_2 V_2) \times 1.4}{W_{sample}}$ | Kjeldahl nitrogen | Protein estimation ($N \times 6.25$) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Kjeldahl formula**, the value **1.4** is the equivalent weight of nitrogen divided by 10 (i.e., $14/10 = 1.4$). This is a *favourite* KTU exam number — memorize it.

## 2.3 Calculating the n-factor: The Heart of Every Normality Problem

The **single most common error** students make in KTU exams is choosing the wrong n-factor. Here's the rigorous logic:

### Step A: Identify the Reaction Type
- **Acid-Base (Neutralization)?** → Use number of H⁺ or OH⁻ actually *transferred* in that specific reaction.
- **Redox (Oxidation-Reduction)?** → Use the total number of electrons transferred.
- **Precipitation or Complexometric?** → Use the total charge on the active ion.

### Step B: Apply the Rule

> [!TIP]
> **For a reaction, the normality of a substance is proportional to the number of H⁺ / OH⁻ / electrons actually exchanged in THAT reaction, not merely present in the formula.**

### Worked Examples of n-factor

| Substance | Reaction Context | n-factor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| HCl | Neutralization | 1 | Donates 1 H⁺ |
| H₂SO₄ | Neutralization | 2 | Donates 2 H⁺ |
| H₃PO₄ | → NaH₂PO₄ | 1 | Only 1 H⁺ replaced |
| H₃PO₄ | → Na₂HPO₄ | 2 | 2 H⁺ replaced |
| H₃PO₄ | → Na₃PO₄ | 3 | 3 H⁺ replaced |
| NaOH | Neutralization | 1 | 1 OH⁻ |
| Ca(OH)₂ | Neutralization | 2 | 2 OH⁻ |
| KMnO₄ | Acidic medium (Mn²⁺) | 5 | 5 e⁻ gained |
| KMnO₄ | Neutral medium (MnO₂) | 3 | 3 e⁻ gained |
| KMnO₄ | Strongly alkaline (MnO₄²⁻) | 1 | 1 e⁻ gained |
| K₂Cr₂O₇ | Acidic (Cr³⁺) | 6 | 6 e⁻ gained per molecule |
| Na₂CO₃ | vs HCl (to CO₂) | 2 | 2 H⁺ accepted |
| Na₂CO₃ | vs HCl (to NaHCO₃) | 1 | 1 H⁺ accepted |
| FeSO₄ | Oxidation to Fe³⁺ | 1 | 1 e⁻ lost |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

In an industrial food lab:

1. **Quality Control (QC):** Every batch of vinegar must meet a minimum acidity (often **4–8% acetic acid**). This is verified by titrating against **standardized 0.1 N NaOH**.
2. **Dairy Industry:** Milk acidity is routinely tested using **0.1 N NaOH** with phenolphthalein indicator. Fresh milk should show acidity ≤ **0.18% lactic acid**.
3. **Protein Labeling:** Packaged food labels state protein content, which is derived from nitrogen determined by the **Kjeldahl method** — a method that runs entirely on normality-based calculations.
4. **Beverages:** Soft drink manufacturers monitor **titratable acidity** to ensure flavour consistency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Mass of Solute Required to Prepare a Given Normality Solution

**Problem Setup:** How many grams of **oxalic acid dihydrate (H₂C₂O₄·2H₂O)** are required to prepare **500 mL of 0.1 N** solution, to be used as a primary standard in food acidity titrations?

### Step 1: Identify the Molecular Formula and Molecular Weight

The molecular formula is $H_2C_2O_4 \cdot 2H_2O$.

- Atomic weights used: H = 1, C = 12, O = 16
- Molecular weight: $M = (2 \times 1) + (2 \times 12) + (4 \times 16) + 2 \times (2 \times 1 + 16)$

Let us expand this:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{H_2C_2O_4} &= (2)(1) + (2)(12) + (4)(16) \\
&= 2 + 24 + 64 \\
&= 90 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{2H_2O} &= 2 \times (2 \times 1 + 16) = 2 \times 18 = 36 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{oxalic acid dihydrate}} &= 90 + 36 = 126 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Determine the n-factor

Oxalic acid ($H_2C_2O_4$) acts as a dibasic acid in neutralization reactions:

$$H_2C_2O_4 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow Na_2C_2O_4 + 2\,H_2O$$

Each molecule donates **2 H⁺** ions, so:

$$n = 2$$

### Step 3: Compute the Equivalent Weight

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Eq. wt} &= \dfrac{M}{n} = \dfrac{126}{2} = 63 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Normality Equation to Find Mass

The governing equation is:

$$N = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{\text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}$$

Rearranging for $W$:

$$W = \dfrac{N \times \text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}{1000}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

Given: $N = 0.1$, Eq. wt $= 63$, $V_{mL} = 500$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{0.1 \times 63 \times 500}{1000}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{0.1 \times 63 \times 500}{1000} = \dfrac{3150}{1000} = 3.15 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ Final Answer

> **3.15 grams** of oxalic acid dihydrate are required to prepare 500 mL of 0.1 N solution.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Titratable Acidity of a Food Sample

**Problem Setup:** **20 mL of a fruit juice** is titrated against **0.1 N NaOH**, and the titre value is **12.5 mL** to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. Calculate the **titratable acidity as % citric acid** (the dominant acid in citrus juices). Take Eq. wt of citric acid = **64 g/eq**.

### Step 1: State the Governing Formula

The formula for titratable acidity expressed as a percentage is:

$$\% \text{ Acidity} = \dfrac{N_{alkali} \times V_{alkali} \times \text{Eq. wt of acid}}{V_{sample} \times 1000} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Substitute All Known Values

Given:
- $N_{alkali} = 0.1$
- $V_{alkali} = 12.5$ mL
- Eq. wt of citric acid $= 64$
- $V_{sample} = 20$ mL

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Acidity} &= \dfrac{0.1 \times 12.5 \times 64}{20 \times 1000} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Numerator

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.1 \times 12.5 \times 64 = 80
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Denominator

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 20 \times 1000 = 20000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Acidity} &= \dfrac{80}{20000} \times 100 = \dfrac{80 \times 100}{20000}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Acidity} &= \dfrac{8000}{20000} = 0.40\%
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ Final Answer

> The **titratable acidity** of the fruit juice is **0.40% citric acid**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Kjeldahl Nitrogen Estimation

**Problem Setup:** **2.0 g of a food sample** is digested and the liberated ammonia is absorbed in **50 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄**. The excess unreacted acid is back-titrated with **0.1 N NaOH**, requiring **15 mL** to reach the endpoint. Calculate the **% nitrogen** in the sample.

### Step 1: Write the Reaction Sequence

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Organic N} &\xrightarrow[\text{ catalysis}]{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4, \Delta} (NH_4)_2SO_4 \\
(NH_4)_2SO_4 + 2\,NaOH &\rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2\,NH_3 + 2\,H_2O \\
2\,NH_3 + H_2SO_4 &\rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 \text{ (absorption)} \\
H_2SO_4 \text{ (excess)} + 2\,NaOH &\rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2\,H_2O \text{ (back-titration)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Equivalent Amount of H₂SO₄ That Reacted with Ammonia

The total H₂SO₄ taken = 50 mL of 0.1 N → its equivalents = $0.1 \times 50 / 1000 = 0.005$ eq

NaOH used in back-titration = 15 mL of 0.1 N → its equivalents = $0.1 \times 15 / 1000 = 0.0015$ eq

H₂SO₄ that actually reacted with NH₃ (and therefore with nitrogen):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{eq of acid reacted with NH}_3 &= \text{eq of total acid} - \text{eq of excess acid}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{eq of acid reacted with NH}_3 &= 0.005 - 0.0015 = 0.0035 \text{ eq}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Convert Equivalents of Acid to Equivalents (and Mass) of Nitrogen

The equivalent weight of nitrogen (N) is **14 g/eq** (since N: 14, and for $NH_3$ it replaces 1 H⁺, so n=1).

Mass of nitrogen present in the sample:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of N} &= \text{eq of acid} \times \text{Eq. wt of N} = 0.0035 \times 14 = 0.049 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the % Nitrogen

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,N &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of N}}{\text{Mass of sample}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.049}{2.0} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,N &= 2.45\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute the % Protein (if required)

Using the standard food factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Protein} &= \%\,N \times 6.25 = 2.45 \times 6.25 = 15.3125\%
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ Final Answer

> The food sample contains **2.45% nitrogen** and correspondingly **~15.31% crude protein**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Normality vs. Molarity vs. Molality

| Property | **Normality (N)** | **Molarity (M)** | **Molality (m)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | eq of solute per L of solution | mol of solute per L of solution | mol of solute per kg of solvent |
| Depends on reaction? | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Unit | eq/L | mol/L | mol/kg |
| Temperature dependent? | Slightly (volume-based) | Slightly (volume-based) | No (mass-based) |
| Best suited for | **Titrations, food QC** | Stoichiometry, kinetics | Colligative properties |
| Changes with reaction? | ✅ (e.g., KMnO₄ in acidic ≠ alkaline) | ❌ | ❌ |
| KTU exam preference | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Module 4 favourite) | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |

---

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: How to Solve Any Normality Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Normality Problem Given"] --> B["Identify the Reaction Type: Acid-Base, Redox, or Precipitation?"]
    B --> C1["Acid-Base"]
    B --> C2["Redox"]
    B --> C3["Precipitation"]
    C1 --> D1["Count number of H+ or OH- actually transferred"]
    C2 --> D2["Count number of electrons transferred"]
    C3 --> D3["Count total charge on the reacting ion"]
    D1 --> E["Determine n-factor for BOTH reactants"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["Calculate Eq.wt = Molecular Weight / n for each species"]
    F --> G["Apply appropriate formula: N = Wx1000 / Eq.wt x V_mL  OR  N1V1 = N2V2  OR  Titratable Acidity formula"]
    G --> H["Substitute numerical values with correct units"]
    H --> I["Compute final answer and attach proper unit N or percent"]
    I --> J["END: Validate with dimensional analysis and logical check"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Kjeldahl Protein Estimation Pipeline (Normality-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Stage 1: Digestion"]
        A1["Food Sample: W grams"] --> A2["Add Conc. H2SO4 + Catalyst"]
        A2 --> A3["Heat until clear: Organic N to NH4+"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Stage 2: Distillation & Absorption"]
        A3 --> B1["Add excess NaOH"]
        B1 --> B2["NH3 liberated and distilled"]
        B2 --> B3["Absorbed in known volume V1 of standard H2SO4 of normality N1"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Stage 3: Back-Titration"]
        B3 --> C1["Titrate excess H2SO4 with standard NaOH: normality N2, volume V2"]
        C1 --> C2["Use formula: percent N = N1V1 - N2V2 x 1.4 / W"]
    end
    subgraph S4["Stage 4: Result"]
        C2 --> D1["percent Protein = percent N x 6.25"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Normality Relationships (Unit Conversion Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MW["Molecular Weight (M)"] --> DivideBy["Divide by n-factor"]
    DivideBy --> EW["Equivalent Weight (Eq.wt)"]
    EW --> FormulaA["W x 1000 / Eq.wt x V_mL = N"]
    Molarity["Molarity (M)"] --> MultiplyBy["Multiply by n-factor"]
    MultiplyBy --> N1["Normality (N)"]
    N1 --> FormulaB["N1 x V1 = N2 x V2 (Dilution & Titration)"]
    FormulaA --> FoodApp1["Food QC: Reagent Standardization"]
    FormulaB --> FoodApp2["Food QC: Acid-Base Titrations"]
    FormulaB --> FoodApp3["Food QC: Kjeldahl Nitrogen"]
    FormulaB --> FoodApp4["Food QC: Salt & Preservative Analysis"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct n-factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["What is the substance?"] --> Q1{"Is it an acid?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q1A["Count replaceable H+ ions in the SPECIFIC reaction"]
    Q1A --> Q1A1["Example: H2SO4 with NaOH: n = 2"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is it a base?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q2A["Count replaceable OH- ions"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is it a salt in neutralization?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q3A["Count total positive charge of metal ions"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is it a redox agent?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q4A["Count total electrons transferred per molecule"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5["Consult standard analytical chemistry reference table"]
    Q1A1 --> End["n-factor determined - proceed to Eq.wt = M / n"]
    Q2A --> End
    Q3A --> End
    Q4A --> End
    Q5 --> End
```
