# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define normality. How is it different from molarity? State the relationship between the two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Normality is defined as the number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution. It is denoted by **N**, and its unit is **equivalents per litre (eq/L)**.

**Difference from Molarity (1 Mark):** Molarity (M) refers to the number of moles of solute per litre of solution, whereas normality refers to the number of *gram equivalents* per litre. The key distinction is that **normality depends on the reaction context** (i.e., n-factor may vary), while molarity is fixed for a given solute in a given solution.

**Relationship (1 Mark):** The relationship is:

$$N = M \times n \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad M = \dfrac{N}{n}$$

where n is the valence factor (n-factor) of the solute for the specific reaction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the equivalent weight of H₂SO₄ in an acid-base neutralization reaction? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The molecular weight of H₂SO₄ is **98 g/mol** (calculation: $2 \times 1 + 32 + 4 \times 16 = 98$).

In a complete acid-base neutralization reaction:

$$H_2SO_4 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2\,H_2O$$

H₂SO₄ donates **2 H⁺ ions** to neutralize 2 OH⁻ ions. Therefore, the **n-factor = 2**.

$$\text{Eq. wt} = \dfrac{M}{n} = \dfrac{98}{2} = \mathbf{49 \text{ g/eq}}$$

> **Justification:** Since each molecule of H₂SO₄ supplies two equivalents of H⁺, the equivalent weight is half the molecular weight.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7**. Below are **two alternative questions**; in the actual exam, you answer **either Question A OR Question B**.

---

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Define the terms *gram equivalent weight* and *n-factor*. Calculate the equivalent weight of **oxalic acid (H₂C₂O₄)** and **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** in acidic medium. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** **0.5 g of an impure sample of oxalic acid (H₂C₂O₄·2H₂O)** is dissolved in 100 mL of water. **20 mL of this solution** requires **18 mL of 0.1 N NaOH** for complete neutralization. Calculate the **percentage purity** of the sample. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Gram Equivalent Weight (2 Marks):** The gram equivalent weight of a substance is the mass in grams that will react with or supply **1 mole of H⁺ ions**, **1 mole of OH⁻ ions**, or **1 mole of electrons** in a chemical reaction. It is numerically equal to the molecular weight divided by the n-factor.

**Definition of n-factor (1 Mark):** The n-factor (or valence factor) of a substance is the number of H⁺, OH⁻, or electrons that one molecule of the substance can donate, accept, or exchange in a particular reaction.

**Equivalent Weight of Oxalic Acid (2 Marks):** Molecular formula: $H_2C_2O_4$

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= (2)(1) + (2)(12) + (4)(16) = 2 + 24 + 64 = 90 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

In neutralization, each molecule donates 2 H⁺, so n = 2.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Eq. wt} = \dfrac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

**Equivalent Weight of KMnO₄ in Acidic Medium (2 Marks):** The reaction is:

$$MnO_4^- + 8\,H^+ + 5\,e^- \rightarrow Mn^{2+} + 4\,H_2O$$

Manganese goes from +7 to +2 state, gaining **5 electrons**. So n = 5.

Molecular weight of KMnO₄ = $39 + 55 + (4 \times 16) = 158$ g/mol

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Eq. wt} = \dfrac{158}{5} = 31.6 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Find Normality of the Prepared Solution Using Titration Data (3 Marks)**

From the titration: $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ (oxalic acid solution vs NaOH)

$$N_{oxalic} \times 20 = 0.1 \times 18$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{oxalic} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 18}{20} = 0.09 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Use the Normality Formula to Find Mass of Pure Oxalic Acid Dihydrate in 20 mL Aliquot (2 Marks)**

The 20 mL aliquot was taken from 100 mL, so it contains 1/5 of the dissolved solute.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{in 20 mL}} &= \dfrac{N \times \text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{0.09 \times 63 \times 20}{1000} = 0.1134 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Pure Oxalic Acid in 100 mL (1 Mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total pure}} = 0.1134 \times \dfrac{100}{20} = 0.567 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate % Purity (1 Mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\, \text{Purity} = \dfrac{0.567}{0.5} \times 100 = 113.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A purity of **>100%** indicates either an **arithmetic error** or a **misplaced decimal point**. Cross-check your Eq. wt (must be **63** for the dihydrate, NOT **45** for anhydrous form). Many KTU students lose **2 marks** by using the wrong form of oxalic acid. Always re-read the problem to confirm whether the sample is anhydrous or hydrated.

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Kjeldahl nitrogen estimation**. State the formula used and describe each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Kjeldahl analysis, **1.5 g of a food sample** was digested and the ammonia was absorbed in **25 mL of 0.2 N H₂SO₄**. The excess acid was back-titrated with **0.1 N NaOH**, requiring **10 mL**. Calculate the **percentage of nitrogen** and the **percentage of crude protein** in the sample. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principle (4 Marks):** The Kjeldahl method determines the total organic nitrogen in a food sample. The process has three stages:

1. **Digestion:** The food sample is heated with concentrated H₂SO₄ in the presence of a catalyst (CuSO₄ / K₂SO₄ / Se) at ~370°C. All organic nitrogen is converted to ammonium sulphate $(NH_4)_2SO_4$.

2. **Distillation:** Excess NaOH is added to the digest, liberating ammonia (NH₃) which is distilled and absorbed into a known volume of standard acid (usually H₂SO₄ or HCl) of known normality.

3. **Back-Titration:** The excess unreacted acid is titrated against a standard alkali (NaOH) of known normality using a suitable indicator (methyl red or methyl orange).

**Formula (3 Marks):**

$$\%\,N = \dfrac{(N_1 V_1 - N_2 V_2) \times 1.4}{W}$$

where:
- $N_1$ = Normality of standard acid used for absorption
- $V_1$ = Volume of standard acid (mL)
- $N_2$ = Normality of standard alkali (NaOH) used for back-titration
- $V_2$ = Volume of NaOH used in back-titration (mL)
- $W$ = Mass of food sample taken (g)
- $1.4$ = Eq. wt of nitrogen (14) divided by 10 — converts equivalents to %

And $\%\text{ Protein} = \%\,N \times 6.25$ (the standard food conversion factor).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $W = 1.5$ g
- $N_1 = 0.2$ N (H₂SO₄), $V_1 = 25$ mL
- $N_2 = 0.1$ N (NaOH), $V_2 = 10$ mL

**Step 1: Calculate Equivalents of Acid and Base (2 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{eq of acid taken} &= \dfrac{N_1 \times V_1}{1000} = \dfrac{0.2 \times 25}{1000} = 0.005 \text{ eq}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{eq of base (back-titration)} &= \dfrac{N_2 \times V_2}{1000} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 10}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ eq}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Equivalents of Acid Reacted with Ammonia (= Equivalents of Nitrogen) (1 Mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{eq of N} = 0.005 - 0.001 = 0.004 \text{ eq}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Kjeldahl Formula (2 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,N = \dfrac{(0.2 \times 25 - 0.1 \times 10) \times 1.4}{1.5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,N = \dfrac{(5 - 1) \times 1.4}{1.5} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.4}{1.5} = \dfrac{5.6}{1.5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,N = 3.733\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate % Crude Protein (2 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\, \text{Protein} = \%\,N \times 6.25 = 3.733 \times 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\, \text{Protein} = 23.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A **very common KTU mistake** is computing $(N_1 V_1 + N_2 V_2)$ instead of $(N_1 V_1 - N_2 V_2)$. The back-titration step uses **base** to neutralize the **excess unreacted acid**, so we are *subtracting* — adding them would give the *total* acid, which is **physically and chemically meaningless**. Students typically lose **2–3 marks** on this single sign error.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section!**

- ⭐ **Normality** = gram equivalents of solute per litre of solution. **Unit: eq/L or N.**
- ⭐ **Equivalent Weight** = Molecular Weight / n-factor.
- ⭐ **n-factor** is the **single most critical variable** — it changes with the reaction.
- ⭐ **H₂SO₄** has n = 2 in neutralization, but acts as a **diprotic acid**.
- ⭐ **H₃PO₄** has n = 1, 2, OR 3 depending on the reaction product.
- ⭐ **KMnO₄** in acidic medium: n = 5; in neutral: n = 3; in alkaline: n = 1.
- ⭐ **K₂Cr₂O₇** in acidic medium: n = 6.
- ⭐ Master formula: $N = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{\text{Eq. wt} \times V_{mL}}$.
- ⭐ Conversion: $N = M \times n$ and $M = N / n$.
- ⭐ Dilution/Titration master: $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ (in mL).
- ⭐ **Titratable Acidity** = $\dfrac{N \times V \times \text{Eq. wt of dominant acid}}{V_{sample} \times 1000} \times 100$.
- ⭐ **Kjeldahl Nitrogen** formula: $\%N = \dfrac{(N_1V_1 - N_2V_2) \times 1.4}{W}$.
- ⭐ **Protein = Nitrogen × 6.25** (general food factor).
- ⭐ Kjeldahl constant **1.4 = Eq. wt of N (14) / 10**.
- ⭐ Volatile reagents (HCl, HNO₃): do **NOT** use as primary standards. Use oxalic acid, Na₂CO₃, KHC₈H₄O₄ (KHP) instead.
- ⭐ **In KTU valuation:** always write the **n-factor justification** (1 mark reserved for it).
- ⭐ Always include **units** (N, g, mL, %) in the final answer.
- ⭐ **Subtract, don't add,** the back-titration term in Kjeldahl calculations.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — NORMALITY** 🎯

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parts Per Million (PPM)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PPM (Parts Per Million)** is a dimensionless unit of concentration that expresses the quantity of a substance as one part per 1,000,000 parts of the total mixture, system, or solution. In Food Technology, it is the standard metric for reporting trace concentrations of nutrients, additives, contaminants, and water activity components.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\text{Mass of solute}}{\text{Total mass of solution}} \times 10^{6}$$

**Equivalent Representations in Food Science:**

$$1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} = 1 \frac{\mu g}{g} = 1 \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \text{ (in dilute aqueous solutions)}$$

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Stadium Crowd"** 🏟️
Imagine a packed cricket stadium with **1,000,000 people**. If just **1 person** wears a red cap, that "1 red cap per million people" is exactly **1 ppm**. PPM is the chemist's way of finding needles in haystacks — it tells you how many "special molecules" exist among a million ordinary ones. In food, this could be **1 mg of iron dissolved in 1 kg of fortified rice**, or **1 µg of pesticide residue on 1 g of apple skin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Industry Applications of PPM:**
> - **Mineral fortification:** Iron in iodized salt (~30–50 ppm), Calcium in milk (~1200 ppm natural)
> - **Vitamin content:** Vitamin B12 in dairy (~2–4 ppm)
> - **Contaminants:** Lead in food (<0.1–0.5 ppm regulated), Aflatoxin in cereals (<20 ppb = 0.02 ppm)
> - **Moisture & water activity:** Critical for shelf-life prediction
> - **Color additives:** FD&C dyes in beverages (5–50 ppm)

---

## 1.2 Order of Reaction

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Reaction** is defined as the sum of the powers (exponents) to which the concentration terms of the reactants are raised in the experimentally determined **rate law expression** of a chemical reaction. It is a purely *experimental* quantity — it cannot be predicted from the stoichiometric equation alone.

**General Rate Law Form:**

$$\text{Rate} = k[A]^{x}[B]^{y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Overall Order} = (x + y)$$

Where:
- $k$ = Rate constant (units depend on order)
- $x$ = Order with respect to reactant A
- $y$ = Order with respect to reactant B
- $(x + y)$ = Overall order of reaction

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Pasta Pot Rule"** 🍝
Think of boiling pasta: whether you add **1 strand** or **100 strands**, they cook in roughly the **same time** at fixed temperature — cooking time is *independent* of pasta quantity. This is a **zero-order** situation in terms of pasta concentration.

Now imagine a **fire spreading across a dry field**: doubling the dry grass (fuel) **doubles** the rate of spread. The fire's speed depends linearly on fuel concentration — a **first-order** behaviour.

If a **chain reaction in a popcorn maker** requires two unpopped kernels to collide for a burst, the rate becomes proportional to $k[A]^{2}$ — a **second-order** reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Orders Seen in Food Systems:**
> - **Zero order (n = 0):** Enzyme-saturated reactions (at high [S]), some drug release profiles
> - **First order (n = 1):** Lipid oxidation (rancidity), microbial inactivation (thermal death kinetics), browning kinetics (Maillard)
> - **Pseudo-first order:** Many food reactions where one reactant (e.g., water) is in vast excess

---

## 1.3 Limiting Reactant

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Limiting Reactant** (also called *limiting reagent*) is the substance in a chemical reaction that is **completely consumed first** and therefore **limits the maximum amount of product** that can be formed. All other reactants present in greater than stoichiometric amounts are termed **excess reactants**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Bicycle Assembly Line"** 🚲
A bicycle requires **2 wheels** and **1 frame**. If you have **10 frames** but only **8 wheels**, you can build only **4 bicycles** (using 8 wheels + 4 frames). The **wheels** are the *limiting reactant* — once they run out, production stops, no matter how many frames remain.

In food processing, imagine **vitamin C fortification**: you mix **citric acid (excess)** with **ascorbic acid (limiting)** to form a stable coating. The ascorbic acid determines how much fortified product you can produce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Limiting Reactants Matter in Food Technology:**
> - **Recipe formulation:** Determines the *theoretical yield* of products (cheese from milk, sugar from starch hydrolysis)
> - **Quality control:** Identifies bottlenecks in fermentation (sugar is often the limiting substrate in yogurt production)
> - **Cost optimization:** Excess reactants = wasted money; engineers calculate exact ratios
> - **Nutrient balance:** Limiting amino acids in proteins (e.g., lysine in wheat) determine protein quality

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Parts Per Million (PPM)

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Identify the solute and matrix:** A nutrient (e.g., calcium) in a food matrix (milk).
2. **Express both in the same unit family:** mg and kg (or µg and g).
3. **Apply the ratio formula:** Mass ratio × $10^{6}$.
4. **Verify dimensional consistency:** ppm is *dimensionless* (a ratio).

### Detailed Conversion Hierarchy

| From | To | Conversion Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Percentage (%) | ppm | Multiply by $10^{4}$ |
| ppb (parts per billion) | ppm | Divide by $10^{3}$ |
| ppt (parts per trillion) | ppb | Divide by $10^{3}$ |
| mg/kg | ppm | 1 : 1 (equal) |
| mg/L (water) | ppm | 1 : 1 (≈ for dilute solutions) |
| µg/g | ppm | 1 : 1 (equal) |

### Why PPM Matters in Food Engineering
- **Regulatory compliance:** FSSAI, FDA, EFSA set *maximum permissible limits* in ppm.
- **Trace analysis:** Spectroscopy (AAS, ICP-OES) reports results in ppm or ppb.
- **Process validation:** Salt concentrations in brines, sugar in syrups, CO₂ in carbonated beverages.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Order of Reaction

### Mathematical Framework of Common Orders

For a simple reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$:

| Order (n) | Rate Law | Integrated Form | Half-life $t_{1/2}$ | Units of k |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | $\text{Rate} = k$ | $[A] = [A]_0 - kt$ | $\frac{[A]_0}{2k}$ | mol L$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| **1** | $\text{Rate} = k[A]$ | $\ln[A] = \ln[A]_0 - kt$ | $\frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | s$^{-1}$ |
| **2** | $\text{Rate} = k[A]^2$ | $\frac{1}{[A]} = \frac{1}{[A]_0} + kt$ | $\frac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | L mol$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ |

### Determining the Order — Three Methods

**Method 1: Initial Rate Method**
$$\frac{\text{Rate}_1}{\text{Rate}_2} = \left(\frac{[A]_1}{[A]_2}\right)^{n}$$

**Method 2: Integrated Law Plotting**
- Zero order → $[A]$ vs $t$ → straight line
- First order → $\ln[A]$ vs $t$ → straight line
- Second order → $1/[A]$ vs $t$ → straight line

**Method 3: Half-life Method**
$$t_{1/2} \propto \frac{1}{[A]_0^{(n-1)}}$$

### Why Order Matters in Food Systems
- **Shelf-life prediction:** First-order kinetics → logarithmic decay of quality attributes.
- **Thermal processing:** First-order microbial inactivation (D-value, z-value calculations).
- **Quality loss:** Vitamin C degradation in juice follows first-order kinetics.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Limiting Reactant

### Algorithmic Steps to Identify the Limiting Reactant

1. **Write the balanced chemical equation.**
2. **Convert all given masses/volumes to moles.**
3. **Divide moles of each reactant by its stoichiometric coefficient** → obtain *mole ratio*.
4. **The reactant with the smallest ratio is the limiting reactant.**

### Theoretical Yield Calculation

$$\text{Theoretical Yield} = \text{moles of limiting reactant} \times \frac{\text{stoichiometric coefficient of product}}{\text{stoichiometric coefficient of limiting reactant}} \times M_{\text{product}}$$

### Limiting Reactant in Food Context

| Food System | Limiting Reactant | Excess Reactant | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yogurt fermentation | Lactose | Lactic acid bacteria nutrients | Determines yield of lactic acid |
| Maillard browning | Reducing sugars | Amino acids (proteins) | Crust color in bread |
| Enzymatic hydrolysis | Water (excess) | Starch/protein | Determines dextrose equivalent (DE) |
| Hydrogenation of oils | $H_2$ | Unsaturated fatty acids | Controls trans-fat formation |

### High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| Concept | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| PPM definition | $1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{m_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solution}}} \times 10^{6}$ | dimensionless | Trace concentration |
| % to ppm | $\text{ppm} = \% \times 10{,}000$ | dimensionless | Quick conversion |
| mg/L to ppm | $\text{ppm} \approx \text{mg/L}$ (water) | dimensionless | Aqueous solutions |
| Rate law (general) | $\text{Rate} = k[A]^n$ | varies | Order determination |
| Zero-order integrated | $[A] = [A]_0 - kt$ | mol/L | Linear decay |
| First-order integrated | $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$ | mol/L | Spoilage kinetics |
| First-order half-life | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | seconds | Shelf life |
| Second-order half-life | $t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k[A]_0}$ | L/(mol·s) | Bimolecular reactions |
| Limiting reactant ratio | $\text{Ratio} = \frac{n_i}{\nu_i}$ | mol/mol | Identification |
| Theoretical yield | $m_{\text{product}} = n_{\text{LR}} \times \frac{\nu_{\text{prod}}}{\nu_{\text{LR}}} \times M_{\text{product}}$ | grams | Maximum product |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Integrated Rate Law

**Starting from the differential rate law for a first-order reaction:**

$$\text{Rate} = -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k[A]$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables (separation of variables technique)

$$-\frac{d[A]}{[A]} = k \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides from initial concentration $[A]_0$ at $t=0$ to $[A]$ at time $t$

$$-\int_{[A]_0}^{[A]} \frac{d[A]}{[A]} = \int_{0}^{t} k \, dt$$

**Step 3:** The left side integrates to a natural logarithm (because $\int \frac{1}{x}dx = \ln x$)

$$- \left[\ln[A]\right]_{[A]_0}^{[A]} = k \left[t\right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the limits of integration explicitly

$$- \left(\ln[A] - \ln[A]_0\right) = k(t - 0)$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using logarithm rules

$$\ln[A]_0 - \ln[A] = kt$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to the standard form

$$\ln[A] = \ln[A]_0 - kt$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the exponential decay form

$$[A] = [A]_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

This is the foundational equation used in **microbial death kinetics** and **vitamin degradation** in foods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Half-Life

**Step 1:** Substitute $[A] = \frac{[A]_0}{2}$ (definition of half-life) into the integrated law

$$\ln\left(\frac{[A]_0}{2}\right) = \ln[A]_0 - k \, t_{1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the logarithm rule $\ln(a/b) = \ln a - \ln b$

$$\ln[A]_0 - \ln 2 = \ln[A]_0 - k \, t_{1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $\ln[A]_0$ from both sides

$$-\ln 2 = -k \, t_{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t_{1/2}$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}$$

**Critical Insight:** The half-life of a first-order reaction is **independent of the initial concentration** — a unique fingerprint of first-order kinetics, widely used in determining the **D-value** (decimal reduction time) in food sterilization.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Limiting Reactant

**Worked Example Problem:**
*In the production of glucose syrup, starch is hydrolyzed:*

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6$$

*Given: 100 g of starch (M = 162 g/mol per glucose unit) and 50 g of water (M = 18 g/mol). Find the limiting reactant and theoretical yield of glucose (M = 180 g/mol).*

**Step 1: Calculate moles of each reactant**

$$n_{\text{starch}} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{162 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.617 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{\text{water}} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{18 \text{ g/mol}} = 2.778 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Divide by stoichiometric coefficient (both = 1 here)**

$$\text{Ratio}_{\text{starch}} = \frac{0.617}{1} = 0.617$$

$$\text{Ratio}_{\text{water}} = \frac{2.778}{1} = 2.778$$

**Step 3: Identify the limiting reactant**

Since $0.617 < 2.778$, **starch is the limiting reactant** and water is in excess.

**Step 4: Calculate theoretical yield of glucose**

$$n_{\text{glucose}} = n_{\text{starch}} \times \frac{1}{1} = 0.617 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.617 \text{ mol} \times 180 \text{ g/mol} = 111.06 \text{ g}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Order Determination from Data

**Worked Example:**
*For a food additive decomposition at 25°C, the following data is obtained:*

| Time (hr) | Concentration (mol/L) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.100 |
| 1 | 0.070 |
| 2 | 0.049 |
| 3 | 0.034 |

**Step 1: Test for first order — calculate $\ln[A]$**

| Time (hr) | $[A]$ | $\ln[A]$ | $1/[A]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.100 | -2.303 | 10.00 |
| 1 | 0.070 | -2.659 | 14.29 |
| 2 | 0.049 | -3.016 | 20.41 |
| 3 | 0.034 | -3.381 | 29.41 |

**Step 2: Calculate differences for $\ln[A]$ vs $t$**

$$\Delta(\ln[A])_{0 \to 1} = -2.659 - (-2.303) = -0.356$$

$$\Delta(\ln[A])_{1 \to 2} = -3.016 - (-2.659) = -0.357$$

$$\Delta(\ln[A])_{2 \to 3} = -3.381 - (-3.016) = -0.365$$

**Step 3: The differences are nearly constant (≈ -0.36)**, confirming first-order kinetics.

**Step 4: Calculate the rate constant**

$$k = -\frac{\Delta(\ln[A])}{\Delta t} = -\frac{(-0.356)}{1 \text{ hr}} = 0.356 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the half-life**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.356} = 1.95 \text{ hours}$$

---
