**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Green blocks (left):** Energy and material flowing INTO the process.
- **Yellow center:** The food processing unit (e.g., heat exchanger, reactor, dryer).
- **Orange blocks (right):** Energy and material flowing OUT (useful products + waste heat).
- **Teal block:** Energy accumulated (e.g., temperature rise of the product).

The First Law dictates: **Sum of all "in" = Sum of all "out" + Accumulation.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Yield
(b) Selectivity
(c) Standard heat of formation

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Yield:** It is the ratio of the actual amount of product obtained to the theoretical maximum possible amount, expressed as a percentage. **Example:** In sugarcane processing, if 1000 kg of sugarcane theoretically yields 100 kg of sugar, but only 88 kg is recovered, the yield is **88%**.

(b) **Selectivity:** It is the ratio of the moles of desired product formed to the total moles of all products formed, expressed as a percentage. **Example:** In catalytic hydrogenation of vegetable oil, if out of 100 moles of products, 95 are the desired saturated fat and 5 are trans-fat, the selectivity is **95%**.

(c) **Standard Heat of Formation:** The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states (1 atm, 25°C). **Example:** The formation of liquid water from H₂(g) and ½O₂(g) has ΔH°f = **−285.8 kJ/mol**.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]
**State Kopp's rule. Estimate the molar heat capacity of sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) at 20°C using this rule. Given atomic heat capacities: C = 7.5, H = 9.6, O = 16.7 J/(mol·K).**

**Model Answer:**

**Kopp's Rule:** It is an empirical method for estimating the molar heat capacity of a solid or liquid compound at room temperature (~20°C) by adding the atomic heat capacities of its constituent elements.

**Estimate for Sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁):**

Step 1: Identify the number of atoms.
- C: 12 atoms
- H: 22 atoms
- O: 11 atoms

Step 2: Apply the rule.
$$C_p = (12 \times 7.5) + (22 \times 9.6) + (11 \times 16.7)$$

$$C_p = 90.0 + 211.2 + 183.7 = \boxed{484.9 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}}$$

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]

**a)** Define yield and selectivity. A reaction A → B + C produces 60 mol of desired product B and 20 mol of undesired byproduct C. The theoretical yield of B is 100 mol. Calculate:
(i) Actual yield
(ii) Selectivity
(iii) Conversion if 100 mol of A was fed and 80 mol reacted. **[7 marks]**

**b)** Discuss the energy balance for a milk pasteurization process. Calculate the heat required to pasteurize 1000 kg of milk from 4°C to 72°C. Take cₚ (milk) = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K). Comment on the energy efficiency if the boiler operates at 80% efficiency. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given:
- Actual moles of B = 60 mol
- Theoretical moles of B = 100 mol
- Moles of C (byproduct) = 20 mol
- Moles of A fed = 100 mol
- Moles of A reacted = 80 mol

**(i) Actual Yield:**
$$\eta = \frac{\text{Actual B}}{\text{Theoretical B}} \times 100 = \frac{60}{100} \times 100 = \boxed{60\%}$$

**(ii) Selectivity:**
$$S = \frac{\text{Moles of B}}{\text{Total products}} \times 100 = \frac{60}{60 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{60}{80} \times 100 = \boxed{75\%}$$

**(iii) Conversion:**
$$X = \frac{\text{Moles of A reacted}}{\text{Moles of A fed}} \times 100 = \frac{80}{100} \times 100 = \boxed{80\%}$$

**Verification using Y = X × S relationship:**
$$Y = 0.80 \times 0.75 = 0.60 = 60\% \checkmark$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Energy Balance on Pasteurizer (Steady State, no accumulation):**
$$Q_{steam} + Q_{raw\ milk} = Q_{pasteurized\ milk} + Q_{losses}$$

In a simplified case (no heat loss), all steam heat goes into raising milk temperature.

**Calculation:**
Given: m = 1000 kg, cₚ = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K), T₁ = 4°C, T₂ = 72°C.

$$\Delta T = 72 - 4 = 68 \ \text{K}$$

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \times 3.9 \times 68$$

$$\boxed{Q = 265{,}200 \ \text{kJ} = 265.2 \ \text{MJ}}$$

**Accounting for Boiler Efficiency:**
At 80% efficiency, only 80% of the fuel energy becomes useful steam heat. Therefore, the *fuel energy input* required is:

$$Q_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta_{boiler}} = \frac{265.2}{0.80} = \boxed{331.5 \ \text{MJ}}$$

**Wasted Energy:**
$$Q_{waste} = 331.5 - 265.2 = 66.3 \ \text{MJ} \ \ (20\%\ \text{loss})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** A plate heat exchanger can recover up to 90% of this heat by pre-warming incoming cold milk using outgoing hot pasteurized milk — drastically reducing operating cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]

**a)** What is the standard heat of formation? The standard heat of combustion of methane is −890 kJ/mol. Given ΔH°f(CO₂, g) = −393.5 kJ/mol and ΔH°f(H₂O, l) = −285.8 kJ/mol, calculate the standard heat of formation of methane. The reaction is:
CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(l). **[7 marks]**

**b)** Explain Kopp's rule. Estimate the molar heat capacity of solid oxalic acid (C₂H₂O₄) at 20°C. Use atomic heat capacities: C = 7.5, H = 9.6, O = 16.7 J/(mol·K). If 1 kg of oxalic acid is heated from 25°C to 95°C, calculate the heat required. (Molar mass of oxalic acid = 90 g/mol) **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** Standard heat of formation (ΔH°f) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states (1 atm, 25°C). For elements in their standard states, ΔH°f = 0.

**Given:**
- ΔH°c(CH₄) = −890 kJ/mol
- ΔH°f(CO₂, g) = −393.5 kJ/mol
- ΔH°f(H₂O, l) = −285.8 kJ/mol
- ΔH°f(O₂, g) = 0

**Step 1:** Apply Hess's Law.

$$\Delta H°_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{reactants})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$-890 = [1 \times (-393.5) + 2 \times (-285.8)] - [\Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4) + 2 \times 0]$$

$$-890 = [-393.5 - 571.6] - \Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4)$$

$$-890 = -965.1 - \Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for ΔH°f(CH₄).

$$\Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4) = -965.1 - (-890)$$

$$\Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4) = -965.1 + 890$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H°_f(\text{CH}_4) = -75.1 \ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Kopp's Rule:** An empirical rule used to estimate the molar heat capacity of solid or liquid compounds at room temperature (~20°C) by summing the atomic heat capacities of their constituent elements.

**Step 1:** Identify atoms in oxalic acid (C₂H₂O₄).
- C: 2 atoms
- H: 2 atoms
- O: 4 atoms

**Step 2:** Apply Kopp's rule.

$$C_p = (2 \times 7.5) + (2 \times 9.6) + (4 \times 16.7)$$

$$C_p = 15.0 + 19.2 + 66.8 = \boxed{101.0 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to specific heat capacity.

$$c_p = \frac{C_p}{M} = \frac{101.0 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}}{90 \ \text{g/mol}} = 1.122 \ \text{J/(g·K)} = 1122 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate heat required to heat 1 kg of oxalic acid from 25°C to 95°C.

Given: m = 1 kg, cₚ = 1.122 kJ/(kg·K), ΔT = 95 − 25 = 70 K.

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1 \times 1.122 \times 70$$

$$\boxed{Q = 78.54 \ \text{kJ}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors:** Students frequently forget that combustion ΔH is *negative* (exothermic) and inadvertently flip signs when applying Hess's Law. Always re-check: products of combustion are *more stable* → ΔH < 0.
> 2. **Element ΔH°f Mistake:** Forgetting to multiply ΔH°f(O₂) = 0 by the stoichiometric coefficient 2. Although the value is 0, the *count* of moles matters in summations.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing J and kJ within the same calculation is the single most common reason for mark deductions. **Convert everything to one unit (preferably kJ) at the start.**
> 4. **Kopp's Rule Scope:** Applying Kopp's rule to gases or at very high temperatures — both outside its validity range. The rule is for solids/liquids at ~20°C.
> 5. **Theoretical vs. Actual Yield Confusion:** Theoretical yield is calculated from the **limiting reactant** only, not from the actual feed. Identify the limiting reagent first.
> 6. **Latent Heat Misapplication:** Using $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ across a phase change. If temperature stays constant but energy flows (e.g., boiling water), you *must* use $Q = mL$.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Yield & Selectivity
- [ ] **Yield (η):** Actual product / Theoretical product × 100. Stoichiometric basis.
- [ ] **Selectivity (S):** Desired product / Total products × 100. Pathway purity.
- [ ] **Conversion (X):** Reacted reactant / Fed reactant × 100.
- [ ] **Identity:** Y = X × S (for single side reaction).

### Energy Balance & Heat Capacity
- [ ] **First Law:** ΣE_in = ΣE_out + ΔE_acc (the foundation of all process calculations).
- [ ] **Specific heat (cₚ):** J/(kg·K); **Molar heat (Cₚ):** J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Sensible heat equation:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (no phase change).
- [ ] **Mean heat capacity:** $\bar{C}_p = \frac{\int C_p \, dT}{\Delta T}$ — average over a temperature range.

### Kopp's Rule
- [ ] **Use case:** Estimating Cₚ of solids/liquids at ~20°C.
- [ ] **Equation:** $C_p \approx \sum n_i C_{p,i}$ (sum atomic contributions).
- [ ] **Key atomic values:** C = 7.5, H = 9.6, O = 16.7, S = 22, P = 23, F = 21 J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Limitation:** Errors of 5–25% common; avoid for H-bonded compounds and high T.

### Phase Change Enthalpies (Water Reference)
- [ ] **Fusion (0°C):** L_f = 334 kJ/kg
- [ ] **Vaporization (100°C):** L_v = 2257 kJ/kg
- [ ] **Sublimation (0°C):** L_s ≈ 2838 kJ/kg
- [ ] **Equation:** $Q = m \cdot L$ (constant T during transition).

### Heat of Mixing
- [ ] **Exothermic (ΔH < 0):** Mixing releases heat (e.g., H₂SO₄ + water).
- [ ] **Endothermic (ΔH > 0):** Mixing absorbs heat (e.g., NH₄NO₃ + water, sucrose + water).
- [ ] **Ideal solutions:** ΔH_mix = 0.

### Standard Heat of Formation & Reaction
- [ ] **Reference:** Elements in standard state (1 atm, 25°C) have ΔH°f = 0.
- [ ] **Hess's Law:** $\Delta H°_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{reactants})$.
- [ ] **Multiplication rule:** Multiply each ΔH°f by its stoichiometric coefficient before summing.
- [ ] **Sign interpretation:** Negative = exothermic, Positive = endothermic.

### Food Tech Applications
- [ ] **Pasteurization:** Sensible heat dominates.
- [ ] **Freeze-drying:** Sensible + Latent (fusion) + Latent (sublimation).
- [ ] **Spray drying:** Latent heat of vaporization dominates.
- [ ] **Baking:** Combination of sensible heat and exothermic Maillard reaction.
